eTax is looking for a talented and motivated Mid-Level Flutter Developer to join our dynamic engineering team. In this role, you’ll be responsible for building high-quality, performant, and scalable mobile applications using Flutter. Key Responsibilities
Develop and maintain cross-platform mobile applications using Flutter .

Integrate mobile applications with RESTful APIs and backend services.
Write clean, maintainable, and testable code following best practices.
Troubleshoot and debug issues to improve app performance and stability.

Required:
1-2 years of experience in mobile development.
Strong knowledge of Dart and Flutter widget system (Stateless, Stateful, Custom Widgets).
Experience with state management solutions (Provider, Bloc, Riverpod, etc.).
Solid understanding of RESTful APIs, JSON, and integration patterns..
Familiarity with app deployment on Google Play Store and Apple App Store.
Nice to Have:
Experience with Firebase services (Firestore, Auth, Crashlytics, Cloud Messaging).
Knowledge of native Android (Kotlin/Java) or iOS (Swift) development.
Understanding of software architecture patterns (MVVM, Clean Architecture).
Experience with automated testing (Unit, Widget, Integration).
Performance optimization for mobile apps.

text:
We’re Hiring: Flutter Developer 🚀

Company : Smarther Technologies
 Experience : 1–3 Years
 Location : [chennai - Perumbakkam]

Job Overview:
We are looking for a passionate Flutter Developer to join our development team. The ideal candidate should have experience in building high-quality mobile applications for Android and iOS using Flutter. You will work closely with our design and backend teams to develop scalable and user-friendly mobile applications.

Key Responsibilities:
·        Develop and maintain mobile applications using Flutter framework.
·        Build reusable and efficient code for mobile platforms.
·        Integrate RESTful APIs and third-party services.
·        Collaborate with UI/UX designers to implement responsive designs.
·        Debug, test, and optimize application performance.
·        Ensure the best performance, quality, and responsiveness of applications.
·        Work with cross-functional teams to define, design, and ship new features.

Required Skills:
·        Strong experience in Flutter and Dart programming.
·        Experience in building Android and iOS applications.
·        Knowledge of REST APIs, JSON, and third-party libraries.
·        Understanding of state management (Provider, GetX, Bloc, etc.).
·        Familiarity with Git or version control systems.

text:
"هو انت معاك Mac؟"
السؤال ده أي Flutter Developer بيشتغل iOS أكيد اتسأله قبل كده 😅
الحقيقة؟
معظمنا لأ.
أنا شخصيًا كنت بعتمد على Mac الشركة.
كل ما أحتاج أطلع iOS build أروح أقعد عليه شوية وأخلص شغلي.
الموضوع كان ماشي… لحد ما جه اليوم اللي غير تفكيري.
كنت محتاج أطلع build ضروري.
الـ Mac كان مشغول.
والـ deadline قرب.
ساعتها بدأت أحس إن عندي "Single Point of Failure"
جهاز واحد واقف بيني وبين إن التطبيق ينزل TestFlight 
قلت خلاص… لازم CI/CD.
دورت شوية، وجربت، ولقيت إن Codemagic كانت أسهل بداية:
بتربط الـ GitHub repo
بترفع certificates & provisioning profiles
وبتعمل build على macOS machines جاهزة
وبتطلعلك IPA أو ترفعه مباشرة على App Store Connect
الإحساس إنك تعمل build وانت قاعد على Windows أو Linux كان عظيم 🔥
بس…
بعد أول مشكلة build على CI فهمت درس مهم جدًا 
CI/CD مش بديل للـ Mac.
ليه؟
لأنك لو:
محتاج تفعل Push Notifications
تضيف Capabilities
تعدل Signing
تحل مشكلة في CocoaPods
أو تعدل في Podfile
لازم تفتح Xcode بنفسك.
لازم تفهم provisioning profiles، certificates، signing، وفرق debug و release.
لو build وقع على CI وطلع error في iOS runner…
الأداة مش هتيجي تحلها مكانك.
لازم تكون فاهم البيئة نفسها.
الخلاصة اللي خرجت بيها 
الـ CI/CD بيحل مشكلة “availability”
لكن مش بيعوض نقص فهمك للـ iOS ecosystem.
أفضل سيناريو؟
تخلص setup على Mac
تجرب على Simulator
تتأكد إن كل حاجة stable
وبعدها تعتمد على CI للـ builds المتكررة

لو انت Flutter Developer ولسه معتمد 100% على Mac الشركة
ابدأ فكّر من دلوقتي:
إزاي تقلل الـ dependency دي؟
اللي بيتحكم في عملية الـ build…
بيتحكم في سرعة تسليم المنتج 🚀

text:
Just built something I'm genuinely proud of — FitMotion Pro 🏋️

What's inside FitMotion Pro:
→ Animated splash screen with particle physics
→ 3-page onboarding with custom animated workout characters
→ Glassmorphism UI with dark & light mode switching
→ Animated calorie progress ring + weekly chart
→ 6 workout categories with full exercise plans
→ Hero transitions and staggered list animations
→ Custom painted cartoon characters per workout type
→ Floating bottom navigation with expand animations
→ Built entirely with Flutter + CustomPainter
This project pushed my skills in Flutter animation, CustomPainter, theme systems, and clean architecture. Every transition, every button press, every card entrance — all handcrafted.

Tech stack: Flutter 3 · Dart · fl_chart · flutter_animate · google_fonts · percent_indicator

The goal was simple: build something that looks and feels like a paid App Store product, with no shortcuts.
If you're a Flutter developer, product designer, or just someone who loves clean mobile UI — I'd love your thoughts.

What feature would you build next? Drop it in the comments 👇

text:
🚀 From “It Works on My Device” to Production-Ready React Native Apps
Every React Native developer has said this at least once:
“It works perfectly on my device.”
But real-world apps don’t run on just one device.

They run on:
• Different screen sizes
• Different OS versions
• Low-end & high-end phones
• Slow and unstable networks
That’s where real development begins.
A production-ready React Native app should have:
✅ Proper error handling
✅ Loading states for every API call
✅ Optimized images and assets
✅ Clean state management
✅ Testing on multiple devices
Because users don’t care if it works on your phone.
They care if it works on theirs.
Building apps is easy.

Building reliable apps is the real skill.
Are you testing your app like a developer —
or like a real user?
Let’s talk 👇

What’s the current scene in mobile development? 
Are we now in consensus that we are not going to do Flutter anymore? 

The React Native IMO is interesting space. The vibe coding platforms are doubling down it. 

And the enterprise (or anyone with an actual developer) is feeling that with code generation not being a bottleneck anymore it’s better to write Swift and Kotlin. 

My thoughts are more on this line: if you are starting a new mobile development project today you will use native (as a dev) and will be forced to use React Native if you are a viber. 

How off am I in my reading of this space?

text:
Stop fixing scripts. Start defining intent. 🚀 

After 13+ years in mobile engineering, I’ve realized we are hitting a ceiling with traditional automation. We spend 30% of our sprints babysitting "flaky" tests and updating brittle XPaths. The industry is shifting, moving from Deterministic Automation to Agentic AI.

I just published a brief path on "The Agentic Shift: Future-Proofing Your Mobile Engineering Stack." In this blog, I break down why the future of mobile CI/CD isn't a better script—it's a reasoning agent. 

Key highlights: 
✅ Beyond the Maintenance Trap: Why Agents don't break when your UI changes. 
✅ Solving Fragmentation: One "Goal" that works across the different screens.
✅ The Hybrid Roadmap: Keeping your unit tests deterministic while making E2E journeys autonomous.

If you’re tired of "Red Builds" caused by minor UI tweaks, it’s time to move from Instructions to Intent. Fellow Android devs and Tech Leads—are you still fighting with legacy scripts, or are you exploring Agentic workflows? Share your insights in comments.

text:
One underrated skill in mobile development is being able to work on CI/CD pipelines. Many mobile developers aren’t comfortable tinkering with them, but there’s a lot of value in being confident with scripting, workflows, and digging through CI logs.

DevOps is underrated in general, and it becomes even more prominent on mobile platforms. If you’re comfortable enough, you can build powerful automation, clean one-shot DX setups, and robust CI/CD pipelines with Fastlane. Fastlane paired with GitHub Actions is more than good enough for both iOS and Android, as long as you’re comfortable with mobile DevOps.

Trust me when I say one of the best feelings is getting an automation to work exactly as planned, or seeing your CI/CD pipeline run end-to-end the way you designed it.

CI vs CD — They Solve Different Problems

I used to think CI/CD was one big concept.

Now I see the difference.

CI (Continuous Integration) answers:
Did this new change break anything?

CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment) answers:
How do we release safely and consistently?

One protects integration.
The other protects releases.

Together, they reduce deployment anxiety.

And that clarity matters.

text:
Stop letting "Blob" PRs kill your App Performance

💁‍♀️ Follow me for more technical insights on mobile engineering, CI/CD, and performance optimization.

We’ve all been there. You ship a "small" feature, but somehow the app bundle size jumps by 10MB.
By the time you notice it in production, it’s too late. Your conversion rates drop because of slow download times, and your team is stuck playing "detective" through old commits to find the culprit.
Sentry just solved this.
They’ve introduced Size Analysis Integration into CI, and it’s a game-changer for anyone building iOS or Android apps.

I’ve seen the pain of last-minute mobile build surprises, and that’s why integrating size analysis into CI isn’t a “nice-to-have”; it’s a must-have.
It transforms app size from a passive metric into an active quality gate, helping teams catch regressions early, enforce performance budgets, and ship smarter.

Shipping fast should never mean shipping bloated apps.

By bringing size awareness into CI, we shift quality left, prevent silent growth, and stay in control of our mobile performance from build to store release.
We already automate tests and security. It’s time we automate performance budgets too.

♻️ Repost this article to help your developer network build leaner, smarter apps.

text:
9 березня я завершую свій шлях у Devlight.

Рік тому я прийшов як iOS Department Lead, а згодом отримав промоушн на позицію Head of Mobile.
Цей шлях став для мене історією про те, як технологічна трансформація змінює бізнес-результати.

Сьогодні я йду, залишаючи систему, яка працює автономно. Але головний результат для мене — це зміна культури та мислення команди.

Що вдалося реалізувати:

Від «виконавців» до партнерів.
Усі інженери iOS/Flutter-департаменту сьогодні мислять категоріями продукту. Команда перестала просто писати код за ТЗ — сьогодні вони ставлять критичні запитання, надають пропозиції та дбають про бізнес-результат як справжні партнери. Ми впровадили культуру Continuous Improvement та Continuous Learning: кожен процес проходив цикл «пропозиція — валідація — впровадження — фідбек». Інженери стали співавторами системи, а не просто користувачами гайдлайнів.

AI-driven інженерія.
Ми інтегрували AI-інструменти в повсякденну роботу — від аналізу коду до оптимізації рутинних процесів. Це дозволило сфокусувати більше часу на архітектурних рішеннях та якості, а не на рутинних задачах.

Mobile DNA та масштабування.
Ми вивели інженерні стандарти (архітектура, безпека, SDLC) на новий рівень. На рівні Mobile-департаменту ми ініціювали розширення CI/CD для Flutter-напрямку, що забезпечило QA-команду артефактами для підключення AI-інструментів у процес тестування.

Я вдячний CTO за підтримку підходу, де інженерія є повноцінним партнером продукту.

Компанія переходить у фазу активного масштабування. Я мотивований ролями, де потрібно запускати або системно розвивати інженерні функції.

Мій фокус — Engineering Excellence: створення стійких культур, де код стає інтелектуальним драйвером продукту.
Фундамент у Devlight закладено — і він працює.

Я відкритий до нових можливостей на позиціях:
Head of Mobile / Engineering Manager / Engineering Lead.

Там, де інженерія має бути стратегічним партнером бізнесу.

Devlight — дякую за цей рік.

Команда, продовжуйте ставити правильні запитання і не зупиняйтеся в інноваціях! 🚀

text:
Every time a developer writes code, this kicks in automatically. Here's what it does.

🔵 One Tech Term #8

CI/CD — Continuous Integration & Continuous Delivery

In plain English: CI/CD is like a factory conveyor belt for software. Every time a developer adds new code, it automatically gets tested, built, and prepared for release without anyone having to do it manually. No waiting for a big monthly deployment. No crossing fingers hoping nothing breaks. Small updates ship safely and frequently — sometimes dozens of times a day. Think of it as the difference between releasing one massive album once a year versus dropping singles every week.

Why it matters: CI/CD directly impacts how fast your team can ship value to users. When engineers talk about "pipeline failures," "deployment frequency," or "release cycles" — they're talking about CI/CD. As a PM or TPM, understanding this helps you set realistic timelines, diagnose slowdowns, and push for faster, safer delivery.

text:
Integrating Data CI/CD Pipeline to Machine Learning (ML) Applications

"Building robust Machine Learning (ML) applications demands meticulous version control for all components: code, models, and the data that powers them.
For data specifically, it's critical to maintain data quality and detect destructive data drift once the application is running in production."

"A Data CI/CD pipeline is an extension of the Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery practices, specifically tailored to automate the entire process of getting data into the model lifecycle. [...]
Continuous Integration (CI) automates the testing and validation of data processing code and the data itself. The automation process involves:
- Running the ETL pipeline to extract, transform, and load updated data into the feature store, and
- Validating data by checking the incoming data for quality issues and detecting schema changes, missing values, outliers, and distribution shifts.
Continuous Delivery/Deployment (CD) automates the delivery of validated, production-ready data to downstream systems like model training process. The automation process involves:
- Versioning the data to ensure reproducibility where the data and model artifacts trained on the data are linked together, and 
- Deploying the data (features) to a feature store for training and serving, ensuring consistency of the data."

By Kuriko IWAI at Level Up Coding

text:
CI/CD, aka Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery, is your pipeline to get new software to users quickly and reliably. It's all about automating the build, test, and deployment process, so you can ship code faster and with fewer headaches.

A typical CI/CD pipeline has several connected stages:

➟ Developer commits code changes to source control (such Git)

➟ CI server detects changes and triggers build 

➟ Code is compiled, tested (unit, integration tests) 

➟ Test results reported to developer 

➟ On success, artifacts are deployed to staging environments 

➟ Further testing may be done on staging before release (e.g., User Acceptance Testing, Performance Testing) 

➟ CD system deploys approved changes to production (Manual approvals required in few cases)

➟ Continuous Monitoring of the application in production 


Stay connected to Aashay Gupta, CISM,GCP for content related to Cybersecurity.

text:
🚀 CI/CD: The Silent Superpower Behind Modern Software Delivery
In today’s fast-paced tech world, speed is important — but reliable speed is everything. That’s where CI/CD pipelines step in and completely transform the game.

🔹 Continuous Integration (CI) ensures that every code change is automatically built and tested.
🔹 Continuous Deployment/Delivery (CD) makes sure validated code reaches production quickly and safely.

But CI/CD is not just a DevOps buzzword anymore — it’s a business enabler.

✨ Why CI/CD is Critical in Today’s Workflow

✅ Faster time to market — ship features in hours, not weeks
✅ Early bug detection — catch issues before they become costly
✅ Consistent deployments — eliminate the “works on my machine” problem
✅ Better team collaboration — developers, QA, and ops stay in sync
✅ Higher confidence in releases — automation reduces human error

In an era of microservices, cloud-native apps, and AI-driven systems, manual deployments simply can’t keep up. Organizations that embrace CI/CD don’t just move faster — they innovate faster.

💡 Pro tip: A good pipeline is not just about automation. It’s about building a culture of continuous improvement, quality, and feedback.

Whether you’re working with Jenkins, GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps, or GitLab CI, investing in a solid CI/CD pipeline today is investing in your product’s future.

text:
🚀 How We Implement CI/CD for Mobile Apps Using GitHub Actions
One of the biggest productivity improvements in mobile development comes from implementing a proper CI/CD pipeline.
Without automation, teams spend too much time on manual builds, testing, and sharing APK or IPA files.
Here is the standard CI/CD flow we follow for mobile apps (Flutter or Android).

⚙ 1. Code Integration Stage
Every developer works on a feature branch.

Typical workflow
Developer pushes code → Pull Request is created → GitHub Actions runs validation checks.
The pipeline runs
• Code linting and formatting
 • Static analysis
 • Unit tests
 • Dependency checks

If any step fails, the PR cannot be merged.

Purpose
 Maintain code quality before merging into the main branch.

🏗 2. Automated Build Pipeline
Once the PR is merged into the main or develop branch, the build workflow starts automatically.

Typical pipeline steps
• Checkout repository
 • Setup Flutter or Android SDK
 • Install dependencies
 • Run tests
 • Build APK, AAB, or IPA

Purpose
 Ensure every commit generates a valid build.

📦 3. Artifact Management

After a successful build, CI uploads build artifacts automatically.
Examples
• APK
 • AAB
 • iOS IPA
 • Debug builds for QA

QA teams can download builds directly from the pipeline.

Purpose
 Remove the need for developers to manually share builds.

📲 4. Automated Distribution

The next stage distributes builds automatically to testers.
Common integrations
• Firebase App Distribution
 • TestFlight
 • Play Store Internal Testing

Typical flow
Main branch commit → Build generated → Uploaded to Firebase → QA receives install link.

Purpose
 Faster testing and feedback cycles.

🚀 5. Release Pipeline
Production releases usually run through a controlled workflow.
Typical steps
• Version update
 • Production build
 • Code signing
 • App Store or Play Store deployment

Purpose
 Secure and reproducible releases.

This transforms mobile development from a manual process into an automated system.

text:
🚀 Understanding CI/CD in 2 Minutes
Think of a CI/CD pipeline as a highly efficient production system for your software.
Every code change moves through a structured, automated workflow:
🔹 Code Commit – Developers push changes to a version control system like Git.
🔹 Build – The application is compiled, packaged, and dependencies are resolved.
🔹 Test – Automated unit, integration, and security tests validate quality.
🔹 Deploy – The validated build is released to staging or production environments.
This automated flow is what we call a CI/CD pipeline — Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery (or Deployment).
The goal is simple:
✔ Faster release cycles
✔ Reduced human error
✔ Higher software quality
✔ Consistent, repeatable deployments
Modern tools such as Jenkins and GitHub Actions help teams automate this entire lifecycle — from commit to production.
When implemented correctly, CI/CD transforms software delivery from a risky manual process into a predictable, scalable engineering practice.
How mature is your CI/CD pipeline today?

text:
𝗔 𝗪𝗢𝗥𝗟𝗗 𝗪𝗜𝗧𝗛𝗢𝗨𝗧 𝗖𝗜/𝗖𝗗

We joke about it… but this is what most teams still look like under the hood.

• Manual deployments
• “It works on my machine”
• No automated tests
• No rollback strategy
• Friday releases with crossed fingers

And when something breaks?

Everyone jumps into Slack.
Nobody knows what changed.
Logs are half-configured.
Hotfix in production.
Repeat next week.

CI/CD isn’t about speed for the sake of speed.

It’s about:

• Predictability
• Traceability
• Fast feedback loops
• Safe, repeatable deployments
• Reducing human error

A solid pipeline means:
Code → Test → Scan → Build → Deploy → Monitor

Every single time.
No guesswork. No chaos.

If your deployment process depends on one “DevOps guy” remembering steps from memory… you don’t have a system. You have risk.

Automation is stability.

CoderCo

text:
CI/CD, aka Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery, is your pipeline to get new software to users quickly and reliably. It's all about automating the build, test, and deployment process, so you can ship code faster and with fewer headaches.

A typical CI/CD pipeline has several connected stages:

➟ Developer commits code changes to source control (such Git)

➟ CI server detects changes and triggers build 

➟ Code is compiled, tested (unit, integration tests) 

➟ Test results reported to developer 

➟ On success, artifacts are deployed to staging environments 

➟ Further testing may be done on staging before release (e.g., User Acceptance Testing, Performance Testing) 

➟ CD system deploys approved changes to production (Manual approvals required in few cases)

➟ Continuous Monitoring of the application in production 

Please follow Divye Dwivedi for such content.

text:
No more endless manual deployments! 🚀

After several iterations (and a few battles with Apple and Google configurations 😅), the CI/CD architecture for @Suntro is finally up and running, fully automated on GitHub Actions.

When building a cross-platform mobile app, build and deployment times can quickly become a bottleneck. Here is how I structured our pipeline to combine safety and speed:
🛡️ 1. The Gatekeeper (Analyze & Test): The crucial first step. If static analysis or a single test fails, everything stops. We don’t waste build minutes on broken code. 

⚡ 2. Massive Parallelization: Once the code is validated, we simultaneously trigger the AAB (Android) build, the IPA (iOS) build, AND the Shorebird release preparation for our future over-the-air (OTA) updates. 

📦 3. Continuous Deployment: As soon as the builds are ready, they are automatically shipped to the Google Play Console and Apple TestFlight.

The result? A monumental time saver and significantly reduced mental load. I can now focus 100% on shipping value (especially our family "Clan" sync 🐾) instead of watching loading bars.

What does your mobile pipeline look like? Are you team GitHub Actions, Fastlane, or Codemagic? 👇

 🏢 Work model: Hybrid (2–3 days/week from the office)
 📄 Contract: Full-time employment (CIM)

📱 For Android Developers
We’re especially interested if you have experience with:
• Kotlin & modern Android development
 • Jetpack Compose (solid hands-on experience)
 • REST API integrations
 • Mobile architecture patterns
 • CI/CD pipelines and modern development workflows
Nice to have: Firebase services, monitoring tools, or integrations with SDKs like camera, document scanning or e-signature.

🍏 For iOS Developers
We’d love to talk if you’re strong in:
• Swift & SwiftUI
 • Building scalable mobile applications
 • REST API integrations
 • Modern iOS architecture patterns
 • CI/CD pipelines and development best practices
Nice to have: Firebase tools, logging/monitoring solutions or experience integrating external SDKs.

💡 Why this role is interesting for developers
You’ll work in cross-functional teams, build production-grade mobile apps, and have the chance to contribute to architecture, performance and product quality, not just feature delivery.

💬 If you're an Android or iOS developer based in Bucharest and open to hybrid work, let’s connect.

📩 Send me a message or tag someone who might be interested.

<< Mobile Claude Code 

“take a walk, see the sun, walk your dog without losing your flow."

This "Remote Control" is not a cloud-based replacement for local development, but a portal into it. According to official documentation, the core value is that "Claude keeps running on your machine, and you can control the session from the Claude app." 

This ensures that local context—filesystem access, environment variables, and Model Context Protocol (MCP)servers—remains active and reachable even if the user is miles away from their desk.

Claude Code in CI/CD Pipelines

Claude Code shouldn’t just assist developers — it should live inside your CI/CD pipeline. 🔄

Imagine every pull request automatically triggering:
🧪 Test generation suggestions
📝 Documentation updates
🔍 Static analysis explanations
🛡️ Security improvement hints

It becomes your 24/7 junior reviewer.

Humans still approve.
But obvious mistakes get filtered earlier.

That shortens feedback loops.

And shorter loops = faster shipping. 🚀

The key?
Automate carefully.
Start with suggestions, not auto-merges.

Control the system. Don’t let it control you.

👉 Drop `AI` and I’ll send you the CI/CD integration blueprint.

AI panic is loud.

Reality is quieter.

Claude Code removes:
❌ Boilerplate
❌ Repetitive debugging
❌ Manual documentation

It enhances:
✅ Architecture thinking

Developers become higher-leverage operators.

This is augmentation. Not replacement.

Companies that frame it correctly see adoption explode.

Fear slows innovation.
Clarity accelerates it.

text:
Most teams stop at automated builds and call it “mobile CI/CD”. That’s the easy part.

The real pain shows up later:
1. Store submissions still happen manually
2. Hotfixes wait days for reviews
3. Release branches drift from what users actually run
4. Every “small fix” feels risky

Expo EAS gets interesting after builds. When:
1. eas submit removes Transporter + Play Console gymnastics
2. OTA updates turn small bugs into minutes, not weeks
3. eas.json becomes a release contract, not config noise

At that point, releases stop being events and start being infrastructure.
This post goes deep into:
1. automating submissions end-to-end
2. using OTA updates safely (not recklessly)
3. designing workflows that scale past “one app, one dev”

Read the full blog by Pushkar Thakur: https://lnkd.in/g_TgadmJ

text:
Why every serious iOS team should invest in CI/CD early

Recently, I implemented a CI/CD pipeline for an iOS project using GitHub Actions + Fastlane — and the impact is immediate.

GitHub Actions handles the workflow automation with macOS runners, while Fastlane manages builds, testing, code signing, and TestFlight distribution.

Current flow:
Code push → Automated build → Tests → TestFlight release

What looks like a small engineering improvement actually compounds fast:
• Faster release cycles
• Fewer manual errors
• Consistent build quality
• Easier team collaboration
• Production-ready delivery process

For early-stage products, this is one of the highest-leverage investments you can make in engineering velocity.

Next on my list:
• Auto versioning
• Build health monitoring
• Release confidence metrics

text:
I set out to build an AI-powered sports analytics copilot for iOS.
The hardest problem wasn't the ML models. It wasn't the data pipelines. It wasn't real-time analytics.

It was securing an LLM that talks directly to users on their phones.

Shipping AI inside a native iOS app changes the threat model completely. There's no corporate proxy, no WAF, no SSO layer between your model and every user who downloads it. You're handing your system prompt, your tools, and your API budget to the public.

So I built a 6-layer security architecture from scratch — starting with on-device NaturalLanguage filtering before a single byte hits the network, all the way through MCP tool invariants, JWT user isolation, and a response sanitizer that scrubs the AI's own output for IP leakage on the way out.

Full breakdown in the article. If you're building AI-powered consumer apps, this shift in security thinking matters more than most people realize.

P.S. The NBA model is sitting at 62.6% accuracy. This was science. Mostly.

text:
🚀 End-to-End CI/CD + Observability Pipeline (Node.js)
I built a production-style DevOps pipeline designed around reliability, traceability, and lifecycle management not just deployment.
🏗 Stack Overview
API: Node.js
CI: Jenkins
Containerization: Docker
Registry: Docker Hub
Monitoring: Prometheus
Visualization: Grafana
⚙ Pipeline Design
1️⃣ Quality Gate (Fail Fast Model)
Jenkins triggers npm test before image build
Pipeline halts on failure
Prevents unstable artifacts from reaching registry
2️⃣ Deterministic Image Strategy
Docker images tagged with unique Build IDs
Enables precise rollback
Avoids “latest tag” ambiguity
3️⃣ Automated Deployment Flow
Post-push trigger refreshes running container
Minimal downtime strategy
Reduced manual intervention
4️⃣ Observability Layer
Prometheus scrapes:
CPU
RAM
Request rate
Grafana dashboards visualize live metrics
Enables proactive performance management
🧠 Engineering Insight
The hardest part wasn’t integration — it was lifecycle control.
I implemented:
Post-build cleanup automation
Image pruning strategy
Workspace management
Because CI servers that aren’t maintained become the next production outage.
🔗 Repo + IaC Setup: 
https://lnkd.in/eT82zYyn
Happy to discuss pipeline optimization, scaling strategies, or observability patterns.

text:
☁️ Building Reliable Data Pipelines on Azure
Modern data platforms demand more than just ETL scripts. They require automated, secure, and scalable pipelines that can handle production workloads without constant manual intervention.
In a recent setup, we built data pipelines using Azure Synapse and Azure SQL, with deployments fully automated through Azure DevOps. Infrastructure and pipeline definitions were version-controlled, and every change moved through CI/CD stages before reaching production. This reduced manual errors, improved traceability, and made rollbacks straightforward during incidents.
Monitoring, alerting, and access controls were built directly into the platform. The result was consistent data movement, faster release cycles, and fewer production surprises. Strong data engineering today is as much about DevOps discipline as it is about SQL or transformation logic.

text:
Common pipeline mistakes (and simple fixes) 🧰

I see the same failures everywhere — not because tools are bad, but because rules are missing.

Mistake → Fix
 1. Blocking on severity only
 → Add reachability / exploitability filter
 2. Findings have no owner
 → Map every finding to a service/team automatically
 3. No time rule
 → Define SLAs (High=7d, Medium=30d, Low=backlog)
 4. DAST treated like a one-off
 → Integrate into pre-release stage
 5. Reporting raw counts
 → Report actionable + owned + trending

The pipeline isn’t broken.
The rules are.

Which mistake do you see most?

𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗢𝗽𝘀 𝗹𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 #𝟲:

Over time, CI/CD pipelines tend to become “catch-all systems.”

We add:

 • 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝘀𝗰𝗮𝗻𝘀
 • 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴
 • 𝗜𝗺𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝘀
 • 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆 𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗰𝗸𝘀
 • 𝗧𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝘀𝘂𝗶𝘁𝗲𝘀
 • 𝗣𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗰𝘆 𝘃𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
 • 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗹𝗼𝗴𝗶𝗰
 • 𝗡𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀

Individually, each addition makes sense.
Collectively, they can turn a simple build into a fragile orchestration layer.

The more responsibilities a pipeline carries,
the harder it becomes to debug, scale, and reason about.

Good pipelines don’t just automate — they stay intentional.

Sometimes the best optimization isn’t adding another step — it’s removing one.

text:
🔁 Building CI/CD Pipelines Is Easy. Maintaining Them Is the Real Work.
Most teams can set up a pipeline that builds and deploys code. The real challenge starts months later, when the pipeline grows complex, slow, and fragile.
We recently inherited a CI/CD setup where builds took over 40 minutes. Multiple redundant steps, environment-specific scripts, and manual approvals were slowing everything down. Developers were waiting longer for the pipeline than they were for code reviews.
We fixed it by simplifying stages, reusing build artifacts, and removing environment-specific logic from the pipeline. Build times dropped by more than half, and deployments became predictable again.
CI/CD isn’t a one-time setup. It’s an evolving system that needs regular cleanup, standardization, and performance tuning.
How often does your team review and optimize its pipelines?

text:
How AI Can Transform DevOps Pipelines?

AI has the potential to significantly evolve how we design, manage, and optimize DevOps pipelines. Here are a few impactful use cases:

🔁 **Self-Healing Pipelines**
Imagine pipelines that can automatically detect failures related to third-party dependencies, fix the underlying bash scripts, and re-run the jobs without manual intervention.
While certain exceptions (like permission-related issues) may still require human oversight, AI can proactively suggest precise remediation steps — reducing downtime and accelerating delivery.

🔍 **Self-Auditing Pipelines**
AI can continuously scan pipeline configurations to identify hard-coded parameters, exposed secrets, or insecure patterns.
Instead of discovering these during audits or incidents, teams can receive real-time recommendations to fix vulnerabilities before they become risks.

🧠 **From YAML to Prompt-Driven Configurations**
What if, instead of manually writing YAML files, we defined pipelines using high-level prompts?
AI could generate and manage the YAML behind the scenes — enabling:

* Faster pipeline creation
* Reduced configuration errors
* More intuitive DevOps workflows

The future of DevOps may not just be automated — it may be intelligent, adaptive, and self-optimizing.

What are your thoughts on AI-native pipelines? 👇

text:
Manual releases are career risks waiting to happen.

Early in my iOS career, releases meant someone running scripts from their laptop. One wrong keystroke. One missed step. Chaos.

Here's what I learned building mobile CI/CD pipelines:

Automate certificate and provisioning management.
Nothing breaks builds like expired certs at 11pm before a deadline. Use Fastlane match or similar. Your future self will thank you.

Build and test on every PR.
Catching issues before merge is 10x cheaper than catching them in production. No exceptions.

Keep build times under 15 minutes.
Slow pipelines kill productivity. Developers stop running them. Parallelize. Cache dependencies. Optimize.

Version everything automatically.
Manual version bumping is error prone. Let your pipeline handle build numbers. Consistency beats heroics.

Test on real devices, not just simulators.
Simulators lie. That animation that's smooth in the simulator? It stutters on a 3 year old phone.

Make rollbacks trivial.
Something broke in production? Flipping back should be one button. Not a 2 hour scramble.

The goal isn't automation for its own sake.

It's confidence.

Confidence that every release is consistent, tested, and reversible.

What's your biggest CI/CD lesson?

Last time I was here, I was celebrating publishing Fua Chap Chap on Google Play.
Today, I’m celebrating something even more complex.

I love challenging myself, and this time I decided to fully experience what it takes to ship an iOS app into Apple’s ecosystem.
If you’ve ever published on the App Store, you know it’s not just “click and upload.”
This journey involved:
Managing Apple certificates and provisioning profiles
Fighting with code signing in Xcode
Archiving and exporting production builds
Configuring App Store Connect correctly
Setting up internal & external TestFlight testers
Preparing screenshots, compliance information, and metadata
There were failed builds.
 There were signing mismatches.
 There were moments Xcode truly tested my patience 😅
But step by step, it came together.
Beyond just publishing, this experience strengthened my understanding of:
Production deployment pipelines
Apple’s strict review standards
Beta testing workflows
What it truly means to ship production-ready software
Building is exciting.
 Shipping is discipline.
Now we wait for Apple’s feedback.
On to the next challenge 💪

text:
🚀 Automating Flutter App Deployment with GitHub Actions!

As mobile developers, we spend a lot of time on repetitive tasks — building, testing, and deploying. What if you could automate all of that with just a YAML file? 🤔

That's exactly what GitHub Actions does for my Flutter app! Here's what my CI/CD pipeline handles automatically:
✅ Build & Test — Every pull request triggers automated unit & widget tests, so broken code never reaches main
📦 Build APK & IPA — The pipeline builds both Android & iOS artifacts on every merge, no manual steps needed
🔍 Code Quality Checks — Flutter analyze & dart format run on every push to keep the codebase clean
🚀 Automated Deployment — Pushes directly to Firebase App Distribution or the Play Store with zero clicks
🔐 Secure Secrets Management — Keystore files, API keys, and signing certs are stored safely as GitHub Secrets

The result? More time writing features, less time babysitting deployments. 🎯
💡 Pro tip: Use flutter pub get caching in your workflow to cut build times by up to 40%!
If you're still building and deploying your Flutter app manually, it's time to level up! 💪

Have you set up CI/CD for your Flutter projects? Drop your experience in the comments! 👇

When I moved into Mobile Development (React Native) last November, my first observation wasn’t about the code — it was about the clock ⏱️.

While learning the mobile stack, I noticed something odd.

My local machine generated APKs in under 20 minutes.
Our CI pipeline was taking nearly 1 hour 40 minutes.

Waiting that long for a simple bug-fix build was slowing everyone down. Even as someone new to the team, I couldn’t accept that as normal.

Instead of writing it off as “CI overhead,” I started using part of my day to dig into where that time was actually going — and in the process, deepened my understanding of how React Native and native builds compilation actually work. 🔎

With strong coordination from our DevOps team — especially Shailesh Singh Rawat for enabling cloned pipelines for experimentation, and Harsh Kumar and Jitesh Aggarwal for infrastructure support — I identified several system-level optimizations:
 • Pipeline caching strategies
 • Proper Gradle cache reuse for Android
 • CocoaPods behavior in ephemeral environments
 • Runner architecture differences (Intel vs Apple Silicon)

The Impact
🚀 Android: 1h 38m ➝ 18m (~80% faster)
 • Enabled Gradle build cache + parallel execution
 • Migrated macOS ➝ ubuntu-latest

🍏 iOS: 1h 42m ➝ ~35m (~65% faster)
 • Implemented Pods caching
 • Upgraded to Xcode 16.4
 • Migrated to macOS-15-arm64 (Apple Silicon) runners

What started as a simple question turned into a team-wide improvement that now saves hours of CI waiting time every week. 📈

Grateful to Manas Kumar for onboarding me and building my fundamentals around compilation, and to our manager Shashikant Singh for the trust and support to pursue this initiative.

Sometimes bottlenecks persist not because they’re complex — but because they’ve stopped being questioned.

text:
Yesterday, Android refused to build.
iOS worked.
macOS worked.

But Android? Completely broken.

What looked like a simple flutter run turned into:

• JVM target mismatch errors
• Corrupted Gradle cache
• Kotlin configuration conflicts
• Java not detected on my machine
• Build pipeline failures

At one point, nothing was compiling.

Instead of jumping to StackOverflow for copy-paste fixes, I decided to understand what was actually happening:

Why was Kotlin targeting 21?
Why was Java compiling at 1.8?
How does Gradle actually resolve toolchains?

After installing and configuring OpenJDK 17 properly, aligning Kotlin + Java targets, cleaning corrupted Gradle caches, and regenerating the Android project…

Everything finally ran.

Android ✅
iOS ✅
macOS ✅

All simultaneously.

Moments like this remind me:

Software engineering isn’t just about writing features.
It’s about understanding systems deeply enough to fix what breaks.

Now the environment is stable.

Time to build real products 🚀

What was the most frustrating setup bug you’ve ever fixed?

The developer experience getting there shouldn’t be painful though.

If you haven’t read his post, “The layer that was missing on your phone”, I highly recommend it:
https://lnkd.in/ec9P3BRF

I wanted to contribute to this vision by tackling one of the most frustrating parts of iOS development: dynamic app containers.
If you’ve ever built on-device LLM apps for iOS, you know the pain:
• Tight sandboxing
• Randomized UUID paths
• Multi-GB models
• Broken hardcoded paths
• Painful physical-device testing

So I shipped a PR to dust-llm-capacitor to remove this friction.
What I built:
- Dynamic Swift Path Resolution
Paths starting with Documents/ now automatically resolve to the correct sandbox path.
- Zero-touch Physical Device Orchestration
Rewrote the Node.js E2E runner to:
Detect connected iPhones
Handle Xcode provisioning
Push multi-GB GGUF models via devicectl
- Pre-Compilation Config Injection
Test orchestrator is patched before signing, fully compliant with iOS security.
- Optional Simulator UI
Added --open-simulator flag for visual debugging.

The result?
A true one-click developer experience.
Run:
npm run test:ios
And it:
✔️ Signs the app
✔️ Pushes the model
✔️ Installs on your iPhone

✔️ No manual steps
Hands-free 🫳 🎤

text:
🚀 Demystifying CI/CD Pipelines: A Step-by-Step Guide for Modern Software Development 🚀

If you’re new to DevOps or want to deepen your understanding of Continuous Integration (CI) and Continuous Delivery (CD), this post breaks down the essential pipeline stages that power fast, reliable software delivery. Let’s dive in! 👇

🔍 What is a CI/CD Pipeline?

A CI/CD pipeline is an automated workflow that takes your code from development to production ensuring every change is tested, validated, and deployed safely and efficiently.

🛠️ Key Stages of a CI/CD Pipeline

1️⃣ Code Commit & Version Control 
- Developers write code and push changes to a Git repository (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) 🐙 
- Version control tracks every change, enabling collaboration and rollback if needed 🔄 

2️⃣ Build & Automated Testing 
- The pipeline automatically pulls the latest code and builds the application 🏗️ 
- Runs unit tests to verify individual components work correctly 🧪 
- Executes integration tests to ensure different parts of the app work together 🤝 
- Runs end-to-end (E2E) tests simulating real user scenarios to catch bugs early 🕵️‍♂️ 

3️⃣ Continuous Delivery & Deployment 
- If all tests pass, the pipeline packages the build and deploys it to staging or production environments 🚀 
- Automated deployment reduces human error and speeds up release cycles ⏩ 

4️⃣ Monitoring & Feedback 
- Logs and metrics provide real-time insights into application health and performance 📊 
- Enables quick detection and resolution of issues, improving reliability and user experience ⚡ 

💡 Why CI/CD is a Must-Have

- Faster Releases: Automate repetitive tasks and ship updates multiple times a day ⏱️ 
- Higher Quality: Early bug detection through automated testing reduces costly production issues 🐞 
- Better Collaboration: Developers, testers, and operations teams work in sync with transparent workflows 🤝 
- Enhanced Security: Integrate security scans early to catch vulnerabilities before deployment 🔐 

🔥 Pro Tip: 
Incorporate security testing (SAST, DAST) into your CI/CD pipeline to build secure software from day one. Security is not an afterthought! 🛡️

CI/CD pipelines are the foundation of modern software engineering enabling teams to deliver faster, safer, and smarter.

💡 The goal?
 To design a production-style pipeline that enforces automation, quality control, and scalable deployments — just like modern engineering teams use in real-world environments.

🔄 What This Pipeline Does

📥 Automated Code Fetching
 Pulls source code directly from GitHub, enabling continuous integration workflows.

🔨 Build & Package
 Compiles the application using Maven & JDK 17 and archives artifacts for traceability.

🧪 Unit Testing
 Runs automated tests to validate application stability before moving 
forward.

🔍 Static Code Analysis
 Integrates Checkstyle and SonarQube to analyze:
 • Code quality
 • Technical debt
 • Code smells
 • Maintainability

🚦 Quality Gate Enforcement
 The pipeline automatically stops if quality standards aren’t met — ensuring only production-ready code moves forward.

🐳 Docker Image Build (Multi-Stage)
 Creates optimized container images for efficiency and smaller footprint.

📦 Image Versioning & Registry Push
 Tags and pushes images to Amazon ECR using both build numbers and latest for traceability.

☁️ Deployment to ECS
 Triggers rolling updates on Amazon ECS, enabling zero-downtime deployments.

🎯 Key Skills Demonstrated (Recruiter Highlights)
✔ End-to-end CI/CD pipeline design
 ✔ Containerization & image lifecycle management
 ✔ DevSecOps practices with enforced quality gates
 ✔ Secure AWS credential integration in pipelines

 ✔ Troubleshooting & build optimization

This project reinforced something critical:
⚙️ Automation reduces risk.

I’m continuously refining my expertise in CI/CD architecture, container orchestration, and AWS-based deployments — and I’m excited to contribute to teams focused on high-performance, cloud-native engineering.

text:
New Swift in Practice: Building Real-Time Computer Vision Pipelines in Swift (Without Dropping Frames)

The production lessons:
Memory bandwidth matters Copying pixel buffers at 60 FPS = 475MB/second of bandwidth. Your GPU throttles in seconds.

Frame budget is tight 16.67ms per frame at 60 FPS. Task creation overhead (100-200μs) is nearly 1% of your budget. Every microsecond counts.

Thermal kills performance silently At 85-92°C, iOS throttles GPU performance by 40%. Your 60 FPS pipeline drops to 35 FPS. No crash. No error. Just slower.

What's inside:
Zero-copy bridging with CVMetalTextureCache
Threading patterns that survive sustained load
Production failure modes (thermal, lifecycle, GPU starvation)
When to use Core Image/Vision/Accelerate instead
When NOT to optimize
From iPhone 5s to A17. Under sustained load. In production.👇
What's the hardest camera pipeline challenge you've solved?

text:
🚨Apple recently dropped some big news: by April 2025, every iOS app hitting the App Store must be built with Xcode 16 & iOS 18 SDK. No exceptions.

For dev teams, that means:
 • Rebuilding apps before deadlines 
 • Pipeline compatibility with ARM64 
 • Testing on current & beta versions early

The risk? App Store rejections, delays, outdated pipelines, and security risks.

The flip side? Teams shifting to Mac Arm infra are not only compliant — they’re shipping faster (30–40% faster builds)

What’s the biggest headache for your iOS pipeline right now — staying compliant or keeping velocity?

text:
Small tweak, big impact: Cutting CI build time by ~62%. 🚀

I was recently working on improving the CI pipeline for my personal iOS project. I noticed that running unit tests was taking way too long—around 14 minutes per run.

The main culprits? Fetching 5 SPM (Swift Package Manager) dependencies and rebuilding the project from scratch every single time.

🛠️ The Fix: I implemented caching for both the dependencies and the build folders.

⏱️ The Result: The total runtime dropped from ~14 minutes to just ~5 minutes.

It’s a satisfying win. A faster feedback loop means less context switching and a happier developer experience.

How do you optimize your iOS CI pipelines? Let’s connect!

text:
Over the past month, I've been building MolBuilder, an open-source Python toolkit for process chemistry.

The problem: most cheminformatics tools stop at molecular properties. If you're a process chemist, you still have to manually figure out reactor type, reaction conditions, safety assessment, and cost estimation for each synthesis step.

MolBuilder automates that full pipeline. 

Give it a SMILES string:
 1. It plans a synthesis route (91 reaction templates, beam search)
 2. Predicts optimal conditions (analyzing substrate sterics and electronics)
 3. Selects a reactor (batch/CSTR/PFR/microreactor)
 4. Runs GHS safety assessment (69 hazard codes, PPE, emergency procedures)
 5. Estimates costs (materials, labor, equipment, energy, waste)
 6. Analyzes scale-up (batch sizing, capital costs, annual capacity)

It's pure Python, pip-installable, no RDKit needed (though RDKit can be used as an optional backend). Apache 2.0 licensed with 1,280+ tests.

Especially interested in feedback from process development teams at CDMOs and small biotechs - does this align with your workflow needs?

GitHub: https://lnkd.in/eAwmMiqw
PyPI: pip install molbuilder

text:
AI is often described as if it could replace design work. In this project, I treated AI as a partner: useful for automating repetitive tasks like formatting JSON, checking consistency, and smoothing the messy handoff between design and code, but never a substitute for architectural judgment. My focus was on building a design system that holds up once it reaches development, not on chasing AI hype.

I built a JSON-to-multi-platform pipeline that transforms Figma exports into production-ready tokens. By layering primitives, aliases, and semantic tokens, the system automatically generates brand- and theme-specific outputs for web, iOS, and Android. 

This reduced manual work, kept design and code aligned, and proved that scalable, multi-brand systems depend as much on clear structure and modularity as they do on automation.

text:
🚀 iOS CI/CD: One-Click TestFlight Deployments with Fastlane + GitHub Actions
I recently built a full CI/CD pipeline for our iOS app that automates the entire TestFlight process — no more manual uploads, build versioning, or local signing steps.

🧠 What’s in the pipeline:

✅ GitHub Actions
Triggered via workflow_dispatch with inputs for branch and environment
Intelligent caching for:
Node modules
CocoaPods
Ruby gems
Xcode DerivedData
Dynamically writes the .env file based on selected environment.

✅ Fastlane (Beta Lane)
Uses App Store Connect API Key (no username/password or session refresh issues)
Fetches signing certificates & provisioning profiles from a secure private Git repo using match
Increments build number using latest_testflight_build_number
Generates changelog from Git commits
Builds the iOS app with Xcode
Uploads to TestFlight
Sends a slack notification to internal team

📸 Attached:
A real Slack notification from a successful release.

🚀 Result: Push to TestFlight in minutes — with full automation and no manual steps.

text:
🚀 Why CI/CD Pipelines Matter in Mobile App Development

In mobile development, shipping code is only half the job.
Shipping it reliably, repeatedly, and confidently is where pipelines make the real difference.

Over time, I’ve learned that a solid mobile CI/CD pipeline helps teams:
 ✅ Catch bugs early with automated builds & tests
 ✅ Reduce “works on my machine” issues
 ✅ Speed up release cycles for iOS & Android
 ✅ Improve collaboration between developers and QA
 ✅ Ship with confidence — not anxiety

A typical mobile pipeline I’ve worked with includes:
 🔹 Code pushed to repo (GitHub / GitLab)
 🔹 Automated linting & unit tests
 🔹 Build generation (Android & iOS)
 🔹 Environment-based configs (dev / staging / prod)
 🔹 Distribution via TestFlight / Firebase App Distribution
 🔹 Release-ready artifacts for App Store & Play Store

It’s an upfront investment — but it saves countless hours and prevents last-minute chaos before releases.

Curious how other teams are setting up their mobile pipelines. What’s working well for you?

A well-designed diagram can simplify even the most complex engineering concepts. 

This CI/CD Pipeline illustration provides a structured, professional view of how modern applications move from development to production in a reliable, automated manner.

Pipeline Overview:

• Developer commits code changes 
• GitHub PR & Merge enables collaboration and controlled integration 
• CI Automation (GitHub Actions) builds and tests the application automatically 
• Code Quality Analysis ensures standards and reliability before deployment 
• Docker Image packages code with its runtime environment for consistency 
• Docker Hub acts as the centralized image repository 
• Staging Environment validates application behavior before release 
• Kubernetes Deployment manages application versions and scalability 
• Monitoring provides real-time system insights 
• Canary Deployment (Argo Rollouts) releases updates gradually to minimize risk

text:
React ≠ React Native.

And this confusion is costing startups months.

I’ve seen founders hire a “React developer” thinking they can handle the mobile app too.

Then the delays begin.

React (Web):
- Runs in the browser
- Uses HTML & CSS
- Optimized for desktop/mobile web
- Ships as a website

React Native (Mobile):
- Runs on iOS & Android devices
- Uses native components (no HTML)
- Different navigation & performance rules
- Ships through App Store & Play Store

Yes, the syntax feels similar.

But building a production-ready mobile app is a completely different execution game.

Different build pipelines. 
Different debugging process. 
Different performance constraints. 
Different deployment cycles.

I’ve seen teams lose 8–12 weeks trying to “reuse web code” that was never meant for mobile.

When investors are waiting, that delay is expensive.

If mobile is part of your roadmap, hire for the right expertise from day one.

It’s not about saving money.
It’s about protecting momentum.

Have you seen this mistake happening around?

text:
I just made Zed's GPUI framework run on iOS and Android. 🦀📱

That means you can now build fully native mobile apps in pure Rust.
No Swift bridging. No JNI boilerplate. No JavaScript runtime. Just Rust — all the way down.

Here's what's happening under the hood:
→ GPUI handles the UI layer (the same GPU-accelerated framework powering Zed editor) → wgpu talks to Metal on iOS and Vulkan on Android → Zero intermediate layers between your code and the GPU

What this unlocks: — One language for UI, logic, networking, and state — One codebase across macOS, Linux, Windows, iOS, and Android — Native GPU performance with Rust's memory and thread safety guarantees

The "write once, run everywhere" promise — but without the compromises.
This is early. Touch input, accessibility, and platform lifecycle handling still need work. But the hardest part — getting pixels on screen through a pure Rust pipeline on mobile hardware — is done.

If you've been waiting for a serious reason to bring Rust to mobile, here it is.

Built on the shoulders of: 🔗 Zed: https://lnkd.in/duBXcbnC 🔗 wgpu: https://lnkd.in/d6gu_-C2

text:
🍎 A small KMP trick when you’re short on resources (or patience): run iOS without opening Xcode or Android Studio ⚡

When working on a KMP project, there are days when you just want to run the iOS app, not spin up Android Studio, not open Xcode, not wait for IDE indexing, not burn half your RAM. Or perhaps you’re using an older 8GB M1 Mac Air and don’t want to close all your Chrome tabs just to open the IDE.

🐚 One approach that has worked extremely well for me is having a simple shell script that builds and runs the iOS app directly via CLI.

No IDEs. No clicking. Just one command.

🧙‍♂️ What this script does:
1. Lists available simulators and connected devices
2. Lets you select a target from the terminal
3. Builds the KMP shared code (XCFramework) via Gradle
4. Invokes xcodebuild directly
5. Installs and launches the app on the selected device or simulator

All from the command line.

🥷 This is especially useful when, as I already mentioned, you are low on machine resources, or multitasking, and want fast feedback.

Why does this work well for KMP? 
KMP already splits responsibilities clearly:
1. Gradle builds the shared Kotlin code
2. Xcode builds the native iOS wrapper

By scripting that boundary explicitly, you get deterministic builds and a workflow that mirrors CI more closely than IDE runs

It also forces you to understand your iOS build pipeline, which pays off massively when setting up CI later.

⁉️ Important note: This does not replace Xcode forever.
You will still need it for:

1. Debugging complex UI issues
2. Inspecting constraints or layout problems
3. Signing and provisioning troubleshooting

But for day-to-day development and quick validation, this approach is incredibly effective.

text:
I just 𝗮𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 my entire mobile deployment workflow, and the productivity boost is massive. 

🚀 I wanted a "one-command" solution to handle everything: screenshot uploads, metadata management for App Store Connect, and staged Android releases (Internal ➡️ Open Testing ➡️ Production).

Here’s how I tackled it and what I learned:

1. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: 🏎️ While Android was a breeze, iOS presented a challenge due to Flutter’s specific build requirements and certificate management.
 • The Learning Curve: I initially spent too much time manually managing p12 certificates.
 • The Fix: I realized that leveraging Xcode’s automatic distribution within the Fastlane lane simplified the entire pipeline. Don't over-engineer what the native tools already solve!

2. 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗦𝗵𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗯𝗶𝗿𝗱 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗣𝘂𝘀𝗵: 🐦 I’ve been using Shorebird for over-the-air updates, but scaling it meant removing manual intervention.
 • Pro Tip: Use the --no-confirm flag in the Shorebird CLI. This bypasses interactive prompts, making it truly compatible with automation scripts.

3. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 "𝗗𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗸 𝗮 𝗖𝗼𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗲" 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁: ☕ I can now trigger the entire process locally and walk away. By the time I’m back, the builds are where they need to be.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁’𝘀 𝗡𝗲𝘅𝘁? The final step is migrating this to 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀. I’ll be using tags on the main branch to trigger builds. This creates a fail-safe environment where I can push bug fixes via Shorebird instantly—without waiting for a full store review cycle.

If you’re a Flutter dev looking to scale, I highly recommend combining Fastlane + Shorebird. It turns hours of manual work into a background task.

Stay tuned—next, I’ll be sharing how I implemented In-App Purchases (IAP) and subscriptions across both stores.

text:
After going through one too many Kafka tutorials, I decided to learn it by building something I would actually use.
A universal clipboard.
Copy on my Android phone, tap once, and instantly paste anywhere on my laptop.
It took about two days. Docker networking broke a few times, Kafka refused connections until it was fully ready, and consumers silently ignored messages because of offsets. Fixing those problems taught me more than any tutorial did.
Yes, it is overkill. WebSockets or a simple HTTP setup would have worked faster. The objective was to actually learn Kafka and understand event driven systems by building something real.
Android sends clipboard content to a FastAPI endpoint -> Kafka publishes the event -> a Python consumer updates my laptop clipboard. Ctrl + V just works.
Now I am extending it into a cross device notification system. Any notification from selected Android apps can be forwarded and shown as real time popups on my laptop using the same event pipeline.

Turns out the fastest way to understand a tool is to build something you actually depend on.

I recently took on the challenge of building a Beer Keg Tracker to solve a real-world logistics problem: using the Solana blockchain to create an immutable history of keg inventory.

The catch? I wanted to build it entirely in Python using the Kivy framework.

Migrating a complex Python backend to a native Android APK was a masterclass in DevOps and mobile architecture. It wasn’t just about writing code; it was about building a pipeline.

Cross-Architecture Compiling: 
Getting the Rust-based Solana libraries to play nice with Android’s ARM architecture required some creative workarounds.

I navigated "Dependency Hell" specifically pinning Cython versions and managing Java 17/11 mismatches in the Gradle build tool.

The Environment: 
I set up a Linux-based toolchain using WSL2 and Buildozer to bridge the gap between my Windows development environment and the Android SDK's strict requirements.

It was a steep learning curve, but seeing that first successful build running on the Android Emulator was worth it.

text:
I wanted the Galaxy S26 Ultra’s viral "360° Horizon Lock" camera feature, but I didn't want to buy a new phone. So, I built it myself.

It’s been 8 years since I last wrote Android code. Today, I’m open-sourcing my return to mobile dev: 🎥 HorizonCam.

I work as a Data Engineer, but I recently got the itch to build a real-time rendering pipeline. Jumping back in after almost a decade meant catching up on Kotlin and the notoriously complex Camera2 API. I used Anthropic Claude and Google's Gemini as accelerators to navigate the modern boilerplate, allowing me to focus entirely on the math and sensor fusion.

The engineering was significantly harder than I expected. Here are 3 massive hurdles I had to solve:

1️⃣ The SurfaceTexture Trap: The naive approach: rotating texture coordinates in a GL shader using the gyro angle, doesn't work. Android applies a hidden orientation matrix (90° rotation + Y-flip) that breaks any rotation you add. The fix? A two-pass pipeline. I bake the orientation into a clean framebuffer (FBO) first, then apply the counter-rotation.

2️⃣ Math vs. Reality: Double-integrating accelerometer data for translation correction sounds great on paper, but in practice, it produces terrible drift. Hand tremor is almost entirely rotational. The fix is to let the physical lens hardware (OIS) handle the jitter, and use software strictly for the rotation.

3️⃣ The Stabilization Feedback Loop: Android's built-in Electronic Image Stabilization (EIS) literally fights custom rotation shaders. It sees the counter-rotation as "movement" and tries to undo it, causing wild oscillations. Solution: OIS on, EIS strictly off.

The final app features a 360° real-time horizon lock, aspect ratio selection, ultra-wide support, and processes entirely on-device with zero cloud dependencies.

My last deep dive into Android was porting Face Unlock to the Redmi Note 4 in 2018 (which ended up on XDA Developers!). It feels great to be back building for mobile.
If you find the rendering pipeline interesting, I'd hugely appreciate a ⭐️ on GitHub or any feedback on the implementation!

text:
If you’re an Android dev and still think R8 and ProGuard are the same thing, let’s clear that up 👇

A lot of us started with ProGuard.
It shrinks, optimizes, and obfuscates bytecode. Simple. Reliable. For years, it was the default in the Android build pipeline.

Then came R8 — and many developers just treated it as “ProGuard 2.0”.

It’s not.


---

🔍 The Real Difference

ProGuard

Separate tool in the pipeline

Shrinks + obfuscates bytecode

Works after compilation

Slower builds compared to modern setups


R8

Replaces ProGuard (and also D8)

Shrinks + optimizes + desugars + converts to DEX in one step

Tighter integration with Android Gradle Plugin

Better optimization → smaller APK/AAB size

Faster builds in most real projects


In short:
R8 isn’t just an obfuscator. It’s a full compiler optimization pipeline.


---

⚙️ Why This Actually Matters

In production apps:

Smaller app size → better install conversion

Better optimization → fewer methods → less 64K pressure

Integrated pipeline → fewer build surprises


And yes — your proguard-rules.pro file still works with R8.
But under the hood, R8’s optimizer is far more aggressive. That’s why sometimes reflection-heavy libraries break if rules aren’t precise.


---

💡 My Take

If you're working on serious Android apps in 2026 and haven't deeply understood R8 behavior, you're missing out on low-hanging performance and size gains.

We spend hours optimizing Compose recompositions, networking layers, and startup time…

…but ignore the compiler-level optimizer sitting quietly in our build process.


---



Let me know what you’d like next 👇

text:
You NEED to LEARN Apache Airflow in 2026 (even if you have already mastered it). 
WHY? Bcz of the upgrade - Apache Airflow 3.0

➡️ The 3.0 upgrade isn’t just a version bump - it’s a massive architectural shift. From TaskFlow APIs to the new "Assets" concept, the game has changed.

The real questions now are: 
✅ What actually changed in the architecture? 
✅ Is TaskFlow API now the "default" way to write DAGs? 
✅ How do "Assets" change the way we schedule pipelines?

If you’re looking to master Airflow 3.0, here is the realistic roadmap:
▶️ Start with the New Architecture. Understand the shift in how the Scheduler and Webserver interact. 
▶️ Master the TaskFlow API. Forget the old-school PythonOperators and messy XComs. TaskFlow is how you write clean, Pythonic pipelines. 
▶️ Dive into Assets (Data-Aware Scheduling). This is a game-changer. 

📍 And throughout the journey - Don’t just read the docs. Build a DAG. Break the scheduler. Fix the dependencies. Repeat.

Top Resources to Learn Airflow 3.0:
- Data Pipelines with Apache Airflow 2nd Edition (Book) 
- Airflow 3 eBook by Astronomer (Book)
- Airflow Full Course From Scratch By Ansh Lamba (YT Video)

Airflow 3.0 isn't harder; it's just smarter. It’s time to upgrade your skills.


♻️ Follow Ansh Lamba for daily insights ♻️

text:
Ever wondered how Kotlin Multiplatform code becomes an Android app? 🤯
From .kt → JVM bytecode → DEX → APK —
KMM goes through a full Android-native build pipeline before your code runs on a device.

Behind the scenes:
• Kotlin compiler turns shared .kt code into JVM bytecode
• Android toolchain (D8/R8) converts bytecode into DEX
• Gradle + Android plugin package everything into a signed APK
• Shared KMM code is merged just like any other Android library
• Coroutines, Flow, and suspend functions run on Android without any bridge or wrapper

One codebase. Two platforms. Fully native.

text:
“Opportunities are rarely handed to you — they are built through courage, conviction, and consistency.”

When my incredibly dedicated and resilient teammate, Mahak Agarwal, and I decided to take on the AI for Bharat Hackathon by AWS, we didn’t know what the outcome would be.

But we knew one thing — we wanted to build something meaningful.

🚀 The Problem

In rural India, unreliable internet still limits access to quality education.

Brilliant teachers. Curious students.
But connectivity decides who gets to learn.

That’s how 
hashtag
#BharatVidya was born.

An AI-powered platform that converts simple educational text into animated video lessons.

Built to:
• Work on 2GB RAM Android devices
• Support 12+ Indian & 5+ foreign languages
• Sync using ultra-low data (~2KB)
• Remove internet dependency from learning

Our PPT wasn’t just slides.

It had clarity.
Clear problem. Clear users. Clear architecture. Clear roadmap.

And most importantly, every slide had a story.
Every slide answered why this must exist.

That clarity got us selected for the Prototype Development Round.

🔥 7 Days. Version 0. No Excuses.

We built everything from scratch — backend APIs, frontend UI, AI pipeline, rendering engine, and deployment.

For 6 sleepless nights, we debugged, rebuilt, and kept pushing.

Our incredibly supportive and hands-on senior ABDUL KALAM AZAD didn’t just mentor us — he stayed awake with us through late-night crashes and deployment failures.

And deployment?

That was the real test.

First AWS EC2 instance.
Ubuntu configuration.
FastAPI hosting.
Vercel integration.

8–10 breaking issues.
Port conflicts. Dependency errors. Server crashes.

We failed. Fixed. Failed again. Fixed again.

Until it finally went live.

🧠 BharatVidya V0 now runs a complete text → video pipeline using:
Phi-3:mini (via Ollama), edge-tts, PIL, and MoviePy.

Is it fully polished? Not yet.
There’s still optimization, refinement, and improvement ahead.

We are currently in the Prototype Round, hoping to advance to the final stage — and determined to keep improving the system regardless of the results.

Because this wasn’t just a hackathon project.

It was where we pushed beyond comfort.
Where every bug sharpened us.
Where every crash built resilience.

We didn’t just build a prototype.

We built ourselves.

And we’re just getting started.

text:
🥲Most Android apps work.
But most Android apps are not built correctly.

Modern Android Architecture Demo | Jetpack Compose + Clean Architecture

I recently built a demo Android application implementing a scalable and production-ready architecture using the modern Android stack:
 🔹 Jetpack Compose – Declarative UI
 🔹 Clean Architecture + MVVM
 🔹 Kotlin Coroutines + Flow
 🔹 Hilt (Dependency Injection)
 🔹 Retrofit – REST API integration
 🔹 Room Database – Local persistence
 🔹 Offline-first approach

🏗 Architecture Flow Implemented:
API (Retrofit)
 → Remote Data Source
 → DTO → Domain Mapper
 → Repository
 → Room Database (Single Source of Truth)
 → Flow (Reactive stream)
 → Use Case (Business logic)
 → UI State
 → ViewModel
 → Compose UI

Instead of returning raw data from the repository, I implemented:

Flow<UiState>

Which allowed me to properly handle:

 → Loading
 → Success
 → Error

🔄 Data Strategy
Instead of directly exposing API responses to the UI, I implemented a proper data pipeline:
 ✔ API response is mapped to domain models
 ✔ Data is stored in Room DB
 ✔ UI observes local database using Flow
 ✔ ViewModel exposes immutable UI state
 ✔ Compose reacts automatically to state changes

This ensures:
 ✅ No tight coupling.
 ✅ Better separation of concerns
 ✅ High testability
 ✅ Lifecycle-safe reactive updates
 ✅ Offline capability
 ✅ Scalable & maintainable architecture

✨Key Takeaway
Using Room as the Single Source of Truth, combined with Flow and UseCases makes state predictable and avoids tight coupling between layers.

Writing clean architecture is not about complexity - it's about clarity, scalability, and long-term maintainability.

I’m continuously refining my Android fundamentals to build production-ready systems.

Happy to connect with Android developers and discuss architecture patterns 🚀

GitHub repo: https://lnkd.in/gr4msk3J

text:
BUILD SUCCESSFUL.

The joy that comes with seeing those two green words in caps? Unmatched.

It’s normal for React Native developers to build their app bundles online using EAS. And honestly, it saves a lot of time. You push your code, join the queue, wait your turn, and eventually your build is ready.

But today I asked myself — why follow a queue when I can build it locally?

The last time, I generated an APK. This time, I decided to go further and build an AAB (Android App Bundle) for release. That decision alone signed me up for hours of “unexpected character development.”

Errors here.
Configuration issues there.
Version mismatches.

I had to properly install and configure Java, set up SDK 17, align Gradle versions, generate and sign a release key, fix environment paths — everything that online builds quietly handle for you behind the scenes.

Local builds don’t hide anything. They expose everything.

At some point, it stops being about the app and starts being about understanding the build system itself. And that’s where the growth happens. You’re no longer just writing React Native code — you’re dealing with Android internals, build pipelines, and release architecture.

But after hours of configuring, retrying, and reading logs line by line…

It finally built.

No queue.
No remote server.
Just my machine and persistence.

There’s something powerful about owning your build process. It’s slower at first, more frustrating, but you come out understanding your stack at a deeper level.

Today wasn’t just about generating an AAB file.

It was about leveling up.

text:
أخطاء في Flutter تقتل أداء التطبيق (Production Killers) #1
Flutter Mistakes That Kill App Performance

Flutter Mistake #1

Calling APIs inside build()
 تنفيذ API calls أو أي async request داخل دالة build()

إرسال طلبات متكررة للسيرفر بدون قصد 

دي اللحظة اللي بتكتشف فيها إنك عملت (DDoS Attack) على السيرفر بتاعك وأنت مش واخد بالك

من أكثر الأخطاء التي أراها في مشاريع Flutter — خصوصًا في المراحل المبكرة —
هو تنفيذ API calls أو أي async request داخل دالة build().
قد يبدو الأمر بسيطًا… لكنه خطأ معماري خطير.

دالة build() في Flutter:
يمكن استدعاؤها عشرات المرات
تُستدعى عند أي تغيير في الـ State
تُستدعى عند إعادة بناء الأب (Parent rebuild)
تُستدعى عند تغيير MediaQuery أو Theme أو Orientation

بمعنى آخر:
build() ليست lifecycle method تُنفذ مرة واحدة…
بل هي pure function يجب أن تكون side-effect free.

 ماذا يحدث عند وضع API call داخل build()؟
 إرسال طلبات متكررة للسيرفر بدون قصد
 استهلاك غير ضروري للذاكرة والـ CPU
 إعادة تحميل البيانات عند كل rebuild
 سلوك غير متوقع وصعب التتبع
 Bugs مرتبطة بالـ race conditions
وفي التطبيقات الحقيقية…قد يتحول الأمر إلى مشكلة Production خطيرة.

build() يجب أن ترسم UI فقط.
لا منطق أعمال. لا Networking. لا Side Effects.
افصل منطق البيانات عن منطق العرض (Separation of Concerns).

أين يجب تنفيذ الـ API Call؟
داخل initState() إذا كانت البيانات تُجلب مرة واحدة
داخل BLoC / Cubit / ViewModel
باستخدام FutureBuilder مع Future محفوظ مسبقًا
أو عبر Repository Layer مع إدارة حالة واضحة


***** الفرق بين كود مبتدئ وكود احترافي
المبتدئ يجعل build() مركز كل شيء.
المحترف يفهم أن build() مجرد renderer للحالة الحالية.

 في Flutter، الأداء الجيد لا يأتي من قوة الإطار…
بل من فهمك لدورة حياة الـ Widgets وكيفية إدارة الحالة بشكل صحيح.

text:
Client: Can you send the new APK?
Me: "It's already in the folder.
I used to waste so much time manually building and uploading apps every time my client wanted to see a small change.
But the real killer was the Time Zone mismatch. My client would ask for an update while I was sleeping. By the time I woke up, built the app, and sent it... they were asleep. We were losing 24 hours just waiting for a file transfer. 
So, I decided to treat my mobile development like a DevOps engineer. 
The Fix: 
I built a fully automated CI/CD Pipeline using GitHub Actions + the Google Drive API.
Now, I just share one Google Drive link with the client at the start of the project.
I push code to GitHub (even at 2 AM). The pipeline automatically builds the Android APK. It uploads the fresh file to the shared Drive folder instantly.
And yes, this scales to iOS too.
I can easily extend this pipeline to build and upload the iOS IPA file to the same folder, so both Android and iPhone users get their updates without waiting for me to wake up.
No more emails. No more time zone delays. No more "Wait, let me upload it."
If the code is live, the app is live. Simple as that.

🚀 Advanced Android Development Skills for 2026

After learning the fundamentals of Android development, the real growth begins when you start mastering advanced skills that make your apps scalable, efficient, and production-ready.

Here are some important skills every Android developer should focus on:

🔹 Dependency Injection (Hilt)
Helps build modular and maintainable Android applications.

🔹 Automated Testing
Using tools like JUnit and Espresso ensures your apps remain stable and reliable.

🔹 CI/CD
Automating build, testing, and deployment pipelines improves development efficiency.

🔹 API Integration
Connecting apps with external services to deliver dynamic and real-time experiences.

🔹 Firebase Backend Services
Authentication, Firestore, and Cloud Messaging make backend integration faster.

🔹 Advanced State Management
Managing UI states effectively is essential for complex Android applications.

🔹 Play Store Optimization
Understanding release management and optimization helps apps reach more users.

As Android developers grow, the focus shifts from just writing code to building scalable, maintainable, and production-ready applications.

Which advanced Android skill are you currently improving? 👇

text:
Android Engineering: Building for Fragmentation & Scale

Android development is not just about writing Kotlin code — it’s about engineering for diversity.

As a Mobile Engineering Manager, I see Android as a platform that demands architectural discipline. Thousands of device types, screen sizes, OS versions, and hardware variations require thoughtful system design.

My focus in Android applications:

• Architecture that handles fragmentation
• Kotlin-first development with modern patterns
• Scalable backend integrations
• Performance tuning across device tiers

Great Android apps succeed because they adapt —
to different users, devices, and performance constraints.

Android engineering isn’t about building once.
It’s about building resilient systems that work everywhere.

بإختصار... إعداد تصميم و شوب دروينج لوحة السليفات (Sleeves) الخاصة بلوحات ال IRR Systems 
أولا : يتم تجهيز اخر Update لل Layout المشروع وتحديد عناصر ال Soft & Hard على اللوحة والقيام ب Lock لكل العناصر الموجودة في اللوحة من خلال اظهار قائمة ال Layers والضغط right click واختيار Select All ثم الضغط على Lock وبيتم ذلك لتخفيف حمل اللوحة و لإظهار اللوحة عند الطباعة بشكل جيد والتركيز على التخصص الذى تريد اعتماده من المالك والاستشارى من خلال ظهور أماكن ال Soft & Hard وبالتالى تحديد ومراجعة أماكن ومقاس وطول ونوع إن أمكن تغيره لل Sleeves of Pipes مع الاستشارى والمالك بشكل أسرع 
ثانيا : فتح كل لوحة من لوحات ال IRR Systems مع لوحة ال Sleeves مع التأكيد أن اللوحة متوجه ولذلك لسهولة عمل لوحة ال Staking وتنفيذ التوقيع فى الموقع بشكل سليم وصحيح 
يتم فتح كل لوحة تم اعتمادها والقيام بعمل Sleeve لكل Pipe تحتاج لذلك مع كتابة البيانات اللازمة لها لسهولة تنفيذها لدى مهندس الموقع ويتم عمل ذلك من خلال تحديد ذلك على Layer جديد أولا وذلك لسرعة حصرها فى اللوحة التى تم اعتمادها ثم القيام ب copy لهذا ال Layer لدى كل لوحة ثم الانتقال الى لوحة ال Sleeves واختيار من قائمة ال Clipboard : الاتى
 Paste to Original Coordinates 
ثالثا : بالتزامن مع الخطوة الثانية يتم عمل لوحة Compoiste وهى لوحة تقوم بعملها لشبكات الإنفرا مع تحديد أماكن ال Soft & Hard فى ال project ولذلك لدراسة التعارضات ومنع حدوثها فى الموقع ولمراجعة مساراتك لل pipes فى نفس الوقت وربط الموقع فى الوقت الحالى مع العمل الذى سوف يتم بداية من اللوحة وتحديد أولوية الأعمال بالموقع أو تقديم RFI أو RFP فى حالة وجود اختلاف أو اكتشاف أمر جديد فى لوحة التصميم إذا توفرت وفى هذه الحالة تقوم بإعداد ال Shop Drawing فقط 
أخيرا بعض الصور المرفقة لزونة معينه ويتم الإيضاح من الليجند القيام بعمل Layer وشكل مختلف لكل Sleeve مستخدم لنوع معين من لوحات ال IRR Systems والقيام بعمل Sleeve ATT وذلك لضمان إظهار البيانات فى طباعة هذه الزونة فى لوحة واحدة وإيضاح البيانات بشكل أفضل وأسرع لمهندس الموقع للقيام بإجراءات التنفيذ بشكل سليم
