text:
𝗦𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝟱𝟬+ 𝗪𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲-𝗟𝗮𝗯𝗲𝗹 𝗠𝗼𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗲 𝗔𝗽𝗽𝘀 — 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗲

White-labeling mobile apps starts simple… and becomes unmanageable fast.

Too many Android flavors. Too many iOS targets. Duplicated pipelines. One brand breaks when another changes.

We at Wemotive recently designed a production-ready white-label architecture for React Native that ships 50+ branded apps from a single codebase — no separate projects, no per-brand branches.

Here's the core idea:

𝗧𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝗯𝗿𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝘀 𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗮, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲.

Each customer is just configuration + assets:
→ App name & bundle ID
→ Icons & Firebase config
→ API base URLs & store links
→ Optional theme colors

Business logic, UI flows, features, and release cadence stay exactly the same across every brand.

𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀:

One main app repo. One CI/CD workflow. At build time, a single script injects the brand config — rewrites Android applicationId, iOS CFBundleIdentifier, replaces icons, swaps Firebase files, and generates environment variables.

The pipeline takes just three inputs: brand_id, environment, and platform — and produces the correct APK/IPA. A batch workflow loops through all brands and triggers parallel builds for monthly releases.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘄𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗯𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗹𝘆 𝗮𝘃𝗼𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗱 𝗳𝗹𝗮𝘃𝗼𝗿𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗲𝘁𝘀:

At scale, product flavors and iOS schemes cause exponential CI complexity, fragile native configs, and brutal maintenance costs. This architecture stays flat no matter how many customers you add.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘆:

White-label systems fail when teams scale by duplicating projects.

They succeed when branding is injected at build time as configuration and assets — not baked into application structure.

If you're planning multi-brand mobile delivery in 2025, this pattern is a safe and scalable baseline.

—

text:
Last week our team built 'snipe' at the 2026 Startup Week Buildathon, a developer security tool that catches bugs before you even save your code.

Most static analysis and security tools operate somewhere in this workflow:

You type → You save → You commit → You push → CI runs → PR review

Tools like SonarQube, Semgrep, Snyk, CodeRabbit, & CodeQL typically analyze code after the file exists on disk, in pre-commit hooks, CI pipelines, or pull requests. But bugs appear earlier than that, while you are typing. snipe operates exactly at this stage.

It performs real-time cross-file semantic analysis on unsaved editor buffers, detecting issues instantly while you are still writing code.

No save. No commit. No CI pipeline.
Just immediate feedback inside your editor.

snipe does not analyze files in isolation. It builds a blast radius & repository-wide symbol knowledge graph mapping every function, variable, struct, & import across the entire codebase.

Because of this, it can detect issues that many tools miss until runtime. Moreover as LLM generated codes are insecure, the need for snipe increases.

The team behind snipe

I built the C static analysis engine; implemented AST-based detection for various bugs, & unsafe C functions. This runs using Tree-sitter parsing without a compiler & integrates with the repo-wide symbol graph for real-time diagnostics.
Vishva Patel built the repository-wide symbol graph & AI endpoint (explains errors & suggests fixes). This maps everything across the repo and makes the blast radius analysis.
Chiranjeev K. built the Python real-time analysis pipeline. This detects issues like wrong argument types, undefined variables across files, insecure `eval` & `pickle` usage, & function signature mismatches when definitions change upstream.
Raunak Ahmed built the project website. He designed the product interface & budget flow that helped us keep a note of the USD spent.

Feedback from the judges

One of the most encouraging moments was hearing feedback from our judges Chris "The Wiz 🪄" Alexiuk (NVIDIA), Anu Srivastava (NVIDIA & Ex-Google) & Chris Brown (NVIDIA).

They mentioned that the market genuinely needs a tool like snipe. Many companies attempt to build internal tooling to detect bugs early, but there is no widely adopted standard tool that works directly while developers are writing code.

That feedback made us start thinking seriously about building snipe beyond the hackathon.

Website: https://lnkd.in/edaUeXEr
Repo: https://lnkd.in/eWe6QAmi
Demo: https://lnkd.in/eqeghiG8

Huge thanks to tech@nyu, HOF Capital, Anthropic & NVIDIA for supporting builders & early-stage innovation.

This started as a hackathon project. But the idea is serious.

And if this ever becomes a company someday, I guess the roles are already decided 😅
CEO: Anmol Vats
CISO: Vishva Patel
CTO: Chiranjeev K.
CFO: Raunak Ahmed

text:
✅ The Correct ALM Design Pattern (Enterprise-Grade)

For large-scale implementations (5,000+ users), this is the recommended structure:

🏗 1️⃣ Environment Strategy

Minimum setup:
Dev (Unmanaged solutions)
Test / UAT (Managed solutions)
Prod (Managed only — locked down)
Optional (Enterprise):
Feature branch Dev environments
Pre-Prod staging
Dedicated Support environment

🔄 2️⃣ Solution Strategy

In Dev:
✔ Work only with Unmanaged Solutions
 ✔ Source control integration (Azure DevOps / GitHub)
 ✔ Follow solution segmentation (Core, Extensions, Integrations)
Deployment to Test/Prod:
✔ Export as Managed Solution
 ✔ Deploy via CI/CD pipeline
 ✔ No manual imports
🔒 Production should NEVER allow unmanaged customizations.

🚀 3️⃣ CI/CD Pipeline Pattern

1️⃣ Developer commits changes
 2️⃣ Pipeline validates build
 3️⃣ Solution exported automatically
 4️⃣ Managed solution deployed to Test
 5️⃣ Automated validation
 6️⃣ Approval gate
 7️⃣ Deploy to Prod

🛠 4️⃣ Patch & Upgrade Strategy

For small fixes:
Create Patch
Merge into parent
Deploy new version
For major updates:
Clone solution
Increment version properly
Upgrade in controlled sequence
Never edit directly in Prod.
No manual deployments.

🔐 5️⃣ Governance Controls

✔ Disable customization in Production
 ✔ Restrict security role modification
 ✔ Implement DLP policies
 ✔ Use environment variables
 ✔ Use service principals for deployment

🧠 Golden Rule
Development = Flexible
 Production = Controlled
Architecture maturity is measured by how well you protect Production.

text:
Introducing Buildkite's build canvas: A new way to visualize your CI/CD pipelines.

The canvas helps you:
- Quickly understand pipelines: View your pipeline as a directed acyclic graph (DAG).
- Decode dependencies: Highlight key paths through the build.
- Troubleshoot issues: Jump to failed steps for faster debugging.
- Follow a build's progress: Use the follow mode to see the status of running steps.

Andy Tang, Staff Platform Engineer at SEEK, shared:

"The build canvas is great because it lets me visualize another team's pipeline very quickly when debugging an issue. This saves me time, as previously, I had to unpick the logic manually by inspecting their generated pipeline.yaml. For teams that have multiple levels of pipeline abstractions, this is a game changer."

Eathan Langford, Developer Experience at MYOB, shared:

"The canvas has made it easier for us, as the Platform team to easily see dependency pathways for steps in a build. This lets us see at a glance how the pipeline has performed and where things go wrong, easily letting us debug with our engineers."

For all the details about the build canvas and a demo of the key features, see the announcement blog: https://lnkd.in/gh-CXnEu

text:
Using a “No‑Security” Flavor to Skip Security Logic in Android Builds
When you're building Android apps—especially for internal testing, CI/e2e pipelines, or automated QA—you may need a build variant with zero security checks (root detection, pinning, integrity, etc.). Introducing the no‑security flavor pattern:

Why use it?
🧪 Enables test/dev builds without interference from root or security checks.
🧹 Keeps production code clean while separating test behavior.
🕒 Prevents accidental shipping of insecure logic to real users.

🛠️ Two implementation styles
1. Flavor-specific source sets (cleanest)

src/main/RootDetection.kt // actual logic
src/noSecurity/RootDetection.kt // stub: always returns false

✅ Detection code isn't compiled or included in no‑security builds.


2. BuildConfig toggle (more flexible)
productFlavors {
 noSecurity { buildConfigField "boolean", "ENABLE_ROOT_CHECK", "false" }
 secure   { buildConfigField "boolean", "ENABLE_ROOT_CHECK", "true" }
}

if (BuildConfig.ENABLE_ROOT_CHECK && RootDetection(ctx).isRooted()) {
 // block or warn
}

🎯 Best use cases:
Flavor-specific sets : Clean separation, minimal APK footprint
BuildConfig toggle : One codebase, easy toggles for CI/dev teams

📌 Tips to remember
- Use manifestPlaceholders to disable pinning, crash-reporting, and network security.
- Never rely solely on the client-side; enforce security server-side too.
- Label your builds clearly: no accidentally shipping insecure variants.


What approach do you use? Have you created internal-only flavors for testing or automation? Share your strategies or challenges below! 👇

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗔𝗻𝗱𝗿𝗼𝗶𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗰𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗖𝗜/𝗖𝗗
CI/CD (Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment) isn’t just for large teams — it’s for 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗔𝗻𝗱𝗿𝗼𝗶𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿 who wants to ship confidently, fast, and without mistakes.

When building DoTrack, I automated everything from building → signing → versioning → deploying to the Play Store using 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀.

What my pipeline handles:
• Builds the AAB
• Auto-increments version codes
• Signs using GitHub Secrets
• Uploads to Play Store (internal/live)
• Tags releases
• Stores build artifacts

What this gives me:
• Faster, reliable releases
• Less manual error
• Confidence every time I ship

If you’re building Android apps, side-projects, or freelancing — learn CI/CD early. It will 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲 𝗵𝗼𝘄 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲.

text:
The truth about 
hashtag
#AndroidDev that nobody tells you:
- You spend 2 hours fixing an issue.
- Turns out it was a missing = in XML.
- Emulator worked fine… until it didn’t.
- App runs great on Pixel, crashes on a $100 phone.

And just when you breathe…
PM: “Let’s support tablets now.”
Client: “Can we make the bottom sheet float like iOS?”
QA: “This is not working on Android 7.”
Me: “Android 7 still exists?! 😵‍💫”

We aren’t just developers — we’re backward compatibility warriors, design translators, and crash analysts.

Android dev isn’t always glamorous — it’s Gradle builds failing at 98%, CI pipelines breaking randomly, and layouts collapsing for no reason.

But we still ship.
We still solve.
And we still make magic happen on hundreds of devices. 💪

Let’s normalize celebrating small wins — even if it’s just that one flavor finally building without errors.

text:
🚧 𝐖𝐞 𝐰𝐞𝐫𝐞 𝐦𝐚𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐀𝐧𝐝𝐫𝐨𝐢𝐝 𝐚𝐩𝐩 𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐛𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐰𝐚𝐲 𝐡𝐚𝐫𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐢𝐭 𝐬𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝 𝐛𝐞.

For a long time, we distributed our Android builds via Google Play tracks:
 🔹 Internal
 🔹 Closed Beta
 🔹 Open Beta
 🔹 Production

But here’s the problem 👇

Our testers and stakeholders constantly struggled with this process:

1. Internal track: Each build had a new download link every time. Super inconvenient.
2. Beta track: Testers had to manually switch to beta mode in Google Play, which didn’t always work immediately. Plus, switching removed access to the production version or older builds.
3. The entire flow was not intuitive.

I realized:
 👉 We need a better way to distribute Android test builds.
 👉 Something as seamless as iOS TestFlight.
 👉 Something where testers can get builds in one click.

After some research, I found the perfect solution: Firebase App Distribution 🚀

Here’s why it made significant changes:
 ✅ Create tester groups via email and manage distribution across them 
 ✅ Upload APKs or AABs in seconds
 ✅ Testers have a dedicated app with real-time notifications
 ✅ Full build history with release notes
 ✅ One-click installs for any version
 ✅ Easy visibility on who installed what
 ✅ Tracking and direct feedback
 ✅ Super simple CLI integration — just 3 lines in our pipeline

It’s been a huge win for both testers and our team’s delivery speed.

What tools do you use to distribute your Android builds in your CI/CD Pipelines?

text:
I recently learned how to deploy a Flutter app to the App Store (iOS) for the first time.

Honestly, it was not simple.

Understanding certificates, provisioning profiles, code signing, App Store Connect, and TestFlight took time. I got stuck multiple times during the process — especially during signing and uploading the build.

But after going through the full deployment cycle and successfully publishing the app, I realized something:

Many Flutter developers probably struggle the same way when deploying to iOS for the first time.

So I decided to document everything clearly and publish it publicly to help other developers avoid the confusion I faced.

I created a beginner-friendly deployment guide covering:
• Apple Developer ecosystem basics
• iOS signing & provisioning
• Building & archiving Flutter apps
• Uploading to App Store Connect
• Internal & External testing using TestFlight
• Submitting for App Review
• Common beginner mistakes

If you're deploying a Flutter app to iOS for the first time, this may help you.

📖 Full documentation:
https://lnkd.in/guRAp55D

I’ll continue updating it with real deployment issues and fixes as I encounter them.

Hope it helps someone who is currently stuck where I was.

text:
Building an iOS app from a Windows environment comes with its own set of constraints.

The biggest hurdle is the build pipeline. To compile and deploy an iOS app, macOS with Xcode is mandatory. A practical workaround is using a cloud Mac, but the workflow quickly becomes inefficient. Every small change means pushing updates to Git, triggering a remote build, waiting, and sometimes discovering issues that could have been caught earlier. It slows momentum and breaks the creative flow.

That is where local simulation becomes critical.

Unlike Android, there is no native iPhone simulator available for Windows. So I decided to create a lightweight local simulator tailored to my app’s behavior. The goal was simple: validate UI logic, flows, and interactions before sending builds to the cloud environment.

This approach also helped avoid exhausting the daily TestFlight deployment quota, which is surprisingly easy to hit during rapid iteration cycles.

Sometimes the right solution is not finding a tool. It is building the tool you wish existed.

Constraints often drive the most interesting engineering decisions.

text:
Exploring a seamless deployment workflow from GitHub to your Apple Developer account? I recently tackled a similar challenge where the aim was to deploy applications directly from GitHub Actions to the Apple Developer account. 

Utilizing Fastlane as a CI/CD tool, I successfully navigated this process. The key steps involved setting up profile provisioning, certificates for iOS application encryption, and deploying to TestFlight. All made possible with just an App Developer account, an application created with an app identifier, and your Apple Developer keys.

text:
BREAKING XQ-67A first flight footage released.

The Air Force Research Laboratory successfully flew the first of a second- generation of Autonomous Collaborative Platforms known as the XQ-67A demonstrator, built and flown in the Off-Board Sensing Station (OBSS) program Feb. 28, 2024.

The AFRL Off-Board Sensing Station program is the validation of a design, build and test process that has resulted in the XQ-67A. It is the first of its kind to be built on a common chassis or genus — much like that of a motor vehicle frame — and with its first successful flight, the XQ-67A is proof that the genus approach works. This enables a faster and more cost-effective replication of the aircraft. 

This new approach also responds to the challenge of great power competition by speeding delivery of affordable, advanced capabilities to the warfighter. 

The XQ-67A is remotely piloted but is capable of autonomous flight.

The first test flight of the XQ-67A, took place at the General Atomics Gray Butte Flight Operations Facility near Palmdale, California.

Courtesy Video / General Atomics

text:
Get Started with TestFlight 🚀

TestFlight helps you gather real-world feedback on beta builds before going live on the App Store. Here’s how to use it:
✨ Why TestFlight?
• Seamless Beta Distribution: Quickly share builds with internal teams or external testers.
• Easy Feedback: Testers can submit crashes, screenshots, and comments—sent right to you.
• Zero Cost: Included with your Apple Developer Program membership.
🏗️ Steps to Begin
1. Upload a Build
• From Xcode → Product → Archive, choose Distribute → App Store Connect → Upload.
• Ensure version/build # is unique.
2. Add Test Details
• In App Store Connect → TestFlight:
• Provide your “What to Test” notes for testers.
• Enter your Beta App Description & Feedback Email.
3. Add Testers
• Internal Testers: Up to 100 team members from your App Store Connect account.
• External Testers: Up to 10,000 external invites.
• Use Test Groups to manage who sees which builds.
💡 Invite Methods
• Email Invite: Each tester gets an email to install via TestFlight app.
• Public Link: Generate a link with a set tester limit; ideal for social shares or large invites.
🛠️ Collecting Feedback
• Tester Crash Reports: Appear in TestFlight’s dashboard or Xcode Organizer.
• Screenshots: Testers can annotate images; see them in App Store Connect.
• Session Data: Track user sessions, device info, and OS versions.
🤝 Integration Tips
• Continuous Testing: Even post-launch, test new features with TestFlight’s 90-day build availability.
• Use Xcode Cloud: Automate your CI/CD and deploy test builds to TestFlight.
• Groups: Keep QA or specialized user groups for iterative testing.
Start leveraging TestFlight for more robust pre-release validation, better feedback loops, and confident App Store launches.

“With TestFlight, you ship better apps—test swiftly, gather feedback, iterate, and delight users on day one.”

text:
Airbus Proves Formation Flying Could Be The Future Of Aviation.
Airbus has successfully demonstrated that formation flying could be the standard for future long-haul aviation. Inspired by the biological technique of geese flying together to conserve energy, Airbus developed the fello'fly project to test Wake Energy Retrieval. 
During a major transatlantic demonstration, two A350 test aircraft flew from Toulouse, France, to Montreal, Canada, maintaining a strict separation of three kilometers. By positioning the trailing A350-1000 exactly in the upward airflow generated by the leading A350-900's wingtip vortices, the follower aircraft needed less engine thrust. The test saved over six tons of carbon dioxide emissions, validating a fuel reduction of more than 5 percent for the transatlantic journey.
Implementing this flying method presents distinct operational challenges. Guiding two commercial airliners to converge at a specific coordinate requires exact timing, specialized flight control software, and significant updates to air traffic control routing procedures. Safety requirements also dictate that the trailing aircraft must stay exclusively in the safe updraft zone to avoid the severe wake turbulence directly behind the leading plane. Even with these operational demands, aerospace leaders view the results positively. 
Nick MacDonald, the fello'fly project leader, stated the flight was successful in "confirming the potential for more than a five percent fuel savings on long-haul flights." Sabine Klauke, Chief Technical Officer at Airbus, added, "The opportunity to get this deployed for passenger aircraft around the middle of this decade is very promising." With continued testing alongside air traffic regulators, airlines might adopt paired flight formations in the near future.

Test flights in progress – proof, not just promises.

A few months ago, we made a bold promise:

 ✅ To have the first prototype of Rogue One ready around midsummer.

 ✅ To begin full-scale flight testing this summer.

 ✅ To demonstrate that Sweden Dynamics delivers – not just ideas, but real, operationally relevant capability.

Today, we’re proud to say: we’re exactly where we said we’d be.

Rogue One is in the air – and with every flight, we learn, improve, and push forward.

While others enjoy a well-deserved summer break, we’re spending ours hard-testing, fine-tuning, and validating a platform designed to meet Sweden’s and Europe’s urgent defense needs:

1) So simple that even complete beginners can operate it confidently after just 15 minutes.

2) Significantly more cost-effective and faster to produce than comparable alternatives – and built to withstand Nordic conditions.

3) Designed with AI-readiness in the architecture – but prioritizing operational usability here and now.

This is a platform built for the real world, with affordability and scalability baked in from day one.

We believe Sweden and Europe need drones that are ready to deploy – at scale – when it matters most. Rogue One is taking flight to deliver just that.

We’re already in dialogue with several stakeholders who share our belief – and we welcome more to join us.

In our part of the world, Sweden should – and will – lead this endeavor.

📽️ Catch a glimpse of last week’s test flights below 👇

And stay tuned – more insights and results from the Rogue One program coming later this summer.

text:
💡 6 Tips for Building a Prototype Using AI with No Coding Skills

Every month, we hold sessions across our client portfolio for topics they have voted on. Today's session was on how you, with no coding skills, can build a prototype using AI. One of Prodify's fractional product executives, Jeremy Crane, lead the session to share a fully deployed Testflight app he built with no coding skills and a practical guide for how to get started. 

Here are 6 tips he shared:

👍 Start Simple: When developing your first prototype, focus on a basic, self-contained app without a server. This approach simplifies the process and keeps costs low. Use the device for storage and processing to avoid complexities associated with server management.

🗓 Use AI for Planning: Before writing any code, use AI tools like Claude to help you create a detailed build plan. By describing your app’s workflow and key experiences, AI can generate a step-by-step plan that guides you through the development process, ensuring you stay organized and focused.

💻 Leverage Claude Sonnet 3.5 for Coding: These AI tools are game-changers for non-technical product leaders. Claude Sonnet 3.5 can write complete files, guide you through the development process, and provide clear explanations, making coding accessible and manageable.

 🔄 Iterate and Test Frequently: Use the built-in simulator in development environments like Xcode to test your app frequently. Focus on getting the functionality right first, then polish the user interface. This iterative approach helps catch errors early and ensures a smoother development process.

❓ Ask Questions and Leverage Resources: Don’t hesitate to ask the AI tools questions if you’re unsure about any part of the process. Additionally, utilize platforms like GitHub for version control, which allows you to easily roll back changes and manage your code effectively.

⚡ Deploy Quickly with TestFlight: For iOS apps, TestFlight is a great tool for getting your app into the hands of users quickly with minimal scrutiny. This makes it ideal for testing prototypes and gathering user feedback before going through the more rigorous App Store approval process.

Any other tips you would share? Comment below!

text:
During development, developers need to test out their own applications or games internally or externally - especially before launching to the public. Reasons of doing the activity are plentiful, however the most prominent are improving quality, fixing (or balancing) issues, and even getting potential users' beneficial inputs & feedbacks for leveraging those into a successful software after release.

In Apple platforms - let's say iOS, iPadOS, watchOS, macOS, and tvOS - there is a way to get started on software testing, particularly for pre-release ones (alpha/beta).
Introducing TestFlight, the software testing platform from Apple that is accessible from the App Store Connect. By using TestFlight, you're able to make your software products testable by themselves in their own devices, in addition to maximum 10.000 external testers which are really interested to try their products. Each testers could provide useful feedbacks & analytics (including crashes) to them that could shape a product.

During my journey at Apple Developer Academy @BINUS, in Mini Challenge 2, I as a part of DRIM (with Balya Elfata, Geraldo Pannanda Lutan, Mikhael Hose, Sony Chandra, and Valentinus Kenji) was building an adventure game with fantasy aesthetics called Shuttle of Destiny. The game, which is taking a unique story of navigating your protagonist, an aspiring badminton player reaching his dream (read: drim) of achieving the World Champion. By selecting choices that really matters to him, you could make that happens. 🏸 In its development, we found that our game could be tested with quick & easy way, by using TestFlight. Just within hours (not days), we could deploy the testing game into both our internal team and external people.

In this article, I discussed about how to get started on TestFlight by using Shuttle of Destiny case (and make sure to test out our game - available on iOS, iPadOS, and macOS!). Read more:

https://lnkd.in/g9cZJKBR

One of the biggest milestones I’ve achieved in this journey has been setting up a solid CI/CD pipeline with GitHub Actions.

Now every push automatically builds and deploys different flavors of the app:
 • Dev → for fast testing and iteration
 • Staging → for pre-release validation
 • Studio (Production) → the version that will soon reach real users

This workflow saves hours of manual work, reduces errors, and makes sure the app stays stable as new features are added.

Building TrackFlow has been a real lesson in how important automation and discipline are in software development — not just coding features, but ensuring scalability, reliability, and a smooth release cycle.

text:
Emergency Landing Gear Test: Why Great Companies Rehearse the “Unthinkable”

In this video you’re watching a test of an emergency landing gear extension.

Under normal operations, landing gear is powered by primary systems and controlled through standard procedures. But aviation assumes a hard truth: complex systems can fail. So every aircraft has an alternate way to get the gear down—designed, tested, and trained—because when you need it, you won’t have time to improvise.

That’s the part most industries miss.

In business, we often run on “normal mode” until a crisis hits:
 • a key supplier fails
 • a cyber incident shuts down operations
 • liquidity tightens overnight
 • a critical hire leaves
 • a major client churns
 • regulation changes mid-quarter 
 • markets swing violently
 • geopolitical shocks disrupt demand, routes, payments, or supply chains


Aviation’s mindset is simple: hope is not a plan.

What high-performing companies do (aviation-style):
 • Build emergency procedures (clear decision trees, roles, checklists)
 • Define triggers (when the emergency plan activates)
 • Create redundancies (data, suppliers, cash buffers, approvals)
 • Run simulations (tabletop exercises, stress tests)
 • Debrief and improve after every drill

Because the goal isn’t to look calm in a crisis—
it’s to have rehearsed so well that calm is the only option.

At Welojets , this is exactly how we operate: aviation-grade discipline, contingency planning, and 24/7 execution—so our clients don’t feel the turbulence behind the scenes.

If your business had a “gear won’t deploy” moment tomorrow, would your team know exactly what to do?

Mobile app deployment just got a whole lot easier.

Expo has launched Expo Launch, a SaaS that lets you publish your app to the App Store directly from your browser. No setup, no CLI, no config headaches.

EAS already solved the technical complexity, but deployment still required command-line expertise. This meant:

1. Simple releases became bottlenecks.

https://launch.expo.dev/ changes this:

1. Paste your GitHub repo link
2. Click Launch from any browser
3. Auto-creates your Apple app listing
4. Handles code signing automatically
5. Submits to TestFlight
6. Deploys the web version at the same time
7. TestFlight invite arrives in minutes

And it works without opening a terminal — from Windows, Linux, or even a phone.

The real shift: now junior developers, QA, or even non-technical team members can ship builds independently. No more waiting on senior developers. No more knowledge bottlenecks. No more CLI gatekeeping.

Built on the same EAS infrastructure, delivered through a SaaS interface that finally makes deployment simple.

text:
✈️ Method Meets Momentum

On April 22, 2025, Joby Aviation completed its first piloted transition flight — a key milestone for eVTOL aircraft, moving smoothly from vertical takeoff to wing-borne cruise.

It’s not just a technical breakthrough, but a demonstration of how to turn a bold concept into a certifiable, manufacturable product.

As someone who closely follows this space, it’s encouraging to see a roadmap that blends vision with discipline:

• Stepped flight testing
From unmanned tests in 2017 to 40,000+ miles flown — including 100+ piloted flights — Joby wisely progressed through hover and low-speed regimes before achieving full transition.

• Canonical test pilots
Transition flights were conducted by trained experimental test pilots, ensuring both safety and credible data.

• Multiple prototypes
A fleet of five aircraft allows for parallel testing, faster iteration, and reduced risk.

• Certification alignment
In continuous engagement with FAA and NASA, Joby is now in Phase 4 of 5 for type certification.

• Manufacturing scale-up
From low-rate production in California to upcoming expansion in Ohio.

• Forward-thinking sustainability
A hydrogen-electric variant recently completed a 523-mile nonstop flight, showing long-term potential.

This is a solid blueprint for the AAM sector — balancing innovation with engineering rigor and smart industrialization.

text:
🚀 Deploying a React Native App

When working with both iOS and Android, deploying a React Native application can be more difficult than anticipated. Unlike web apps, mobile deployments require handling app stores, certificates, and automated pipelines. 

Here’s a breakdown of all available methods for deploying your React Native app efficiently!

📱 Deploying to Android

1️⃣ Google Play Console – The standard way to publish an app via .aab (Android App Bundle) or .apk. Requires signing the app and setting up store listing details.
 2️⃣ EAS Build & Submit (Expo) – Ideal for Expo-managed projects. It simplifies the build and upload process to Google Play.
 3️⃣ Fastlane – Automates signing, building, and deployment to the Play Store, reducing manual effort.
 4️⃣ CI/CD Pipelines (GitHub Actions, Bitrise, Codemagic, or Appflow) – Automates testing, building, and deploying for a smooth workflow.

🍏 Deploying to iOS
1️⃣ TestFlight (via App Store Connect) – Required for beta testing before going live on the App Store. Apps need to be built using Xcode or automated tools.
 2️⃣ EAS Build & Submit (Expo) – Eases the deployment process for Expo-managed projects.
 3️⃣ Fastlane – Automates certificate handling, signing, and publishing to TestFlight/App Store.
 4️⃣ CI/CD Pipelines (Bitrise, GitHub Actions, Codemagic, Appflow) – Automates iOS deployment, making the process seamless.

⚡ Key Challenges & Solutions
✅ Signing Issues – Properly managing Keystore (Android) and Provisioning Profiles (iOS) is crucial.
 ✅ App Store Approvals – Apple has stricter review guidelines; testing with TestFlight is recommended.
 ✅ OTA Updates – Tools like Expo Updates allow updating JS code without a full redeployment.

🚀 Whether you’re deploying manually or using CI/CD pipelines, choosing the right method can save time and headaches. I'd prefer using EAS build for deployment of you expo managed react native app. You can also create EAS workflow if you want to automate the build process.

The best part about EAS Build is that even if you don't have a Mac, and want to deploy your app to app store, you can build and submit your app to the App Store using EAS (Expo Application Services) by providing your Apple Developer account credentials. However, you'll need to subscribe to the EAS Build plan, since iOS builds are not free. For Android, EAS Build is free but comes with a limited number of builds.

If you do have a Mac and don’t want to pay the subscription fee, you can generate the Expo-managed React Native app's build locally using eas-cli and then submit it using EAS. However, you’ll need to configure Fastlane locally on your Mac, which can be done using Homebrew (The Missing Package Manager for macOS).

What’s your go-to deployment method for React Native apps? Drop your thoughts in the comments! 👇

text:
Simulators are not just for pilot training! This month, we spoke to Andrea Alaimo from University of Enna "Kore"​ about his team’s use of an AXIS Flight Simulation Level D full flight simulator for aviation research.

Replicating a Cessna Citation C560 XLS, Kore’s simulator is being used to research:

✔ Pilot workload and behaviour under normal and abnormal conditions
✔ Human factors and pilot stress using biometric data
✔ Cabin comfort and its impact on performance
✔ Virtual and augmented reality for enhanced research environments

As Professor Alaimo explains: "The simulator’s value lies in its ability to replicate abnormal flight conditions that cannot safely be tested in real-world environments. From system malfunctions to adverse weather and high-workload scenarios, it allows us to conduct deep, data-driven research that goes far beyond pilot training."

The university is now upgrading the simulator to meet the latest EASA CS-FSTD(A) standards, with plans to qualify for pilot training in collaboration with ATO instructors.

text:
🚀 Mastering RESTful APIs in Flutter: The Right Way to Fetch Data

If you're working with RESTful APIs in your Flutter app, understanding the correct HTTP methods is crucial.

One common task is fetching user data from the server — and this is where many beginners go wrong.

❌ Using http.post() or http.put() to retrieve data?
That’s not REST-compliant.

✅ The correct approach:
Use http.get() with proper headers (like auth tokens) and query parameters to fetch data safely and efficiently.

Here’s a quick example using the http package:

final response = await http.get(
 Uri.parse('https://lnkd.in/g7Ydwqsm'),
 headers: {
 'Authorization': 'Bearer YOUR_TOKEN_HERE',
 'Accept': 'application/json',
 },
);

if (response.statusCode == 200) {
 final data = jsonDecode(response.body);
 // Process user data
} else {
 // Handle error
}


💡 Pro Tip: Always check for the correct status code (like 200 OK) and handle errors gracefully.

text:
Expo just broke the biggest barrier in React Native. For years, EAS made building apps easier, but deploying was still a nightmare. You needed the CLI, you needed configs, and usually only senior developers knew how to get it done. That created bottlenecks, delays, and constant “can you deploy this for me?” requests.

Now, that changes. With Expo Launch, you can deploy your React Native app directly from your browser. No setup, no CLI, no config files. Just paste your GitHub repo, hit Launch, and within minutes your TestFlight invite arrives. It works on Mac, Windows, Linux, or even your phone.

Here’s what Expo Launch does for you:

- Auto-creates your Apple app listing
- Handles code signing behind the scenes
- Submits directly to TestFlight
- Deploys the web version at the same time
- Sends you a TestFlight invite within minutes

The real breakthrough is accessibility. QA engineers can now push builds themselves. Junior developers can release updates without waiting. Designers and product managers can preview changes instantly. Teams are no longer blocked by a handful of people who know the deployment process.

Built on the same reliable EAS infrastructure, Expo Launch wraps it in a simple SaaS interface that finally makes sense for everyone. Deployment is no longer a bottleneck. It’s democratized. And React Native teams just leveled up

text:
Strategic Proposal: Dual-Role Amphibious Aircraft for U.S. Homeland Security

Over the past 20 years, floods and wildfires in the U.S. have claimed hundreds of lives and left thousands stranded. Current rescue and firefighting assets—helicopters, boats, and small water bombers—are limited in capacity and speed, leaving populations at risk during critical moments.

A fleet of dual-role amphibious aircraft capable of evacuating 150+ people per sortie and delivering 10,000–15,000 gallons of water for firefighting would transform disaster response. Events like Hurricane Katrina, Hurricane Harvey, and the 2022 Kentucky floods illustrate the tangible difference such aircraft could make in saving lives and protecting communities.

Proposal Summary:
We recommend procuring and strategically deploying 20 dual-role amphibious aircraft. They combine amphibious hulls, modular interiors, high-capacity water tanks, and modern engines with 600+ mile range, allowing rapid response from both runways and water surfaces.

Strategic Placement:
Aircraft would be based across the U.S. in high-risk flood and wildfire regions: Northern & Southern California, Pacific Northwest, Boise/ID, Denver/CO, Dallas–Fort Worth, New Orleans, Raleigh/NC, Hudson Valley/NY, and Elizabeth City/NC (training/maintenance hub).

Operational Benefits:
Flood Evacuation: Rapidly move large populations to safety, reducing fatalities and exposure.
Wildfire Suppression: High-capacity water drops complement existing firefighting fleets.
Year-Round Use: Dual mission ensures maximum utility and cost-effectiveness.
Rapid Deployment: Access flooded areas and water sources beyond land-only aircraft.

Economic Considerations:
Estimated production for 20 aircraft: $1.6–$2.8 billion. Modern design and modular systems reduce maintenance costs while keeping fleets ready for year-round deployment.

Bottom Line:
This fleet doesn’t just fill a gap—it transforms U.S. disaster response, saving lives, protecting property, and strengthening homeland security. Immediate investment ensures the nation is ready wherever disaster strikes.

✈️ Living Digital Twins Have Arrived in the Airport C-Suite! They Are No Longer "Just" an IT Project.

For years, Digital Twins lived inside innovation labs.

Cool demos.
Nice 3D visualizations.
Interesting pilots.

But the most successful airports are now doing something very different.

In a recent discussion with David Cheung, we talked about what separates airports that talk about Digital Twins from those that actually make them work.
The difference is leadership.

The leading airports are no longer treating Digital Twins as a technology experiment.

They are treating them as an executive decision platform.
What they do differently:

• They connect real operational data — flights, passengers, security, gates, baggage.
• They integrate indoor GIS and 3D infrastructure models.
• They use the twin to run live operational decisions, not just planning scenarios.

The result: Airports move from reacting to problems→ to seeing them before they happen.

Traffic Congestion.
Gate conflicts.
Passenger flow.
Security wait times.
Weather Patterns.

When the digital twin becomes live, leadership gains a completely new operating model.

That’s why the conversation has moved upstairs.

From the IT department → to the CEO’s office.

Airports that understand this shift will run the most efficient hubs in the world.
And this is only the beginning.

The next decade of aviation infrastructure will be built on Living Digital Twins.

text:
I have often seen some developer wondering about a proper git branching strategy for mobile apps so here it is - Best Git Tagging & Branching Strategy for Flutter Developers 🎯

When working on a Flutter mobile app, maintaining a clear Git tagging and branching strategy is essential for seamless releases, hotfixes, and testing. Here’s a structured approach that can help you keep your repository organized.

📌 Git Tagging Strategy
A good tagging convention makes tracking releases and fixes much easier. Use the following format:

🐣 release/1.0.0 – Major production release
🐣 testflight/1.1.0-beta – iOS TestFlight beta version
🐣 playstore/1.2.0 – Google Play Store release
🐣 hotfix/1.0.1-crash-fix – Urgent bug fix
🐣 patch/1.3.1-performance – Minor performance improvements
🐣 feature/dark-mode – New feature development

Following semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) ensures clarity and version control across teams.

🌱 Git Branching Strategy
A well-structured branching model is key to smooth collaboration. Here’s how to organize your Git branches:

📌 Main Branches (Stable Releases)
🐣 main – The stable production-ready branch also you can deploy app on play store app store.
🐣 prelive – Staging environment before production also you can give your app tester TestFlight builds from here.
🐣 develop – Ongoing development work also you can keep all your fixes and QA builds should be release from child branch of develop 

📌 Feature & Fix Branches (Active Development)
🐣 feature/location-system – A new feature in progress
🐣 bugfix/payment-error – Fixing a bug in checkout
🐣 hotfix/1.0.1-crash-fix – Urgent fix for a production issue

📌 Testing & Pre-Release Branches (Quality Assurance) - Additional 
🐣  testflight – iOS TestFlight beta testing
🐣 playstore-beta – Google Play beta releases
🐣 qa – Internal testing


📌 Best Practices for Git Versioning
🐽 Keep branch names consistent using lowercase and dashes (/)
🐽 Use release/, prelive/, hotfix/, feature/, and bugfix/ prefixes for clarity
🐽 Avoid merging feature/* branches directly into main
🐽 Use Git tags to track all releases, patches, and bug fixes

text:
📱 I just converted my React Web App to React Native in 2 days — and pushed it to TestFlight.
Not weeks. Just 2 days. 🚀

Most developers say “Web-to-mobile takes forever.”
I used Cursor AI and decided to test that belief.


🧠 Day 1 — Setup & Refactor
 • Created a clean React Native project structure
 • Let Cursor AI help refactor my React components for mobile
 • Swapped out web-specific libraries for React Native equivalents
 • Fixed navigation patterns to feel natural on mobile


⚙️ Day 2 — Polish & Deploy
 • Tweaked platform-specific styling for iOS
 • Configured builds & dependencies
 • Submitted to TestFlight, and got approved the same day! 🎯


What made it work?
Not just the AI — but good architecture from day one.
Because my components were modular and well-structured, conversion was frictionless.

Cursor AI handled the repetitive grunt work.
I focused on what actually mattered — mobile performance and UX.


💡 Lesson:
AI isn’t replacing developers — it’s amplifying the ones who know how to use it.
Good structure + smart tools = massive productivity boost.

⏱ Time saved: easily 2–3 weeks of manual work.


Have you tried AI-assisted mobile development yet?
I’m honestly blown away by how much faster it makes the workflow.

👉 DM me if you want to know my setup or workflow — happy to share!

🚨 Open to new opportunities – Senior/Staff iOS Engineer 🚨

Due to recent layoffs in my current project at Flip, I’m now looking for a new opportunity as an iOS developer.

I bring 14+ years of experience in software development, including over 11 years dedicated to iOS. I've worked with Swift, SwiftUI, Objective-C, and React Native, often integrating native modules into cross-platform apps. I'm passionate about clean architecture, testability, and delivering smooth, user-focused experiences.

Beyond mobile, I also have hands-on experience with backend development in Server-Side Swift (Vapor), Python, PHP, and Node.js – which helps me better understand full-stack challenges and collaborate effectively with backend teams.

I’m comfortable configuring CI pipelines using tools like Jenkins, Fastlane, Bitrise, and SwiftLint, often taking ownership of the entire delivery setup to ensure stability and efficiency.

I'm open to roles as a Senior, Staff, or iOS Architect, and would also consider leadership or managerial positions. I’ve successfully led teams, mentored developers, and shipped production-ready apps in both startups and larger international teams.

🧰 Highlights: 
• Led iOS teams and defined app architecture from scratch
• Migrated complex UIs from UIKit to SwiftUI
• Integrated native modules with React Native
• CI setup: Jenkins, Fastlane, Bitrise, SwiftLint
• Backend experience with Vapor (Swift), Python, PHP & Node.js
• Built SDKs and integrated websockets, async/await, and modern testing practices
• Fluent in Objective-C when needed for legacy modules

✅ Looking for: 
• Remote work in an English-speaking environment
• European time zones preferred (US East Coast possible)
• B2B contract
• Available to start around mid-May

If you’re hiring or know someone who is, feel free to reach out or connect. CV available on request.

ЧТО БУДЕШЬ ДЕЛАТЬ:
Владеть end-to-end разработкой ключевых фич в банковских iOS-приложениях — от архитектурного дизайна до релиза в App Store. Работать над secure payment flows, биометрической аутентификацией, offline-first синхронизацией. Менторить младших разработчиков и влиять на технические решения.

Конкурентная зарплата по рынку, определяется по результатам интервью. Бюджет на обучение (курсы + конференции), четкий карьерный path.

ФОРМАТ РАБОТЫ:
Первые 3 месяца офис (Алматы), затем обсуждается удаленка.

Friendly chat со мной и Ilias Adilkhanov, CEO компании. Мы просто узнаем друг друга получше — насколько мы подходим друг другу. Расскажем о компании, вы расскажете о себе. Можем поспрашивать про ваш подход к работе, карьерные победы и фейлы, общие software engineering принципы. Без специфичных iOS вопросов — просто знакомство.

Security в финансовых приложениях (Keychain, biometrics, encryption)
Core Data и persistence strategies
Performance optimization и debugging
Возможно coding challenge или code review существующего кода

ВАЖНО:
Если вы прошли screening CV и попали на интервью — мы тоже заинтересованы в вашем успехе. Постараемся обеспечить максимально дружескую атмосферу. Если вы пару раз ответите неточно или ошибетесь — ничего страшного, мы смотрим на общую картину. Не стесняйтесь показывать свои сильные стороны!
Приветствуется отправка cover letter и проектов, которыми вы гордитесь (GitHub, портфолио, App Store links).

text:
If you use Fastlane for creating your iOS build with xcodebuild command be sure that you consider two important things:
1) Xcode 16 uses another location for storing provision profiles: ~/Library/Developer/Xcode/UserData/Provisioning Profiles. 
Before Xcode 16 it was: ~/Library/MobileDevice/Provisioning Profiles.

2) You use correct Xcode command line tools version. To check which one you use: xcodebuild -version and if it returns Xcode 15.x then you should change it to 16 using this command: sudo xcode-select -s /Applications/Xcode16.app.

Hope it will save some time for you :)

text:
السلام عليكم ورحمة الله وبركاته.... 
أصحابنا مبرمجين ال Flutter 😃

(pictures just for exapmle)

من فترة كنت شغال على "CI/CD Cycle" عندي في الشركة عن طريق
"GitHub Actions and fastlane and firebase distribution"
 وتمت بحمد الله.✅ 
وعالجنا بيها بعض المشاكل الخاصة بإرسال ال APKs للتيم التيست وهو إن كنا بنبعتها
 "whatsapp, slack" ودا طبعا كان بيعرضنا لمشاكل رخم ومتكررة أهمها:
- أولا: دا بيفصل ال developer من شغله الحالي لإنه مطالب يوقف الشغل اللي في إيده حاليا ويعمل "Extracting APK" و بعدين يعمله upload وما أدراك سرعة النت في مصر والuploading تحديدا.⚠️ 
- ثانيا: ممكن ال Tester بعد ما ينزل ال APK ولو عنده APKs قديمة منزلها قبل كدا وعمل install لواحد فيهم هيجيلك ب bugs قديمة أنت حليتها قبل كدا ودي مش طريقة شغل أبدا.🙂

الأدوات اللي تم إستخدامها في cycle بتاعتنا:
- Fastlane:
هو مجموعة من الأدوات مفتوحة المصدر التي تساعد في أتمتة العمليات المتكررة أثناء تطوير ونشر تطبيقات الهواتف المحمولة، خصوصًا لتطبيقات iOS و Android. هدف Fastlane هو تسهيل أتمتة مجموعة من المهام المعقدة والتي تتطلب وقتًا كبيرًا مثل بناء التطبيق، توقيعه، رفعه إلى المتاجر، وإدارة التحديثات.
- Firebase App Distribution:
هو جزء من منصة Firebase التي تقدمها Google لتوزيع النسخ التجريبية من التطبيقات بسهولة على المستخدمين من أجل اختبارها قبل إصدار النسخة النهائية على المتاجر مثل App Store و Google Play. يساعد Firebase App Distribution المطورين على جمع ملاحظات من المختبرين بشكل سريع وفعال.
- GitHub Actions:
هي خدمة من GitHub تتيح للمطورين أتمتة سير العمل (workflows) الخاصة بمشاريعهم على GitHub. تتيح هذه الخدمة للمطورين تنفيذ مهام معينة مثل البناء، الاختبار، النشر، والمزيد، بشكل تلقائي كلما تم تعديل الكود أو دفع التحديثات إلى مستودع GitHub.

وطبعا علشان حضرتك كمبرمج تبني ال CI/CD cycle محتاج تدخل على ال docs بتاعة كل أداة فيهم وتفهم الموضوع واحدة واحدة وكل أداة بيتم بنائها إزاي وفي features زيادة ممكن تضيفها في مشروعك على حسب متطلباتك بس نصيحة خدهم بالترتيب التالي:
١- Fastlane✅ 
٢- Firebase Distribution✅ 
٣- GitHub Actions✅ 

وال screen shots اللي موجودة دي for exmple فقط عن طريقة الكتابة لكل جزئ فيهم ولو احتاجتني في أي استفسار تقدر تتواصل معايا وهحاول أفيد قدر المستطاع.

نقطة مهمة جدًا حابب أشكر Omar Ahmed ❤️ 
لإن من غير الفديوهات بتاعته ما كنتش قدرت أفهم الأدوات دي بكل سهولة ومن محتوى عربي على ال YouTube ومجاني كمان من ال Flutter Advanced Playlist ❤️
وهنصحك نشوف الفيديوهات اللي بيشرح فيهم ال CI/CD قبل ما تبدأ وتقرأ ال docs علشان يكون عندك فكرة بشكل كويس عن الأدوات دي وهتعمل بيها ايه بالضبط وإيه التتابع بتاعها قبل ما تحاول تعملها customization على مشاريعك سواء بقى بتستخدم Flavors وخلافه مع الأدوات دي الموضوع بيختلف شوة.

A strong CI/CD pipeline ensures every update is tested, built, and deployed seamlessly—eliminating manual effort and making releases effortless.

In our latest insight article, Yash Savsani writes about setting up CI/CD for Flutter iOS apps using Fastlane & GitHub Actions, covering:
🔹 Automated builds & testing
🔹 Seamless deployment to Firebase & TestFlight
🔹 Optimized workflows for faster releases

📖 Read the full blog now. Link in comments.

text:
Automating React Native Deployments with Fastlane

Manual builds and uploads to TestFlight or Play Store can be time-consuming. That’s where Fastlane comes in — a powerful tool to automate mobile app deployments.

Here’s how I use Fastlane in React Native projects:

1. Install Fastlane
- Install via Homebrew or npm:
 ~brew install fastlane

2. Setup for iOS and Android
- Run in each platform directory:
 ~cd ios && fastlane init
 ~cd ../android && fastlane init
 - Choose manual setup or integrate with existing builds.

3. Configure Fastlane Files
- Inside the Fastfile, define lanes like:
 lane :beta_ios do
 build_ios_app(scheme: "YourApp")
 upload_to_testflight
 end

 lane :beta_android do
 gradle(task: "assembleRelease")
 upload_to_play_store(track: "internal")
 end

4. Use Environment Variables
- Store API keys and credentials securely using .env files or CI secrets.

5. Integrate with CI/CD
- Combine with GitHub Actions, Bitrise, or CircleCI to trigger deployments automatically on merge or release tags.

6. Benefits of Fastlane
- One command = build + sign + upload
- Supports screenshots, changelogs, code signing
- Easy rollback and version bumping

Automating deployment saves time, reduces human error, and keeps your team moving fast. I highly recommend setting it up early in any React Native project.

Are you using Fastlane yet, or still deploying manually?

🔁 Streamline Updates: Push updates to testers seamlessly.

🛠 Improve Workflow: Keep everything organized and reduce human error.


If you're looking to optimize your app delivery process and deliver builds faster, Fastlane + Firebase is a game changer.

One of my goals for 2025 is to write at least one technical post each month, and I’m kicking it off with a guide on how to speed up and simplify multi-app iOS builds using Fastlane resigning! 😁

For example, instead of rebuilding the same app for every environment (dev, staging, prod), you can build once and reuse the same binary by updating environment configurations and resigning it. By doing this you'll save time and create a more structured, reliable release process.

Personally, I’ve set up pipelines where a build starts in development, gets resigned for staging, and is promoted to production once QA gives approval—all without rebuilding. It’s a faster, cleaner way to verify builds at every stage.

Check it out here: https://lnkd.in/dfczUusp

Would love your feedback or to hear how you handle multi-environment builds! 😊

text:
🚀 From Manual Pain to Automated Gain
A few months back, I had a major developer headache.
 On top of my day-to-day coding, bug fixing, and feature building, I also had to upload multiple builds to the App Store.
Now, if you’re an iOS dev, you know the pain. You’re deep in solving a critical bug… and boom 💥 you’re interrupted because you need to push 3 builds to TestFlight. Nightmare, right?
Being the “lazy developer” I am (the good kind of lazy 👀), I asked myself: Can’t this process be automated?
I started digging — StackOverflow, ChatGPT, docs… and stumbled upon Fastlane. Out of the box, it had amazing solutions to automate builds and uploads. But there was a catch — Fastlane was really designed for single-target apps.
 👉 What about a shared codebase with hundreds of apps?
 👉 How do you manage assets, Info.plists, signing & certificates?
 👉 How do you keep it flexible enough to scale?
Weeks of late nights, broken builds, and tons of trial & error later — I had my breakthrough.
 By combining:
 ⚡ A deeper understanding of iOS internals & code signing
 ⚡ Some Bash scripting + Ruby scripting
 ⚡ Tweaks to Fastlane pipelines
 ⚡ Dynamic configuration for assets & plist management
…I built a fully automated release system.
 Now, I just tell my system which client I want, and boom — a build is uploaded to TestFlight. Zero manual clicks. ✅
The best part? The peace of mind knowing I’ll never waste another hour doing repetitive uploads.
Key takeaway for me:
 If you’re dedicated enough (and just lazy enough to hate repetitive work), you can automate anything.
💡 Curious to hear — how do you handle repetitive tasks in your workflow? Do you automate, delegate, or just grind through them?

text:
🚀 𝗥𝗲𝗰𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗹𝘆 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗱: 𝗙𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗲 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝗻 𝗙𝗹𝘂𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿

This week, I explored 𝗙𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗲, an open-source automation tool that simplifies the entire 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱, 𝘁𝗲𝘀𝘁, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆 process for Flutter apps - and I have to say, it's incredibly powerful.

Fastlane is designed to automate those repetitive and error-prone steps we usually handle manually - like uploading builds, managing version codes, handling certificates, and pushing apps to 𝗚𝗼𝗼𝗴𝗹𝗲 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝘆 𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗲 or 𝗧𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗙𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁. Once it's properly set up, deployment becomes as simple as running a single command!

💡 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗜 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗙𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗲

As a Flutter developer, managing both Android and iOS builds can get time-consuming, especially when you’re maintaining multiple versions or environments.
 That’s where Fastlane steps in - it helps you:
◉ Automate builds and deployments for 𝗔𝗻𝗱𝗿𝗼𝗶𝗱 and 𝗶𝗢𝗦
◉ Handle versioning and code signing automatically
◉ Save time by reducing manual uploads and configuration
◉ Maintain a consistent and reliable release process

⚙️ 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗖𝗜/𝗖𝗗 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀

After testing Fastlane locally, I integrated it into a continuous integration (CI) setup.
 Fastlane works seamlessly with a variety of CI/CD systems, including:
◉ 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 : Perfect for automated Flutter builds, tests, and deployments directly from your repo.
◉ 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗟𝗮𝗯 𝗖𝗜/𝗖𝗗 : Great for larger projects needing flexible pipelines.
◉ 𝗖𝗶𝗿𝗰𝗹𝗲𝗖𝗜 (https://circleci.com/) : Ideal for automating multi-platform releases.
◉ 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝘃𝗶𝘀 𝗖𝗜 (https://www.travis-ci.com/) : Simple integration for lightweight projects.
◉ 𝗕𝗶𝘁𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗲, 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗰, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗔𝗽𝗽𝗰𝗶𝗿𝗰𝗹𝗲 : All-in-one mobile CI/CD platforms with built-in Flutter and Fastlane support.

These integrations make it possible to push updates to beta testers or app stores automatically after a successful build - completely hands-free.

🧠 𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗧𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘆𝘀

After setting it up, I realized how much time Fastlane can save.
 It not only streamlines app delivery but also ensures that each release is consistent, traceable, and less prone to manual mistakes.
If you’re managing frequent Flutter releases or planning to implement CI/CD pipelines - 𝗙𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗲 𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗯𝘀𝗼𝗹𝘂𝘁𝗲𝗹𝘆 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗵 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴. It transforms your deployment workflow from manual and time-consuming to smooth and automated.

For more details, visit https://lnkd.in/diaXpqYJ

💬 Have you tried integrating Fastlane in your Flutter projects? I'd love to hear how it improved your release process!

text:
Hey Flutter fam!

Ever stared at your terminal, coffee going cold, while manually building and deploying that iOS app for the 47th time? Yeah, me too, until Codemagic and Fastlane crashed the party like uninvited superheroes. Now my deploys are faster than my dog's zoomies across the kitchen. 😄

Picture this: Codemagic handles the heavy lifting with cloud-based CI/CD tailored for Flutter, while Fastlane automates the boring stuff, code signing, beta uploads, you name it. Set it up once, and boom: PRs trigger builds, tests run like clockwork, and your app hits TestFlight or Play Store without you lifting a finger. It's like giving your workflow a spa day.

A super clean codemagic.yaml like this one (just drop it in your repo root):

workflows:
 flutter-deploy:
 name: Build & Deploy Flutter App
 instance_type: mac_mini_m2
 max_build_duration: 60
 integrations:
 app_store_connect: Your App Store Connect API Key
 google_play: Your Google Play Service Account
 triggering:
 events:
 - push
 branch_patterns:
 - pattern: main
 include: true
 scripts:
 - name: Install dependencies & get packages
 script: |
 flutter pub get
 - name: Build iOS (with Fastlane)
 script: |
 cd ios && bundle install && bundle exec fastlane beta
 - name: Build Android
 script: |
 flutter build appbundle --release
 artifacts:
 - build/**/outputs/**/*.aab
 - build/ios/ipa/*.ipa
 publishing:
 google_play:
 track: internal
 app_store_connect:
 submit_to_testflight: true


One file. Zero tears. Works on every push to main.

Ready to stop babysitting builds? Start here:
📚 Codemagic + Flutter guide → https://lnkd.in/d9KyZ38n
⚡ Fastlane docs → https://lnkd.in/dwX7XVFp

Who else is living that automated life now? Drop a 🔥 if you’ve already set this up or a 🙋 if you’re doing it this weekend!

Wanna level up your whole Flutter game? I just redesigned my portfolio — come say hi and steal some inspiration 😉
https://lnkd.in/dRFRwSKK

text:
Building a Rock-Solid CI/CD Pipeline with React Native, Fastlane & GitHub Actions 

CI/CD is essential for mobile development, where seamless app updates and robust testing processes can make or break the user experience. Here's a breakdown of how to set up an efficient CI/CD pipeline for a React Native app using Fastlane and GitHub Actions.

🔹 Why CI/CD for React Native?
Implementing CI/CD ensures quick feedback on every push, builds quality, and helps avoid nasty surprises during releases. Automating testing, building, and deployment makes the entire process scalable and reliable.

🛠️ Setup with Fastlane & GitHub Actions

1️⃣ Automate Builds with Fastlane
Fastlane is a game-changer for mobile app automation. Set up lanes for testing, building, and deployment:

Test Lane: Run unit and integration tests to catch bugs early.
Build Lane: Automate builds for both iOS and Android.
Deploy Lane: Push beta versions to TestFlight/Play Store for quick QA feedback.
Pro Tip: Use environment-specific configurations in Fastlane to handle different build types like dev, staging, and production.

2️⃣ GitHub Actions for Pipeline Orchestration
Set up GitHub Actions to run on every push, PR, or tag:

Triggers: Run workflows on pushes to main or specific branches.
Jobs: Divide workflows into stages: install dependencies, run tests, build, and deploy.
Caching: Use caching for dependencies to speed up builds significantly.
3️⃣ Secrets Management
Store sensitive information (API keys, signing certificates) securely in GitHub Secrets. Reference these in your GitHub Action workflows to ensure they’re only available during secure builds.

4️⃣ Parallel Testing
Use GitHub Actions matrix builds to run tests on multiple device configurations in parallel, identifying issues across various environments.

5️⃣ Slack & Email Notifications
Set up notifications to keep your team updated on build statuses. Fastlane’s Slack integration is perfect for this, and GitHub Actions can also trigger email alerts.

🌟 Benefits of This Setup
Faster Releases: Automate testing and deployment to deliver new features faster.
Reliability: Catch issues early with automated tests and reliable builds.
Team Alignment: Notifications ensure everyone is on the same page about the app’s status.
A solid CI/CD pipeline with Fastlane and GitHub Actions makes React Native app development smoother and more efficient, enabling you to focus on what matters: building great user experiences. 💡

Have you implemented CI/CD for React Native? What tools do you use? 👇

text:
Life update. 🙂

It's been 3 years since I started working as an iOS Engineer at PT. Sarimelati Kencana Tbk., which also marked as the beginning of my iOS Engineer career. Exciting, rewarding, and honestly… challenging in ways I didn’t expect!

A few lessons that shaped me along the way:

1. Self-directed learning isn’t optional.
I joined with very little experience in iOS development, so I had to ramp up fast about app architecture, concurrency, testing, CI/CD, SAST/DAST, and more. The more I learned, the more I realized how much I was quite behind (Stay humble, as Erling Haaland said).

2. I think reactive programming is still relevant today.
Back then, I was quite curious about why my current company required reactive programming skills, especially RxSwift. And now, I understand the complexity of managing state, especially in UI, without reactive programming. 

3. After all this time, taking care of Apple cert and sigh alone are quite tiring.
Because we used manual signing, of course I need to maintain app certificates and profiles which is quite tiring especially when adding new testers or dealing with expirations. Thanks to Fastlane Match for making it all a little easier than before.

4. Working solo has a hidden cost.
I’m grateful for the trust and opportunity to grow here. The good thing is I can move fast alone. But I do miss having fellow iOS engineers to review, brainstorm, and share the “developer life” with, maybe something like "How does it feel to have a lifetime partner as a product manager while you're an iOS engineer?"

5. Tech moves fast, so I need to work smarter.
Deprecations, new tools, new best practices never stop. I’ve learned to stay ahead by learning early and documenting clearly, so delivery doesn’t get disrupted and business processes can still running.

6. Business needs often win over tech stack updates.
I've always wanted to modernize our legacy code. But in many cases, I've encountered shipping value for customers comes first. I still have to learn to balance long-term improvements with short-term impacts.

Even now, I often ask to myself, "Do I need to learn how to create an AI model so I don't get left behind, or are native Android and Flutter enough, as they still have an intersection with my current profession?”. For now, I still enjoy and grateful for my learning process in my current company, and maybe I'll think about the same thing again in the future, hahaha.

Stay excited what's coming next!

text:
🚀 Implemented CI/CD for React Native Using GitHub Actions and Webhooks

CI/CD is a game-changer for mobile development, ensuring smooth, error-free deployments. Recently, I experimented with setting up a CI/CD pipeline for a React Native app, leveraging:

✅ GitHub Actions – Automates builds, tests, and deployments
✅ Jest – Ensures code reliability with automated testing
✅ Husky – Runs pre-commit hooks to enforce code quality
✅ Fastlane – Simplifies Android & iOS app distribution
✅ Discord Webhooks – Sends success/failure updates for real-time tracking

The Workflow
1️⃣ Pre-commit checks with Husky – Linting & formatting before pushing code
 2️⃣ GitHub Actions triggers on push/PR – Builds & tests the app automatically
 3️⃣ Fastlane handles releases – Generates APKs/IPAs & uploads to Play Store/App Store
 4️⃣ Discord Webhook alerts – Notifies the team about build success/failure

Why This Matters?

🔹 Saves time with automation
🔹 Reduces manual deployment risks
🔹 Improves collaboration & visibility

Are you using CI/CD for React Native? Would love to hear your experience!

Today I set up Fastlane on Windows to streamline my mobile app delivery process 🚛📱. While most tutorials focus on macOS, I wanted to share that yes, Fastlane works great on Windows too—especially for Android builds!

🔧 What I did:
 • Installed Ruby + DevKit on Windows
 • Used gem install fastlane -NV to get Fastlane up and running
 • Configured Fastlane for Android builds (uploading .aab to Play Console)
 • Explored cross-platform CI options for iOS (since iOS builds still require macOS)

💡 Fastlane is an incredible tool that saves time and reduces manual errors in publishing apps. If you’re a Flutter developer—or working in any mobile stack—you should definitely look into it.

text:
Essential iOS Developer Tools in 2026 

SwiftUI - 2026 is the year you can no longer "just know UIKit." SwiftUI has matured enough that most new projects start here. 
Charles Proxy or Proxyman - debugging network requests hasn't changed much, but it's still essential. 
Figma - dev mode in Figma bridges the designer-developer gap better than ever.
Fastlane + CI/CD (Xcode Cloud / GitHub Actions) - automating builds, tests, and releases saves hundreds of hours per year. Xcode Cloud has gotten more reliable, but I still prefer GitHub Actions for flexibility.
TestFlight + Firebase Crashlytics - your feedback loop. Ship beta builds fast, catch crashes faster.
Xcode Instruments - place for magic
Time Profiler - which methods consume the most CPU time. 
Key metrics to watch:
• CPU usage per thread (keep main thread < 80%)
• Heaviest stack traces (sort by "Weight" to find hot paths)
• Thread count — excessive threads create overhead
Allocations & Leaks - every heap allocation and retain cycles
Animation Hitches - the old Core Animation instrument is gone. In 2026, the Animation Hitches template is your go-to for UI smoothness. It visualizes the entire Render Loop — commit, render prepare, render execute — and pinpoints exactly where frames are late.
Rendering Debug Overlays - Xcode Debug menu: Color Blended Layers + Color Offscreen-Rendered
The real tool? Curiosity. Frameworks change. Xcode versions come and go. The developers who stay relevant are the ones who never stop experimenting.
What's in your iOS toolkit this year? Drop it below

text:
النهارده كان ليا فرصه كويسه جدا
إني أشارك في Huma-volve 
 بسيشن تقني عن واحدة من أهم المراحل في دورة تطوير التطبيقات، وهو:-

‏Automating Flutter App Deployment with Fastlane✨

السيشن دي كانت فرصة حلوة نشارك فيها تجربة واقعية عن إزاي نقدر نحول عملية نشر ورفع الابلكيشن سواء علي Google play أو App store اللي كانت دايمًا مرهقة ومليانة تفاصيل...
لعملية أوتوماتيكية، منظمة، وسريعة جدًا.✅

النقاط اللي اتكلمنا عنها:

1. التحديات اللي بتقابل المطورين في النشر اليدوي:

❌بياخد وقت طويل جدًا وبيأخر التحديثات.
❌عرضة للأخطاء البشرية في الإعدادات والتوقيع.
❌وصعب تضمن نفس النتائج بين Android و iOS.

2. الحل: Fastlane
✅أداة مفتوحة المصدر بتخلي النشر كله يتم تلقائيًا —
من أول بناء التطبيق لحد رفعه على المتجر، بخطوات بسيطة ومتكررة.

3. ليه Fastlane مهمة لمطوري Flutter؟

✅بتوفر وقت كبير جدًا.
✅بتقلل الأخطاء اللي بتحصل بالإيد.
✅وبتخليك تنشر التطبيق على المنصتين في نفس الوقت بسهولة.

4. الخطوات العملية اللي استعرضناها:

✅تثبيت Fastlane باستخدام RubyGems.
✅ربطها مع مشروع Flutter.
✅إنشاء "lanes" مخصصة لكل مرحلة (بناء – توقيع – نشر).
✅وأتمتة النشر بالكامل على Google Play و App Store.

5. أهم الممارسات اللي لازم تتبعها:

✅استخدم Git أو أي نظام version control لملفات
‏Fastlane.
✅اختبر الـ lanes على أكتر من جهاز قبل الاعتماد عليها.
✅واحرص إن البيانات الحساسة زي الشهادات محفوظة في environment variables.

اللي خرجنا بيه إن Fastlane مش مجرد أداة…
دي خطوة حقيقية في طريق تطوير بيئة CI/CD متكاملة بتخلي عملية النشر أسرع، أدق، وأكثر ذكاءً.✨

text:
🚀 Introducing CultureLens - HackHive 2026

Most workplace miscommunication is not about bad intent. It is about different communication styles, power dynamics, generational gaps, and cultural context colliding in real time.

CultureLens is a consent-based, privacy-first conversation analytics platform that gives real-time, culturally-aware insights into how a conversation is going. It listens (with consent) into arguments as they happen, spots patterns like interruptions and turn-taking imbalance, and surfaces neutral, actionable feedback so people can actually understand each other better. 

We built it with:

ElevenLabs conversational AI for real-time voice interaction 
Firebase Auth + Firestore + Storage for secure, real-time infrastructure 
A FastAPI backend with OpenAPI docs, plus proper linting and testing 
A Next.js + React + TypeScript frontend with a real design system 
And a native iOS companion app (SwiftUI) with ElevenLabs WebSocket integration, plus CI/CD tooling like GitHub Actions and Fastlane 
We tried to build this like something you could actually ship, not just a demo. 

I'd like to thank my teammates Aryan Kashefi-Aazam, Paul Joseph and Sunny Patel who took this project to the next level, and Wasay Aamir Taha Rana and the entire OTU Computer Science Club for making this hackathon possible

The GitHub is here if you'd like to take a look, and keep an eye out for an official release 👀 

https://lnkd.in/ef2EVrg8

text:
Inside the Life of an iOS Developer: Beyond the Code

Being an iOS developer is more than just writing Swift. It’s about building intuitive, high-performance experiences that fit into the palms of millions of users.

Here’s what a typical day looks like:

👥 Morning Collaboration
Daily stand-ups to align with product managers, designers, and QA teams. Understanding not just what we're building, but why.

💻 Focused Development Time
Working in Swift/SwiftUI, navigating Xcode, implementing new features, fixing bugs, and optimizing for performance. Every line of code is an opportunity to improve user experience.

🧪 Testing & Debugging
Unit tests, UI tests, and real-device debugging making sure what works in theory works in production too.

📲 Continuous Integration & Delivery
Using tools like Git, Fastlane, and TestFlight to ship builds efficiently and safely.

📚 Ongoing Learning
iOS evolves fast. Whether it’s adopting new APIs, exploring Apple’s latest frameworks, or reviewing WWDC sessions staying up to date is part of the craft.

🚀 User Impact
The most rewarding part? Seeing your app help people in real life whether it's for productivity, health, learning, or entertainment.

The iOS ecosystem is fast-moving, challenging, and deeply rewarding. If you're passionate about creating world-class mobile experiences, you're in great company.

text:
بما إننا اتكلمنا عن Fastlane، Codemagic، وGitHub Actions / GitLab CI الأيام اللي فاتت…
تعالوا نقول بوضوح الفرق بينهم، وإمتى تستخدم كل واحد فيهم.

⸻

🚀 Fastlane

أداة قوية جدًا للـ automation في عمليات الـ Build والـ Release.

✅ الأفضل في:
 • توقيع التطبيقات (iOS وAndroid)
 • رفع تلقائي على App Store / Play Store
 • إدارة metadata وscreenshots
 • تقدر تستخدمها لوحدها أو جوه أي CI

💡 استخدمها لو:
بتنزل نسخ كتير، وعايز تظبط عملية النشر بشكل شبه تلقائي
وخصوصًا لو بتشتغل iOS، Fastlane هتوفر عليك مجهود ضخم

⸻

⚡ Codemagic

CI/CD جاهز مخصوص لـ Flutter. مفيش إعداد معقد.

✅ الأفضل في:
 • Build تلقائي لأي منصة Flutter
 • توقيع النسخ (حتى iOS بسهولة)
 • تكامل مباشر مع GitHub وFirebase
 • واجهة سهلة جدًا للمبتدئين والمحترفين

💡 استخدمه لو:
بتشتغل Flutter وعايز تبدأ CI/CD بسرعة ومن غير ما تكتب YAML
ومش محتاج تفاصيل أو تحكم متقدم

⸻

🛠️ GitHub Actions / GitLab CI

أدوات CI مرنة وقابلة للتخصيص لأي مشروع بأي حجم.

✅ الأفضل في:
 • كتابة workflow متكامل (build – test – deploy)
 • التحكم الكامل في كل خطوة
 • إدارة بيئات مختلفة (staging / production)
 • تشغيل أوامر Fastlane أو Firebase CLI أو أي script

💡 استخدمهم لو:
عايز تبني CI/CD tailored لاحتياجاتك
وعندك خبرة كافية بـ YAML والتعامل مع الـ Pipelines

كل أداة ليها مكانها حسب احتياج المشروع والتيم.
جرب، وابدأ بأبسط حاجة… وبعد كده طوّر الـ workflow حسب حجم شغلك.

لو عندك تجربة مع واحدة منهم شاركني بيها 👇
أو لو محتار تبدأ بإيه… خليني أساعدك تختار الأنسب.

𝐌𝐚𝐱𝐢𝐦𝐢𝐳𝐞 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐄𝐥𝐞𝐯𝐚𝐭𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐝𝐞 𝐐𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐡 𝐄𝐬𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐚𝐥 𝐗𝐜𝐨𝐝𝐞 𝐓𝐨𝐨𝐥𝐬

In today's fast-paced coding environment, leveraging the right tools can significantly enhance your productivity and elevate the quality of your code. 🚀 Here are some must-have Xcode tools that every developer should consider incorporating into their workflow:

1. 𝐒𝐰𝐢𝐟𝐭𝐋𝐢𝐧𝐭
 - 𝘔𝘢𝘪𝘯𝘵𝘢𝘪𝘯 𝘊𝘰𝘥𝘦 𝘊𝘰𝘯𝘴𝘪𝘴𝘵𝘦𝘯𝘤𝘺: SwiftLint helps enforce a consistent coding style across your projects, ensuring cleaner and more readable code.
 - 𝘊𝘢𝘵𝘤𝘩 𝘗𝘰𝘵𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘪𝘢𝘭 𝘐𝘴𝘴𝘶𝘦𝘴: Detect potential bugs, unused code, and other issues early on, making your code more robust.

2. 𝐂𝐨𝐜𝐨𝐚𝐏𝐨𝐝𝐬
 - 𝘌𝘧𝘧𝘰𝘳𝘵𝘭𝘦𝘴𝘴 𝘋𝘦𝘱𝘦𝘯𝘥𝘦𝘯𝘤𝘺 𝘔𝘢𝘯𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘮𝘦𝘯𝘵: Easily manage and integrate third-party libraries into your Xcode projects, saving time and effort.
 - 𝘝𝘦𝘳𝘴𝘪𝘰𝘯 𝘊𝘰𝘯𝘵𝘳𝘰𝘭: Keep track of library versions and update dependencies seamlessly.

3. 𝐅𝐚𝐬𝐭𝐥𝐚𝐧𝐞
 - 𝘈𝘶𝘵𝘰𝘮𝘢𝘵𝘦 𝘛𝘢𝘴𝘬𝘴: Streamline repetitive tasks like building, testing, and releasing your iOS apps, freeing up your time for more critical development work.
 - 𝘊𝘰𝘯𝘵𝘪𝘯𝘶𝘰𝘶𝘴 𝘐𝘯𝘵𝘦𝘨𝘳𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯: Integrate Fastlane with CI/CD pipelines for efficient and reliable app deployment processes.

4. 𝐗𝐂𝐓𝐞𝐬𝐭
 - 𝘜𝘯𝘪𝘵 𝘛𝘦𝘴𝘵𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘔𝘢𝘥𝘦 𝘌𝘢𝘴𝘺: XCTest provides a robust framework for writing and running unit tests, ensuring the stability and reliability of your code.
 - 𝘛𝘦𝘴𝘵-𝘋𝘳𝘪𝘷𝘦𝘯 𝘋𝘦𝘷𝘦𝘭𝘰𝘱𝘮𝘦𝘯𝘵: Embrace TDD practices to build more resilient and maintainable codebases.

5. 𝐕𝐢𝐞𝐰𝐌𝐨𝐧𝐢𝐭𝐨𝐫
 - 𝘝𝘪𝘴𝘶𝘢𝘭 𝘋𝘦𝘣𝘶𝘨𝘨𝘪𝘯𝘨: Easily inspect and visualize the view hierarchy of your iOS apps during runtime, aiding in UI troubleshooting and optimization.
 - 𝘗𝘦𝘳𝘧𝘰𝘳𝘮𝘢𝘯𝘤𝘦 𝘔𝘰𝘯𝘪𝘵𝘰𝘳𝘪𝘯𝘨: Identify UI performance bottlenecks and optimize your app's user experience.

6. 𝐒𝐰𝐢𝐟𝐭𝐅𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐭
 - 𝘊𝘰𝘥𝘦 𝘍𝘰𝘳𝘮𝘢𝘵𝘵𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘛𝘰𝘰𝘭: Ensure consistent and clean code formatting by automatically applying style rules to your Swift codebase.
 - 𝘊𝘰𝘥𝘦𝘣𝘢𝘴𝘦 𝘔𝘢𝘪𝘯𝘵𝘢𝘪𝘯𝘢𝘣𝘪𝘭𝘪𝘵𝘺: Improve code readability and maintainability with standardized formatting practices.

Incorporate these essential tools into your Xcode development workflow to streamline your coding process, boost productivity, and deliver high-quality apps efficiently. 💻✨

text:
11 days.

That's how long our client waited for a critical payment bug fix to go live.

The app was hemorrhaging revenue. Users couldn't complete purchases. But Apple's review process doesn't care about your emergency.

This happened at HyperBrain Labs last month. We pushed the fix in minutes, but getting it to users? 11 painful days.

The worst part? CodePush — the tool that solved this exact problem — was deprecated in March 2025.

100,000+ React Native teams are now stranded without over-the-air updates.

So I'm building SwiftPatch from scratch. A modern OTA update platform that actually works.

Here's my commitment: 30 days of building in public.

Every breakthrough. Every bug. Every pivot.

Day 1 starts now.

The React Native ecosystem needs this. And frankly, I'm tired of watching founders suffer through App Store hell when there's a better way.

Follow along if you want to see how we build a developer tool from zero to launch in 30 days.

What's the longest you've waited for an App Store review?

text:
OTA updates in React Native — current options 👇

If you’re evaluating OTA updates today, here’s a quick breakdown of what teams are using:

✅ Microsoft CodePush
 • Traditional choice for bare React Native projects
 • Free and reliable for core OTA needs
 • Supports staged rollouts and instant rollbacks
 • Now in maintenance mode with limited new development

✅ Expo EAS Update
 • Default OTA solution within the Expo ecosystem
 • Excellent developer experience and active maintenance
 • Integrates tightly with EAS services
 • Works with bare React Native CLI projects as well

✅ Stallion / Revopush / Hot-Updater
 • Modern third-party alternatives to CodePush
 • Features like automatic crash rollbacks and binary diff updates
 • Often include analytics and monitoring
 • Available as hosted or self-hosted solutions for compliance needs

OTA updates are powerful — but they should be used carefully.
JS and asset updates work great; native changes still belong in store releases.

Curious — which OTA solution are you using in your React Native apps today? 👋

text:
OTA updates in React Native — current options 👇

If you’re evaluating OTA updates today, here’s a quick breakdown of what teams are using:

✅ Microsoft CodePush
 • Traditional choice for bare React Native projects
 • Free and reliable for core OTA needs
 • Supports staged rollouts and instant rollbacks
 • Now in maintenance mode with limited new development

✅ Expo EAS Update
 • Default OTA solution within the Expo ecosystem
 • Excellent developer experience and active maintenance
 • Integrates tightly with EAS services
 • Works with bare React Native CLI projects as well

✅ Stallion / Revopush / Hot-Updater
 • Modern third-party alternatives to CodePush
 • Features like automatic crash rollbacks and binary diff updates
 • Often include analytics and monitoring
 • Available as hosted or self-hosted solutions for compliance needs

OTA updates are powerful — but they should be used carefully.
JS and asset updates work great; native changes still belong in store releases.

Curious — which OTA solution are you using in your React Native apps today? 👋

text:
Day 2: 21 high-severity CVEs are sitting in your React Native app right now. Most teams don't even know it. 
 
I was reviewing our agency's portfolio last week — 50+ React Native MVPs we've shipped for clients. Every single one was running CodePush for OTA updates.

Here's the problem: Microsoft deprecated CodePush in 2022. No security patches. No support. But developers are still using it because there's no clear alternative.

The wake-up call came during a security audit for one of our enterprise clients. The dependency scanner flagged 21 high-severity vulnerabilities in CodePush's dependency tree. Some dating back 3+ years.

What every React Native team needs to know:

 1. CodePush hasn't received updates since 2025
 2. Multiple CVEs in its core dependencies remain unpatched
 3. Microsoft recommends migrating but offers no replacement
 4. Your production apps are sitting ducks

The scariest part? Most teams assume Microsoft-backed tools are automatically secure and maintained. That assumption could cost you everything in a breach.

 If you're running CodePush:
 → Audit your dependencies NOW
 → Start migration planning
 → Don't ship new features until you have a plan

This is exactly why I'm building SwiftPatch — a modern, secure OTA platform built for 2024's security standards.

Are you still running deprecated dependencies in production? What's your migration strategy?
