text:
I shipped my first React Native app on v0.0.7.
Back then there was no “best practice”, no polished navigation stacks, no Expo, and definitely no New Architecture.

We were figuring things out as we went. Reading release notes week after week, spending time upgrading the framework and resolving the breaking changes.

During that fun time I had at Shoutem, my team ( 👋 Luka Bracanović Vladimir Adamić) and I had the privilege to:
• build some of the most widely used RN libraries of that era
 • sit in React Native core contributor meetings
 • contribute to projects like Microsoft CodePush and RNPM
 • built official React Europe app
 • go deep into the internals - bridge, native modules, platform specifics, release tooling

Later, as a consultant in Reactor Studio, I helped teams:
• set up scalable RN architectures
• write the native pieces they were missing
• design CI/CD pipelines with Fastlane & Bitrise

Then around 2019, my focus shifted.
System design. Backend. Leadership.

And slowly - I drifted away from day-to-day React Native (I think it was v0.63.0 when I stopped reading release notes).

Fast-forward to a few weeks ago
I started a new side project.
React Native again.
 But this time with Claude Code as my pair programmer.
And it feels like coming back to a city you once lived in.
Everything is familiar, yet completely different.


1️⃣ The New Architecture is the real deal
TurboModules & JSI finally remove the mental overhead of the old bridge.
The “RN vs native” boundary is no longer the bottleneck it used to be.

2️⃣ Expo grew up
Expo is no longer the “you can’t use it for serious apps” option.
Today it’s:
 • a production-grade toolchain
 • the fastest way to get a project moving
 • deeply aligned with the RN ecosystem
If I were starting a project in 2026, I would default to Expo. No question.

3️⃣ The developer experience is on another level
The combination of:
React Native + modern tooling + AI coding assistants
removes an enormous amount of friction we used to accept as normal.
The interesting shift is that the bottleneck is no longer framework knowledge it’s product thinking and architecture.

The biggest personal insight
Years ago, my value came from: knowing the internals, writing native code, debugging the bridge

Today, that expertise is still useful, but it’s no longer the differentiator.
The differentiator is:
• how you design systems
• how fast you can validate ideas
• how well you leverage the tooling

Coming back to React Native after ~6 years feels a bit like:
meeting a former startup colleague who is now a fully grown company.
Same DNA. Completely different maturity.

And honestly, it’s fun again.
If you’ve been away from React Native for a while, I’d recommend taking another look.

You might be very surprised.

text:
⚔️ Cross-Platform Showdown 2026: Compose Multiplatform vs Flutter vs React Native

Which framework should YOU choose? Here's my breakdown:

🟣 Compose Multiplatform (JetBrains + Google)
• Kotlin everywhere - mobile, desktop & backend
• Native Android performance (Jetpack Compose)
• Type-safe, null-safe, compile-time checks
• Best choice for existing Android/Kotlin teams

🔵 Flutter (Google)
• Pixel-perfect UI on all platforms
• Excellent hot reload
• 40k+ packages, mature ecosystem
• Best for custom UI & new projects

⚛️ React Native (Meta)
• JavaScript/TypeScript - huge talent pool
• Share code with React.js web apps
• Native UI components
• OTA updates with CodePush

🎯 Quick Decision:
• Android/Kotlin team → Compose Multiplatform
• JS/React team → React Native 
• Custom UI priority → Flutter
• Desktop apps needed → Compose Multiplatform

💡 There's no "best" framework - only the best fit for YOUR team!

Which one are you using? Let me know in the comments! 👇

text:
CodePush died in March 2025.

100,000+ React Native teams just lost their lifeline.

We felt this pain at in our one of the client app last month. Critical payment bug in production. Users couldn't checkout. Revenue bleeding.

Pushed the fix → waited 11 days for App Store review.

11 days of lost revenue because Apple's review process doesn't care about your emergency.

That's the problem with depending on deprecated tools. When they die, you're stranded.

So I'm building the solution: **SwiftPatch** — a modern over-the-air update platform for React Native.

Here's what I'm committing to:

1. Build completely in public — every line of code, every decision
2. 30 days from zero to MVP
3. Daily progress updates with real metrics
4. Full transparency on wins AND failures

This isn't just another dev tool. It's infrastructure that 100K+ teams desperately need.

Day 1 starts now.

Follow along if you want to see how we ship critical infrastructure in 30 days. Going to be a wild ride.

What's the biggest infrastructure problem you're dealing with right now?

text:
I saw a post on X where a developer got roasted for not knowing how to implement "OTA updates" in a native Android app.

Honestly? I didn't know either. So I looked into it.

Turns out, OTA updates are a system-level thing. Think Android 15 firmware updates. App developers don't touch that.

The confusion comes from React Native and Flutter, where tools like CodePush let you hot-swap JavaScript bundles. That's not how native Android works. Kotlin compiles to DEX bytecode. You can't swap it at runtime.

What native Android actually has is the Google Play In-App Updates API. It's been around since 2019 and lets you prompt users to update right from inside your app.

I wrote a full breakdown covering:
→ Why "OTA updates" doesn't apply to native Android
→ The difference between Flexible and Immediate updates
→ A step-by-step implementation in Kotlin with Jetpack Compose
→ How to test it properly

text:
We shipped a critical bug fix last Tuesday.

It took 47 seconds to reach 100% of our users.

No app store submission. No review queue. No waiting.

A year ago? That same fix would've taken 5 days.

When Microsoft killed CodePush in March 2025, our team like thousands of others, had a problem. The tool we depended on for OTA updates was gone.

Expo wanted $99+/mo and our whole stack. Other alternatives shipped full 18MB bundles for a one-line fix.

So we built our own. Then we realized every React Native team needed this.

SwiftPatch does three things differently:

1. Binary-level diffing → 98% smaller patches (18MB → 236KB)
2. Auto-rollback in <3 seconds when crashes are detected
3. RSA signing with your own keys, not ours

One CLI command. Works with GitHub Actions, CircleCI, or any pipeline.

We just opened the waitlist → swiftpatch.io

Drop a comment if you're dealing with OTA pain right now. I'd love to hear what your team is using 👇

text:
React Native vs. Flutter in 2026 – Which one wins? ⚔️

The million-dollar question for every mobile dev and startup: React Native or Flutter? After working on multiple projects, here’s my take on where the industry stands in 2026. 🧵

🚀 Flutter is amazing if: • You want pixel-perfect UI consistency across iOS and Android. • Your app is heavy on animations and custom graphics. • You want that "Ahead-of-Time" (AOT) compilation speed.

⚛️ But React Native is still my winner. Here’s why:
✅ The Ecosystem: With the New Architecture (Fabric & TurboModules), the performance gap is almost gone. It’s no longer just a "bridge"—it’s direct and fast.
✅ Web-to-Mobile DNA: For businesses, reusing logic between web (React) and mobile (RN) is a massive cost-saver.
✅ The Talent Pool: JavaScript is everywhere. Hiring and scaling a team is significantly easier and faster for React Native.
✅ OTA Updates: Features like CodePush are a lifesaver. Fixing a bug without waiting for a 2-day App Store review? Yes, please!
The Truth: Neither is "better." It’s about Project Fit.

🎮 Building a high-fidelity game-like UI? Go Flutter. 🛒 Building a content-rich, scalable social or e-commerce app? React Native is the way.

As a React Native dev, I’m biased but seeing how Meta and the community are evolving this framework, the future looks incredibly bright. 🌟
What’s your pick for 2026? Are you team 💙 (Flutter) or team ⚛️ (React Native)?

Let’s debate in the comments! 👇

text:
48 hrs.

That's how long our client waited for Apple to approve a critical payment bug fix.

Their revenue was bleeding while we sat helpless, watching the App Store review queue.

This happened to us at HyperBrain Labs last month. We shipped a React Native app, pushed to production, everything looked perfect. Then users started reporting failed payments.

One line of code. One tiny fix. 11 days of lost revenue.

That's when I realized CodePush being deprecated in March isn't just an inconvenience — it's an existential crisis for 100K+ React Native teams.

So I'm building SwiftPatch. A modern over-the-air update platform built for 2026.

Here's what I'm doing differently:

1. **Zero-config setup** — deploy updates in 30 seconds
2. **Smart rollbacks** — auto-detect crashes and revert instantly 
3. **Granular targeting** — push fixes to specific user segments
4. **Real-time monitoring** — see update adoption as it happens

I'm building this completely in public over the next 30 days. Every decision, every setback, every breakthrough.

Day 1 starts now.

Follow along if you want to see how a modern dev tool gets built from scratch. I'll share the tech stack, design decisions, and probably a few late-night debugging sessions.

What's the longest you've waited for an app store review?

text:
🚀 Introducing Super Native: The New Architecture for NativePHP

We just took NativePHP to a whole new level. Internally, we’re calling it Super Native — a true native runtime for PHP on mobile.

Watch here: https://lnkd.in/eSPh4jYC

Here’s what’s different:
 - No bridges. PHP talks to native code directly in shared memory.
 - No JSON or HTTP overhead. Cross-language communication is in-process.
 - Persistent runtime. PHP state can live alongside the native app.
 - Full control over performance. High-frequency tasks stay native, orchestration stays in PHP.

If you’re familiar with React Native and JSI, you’ll see the parallel, but we’ve gone further. This isn’t about wrapping a web app or simulating native behavior. PHP now runs native.

In our latest video Shane Rosenthal breaks down the architecture, explains why this is faster, and show what it unlocks for mobile developers.

This is more than a performance improvement — it’s a fundamental shift in how PHP can power mobile apps.

text:
Excited to share a sneak peek into my latest project: a secure, user-centric mobile app designed for seamless web integration and top-tier data protection—more features dropping soon!

Mobile app development, especially with React Native, can be incredibly frustrating at times. A tiny oversight crashes the entire system, demanding relentless debugging. Yet, that's the thrill—you can't rest until that feature shines exactly as envisioned. I kicked off this project just last week, and the late-night breakthroughs make it all worthwhile.

What's your go-to technique for making React Native coding fun, efficient, and less error-prone? Sharing tips would greatly help enthusiastic developers like us and others—your insights will make a real difference!

text:
Why build twice when one codebase can do it all? ⚛️

Building separate iOS and Android apps means double the time, double the cost, and double the maintenance burden. In today's fast-moving market, that is a luxury most businesses cannot afford.

React Native changes the equation entirely.

At Hendoi Technologies, we build React Native applications that deliver a genuinely native experience on both iOS and Android from a single, elegant codebase — without compromise on performance, design, or user experience.

What we deliver:
✅ One codebase · Two platforms · Zero compromise 
✅ Native performance — not a WebView, not a wrapper 
✅ Shared business logic with platform-specific UI polish 
✅ 60fps animations and smooth gesture handling 
✅ Live updates via CodePush — ship fixes without App Store delays 
✅ Full access to native device APIs — camera, biometrics, GPS 
✅ Simultaneous iOS and Android launch from day one


The result: 
you reach 100% of the mobile market at nearly half the development cost — with a codebase that is clean, maintainable, and fast to iterate on.

We have delivered React Native apps for startups and enterprises across USA, Canada, and Bengaluru — products that users cannot tell are cross-platform, because they feel completely native.

Stop choosing between iOS and Android. Choose both. 🚀

Let's build your cross-platform app today. 💬

text:
Hey Community! 

In The React Native Rewind #31: 

We explore React Native Grab, a new tool for the "vibe code" era that lets you tap native elements to send their exact source code context directly to your AI debugger. We also dive into the surprise release of AsyncStorage 3.0, which finally moves away from the global singleton model to allow for isolated storage instances and a cleaner API. 

If the Rewind made you nod, smile, or think “oh… that’s actually cool” — a share or reply genuinely helps ❤️

text:
Initial impressions after testing Beadbox, an open-source tool that provides real-time visibility into your AI coding agents.

This is the first time agents working felt legible.

Normally, a swarm turns into a mess of logs, half-finished branches, and “wait… who’s blocked on what?” moments. 

Beadbox flips that. It renders what the agents are doing as a clean issue tracker inside a desktop app — issues pop up as they work, epics actually show progress, and the dependency view makes blockers painfully obvious (in a good way).

What surprised me most: it changes how you manage the swarm.

Instead of babysitting prompts or scrolling terminals, you manage like a PM:

- What’s in flight
- What’s done
- What’s blocked
- What should be split into another issue

And because it’s a native app (Tauri) it feels fast, not like another heavy web dashboard.

Still early, but the direction is clear: less “agent theater,” more operational visibility.

If you’re running coding swarms right now, what’s your biggest pain point: visibility, handoffs, or blockers?

​গত কয়েক মাসের শারীরিক ধকল আর রেগুলার কাজের প্রেশার মিলিয়ে সিদ্ধান্ত নিয়েছিলাম, আপাতত নতুন করে এক্সট্রা কোনো কমিটমেন্টে জড়াবো না। কিন্তু গ্লোবাল টেক ইন্ডাস্ট্রির কিছু প্রস্তাব থাকে, যা ফিরিয়ে দেওয়াটা ক্যারিয়ারের জন্য এক প্রকার বোকামি।

​আবারও সেই European (EU) Global Project থেকে Senior Engineer হিসেবে লিড দেওয়ার প্রস্তাব এসেছে। যিনি আমাকে এই প্রজেক্টের জন্য সিলেক্ট করেছেন এবং আগামী ৪-৬ মাসের একটি চুক্তিতে (contract) কাজ শুরু করার অনুরোধ করেছেন, তিনি কোনো সাধারণ ব্যক্তি নন। তিনি গ্লোবাল টেক জায়ান্ট SAP-এর একজন Global AI Strategist এবং এন্টারপ্রাইজ লিডার। তাঁর মতো একজন ভিশনারি মানুষের কাছ থেকে সরাসরি রিকোয়েস্ট এলে, শত অসুস্থতার মাঝেও "না" বলার উপায় থাকে না।

​দায়িত্বটা এবার বেশ চ্যালেঞ্জিং—সরাসরি Mobile Development (React Native)-এর ওনারশিপ নেওয়া এবং তাদের লেটেস্ট Backend v3.0 আর্কিটেকচারের সাথে AI ও MCP Servers ইন্টিগ্রেট করে পুরো সিস্টেমটাকে গ্লোবাল স্কেলে দাঁড় করানো।
​অনেকেই হয়তো প্রশ্ন করবেন, এই শারীরিক অবস্থায় নন-প্রফিট প্রজেক্টে এক্সট্রা প্রেশার নেওয়ার মানে কী?

​উত্তরটা আমার কাছে খুব পরিষ্কার:
​ইকোসিস্টেম ও মেন্টরশিপ: সরাসরি SAP-এর লিডারশিপের সাথে কাজ করা এবং ইউরোপিয়ান (EU) টেক মার্কেটের কোর নেটওয়ার্কে প্রবেশ করা।

​টেকনিক্যাল বেঞ্চমার্ক: AI এবং MCP-এর মতো ফিউচারিস্টিক টেকনোলজি ব্যবহার করে গ্লোবাল স্ট্যান্ডার্ডের আর্কিটেকচার নিয়ে কাজ করার অভিজ্ঞতা।

​ডেলিভারি ওরিয়েন্টেড মাইন্ডসেট: আমি তাদেরকে পরিষ্কার জানিয়েছি—সাপ্তাহিক ১০ ঘণ্টার হিসাব আমার কাছে মুখ্য নয়; প্রজেক্টের সফল ডেলিভারি নিশ্চিত করাই আমার একমাত্র লক্ষ্য।

​শরীর এখনো পুরোপুরি সায় দিচ্ছে না, কিন্তু মেন্টালি আমি এই চ্যালেঞ্জ নেওয়ার জন্য ১০০% প্রস্তুত। দেখা যাক, স্বেচ্ছায় করা এই "বিষপান" আমার পোর্টফোলিওকে নতুন কোন উচ্চতায় নিয়ে যায়!

​সবাই দোয়ায় রাখবেন। 💻🔥

text:
When App Store and Play Store reviews slow you down, smart Code Push strategies become a critical business continuity strategy.

A critical bug in production, a time-sensitive campaign, or a feature needed for a specific business moment—like a festive sale or seasonal promotion—cannot afford to wait days for store approvals. By the time the update is approved, the window may already be lost, impacting your revenue and user experience.

This is where modern mobile frameworks provide mechanisms to update business logic and UI Over-the-Air (OTA). In Flutter, platforms like Shorebird enable controlled updates. In React Native, CodePush-style approaches allow the fast rollout of fixes and feature toggles.

These tools allow mobile teams to:
1) Deploy urgent bug fixes immediately
2) Launch time-bound campaigns on schedule
3) Maintain a consistent user experience
4) Protect business momentum

It is crucial to understand the boundaries: native code changes in iOS and Android still require store review. Changes involving permissions, SDK integrations, or platform-level functionality must be planned. Therefore, applications must be architected to separate business logic from native dependencies wherever possible.

This is not just an engineering decision; it is a business strategy.

At iLeaf Solutions, we help organizations design mobile applications with OTA-ready architecture, release strategies aligned with business campaigns, and governance models that balance agility with platform compliance.

If your mobile application still depends entirely on store approvals for every change, it may be time to rethink your release strategy.

text:
🚀 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗰𝘁 𝗡𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲: 𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗛𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗮𝘁𝗵𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗼 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵-𝗣𝗲𝗿𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗔𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲

Most people don’t know this 👇

𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗰𝘁 𝗡𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 started as a hackathon prototype inside 𝗙𝗮𝗰𝗲𝗯𝗼𝗼𝗸 (𝗠𝗲𝘁𝗮).

The idea?
👉 Drive native UI using JavaScript.

𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀:

 • JS thread runs your React logic
 • Native thread renders real iOS/Android views
 • JS communicates with native modules
 • Bridge (old architecture) -> JSI (new architecture with direct C++ bindings)

It works on 𝗯𝗼𝘁𝗵 𝗶𝗢𝗦 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗔𝗻𝗱𝗿𝗼𝗶𝗱 by converting React components into real native components (Swift/Objective-C for iOS, Java/Kotlin for Android).

Flow:
JS -> Native Modules -> Native UI -> Render

No WebView. No hybrid tricks. Just native components controlled by JS.

From a hackathon experiment to powering production apps at scale.

Small prototype. Massive ecosystem. 🚀

text:
In this tutorial, we explore Agentic AI with Expo Go — the fastest way to build and test React Native apps without any complex setup! We build a fun 3D ball mobile game, test it live on a real phone by scanning a QR code, and explore production builds. We also cover the limitations of Expo Go and when it's time to switch to a development build. Whether you're a beginner or an experienced developer, this is your complete guide to getting started with React Native development using Expo!

🔑 5 Key Takeaways

📱 Expo Go lets you test React Native apps instantly on a real device just by scanning a QR code — no Xcode, Android Studio, or Apple Developer account needed.
🎮 You can build real interactive apps like a 3D ball game using Expo Go, making it a powerful tool for rapid prototyping and learning React Native.
⚡ Expo Go is a sandbox environment, meaning it comes with pre-installed popular libraries so you can start coding immediately without time-consuming setup.
🚧 Expo Go has real limitations — it doesn't support custom native modules, and features like push notifications, OAuth, and deep linking won't behave like they do in production.
🏗️ Development builds are the next step when your app grows — they give you full control over native code while keeping the great developer experience of Expo.

text:
Day 1 of Building My App in Public

Today, I started documenting my journey of building my mobile app Shop Savvy using React Native.

Today’s focus was on developing the customer-side authentication flow, especially the sign-up experience. This part of any application is extremely important because it’s the first real interaction users have with your product.
While working on it, one of the main principles I focused on was writing reusable code.

Instead of building everything separately for each screen, I tried to design components and patterns that can be reused across multiple parts of the application. This approach helps with:

• Keeping the codebase clean and scalable
• Reducing duplication
• Making future features easier and faster to build

However, building reusable code is not always straightforward.
Some challenges I faced today included:

⚙️ Designing flexible components that work for multiple screens without breaking layouts.
⚙️ Managing different states and validations during the sign-up flow.
⚙️ Structuring the code so that it remains simple today but scalable tomorrow.
It’s a constant balance between moving fast and building things the right way.

This is just the beginning, and over the next 60 days, I’ll be sharing everything from development progress to the challenges and lessons along the way.
If you’re into mobile development, startups, or building products from scratch, follow along

text:
In React Native apps using JWT authentication, most session bugs originate in poorly implemented token refresh flows. 

Handling refresh logic inside screens often causes race conditions, duplicated requests or forced logouts during normal usage.

A stable setup centralizes auth at the network layer using Axios interceptors or a custom fetch wrapper. Access tokens should be short-lived, refresh tokens stored in Keychain or Android Keystore, and concurrent requests queued while a refresh is active.

 Python backends must allow temporary token overlap during rotation to prevent invalidating active sessions.

When refresh is treated as infrastructure, session behavior becomes predictable.

🔐 Token strategy is core architecture.

text:
Have you ever had a user report that your app "won't even open," but your error logs are completely empty? It is a frustrating blind spot. If a crash happens during the native boot sequence before the JavaScript engine has a chance to initialise, standard React Native error handlers are usually left in the dark.

𝗦𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗿𝘆 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗰𝘁 𝗡𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝟴.𝟬 has arrived to close that visibility gap. This major update is a complete overhaul focused on capturing the failures that happen in those critical first milliseconds of your app's lifecycle.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁'𝘀 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱?

Historically, if your app crashed during the native "App Start" phase, the JS-based SDK wouldn't have started yet, leaving you with no stack trace. Version 8.0 re-engineers how the SDK hooks into the native layer (Android and iOS) to ensure that even the earliest crashes are recorded and linked to your project.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁'𝘀 𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗹𝘂𝗱𝗲𝗱?

➡️ 𝗔𝗽𝗽 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁 𝗘𝗿𝗿𝗼𝗿 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴: The SDK now captures native crashes that occur before the React Native layer is loaded. This means you can finally debug why an app is "dying on launch" instead of guessing.
➡️ 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: Beyond crashes, it improves how it tracks cold and warm starts, giving you a clear breakdown of how much time is spent in the native boot process versus the JS bundle loading.
➡️ 𝗦𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗲𝗱 𝗔𝗣𝗜: This release cleans up years of legacy baggage, moving to a more unified configuration that aligns with the latest 𝗦𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗿𝘆 𝗝𝗮𝘃𝗮𝗦𝗰𝗿𝗶𝗽𝘁 𝗦𝗗𝗞 patterns.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗶𝘁 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀?

The biggest hurdle to a 5-star app rating is stability. By providing visibility into the native-to-JS handoff, 𝗦𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗿𝘆 is moving beyond just "catching JS errors" to providing a holistic view of app health. It transforms the "black box" of the startup sequence into actionable data.

text:
React Native's New Architecture isn't the future anymore. It's the present.

If you're still on the old architecture, here's what you're leaving on the table:

43% faster cold starts. 39% better rendering. 20-30% less memory usage.

The New Architecture is built on three things, TurboModules, Fabric, and JSI. Together they completely change how React Native talks to native code. No more bridge bottlenecks, no more async-only communication. Just fast, direct, native performance.

And as of React Native 0.82, the old architecture is permanently disabled. This isn't optional anymore, the ecosystem has moved on.

If you're building production apps in 2026 and haven't migrated yet, now is the time.
Have you made the switch yet? How did it go? 👇

text:
🚀 Just launched: SMOG & Co — a digital platform bringing Spreken Met Ondersteuning van Gebaren (SMOG) to life!

SMOG helps parents, caregivers, teachers, and therapists communicate with people with intellectual disabilities — and even got used to teach a horse the language! 🐴

It’s also useful for anyone learning Dutch: immigrants, young children, or older adults with dementia. And now, thanks to SMOG & Co, it’s all at your fingertips — web, iOS, and Android.

💡 Why this is special
I built this fully solo — from the web app to the native apps, backend, offline sync, sponsorship system, and GDPR compliance. And the best part? I’m only 21 years old, and this is my first app published on the App Store!

✨ Truly Native, Truly Offline

React Native + Expo apps with offline-first gesture videos.
Search, browse, and watch even without internet — perfect for schools, care homes, and on-the-go users.

🖥️ Web App Designed for Focus

React SPA with split-pane layout, fast search, and favorites.
Multilingual (NL / FR / EN) & GDPR-compliant from the ground up.

💡 Unique Sponsorship Model

Organizations can become godfathers/godmothers of a gesture.
Their name/logo is embedded directly in the video for a year.
Supports non-profit sustainability in a meaningful, visible way.

⚡ Built for Reliability

Convex for real-time sync, Hono + oRPC for type-safe APIs, Mux for videos.
Seamless offline sync, partial updates, and GDPR-respecting analytics.

📈 The Results?

More than 250 users in 48 hours (not counting guests).
#6 in the Belgian App Store Education category in week one.
Proof that a niche community needed this tool — and we delivered.

Building two production apps simultaneously, alone, at 21, and seeing real-world impact from day one — this is the moment every developer dreams of.

text:
I've been frustrated by how AI coding agents handle large codebases.

They read everything. Summarize everything. Then still miss the point.

So I built Yantrabodha — an MCP-based retrieval system for coding agents.

Here's how it works differently:

❌ Old way: Dump the entire repo → summarize → fix
✅ Yantrabodha: Understand the issue → find similar past issues → retrieve only relevant context → reason → output

Two MCP servers power it:
• One stores issues as they're solved
• One retrieves semantically similar ones when a new problem comes in

Over time, it builds a knowledge base of your codebase's problem patterns — not just a one-time context window.

Early days. Rough edges. But it's live.

📦 pip install yantrabodha-mcp
🤗 HuggingFace Space: DKethan/yantrabodha-api
💻 GitHub: https://lnkd.in/eteQCuk7

If you're deep in the MCP or coding agent world, I'd love your honest take.

I rewrote a COBOL system into Rust + Flutter over a weekend.

Not as a stunt.
As an experiment.

• ~30 CICS programs
• Rust backend + SQLite
• Flutter frontend (Web + macOS)
• Explicit parity tests

A few hours of focused implementation time.

AI accelerated scaffolding and repetitive translation. 
It did not define domain boundaries, migration scope, or parity contracts.

One detail stood out:
Aligning a legacy error message took longer than scaffolding the endpoint.

Legacy modernization is not constrained by syntax.
It is constrained by clarity and intent.

That’s something I hear more often than you’d think.

In-field workers
Poor offline support
Photos not saved
Data going missing
Operational chaos

They were paying around $20k per year for enterprise software licenses. 

The app still failed the moment connectivity dropped.

With one month left before renewal, they tried building their own mobile app.

They got stuck.

Claude Code pointed them to my article on local-first Flutter architecture.

We talked. I understood the problem in 40 minutes.

My proposal:
• One-month delivery
• $40k
• Problem gone before the renewal

Their response?
→ “We’ll build it ourselves.”

That’s fine. It’s their call.

Here’s the part that experience makes obvious.

When a problem is painful, and the deadline is real, the only thing that matters is whether someone has solved it before.

Everything else is just noise.

text:
Last night, I didn’t fight a production bug.

I fought my own thoughts.

No red screen.
No exception trace.
No failing API.

Just self-doubt.

As Flutter developers, we’re trained to solve problems.

App crashing? Fix it.
State misbehaving? Refactor it.
Performance lag? Optimize it.
Architecture messy? Clean it.

But what happens when the “bug” is in your confidence?

Yesterday I felt nervous.
Questioning my skills.
Replaying mistakes.
Comparing my journey to others.

And then I thought…

Life feels a lot like Flutter.

Sometimes you hit unexpected null values.
Sometimes async responses take longer than expected.
Sometimes the UI doesn’t rebuild the way you imagined.

But that doesn’t mean the app is broken.

It just means it needs time.
It needs adjustment.
It needs a rebuild.

And we know how rebuilds work.

Hot reload.
Small fixes.
Try again.
Improve.
Ship better.

Even the most powerful production apps once started with:

void main() {
 runApp(MyApp());
}

Simple.
Basic.
Imperfect.

Yet full of potential.

So if you’re feeling anxious, stuck, or not “good enough” right now —

Remember:

You’ve fixed tougher bugs.
You’ve learned complex architecture.
You’ve shipped features that once scared you.
You’ve grown quietly, consistently.

You are not a failed deployment.
You are not in beta.
You are not behind.

You are just in the middle of your build process.

And great builds take time.

Keep coding.
Keep rebuilding.
Keep becoming.

We’re not just developing apps.

We’re developing ourselves.

text:
الـ BuildContext: خريطة الطريق جوه Flutter! 🗺️
كتير مننا بيستخدم الـ context في كل مكان، بس هل سألت نفسك هو عبارة عن إيه بالظبط؟ وليه بنحتاجه عشان نوصل لـ Theme أو Bloc؟
💡 الـ Context مش Widget.. الـ Context هو "العنوان"!
خدها قاعدة: الـ Widget Tree هي مجرد "وصف" (Blueprint)، لكن الكائن الحي اللي عايش بجد هو الـ Element Tree. الـ BuildContext هو في الحقيقة الـ Element نفسه! هو اللي بيحدد مكان الـ Widget بتاعتك بالظبط جوه الشجرة ومين جيرانها ومين أهلها.
🌳 ليه الـ Context مهم؟
لأنه حلقة الوصل! لما بتكتب Theme.of(context)، أنت بتقول لفلاتر: "يا context، اطلع في شجرة الـ Elements لفوق لحد ما تلاقي أقرب أب عنده بيانات الـ Theme وهاتها لي".
نصيحة أداء: عشان كدة بنقول بلاش تحط كل الـ Providers في الـ main. حطهم في أقرب مكان محتاجهم فيه عشان الـ Context مياخدش مشوار طويل وهو بيدور لفوق! 🏃‍♂️💨
⚠️ فخ الـ Lifecycle (عايش ولا ميت؟)
الـ Context مش خالد! لو الـ Widget اتعمل لها dispose والـ Context لسه بيحاول يتنفذ (مثلاً بعد Future.delayed), هيضرب في وشك Error.
الحل: استخدم البطل if (!mounted) return; عشان تتأكد إن الـ Element لسه موجود وعايش في الـ Tree قبل ما تستخدم الـ Context.
🚫 أخطاء شائعة:
الـ Context في الـ initState: بلاش تحاول تستخدمه هنا لأن الـ Widget لسه مابقتش جزء كامل من الشجرة (Not fully mounted).
البديل: استخدم didChangeDependencies أو الـ addPostFrameCallback.
الـ Builder Widget: لما تحس إنك محتاج Context "جديد" تحت الـ Widget اللي أنت فيها حالاً عشان توصل لحاجة فوقيها مباشرة، الـ Builder هو صديقك الصدوق.
🎯 الملخص:
الـ BuildContext هو اللي بيخلي الـ Widget Tree "تشم نفسها" وتعرف هي فين ومنين بيودّي على فين في الـ Element Tree. فهمك ليه هو اللي بيفرق بين "كود شغال" و "كود محترف" و هيجنبك اخطاء كتير .
مش تنسوا الصلاه على النبى و استنونا فى مواضيع تانيه ان شاء الله
و مش تنسى تشوف المواضيع التانيه اللى اتكلمنا عليها هتلاقى اللينكات تحت فى الكومنتات و لو عاوز نتكلم فى موضوع اكتب لنا تحت فى الكومنتات

بعد فترة ابتعاد عن الـ Coding، قررت أرجع أبدأ من جديد مش بس بمذاكرة Playlist، لكن عن طريق تنفيذ Tasks عملية أرجع بيها مستوايا خطوة بخطوة .

فكرت كتير العودة دي لازم تبقي ليها مسمي لطيف قولت ليه منعطيهاش اسم Flutter Revive ❤️

والنهارده خلصت أول Task في سلسلة Flutter Revive وكان الهدف منها :
إني أرجع أتعامل مع التفاصيل بإيدي، وأراجع المفاهيم اللي كنت درستها قبل كده خصوصا الـ MVVM اللي أخدته في Nti علشان أرجع أبني مستوايا بشكل منظم وصح ❤️

الـ Task دي كانت بداية حقيقية لرحلة رجوعي، ومبسوطة جدا إني خرجت بحاجة راضية عنها ❤️

text:
This week challenged me more than I expected.
I’m a Frontend Web Developer.
 Web is my space. It’s where I feel confident.
But for the last 4–5 days, I’ve been customizing a Flutter app.
And it wasn’t easy.
SDK problems.
 Dependencies breaking.
 Build failing repeatedly.
 Hours spent fixing one issue after another.
There were moments of frustration.
 Moments of self-doubt.
 Moments where I wondered if I was really “technical enough.”
But I kept going.
I read the errors carefully.
 I searched for solutions.
 I tried. Failed. Tried again.
And today, seeing the app finally run the way I envisioned — that felt different.
Not just because the project works.
 But because I didn’t give up on myself.
Growth is uncomfortable.
 But it’s worth it.
Still learning. Still building.

text:
Every week I get the same message:

"The previous developer disappeared and the app is half-finished."

It's always Flutter. It's always the same story.

The app looks good on the surface. The UI is there. The buttons exist. But nothing actually works. Login doesn't connect to anything. The API calls return errors nobody handled. Push notifications were "set up" but never tested on a real device.

And the client is stuck because they already paid someone thousands of dollars for a project that opens but doesn't function.

Here's what most people don't realize — fixing an app like this is not about rewriting it. It's about reading someone else's code, understanding what they were trying to do, and finishing the job.

That's all I do. I don't build apps from scratch. I fix the ones that got left behind.

I recently took a project that team of developers had already failed on. Fixed it in 2 days.

The client said: "100% happy with Ayman, contracted to bug fix what others failed to do."

If your Flutter app works on the simulator but breaks on real phones — there's always a reason. And it's usually a small one.

• FlutterFlow mobile apps with maps, notifications, and user features
• AI-powered workflows to automate data collection and content creation

It’s an exciting time to build digital products where creativity, marketing, and technology intersect.

Always open to collaborating with innovators, developers, marketers, and founders building interesting things.

text:
Most Flutter Developers Never Open the `android` Folder. That’s the Problem. 🚨

I’ve interviewed and worked with enough Flutter developers to notice a clear pattern.

Strong in `lib/`.
Comfortable with state management.
Clean UI.
Confident with APIs.

But the moment a build fails inside `android/`… everything stops.

In 2026, that gap is expensive.

Flutter powers 1M+ apps on Google Play.
Impeller is now default on Android.
Dart 3.x is mature and production-strong.
WASM is pushing Flutter Web forward.

The ecosystem evolved.

Many developers didn’t.

If you want to move from “feature builder” to “engineering-grade developer”, these 6 Android files are non-negotiable 👇

1️⃣ android/app/build.gradle - Your Release Control Room

This file controls:
• minSdkVersion (breaks packages like local_auth, camera)
• versionCode (Play Store rejection if wrong)
• MultiDex
• Signing configs

One incorrect value = failed CI + delayed release.

2️⃣ android/build.gradle - Project-Level Authority

Here you manage:
• Kotlin version
• Android Gradle Plugin
• Firebase classpath (google-services)

When Flutter upgrades break your build…
This is usually where the fix lives.

3️⃣ AndroidManifest.xml - Permissions & App Identity

Using permission_handler?
You STILL need native declarations.

Camera.
Location.
Notifications.
Internet.

Clean up unused permissions.
It improves Play Store review confidence instantly.

4️⃣ gradle.properties - Performance Booster

Add:

org.gradle.jvmargs=-Xmx4096m
org.gradle.parallel=true
org.gradle.caching=true

On medium-to-large apps, build speed improves noticeably.
Small tweak. Big productivity gain.

5️⃣ local.properties - Where Secrets Belong

API keys do NOT belong in Dart files.

Store them here.
Add to .gitignore.
Access them securely.

Security awareness separates juniors from professionals.

6️⃣ gradle-wrapper.properties - Version Stability

Prevents:
“Gradle version incompatible” errors.

Locking versions saves hours during Flutter or Android Studio upgrades.

2026 Reality:

The market is shifting.

Companies don’t just want Flutter developers.
They want engineers who understand the platform layer.

UI-only knowledge is no longer enough.

Native-aware Flutter developers are:
• Solving build issues independently
• Shipping faster
• Leading architecture decisions
• Earning significantly more

The `lib` folder builds features.
The `android` folder builds engineers.

If you’re serious about growth, spend one weekend exploring those 6 files deeply.

Break something intentionally.
Fix it.
Understand the why.

You’ll eliminate 80% of native build issues permanently.

What’s the most painful Android build error you’ve faced recently?

Let’s discuss 👇

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴: 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗦𝘆𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘅 𝗶𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗟𝗼𝘄𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗺 𝗼𝗳 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴

There is a significant difference between a developer who knows how to use a Widget and an engineer who understands why that Widget exists in the first place.

I see many Flutter developers staying on the surface. They memorize the latest state management syntax or hunt for the "𝘱𝘦𝘳𝘧𝘦𝘤𝘵" UI library. But when the app starts dropping frames on a mid-range Android device, or the state vanishes after the OS kills a background process, the syntax can't save them.

Mobile engineering is not about the framework; it’s about the constraints of the device.

𝘐𝘧 𝘺𝘰𝘶 𝘸𝘢𝘯𝘵 𝘵𝘰 𝘮𝘰𝘷𝘦 𝘧𝘳𝘰𝘮 "𝘸𝘳𝘪𝘵𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘤𝘰𝘥𝘦" 𝘵𝘰 "𝘢𝘳𝘤𝘩𝘪𝘵𝘦𝘤𝘵𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘴𝘺𝘴𝘵𝘦𝘮𝘴," 𝘺𝘰𝘶 𝘩𝘢𝘷𝘦 𝘵𝘰 𝘨𝘰 𝘥𝘦𝘦𝘱𝘦𝘳.

I’ll continue this conversation in my next post.

text:
إذا كنت تبدأ الدراسة بحماس ثم ينتهي اليوم بلا إنجاز حقيقي، فالمشكلة غالباً ليست فيك بل في طريقة إدارة وقتك.

المشكلة في كثير من الأحيان ليست في قلة الجهد، بل في طريقة إدارة الوقت والتركيز.
ومن هنا جاءت فكرة هذا التطبيق الذي عملت عليه مؤخراً: تطبيق Pomodoro لتنظيم المهام والدراسة بطريقة أبسط وأكثر فعالية.

صممت هذا التطبيق ليكون أداة عملية تساعد على تقليل التشتت، وتخفيف الضغط، وتحويل المهام الكبيرة إلى خطوات واضحة يمكن إنجازها بهدوء و استمرارية.

 كيف يعمل التطبيق؟

- تضيف مهامك بسهولة
- تحذف المهام بعد إنجازها
- تبدأ جلسة تركيز لمدة 25 دقيقة
- بعد كل جلسة تحصل على استراحة قصيرة 5 دقائق
- وبعد كل 4 جلسات تحصل على استراحة طويلة 30 دقيقة

هذه الفكرة البسيطة تصنع فرقاً حقيقياً، لأنها لا تدفعك فقط إلى الدراسة بل تساعدك على الاستمرار، وعدم الانقطاع، والشعور بالتقدم خطوة بخطوة.

 تقنياً، كان هذا المشروع فرصة جميلة لتطبيق ما تعلمته عملياً باستخدام:

- Flutter
- GetX لإدارة الحالة (State Management)
- Hive للتخزين المحلي (Local Storage)

كما استخدمت مجموعة من المكتبات المساندة لبناء تجربة خفيفة، منظمة، وسلسة في الاستخدام.

هذا المشروع ليس مشروعاً معقداً جداً، لكنه بالنسبة لي يمثل شيئاً مهماً:
أن المعرفة البرمجية تصبح ذات قيمة حقيقية عندما تتحول إلى منتج عملي قابل للاستخدام.

أرفقت مع هذا المنشور فيديو قصير.

أحب أن أسمع رأيكم:
هل ترون أن الالتزام بطريقة واضحة مثل البومودورو يمكن أن يغيّر فعلاً أسلوب الدراسة والعمل؟
و عطونا أيضا رأيكم بالتطبيق؟

text:
43 CI runs on the Ironclad branch and the root cause of the continuous backend crashes is finally isolated. We spent hours debugging what appeared to be a complex Rust FFI linker issue where Dart failed to lookup the set_model symbol. We assumed the NDK was aggressively stripping exports during the release build. The actual culprit was the Android 15 Scoped Storage model. As the app permission state shows, the OS granted zero file access. When the Rust engine attempted to map the local GGUF file into memory on the Snapdragon 7s Gen 3, it hit a silent I/O panic, collapsing the FFI bridge entirely and masking the file denial as a missing symbol. The solution is shifting away from the C++ toolchain debug and wiring a strict MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE request into the Flutter frontend before the AI core boots. Building fully autonomous, offline AI on mobile forces you to fight the OS security architecture just as hard as the inference engine.

text:
How I Use AI in Flutter Development — and Why It Matters 💪

AI is not about replacing developers.
It’s about building better software, faster, and with fewer mistakes.

Here’s how I use AI in real Flutter projects 👇

How I use AI (practical, not hype)

• Speed up development 
 I use AI to scaffold widgets, screens, and repetitive code so I can focus on core logic and product behavior.

• Improve architecture decisions 
 I use AI to compare state management approaches, review folder structures, and think through scalability before problems appear.

• Reduce bugs early 
 AI helps me walk through async flows, edge cases, and error states that are easy to miss during fast development.

• Strengthen testing and releases 
 I use AI to draft unit tests, edge cases, and CI pipelines, reducing release risks and last-minute surprises.

Where human judgment stays critical

• Choosing the right architecture 
• Performance optimization and frame budget 
• Security, data handling, and UX decisions 
• Final code review and production responsibility 

Advantages of using AI this way

• Faster feature delivery without sacrificing quality 
• Cleaner, more maintainable codebases 
• Fewer production issues and regressions 
• More time spent on product thinking, not boilerplate 

AI is a tool.
Ownership, judgment, and delivery are still the differentiators.

If you’re looking for a Flutter developer who uses AI responsibly and thinks beyond just writing code, let’s connect 👇

text:
أول مشكلة ستواجهك في بداية تعلم Flutter… ليست الـ Widgets.

كثير من المبتدئين يظنون أن التحدي الحقيقي هو فهم الواجهة أو كتابة الكود.
لكن قبل أن يعمل التطبيق أصلًا، هناك منظومة كاملة خلف الكواليس يجب أن تكون مضبوطة.

خليني أشرحها ببساطة 

 ما هو Android SDK؟
Android SDK هو مجموعة الأدوات الرسمية من Google التي تتيح لك بناء وتشغيل تطبيقات أندرويد.

يحتوي على:

Build Tools (أدوات تجميع التطبيق)
Platform Tools (مثل ADB للتواصل مع الأجهزة)
Android Emulator
مكتبات النظام الخاصة بإصدارات أندرويد المختلفة
عند تشغيل مشروع Flutter على أندرويد، يتم استخدام Android SDK لتحويل التطبيق إلى ملف APK أو AAB قابل للتشغيل.
بدونه، لا يمكن تشغيل التطبيق على جهاز أندرويد.

 ما هو Flutter SDK؟

Flutter SDK هو الحزمة الأساسية التي تحتوي على:
إطار عمل Flutter
Dart SDK
أدوات سطر الأوامر (CLI)
محرك Flutter (Rendering Engine)
أوامر مثل flutter doctor و flutter build
هو المسؤول عن إدارة المشروع، وتجهيز ملفات البناء، وربطها بنظام أندرويد أو iOS.
عند كتابة كود Dart، يقوم Flutter بتحويله إلى تطبيق جاهز للبناء عبر أدوات أندرويد أو iOS.

 أكثر مشكلة تواجه المبتدئين: Gradle

ما هو Gradle؟

Gradle هو نظام البناء (Build System) المستخدم في مشاريع أندرويد.
وظيفته:
تجميع الكود
تحميل المكتبات الخارجية (Dependencies)
إدارة الإصدارات
إنشاء ملف APK أو AAB
تنسيق عملية البناء بالكامل

وعند ظهور الخطأ الشهير:
Gradle build failed

فهذا لا يعني بالضرورة أن الكود خاطئ.
في أغلب الأحيان تكون الأسباب:
تعارض في الإصدارات (Version mismatch)
إصدار JDK غير متوافق
عدم توافق بين Gradle و Android Gradle Plugin
انقطاع الإنترنت أثناء تحميل dependencies
تلف في ملفات الكاش (Gradle cache)
تحديث Flutter دون تحديث الأدوات المرتبطة به
فهم Gradle يختصر عليك ساعات من الإحباط.

 أداة لا يجب تجاهلها: flutter doctor
أمر: flutter doctor
يقوم بفحص:
تثبيت Flutter SDK
تثبيت Android SDK
توافق الإصدارات
وجود Git
إعدادات Android Studio أو VS Code
التراخيص المطلوبة (Android Licenses)
وهو أول خطوة تشخيص يجب القيام بها قبل البحث العشوائي في Google.
الخلاصة
تعلم Flutter لا يبدأ بالـ Widgets…
بل يبدأ بفهم البيئة التي يعمل عليها التطبيق.
عندما تفهم كيف يتم بناء التطبيق خلف الكواليس،
 تتحول الأخطاء من مصدر إحباط… إلى مصدر فهم أعمق.

text:
أخطاء في Flutter تقتل أداء التطبيق (Production Killers) #4
Flutter Mistakes That Kill App Performance

Flutter Mistake #4
Heavy Logic Inside the UI Layer

واحدة من أخطر الأخطاء في تطبيقات Flutter…
هي تحميل الـ UI بطبقة منطق ثقيلة (Business Logic).
قد يعمل التطبيق في البداية.
لكن مع الوقت؟
يتحول إلى كابوس صيانة وأداء.

ماذا يعني “Heavy Logic in UI”؟

تنفيذ عمليات معالجة بيانات داخل build()
فلترة وتحويل Lists مباشرة في الواجهة
استدعاء APIs من داخل Widgets
حسابات معقدة داخل setState()
ربط مباشر بين UI و Data Sources

هذا خرق صريح لمبدأ:
Separation of Concerns كل جزء يتعامل مع مسؤولية واحدة فقط.

⚠️ لماذا هذا خطير؟
 إعادة تنفيذ المنطق مع كل rebuild
 صعوبة الاختبار (Testing becomes painful)
 Coupling عالي بين الواجهة والبيانات
 كود غير قابل لإعادة الاستخدام
 مشاكل Performance غير واضحة المصدر

الحل الاحترافي

نقل المنطق إلى ViewModel / Cubit / BLoC
استخدام Repository Layer
جعل build() مجرد Renderer 
للحالة الحالية
الالتزام بـ Clean Architecture

الـ UI يجب أن يكون:
Stateless in behavior, even if Stateful in structure

المبتدئ يسأل:
"كيف أجعلها تعمل؟"

المحترف يسأل:
"كيف أجعلها قابلة للصيانة والتوسع بدون كسر الأداء؟"

💡 Flutter سريع…
لكن وضع المنطق في المكان الخاطئ يجعله بطيئًا بدون أن تشعر.

Essential Flutter Commands Every Developer Should Know
Flutter development is not limited to building UI — having a strong command over the Flutter CLI (Command Line Interface) is equally important for efficient and smooth development.
In this post, I’ve shared some of the most important Flutter commands that are commonly used in daily development:
🔹 flutter create – To create a new Flutter project
🔹 flutter run – To run the app on an emulator or physical device
🔹 flutter build – To generate APK, App Bundle, or iOS builds
🔹 flutter clean – To clear build cache and fix common build issues
🔹 flutter pub get – To install or update project dependencies
🔹 flutter doctor – To check and verify your development environment setup
Understanding and regularly using these commands can significantly improve your development speed and workflow efficiency.
Whether you're a beginner or an experienced developer, mastering Flutter CLI commands will help you become more productive and professional.
💡 Great developers don’t just write code — they optimize their workflow.

text:
🚀 Mastering Flutter: The Ultimate StatefulWidget Lifecycle Guide

في Flutter، بناء الواجهات ممتع وسهل.. لكن التحدي الحقيقي هو إزاي تدير الـ State وتتعامل مع موارد الجهاز بذكاء.

الـ StatefulWidget بيتكون من جزئين:
1️⃣ الـ Widget: وده وصف ثابت (Immutable) مابيتغيرش.
2️⃣ الـ State: وده الكائن اللي بيعيش فترة طويلة وهو اللي بيتغير (Mutable) وشايل البيانات.

فهمك لدورة حياة الـ State دي هو اللي هيخليك تبني تطبيق ثابت وسريع، والأهم إنه بيحترم موارد الموبايل (Memory & CPU).

🔄 The Lifecycle Flow:
createState ➔ initState ➔ didChangeDependencies ➔ build ➔ setState ➔ didUpdateWidget ➔ deactivate ➔ dispose

1️⃣ createState()
دي أول خطوة بتربط الـ Widget (الوصف الثابت) بالـ State (البيانات المتغيرة). الـ Framework بيناديها أول ما يحط الـ Widget في الشجرة لإنشاء كائن الـ State. يفضل تظل بسيطة ومهمتها بس ترجع نسخة (Instance) من الـ State.

2️⃣ initState() 🟢
مرحلة التأسيس: بتتنادي مرة واحدة بس فور ولادة الـ State وقبل رسم الشاشة. ده المكان المخصص لتعريف الـ Controllers (Animation أو Scroll) وبدء طلب بيانات الـ API.
💡 ملحوظة: لازم تنادي super.initState() في أول سطر، والـ context هنا لسه مش "كامل" لعمل Navigation مباشرة.

3️⃣ didChangeDependencies()
بتتنادي فوراً بعد الـ initState، وكمان كل ما الـ InheritedWidgets (زي الـ Theme أو Provider) تتغير. ده المكان الأنسب لو محتاج تستخدم MediaQuery أو Theme.of(context) في بداية تشغيل الصفحة.

4️⃣ build() 🎨
"الرسام" المسؤول عن تحويل كودك لـ Pixels. الدالة دي بتتنادي كتير جداً مع كل تحديث (Rebuild)، فلازم تكون خفيفة ومهمتها "وصف" شكل الواجهة بس. تجنب وضع أي Logic تقيل أو API Calls جواها عشان التطبيق ميهنجش.

5️⃣ didUpdateWidget() 🔄
بتحصل لما الـ Parent Widget يغير البيانات المبعوثة للـ Widget بتاعك. الـ State القديمة بتفضل صاحية، لكن الـ Widget (الوصف) بيتم استبداله بنسخة جديدة ببيانات أحدث، وهنا بتقارن بينهم وتحدث الـ Logic لو محتاج.

6️⃣ setState() ⏰
أداة التحديث اليدوي للواجهة (Rebuild): أول ما تناديها، الـ Framework بيفهم إن البيانات اتغيرت في الـ State فبيعلّم على الـ Widget إنه محتاج إعادة رسم (Dirty). ده بيجبر الـ Engine إنه يجدول فريم جديد بسرعة جداً لتشغيل دالة الـ build بالبيانات الجديدة.

7️⃣ deactivate() & dispose() 🛑
مرحلة التنظيف والوداع:

● deactivate: مرحلة انتقالية؛ الـ Widget بيتشال من الشاشة مؤقتاً (زي التنقل بين الصفحات)، الـ State لسه موجودة في الذاكرة وممكن الـ Widget يرجع لمكانه تاني.

● dispose: النهاية الحقيقية؛ الـ State بتتحذف تماماً من الذاكرة. لازم تنظف كودك هنا: إغلاق الـ Controllers والـ Streams وإلغاء الـ Timers. ده اللي بيمنع الـ Memory Leaks وبيحافظ على سرعة الجهاز.

🎯 الخلاصة: دورة حياة الـ Widget هي خريطة طريق بتعرفنا إمتى نبدأ، إمتى نحدث، وإمتى ننظف الكود بتاعنا.

💡 Quote: "Code is not just written; it is managed. Understand the cycle,
 master the flow."

text:
🚀𝑭𝒍𝒖𝒕𝒕𝒆𝒓 𝑰𝒏𝒕𝒆𝒓𝒗𝒊𝒆𝒘 𝑺𝒆𝒓𝒊𝒆𝒔 — 𝑫𝒂𝒚 9

𝗥𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗽𝗼𝗱 𝘃𝘀 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗿 — 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗿𝗻 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 

Many developers start with Provider…
But lately, more teams are moving toward Riverpod.
Why?
Let’s understand clearly 👇

📌 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗿?

Provider is a simple and widely used state management solution built on top of InheritedWidget.
 ✔ Easy to learn
 ✔ Minimal setup
 ✔ Great for small to medium apps
 ✔ Uses BuildContext
It’s clean and works well — but has limitations in large-scale apps.

📌 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗥𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗽𝗼𝗱?

Riverpod is a modern re-implementation of Provider created by the same author — designed to solve Provider’s limitations.
Key difference:
❌ No dependency on BuildContext
 ✔ Safer and more testable
 ✔ Better compile-time safety
 ✔ Works outside widget tree
 ✔ More scalable

📌 𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝘁𝗼 𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗿?

 ✔ Learning Flutter
 ✔ Small to medium projects
 ✔ Simple app architecture
 ✔ Faster implementation needed

📌 𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝘁𝗼 𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝗥𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗽𝗼𝗱?

 ✔ Large production apps
 ✔ Clean architecture setup
 ✔ Better testability required
 ✔ Long-term scalability

🎯 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄 𝗧𝗶𝗽

If asked:
“Why Riverpod over Provider?”

You can say:

“𝑹𝒊𝒗𝒆𝒓𝒑𝒐𝒅 𝒓𝒆𝒎𝒐𝒗𝒆𝒔 𝑩𝒖𝒊𝒍𝒅𝑪𝒐𝒏𝒕𝒆𝒙𝒕 𝒅𝒆𝒑𝒆𝒏𝒅𝒆𝒏𝒄𝒚, 𝒊𝒎𝒑𝒓𝒐𝒗𝒆𝒔 𝒔𝒂𝒇𝒆𝒕𝒚, 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝒎𝒂𝒌𝒆𝒔 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝒂𝒑𝒑 𝒎𝒐𝒓𝒆 𝒔𝒄𝒂𝒍𝒂𝒃𝒍𝒆 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝒕𝒆𝒔𝒕𝒂𝒃𝒍𝒆.”

That shows deep understanding — not just tool usage.

💡 𝗣𝗿𝗼 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁

In modern Flutter development:
• Provider is still relevant
 • Riverpod is more future-proof
The choice depends on project complexity and long-term vision.

👉𝗧𝗼𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗿𝗼𝘄: 𝘀𝗲𝘁𝗦𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝘃𝘀 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗿𝗻 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 — 𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗦𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗰𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗪𝗶𝗻𝘀

text:
🚀 Android Developers: Stop Ignoring the ios/ Folder in Flutter

If you’re an Android dev using Flutter and still treating the ios/ folder like a black box…
you’re leaving performance, stability, and career growth on the table.

Flutter isn’t “just Android with Dart” anymore.
In 2026, cross-platform means mastering iOS too.

📊 Why this matters right now
👉 42% market share in cross-platform
👉 45% of new apps ship with Flutter
👉 Startups launch 40% faster at ~40% lower cost
👉 One codebase, millions of users, no compromise

Yet most Android devs still say:

“iOS build failed… not sure why 🤷‍♂️”

Let’s fix that.

🧠 Real Flutter devs in 2026 are also doing this

🔥 AI inside Flutter apps
• On-device ML
• Real-time personalization
• Smart recommendations

🖥️ Web + Desktop
• Stable Windows / macOS / Linux
• Same rendering engine everywhere

⚡ Performance
• Faster startup
• Fewer runtime bugs
• Smoother animations

Flutter isn’t a shortcut anymore.
It’s a platform.

💡 iOS-specific habits that separate seniors from juniors

✅ Always open Runner.xcworkspace
❌ Never open Runner.xcodeproj

✅ Test iOS swipe-back gesture on every screen
✅ Create Dev / Staging / Prod schemes
✅ Add all iOS permission strings before runtim

🎯 If you’re an Android dev using Flutter, do this today
1️⃣ Open the ios/ folder (yes, actually open it)
2️⃣ Run the app on an iOS Simulator
3️⃣ Check Info.plist for permissions
4️⃣ Fix signing once properly
5️⃣ Stop calling iOS “someone else’s problem”

They understand it.

Ignoring it is.

hit 👍, save it, or share it with that Android dev friend who still says:

text:
Giving Back: Why I Build Flutter Packages (And You Should Too) 🚀

Three years ago, I hit a wall.

I was building a fintech Flutter app and needed a slidable widget—you know, those swipe-to-reveal actions you see in email apps. I tried the existing packages. They worked, but they were rigid. Limited customization. Couldn't use custom widgets as actions. No control over backgrounds or how items interacted with each other.

So I built my own solution. Got it working. Moved on with my project.

But then something clicked.

If I struggled with this, how many other developers were fighting the same battle right now? How much collective time was being wasted reinventing the same wheel?

That's when I decided: every problem I solve should help someone else.

The result?

I started building packages. Not just for me—for the community that had given me so much through their own open-source contributions.

I also started writing blog posts, sharing how I solved problems across web, Cordova, and Flutter. Because sometimes the real value isn't the code—it's understanding why something works

Today, I'm excited to share slideable – a lightweight, highly flexible slidable widget that's been three years in the making.

What makes it different:
✅ Total Flexibility - Use any widget as an action item, not just icons
✅ Full Customization - Control backgrounds (including transparency), border radii, colors
✅ Smart Interactions - Auto-closes open items when new ones are engaged—keeping UIs clean
✅ Smooth Animations - Customizable curves and speeds for perfect UX
✅ Production-Ready - Comprehensive widget tests ensure stability

The impact so far:
📊 30 likes on pub.dev
⭐ 140 pub points (quality score)
📦 866 downloads and counting

These aren't just numbers—they're 866 developers who didn't have to waste hours solving the same problem I once faced.

Why this matters:
The Flutter community gave me the tools to build my career. Free packages. Open-source libraries. Blog posts from strangers who took time to document their solutions.

Now it's my turn to give back
Every package I publish, every blog post I write, is a small way of saying "thank you" to everyone who helped me when I was stuck at 2 AM debugging something I didn't understand.

If you've ever solved a tricky problem in Flutter (or any tech), consider:
👉🏽 Publishing a package
👉🏽 Writing a blog post
👉🏽 Sharing a GitHub repo
👉🏽 Answering questions on Stack Overflow (well, AI has taken over now sadly🥲)

Your 30 minutes of documentation could save someone else 3 hours of frustration.

That's how we build better together.🤝

🔗 Check out slideable:
📦 Pub.dev: https://lnkd.in/ee4uW_uy
💻 GitHub: https://lnkd.in/emMpEPvp

What problem have you solved that you could share with the community? Let's keep this cycle of giving going

text:
𝐖𝐡𝐲 𝐈’𝐦 𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐩𝐩𝐢𝐧𝐠 "𝐛𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐬" 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐨 "𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐭."

3 years of Flutter development taught me how to build beautiful interfaces. My MSc in AI taught me how to build complex brains. 
In 2026, these two worlds are finally one.

We are moving away from the era of static buttons. We are entering the era of 𝐆𝐞𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐔𝐈.

Imagine an app that doesn't just show a menu, but generates the exact dashboard a user needs in real-time based on their live data. No more "one-size-fits-all" UX.

With the new 𝐅𝐥𝐮𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫 𝐆𝐞𝐧𝐔𝐈 𝐒𝐃𝐊 and 𝐎𝐧-𝐃𝐞𝐯𝐢𝐜𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐝𝐞𝐥𝐬 𝐥𝐢𝐤𝐞 𝐆𝐞𝐦𝐦𝐚 3, we can finally build apps that:
1. 𝐑𝐮𝐧 𝐋𝐨𝐜𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲: Privacy-first, zero-latency AI.
2. 𝐒𝐞𝐥𝐟-𝐀𝐝𝐚𝐩𝐭: The UI rewrites itself via JSON-to-Widget factories.
3. 𝐀𝐜𝐭 𝐚𝐬 𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬: Not just answering questions, but executing tasks.

If you’re still building apps the 2023 way, you’re building for the past. I’m looking to connect with founders and engineers building the 𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐜 𝐅𝐮𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞. 🏗️🤖

text:
أخطاء في Flutter تقتل أداء التطبيق (Production Killers) #2
Flutter Mistakes That Kill App Performance


Flutter Mistake #2

 Not Using const Constructors

 تجاهل استخدام Const Constructors

واحدة من أكثر الأخطاء الصغيرة اللي تشوفها كثير في مشاريع Flutter هي عدم استخدام const constructors في Widgets الثابتة.

قد تبدو تفصيلة بسيطة، لكنها تؤثر بشكل كبير على الأداء:

 ماذا يحدث عند عدم استخدام const؟
Flutter يعيد إنشاء الـ Widget كل مرة يُعاد فيها بناء الـ Widget tree
يؤدي إلى إعادة build غير ضرورية

استهلاك أعلى للذاكرة وCPU، خصوصًا في واجهات كثيفة العناصر

🔹 الحل الاحترافي:

استخدم const لكل Widget لا تتغير بياناته

اجعل الـ Widgets الثابتة compile-time constants

حافظ على الـ Widget tree أخف وأسرع

النتيجة: تحسين الأداء، تقليل الـ rebuilds، وتجربة مستخدم أكثر سلاسة على كل الأجهزة.

حتى التفاصيل الصغيرة في Flutter تؤثر على الأداء.

const Constructors = performance booster مجاني لكل مشروع.

text:
Stop learning Flutter.

Learn Dart first.

90% of beginners skip this step - and it’s why they stay stuck.

🎯 The Harsh Truth:
• Your Flutter skills will NEVER exceed your Dart skills.
• Flutter is just a framework.
• Dart is the engine.
• If the engine is weak, the app is weak.

✔️ What Is Dart?
Dart is the programming language Flutter uses.
Like JavaScript for React.
Like Swift for iOS.
Every widget you write is Dart code.

Container(
 padding: EdgeInsets.all(16),
 child: Text('Hello'),
)

This is not “Flutter magic.”
It’s classes, constructors, named parameters - pure Dart.

✔️ Why Dart Really Matters?
1️⃣ Widgets are Dart objects.
If you don’t understand classes, you don’t understand widgets.

2️⃣ State management is Dart concepts.
• OOP.
• Futures & async/await.
• Streams.
• Generics.
• Extension methods.

3️⃣ Problem-solving depends on Dart.
• Weak with Lists? You’ll struggle with ListView.
• Don’t understand async? API calls will confuse you.
• Weak OOP? Clean Architecture won’t make sense.


🚀 What You Must Learn (8-Week Plan):

→ Weeks 1-2 (Fundamentals):
• Var, final, const.
• Null safety (?, !, ??).
• Data types.
• If/else, loops, switch.
• Functions.
• List, map, set.

→ Weeks 3-4 (OOP):
• Classes & objects.
• Named & factory constructors.
• Inheritance & mixins.
• Encapsulation.
• Abstract classes & interfaces.

→ Weeks 5-6 (Advanced):
• Future & async/await.
• Streams.
• Try/catch & throw.
• Generics.
• Extension methods.

→ Weeks 7-8 (Build Project):
📁 Build a CLI Todo App.
- No UI. No Flutter.
- Just logic, classes and clean structure.


🔴 Red Flags You Skipped Dart:
❌ Copy-paste Flutter code.
❌ Can’t explain final vs const.
❌ Struggle with list manipulation.
❌ Confused by null safety.
❌ Don’t understand async.

If this sounds familiar - go back to Dart.

✔️ Why This Approach Works?
• Strong foundation.
• Better problem-solving.
• Cleaner architecture.
• Easier state management.
• Production-ready mindset.

The developers who struggle:
Jumped into Flutter on Day 1.

The developers who excel:
Spent 2 months mastering Dart first.

✅ My recommendation:
• Spend 40–50% of your early learning time on Dart alone. It pays off 10x when building real apps.
• Build strong Dart skills → Flutter becomes intuitive.

→ Are you learning Dart right now?
→ What’s your biggest challenge?

text:
🧨 90% من تطبيقات Flutter اللي فيها Login… مخزنة الـ Token غلط.

في Flutter ممكن تعمل Auth في 5 دقايق…
وتضيع أمان التطبيق في ثانية واحدة. 😬

خلينا نقول الحقيقة بقى 👇
أغلبنا أول ما الـ Login يشتغل يقول:
"خلاص بقى… نخزن التوكن في SharedPreferences وريح دماغك" 😌
لكن اللي محدش بيقولهولك:
❌ SharedPreferences مش مشفرة

❌ أي حد معاه Root / Jailbreak يقدر يقراها
❌ يعني التوكن = مفتاح بيتك
وإنت سيبته تحت السجادة 🚪🗝️
💡 الحل الصح؟
تخزن الـ Token في مكان معمول أصلاً للأسرار…

📌 ده البوست التاني من سلسلة:
باكيدج تِسهّل شغلك في Flutter
وهنتكلم انهارده عن 

📦 flutter_secure_storage
بتعمل إيه الباكيدج دي؟
🔐 بتخزن البيانات مشفّرة
📱 بتستخدم:
Keychain في iOS
Keystore في Android
يعني حتى لو حد فتح الملفات…
هيشوف شِفرات مش مفهومة 😎
بدل ما تعمل كده:

SharedPreferences.setString("token", token);

تعمل كده:

final storage = FlutterSecureStorage();
await storage.write(key: "token", value: token);

وبنفس البساطة:

await storage.read(key: "token");
await storage.delete(key: "token");

🤔 ليه الموضوع ده مهم؟

text:
🚀 Shorebird for Flutter: Mastering Over-The-Air (OTA) Updates
In modern Flutter development, speed matters. Waiting for app store approvals just to fix a small bug or tweak UI logic can slow down product delivery significantly.
That’s where Shorebird changes the game.

With Shorebird, Flutter apps can:
 ✅ Push Dart code updates instantly
 ✅ Fix production bugs without store resubmission
 ✅ Improve UI layouts and business logic dynamically
 ✅ Deliver seamless background updates
 ✅ Roll back instantly if needed
However, it’s important to understand the boundaries:
 
🔒 Native code, SDK upgrades, permissions, and static assets still require a full store release.
From a production perspective, Shorebird enables:
 ⚡ Faster iteration cycles
 ⚡ Reduced downtime
 ⚡ Better user experience
 ⚡ Agile-friendly deployments
Understanding where OTA updates fit into your Flutter architecture is crucial for building scalable, production-ready applications.

text:
أخطاء في Flutter تقتل أداء التطبيق (Production Killers) #3
Flutter Mistakes That Kill App Performance

Flutter Mistake #3
Overusing setState() in the Wrong Places

كثير من التطبيقات تبدأ بسيطة…
ثم تتحول إلى فوضى rebuilds بسبب الاستخدام المفرط لـ setState().
المشكلة ليست في setState() نفسه.
المشكلة في أين ولماذا تستخدمه.

في Flutter، الأداء لا يعتمد فقط على المحرك…
بل على مدى تحكمك في نطاق إعادة البناء.

 ماذا يحدث عند الإفراط في استخدام setState()؟
عند استدعاء setState():

يتم إعادة بناء الـ Widget بالكامل
يعاد تنفيذ build() بكل عناصره
أي Child Widgets سيتم تقييمها من جديد
إذا كان لديك UI معقد أو List طويلة…
فأنت فعليًا تطلب من Flutter إعادة رسم جزء كبير من الشجرة بدون داعي.

أنماط خاطئة شائعة
استخدام setState() في Widgets كبيرة جدًا
إدارة Business Logic داخل UI Layer
تحديث State عام بدلًا من State جزئي
استخدامه بدل حلول State Management منظمة

البدائل الاحترافية
تقسيم الـ UI إلى Widgets أصغر
استخدام ValueNotifier / ChangeNotifier
اعتماد BLoC أو Cubit
استخدام Selector أو Consumer لتحديث أجزاء محددة فقط

الفكرة ليست منع setState()…
بل منع إعادة البناء غير الضرورية.

في Flutter، الأداء لا يعتمد فقط على المحرك…
بل على مدى تحكمك في نطاق إعادة البناء.

text:
🔥 “5 Real Lessons I Learned While Building a Production Flutter App”
🚀 I’ve been building a production-level Flutter app recently, and here are 5 lessons that changed the way I develop:
1️⃣ State management matters more than UI.
 Using BLoC made my app scalable and predictable. Clean architecture > quick hacks.
2️⃣ Token expiration will break your app at the worst time.
 Always handle 401 errors globally and implement auto logout or refresh token logic.
3️⃣ Pagination is not optional in real apps.
 Fetching large API data without pagination = performance issues.
4️⃣ UI bugs are often logic bugs.
 Most “Flutter issues” I faced were actually poor state handling.
5️⃣ Deploy early, fix fast.
 AWS deployment taught me more than local development ever did.
Building real apps teaches you what tutorials never will.
💡 If you're learning Flutter — build something real. Even if it's messy.

text:
When Designers Try to K!ll You (And You Build a Package Instead) 💀➡️📦

Three years ago, a colleague handed me a design.

"Just make this scroll continuously," they said, pointing at a row of widgets that needed to animate infinitely across the screen.

Simple enough, right? Wrong.

The Problem:
Every Flutter marquee package I found only handled text. But this design had images, logos, custom widgets, containers—everything except plain text.

I tried hacking together solutions. Failed. Tried again. Failed harder.

You know that moment when you realize product designers might secretly enjoy watching developers suffer? Yeah, that was me. 😅

I did what still serves me well today: I asked someone more experienced for guidance.

Called up a senior colleague. Within 15 minutes, he walked me through the dynamics—how animation controllers work, how to handle gesture detection, and how to make widgets loop seamlessly.

Suddenly, it clicked. In HTML/CSS, this would've been trivial. But mobile development has different rules, different constraints, and different solutions.

I got it working. Shipped the feature. Moved on.

That's when the question came: How many hours are being collectively wasted across the Flutter community solving this same issue?

Product designers will keep designing ambitious UIs (and that's a good thing!). Developers will keep needing creative solutions. So why not save everyone the headache?

The result: custom_widget_marquee

A Flutter package that lets you turn any widget into a smooth, customizable marquee.

What makes it special:

✅ Universal Support – Works with any widget: Images, Containers, Rows, Columns, custom widgets
✅ Full Control - Adjust animation duration, pause times, and curves precisely
✅ Interactive - Supports manual swiping gestures for natural feel
✅ Flexible Directions - Single-direction or bi-directional (back-and-forth) scrolling
✅ Production-Ready - Built from real-world needs, tested in real apps

The impact (3 years later):
⭐ 3 likes on pub.dev
📊 130 pub points
📦 86 downloads

86 developers who saved hours by not having to reinvent this wheel. 86 teams who shipped features faster because the solution already existed

The lesson?
When you hit a problem that makes you want to quit, you're probably one conversation away from a breakthrough

When you solve that problem, you're one package away from helping dozens of other developers

Ask for help. Learn deeply. Then share what you learned

That senior colleague who helped me? He paid forward knowledge someone once gave him. Now I'm doing the same. That's how we grow—together.

Next time a designer hands you an "impossible" UI, remember: It's not impossible. It's just undocumented. Yet. 😉

text:
أخطاء في Flutter تقتل أداء التطبيق (Production Killers) #5
Flutter Mistakes That Kill App Performance

Flutter Mistake #5
Using ListView Without Pagination or Caching

من الأخطاء الشائعة في تطبيقات Flutter — خصوصًا عند التعامل مع APIs —
هو تحميل قائمة كبيرة من البيانات دفعة واحدة داخل ListView.
في البداية قد يبدو الأمر بسيطًا…
لكن مع زيادة البيانات يبدأ التطبيق في المعاناة.

مثلا إذا كان لديك 2000 منتج…
فأنت تطلب من التطبيق تحميلهم جميعًا فورًا.


ماذا يحدث فعليًا؟
عند جلب مئات أو آلاف العناصر مرة واحدة:

يتم تحميل كمية كبيرة من البيانات في الذاكرة
زيادة زمن استجابة الـ API
ارتفاع استهلاك الـ RAM
انخفاض أداء التمرير (Scroll Jank)
تجربة مستخدم سيئة في الأجهزة الضعيفة

*****الحل الاحترافي

استخدم Pagination + Lazy Loading
بدل تحميل كل البيانات مرة واحدة، قم بتحميلها على شكل صفحات:Pagination
مع تحميل البيانات عند وصول المستخدم لنهاية القائمة. Lazy Loading

+تحسين إضافي: Caching
يمكن تحسين الأداء أكثر عبر:

تخزين البيانات محليًا
تقليل طلبات الشبكة
النتيجة تسريع تحميل الشاشة

المبتدئ يفكر:"اجلب كل البيانات واعرضها."

المحترف يفكر:"حمّل فقط ما يحتاجه المستخدم الآن."

💡 التطبيقات الكبيرة لا تُبنى على تحميل كل شيء…
بل على تحميل ذكي للبيانات حسب الحاجة.

text:
Most people think building a mobile app is about writing code.
In reality, it’s about guiding decisions across UX, architecture, and real user workflows.
While working on a complex mobile application built with Flutter, the goal wasn’t just delivering features. It was making the experience intuitive, fast, and reliable for real-world usage.
Here’s the approach I followed:
1️⃣ Start with workflows, not screens
 Before designing UI components, I mapped how users actually moved through tasks. Identifying friction points, repetitive actions, and behavioral patterns revealed where the experience could be improved.
2️⃣ UX driven by real usage
 Instead of focusing only on visuals, every interaction was shaped around clarity and speed so users could digest information faster and act with confidence.
Information hierarchy, navigation flow, and response time became core priorities to reduce cognitive load and make interactions feel natural.
3️⃣ Architecture that supports UX
Using modular structures and clean architecture allowed the product to evolve without breaking the user experience.
Strong UX depends on a flexible and maintainable technical foundation.
4️⃣ Observability as feedback
 Integrating analytics, performance monitoring, and error tracking created a continuous feedback loop.
Understanding how users behave in production helps refine decisions beyond initial design assumptions.
5️⃣ Performance as part of the experience
Optimizing rendering, state management, and data flow significantly improved responsiveness.
In high-usage environments, performance directly shapes user trust and efficiency.
Building with Flutter at scale isn’t just about widgets or frameworks.
It’s about aligning product thinking, UX design, and architecture to create systems that feel natural to use.
Great mobile engineering isn’t just shipping features.
It’s reducing friction and making complex workflows feel simple.

text:
If swapping a dependency scares you,
your architecture is already broken ☠️ 

When building a project, it's easy to assume a dependency will be there forever.
But in real-world teams — especially with stakeholders involved — an SDK that was once essential can be replaced in a few months. Cheaper. Faster. Strategic shift.

When that moment comes, your architecture is exposed.

Will the transition be smooth?
Or will you touch half the codebase and ship hotfixes to production?

The difference is structural:
 ✔️ Clear separation of concerns
 ✔️ External dependencies isolated from business rules
 ✔️ UI free of infrastructure and complex logic

While working on my portfolio project, Flutly Store, I applied strict layer segregation using Clean Architecture.

I didn’t plan to remove Firebase.
But I built the project as if I eventually would.

And I did.

I wanted anyone to clone the repository and run it without configuring Firebase.
Because the architecture was already in place, the solution was straightforward:
 • Create fake data sources
 • Override the interfaces
 • Update dependency injection

🔒 No production code changed.
🧠 No domain logic touched.
🟢 Tests kept passing.

Good architecture doesn’t eliminate change.
It makes change safe.

If you're curious about how Clean Architecture looks in a real Flutter project:
https://lnkd.in/dc-aGKYe

text:
The Agentic Mobile Development Pipeline

The traditional mobile development lifecycle moving from high-fidelity mockups in Figma to manual Flutter implementation is being compressed by agentic design systems.

For product teams, the bottleneck has shifted from "writing code" to "orchestrating workflows." By integrating 𝐆𝐨𝐨𝐠𝐥𝐞 𝐒𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐜𝐡 (design) with 𝐀𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐆𝐫𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐭𝐲 (agentic coding) and 𝐅𝐥𝐮𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫 (framework), we can now move from a reference URL to an installed APK without manual UI scaffolding.

Here is the technical framework for managing this pipeline:

1. 𝐀𝐫𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐚𝐥 𝐒𝐞𝐭𝐮𝐩 𝐯𝐢𝐚 𝐌𝐂𝐏
To make an agent reliable, you must equip it with the right context. Use the 𝐒𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐜𝐡 𝐌𝐂𝐏 (Model Context Protocol) and 𝐅𝐥𝐮𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫 𝐌𝐂𝐏 servers. This allows AntiGravity to control the design tool directly and understand the Dart language's specific constraints rather than just guessing syntax.

2. 𝐑𝐞𝐟𝐞𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐞-𝐁𝐚𝐬𝐞𝐝 𝐔𝐈 𝐆𝐞𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧
Instead of prompting from zero, provide Stitch with a reference URL to "scrape" visual design principles. The agent uses this to generate multi-screen interfaces, such as checkouts or dashboards, while maintaining brand consistency.

3. 𝐃𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧-𝐭𝐨-𝐂𝐨𝐝𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧
Once designs are finalized in Stitch, AntiGravity consumes the exported design files to build a working Flutter application. It handles the logic for navigation between screens and links interactive elements to functional code.

4. 𝐌𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐠𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐄𝐧𝐯𝐢𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐃𝐞𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐢𝐞𝐬
Production-grade builds require the Flutter SDK, Android SDK, and Java Development Kit (JDK). Use the agent to troubleshoot these path installations, as environment errors are the primary cause of failed local builds.

5. 𝐍𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐃𝐞𝐩𝐥𝐨𝐲𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭
The final output is an APK file, which functions as a packaged mobile application for testing on physical Android devices.

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐏𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐫'𝐬 𝐑𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐂𝐡𝐞𝐜𝐤:

• 𝐂𝐨𝐬𝐭 𝐨𝐟 𝐄𝐧𝐭𝐫𝐲: Professional deployment still requires developer fees ($25 for Play Store; $99/year for App Store) and hardware constraints (a Mac is required for iOS builds).

• 𝐃𝐲𝐧𝐚𝐦𝐢𝐜 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐭: Agents may struggle with dynamically rendered web elements; manual inspection of the source markup is often necessary to correctly source speaker images or live data.

• 𝐎𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧: One-shotted apps are MVPs; production scaling requires manual auditing of the state management and security layers.

𝘐 𝘢𝘮 𝘣𝘶𝘪𝘭𝘥𝘪𝘯𝘨 SocialMe Ai 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘵𝘦𝘢𝘤𝘩𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘈𝘐 𝘵𝘩𝘳𝘰𝘶𝘨𝘩 𝘮𝘺 𝘴𝘰𝘤𝘪𝘢𝘭 𝘤𝘩𝘢𝘯𝘯𝘦𝘭𝘴. 𝘍𝘰𝘭𝘭𝘰𝘸 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘮𝘰𝘳𝘦 𝘤𝘰𝘯𝘵𝘦𝘯𝘵 𝘭𝘪𝘬𝘦 𝘵𝘩𝘪𝘴.

text:
Most Flutter mobile apps start clean.
Then features grow. Deadlines shrink.
And suddenly… refactoring feels dangerous.
The problem isn’t Flutter.
It’s architecture.

in this series, I’ll explain the SOLID principles and how to apply them in real-world Flutter development to build scalable, testable, and maintainable apps.

L in SOLID — Liskov Substitution Principle
(The most misunderstood SOLID principle in Flutter)
Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of a subclass without breaking the application.
Sounds abstract.
In Flutter… it’s very practical.

Consider a base class:
abstract class FileStorage {
 Future<void> save(File file);
}
Then:
class LocalStorage implements FileStorage {
 @override
 Future<void> save(File file) async {
 await local.save(file);
 }
}
class ReadOnlyStorage implements FileStorage {
 @override
 Future<void> save(File file) {
 throw Exception("Storage is read-only");
 }
}

Problem?
ReadOnlyStorage cannot safely substitute FileStorage.
It breaks the contract.
That’s an LSP violation.
✅ What LSP Really Means in Flutter Architecture
LSP is about behavioral consistency, not inheritance.
If a class implements an interface, it must:
Honor method expectations
Respect return types
Maintain invariants
Not introduce unexpected exceptions
Not weaken postconditions
If your abstraction says:
“This repository logs in users.”
Every implementation must actually log in users.
Not crash.
Not partially work.
Not silently ignore logic.
🎯 Why LSP Matters in Production Flutter Apps
When you violate LSP:
Unit tests become unreliable
Mocks behave differently than real implementations
Feature toggles cause unexpected crashes
Dependency injection becomes risky
This is especially critical when using:
Clean Architecture
Repository pattern
Service abstractions
State management (BLoC, Riverpod, Provider)
If implementations aren’t safely swappable, your architecture collapses.

Most developers think LSP is about inheritance hierarchies.
In Flutter, it’s mostly about:
Designing trustworthy abstractions.

text:
This is the roadmap to go from zero → elite Flutter developer in 12 months.

I’ve trained 50+ developers and built production apps for 7+ years. Follow this step-by-step path: start with Dart, master Flutter, build real apps.

🚀 Phase 1: Dart Basics (Month 1–2):
• Variables, types, null safety.
• Functions & loops.
• Lists, Maps, Sets.
• Classes, inheritance, mixins.
📁 Project: CLI Todo App.
💡 Tip: Null safety & immutability prevent bugs in real apps.

📍 Phase 2: Flutter Basics (Month 3–4):
• Widgets & layouts.
• Stateless vs Stateful.
• Navigation & forms.
📁 Project: Recipe App.
💡 Tip: Understanding widget trees makes UI simple.

📍 Phase 3: Intermediate (Month 5–6):
• APIs & HTTP.
• JSON models.
• Local storage (Hive/SharedPreferences).
• Provider state management.
• Offline-first thinking.
📁 Project: Todo App with Backend.
💡 Tip: Async mastery (Futures & Streams) is critical.

📍 Phase 4: Architecture & Refactoring (Month 7):
• Separation of concerns.
• Repository pattern.
• Refactoring.
📁 Practice: Refactor Project 3.
💡 Tip: Proper architecture makes your code scalable.

📍 Phase 5: Advanced (Month 8–9):
• Clean Architecture (3 layers).
• BLoC / Riverpod.
• Dependency injection (get_it).
• Testing: unit, widget, integration (80% coverage).
📁 Project: Expense Tracker App.
💡 Tip: Test-driven code prepares you for enterprise apps.

📍 Phase 6: Elite Level (Month 10–12):
• Firebase (Auth, Firestore, Storage).
• Push notifications & animations.
• Performance tuning & security.
• CI/CD & App Store deployment.
📁 Projects: Social Media App, E-Commerce App (Capstone).


📝 Summary (Time, Metrics & Tips):
• Time Commitment: 2–3 hrs/day weekdays, 4–6 hrs/weekends (~1,000 hrs).
• Success Metrics: 6 GitHub projects, 2 live apps, 80%+ tests, production-ready, clean architecture.
• Avoid: Tutorial hell, skipping Dart, no testing, ignoring architecture, not deploying.
💡 Pro Tips: Build daily, test everything, learn from open source, deploy real apps, join the Flutter community, document learning.

If you found this roadmap helpful:
💾 Save it for your journey.
♻️ Repost for other developers.
✉️ DM me for personalized guidance.

text:
Clean Architecture مو Overengineering

قبل فترة كنت أدرس Clean Architecture، ومرّيت بنفس اللي كثير يواجهه:
كثير ناس يستخدمون كل الطبقات بدون تفكير: Domain، Data، Presentation… كل شيء Layer فوق Layer 😅

عشان يبرزوا عضلاتهم ويبينوا إنهم فاهمين الدنيا ويطبقوها على كل Feature في المشروع.

المهندس Omar Ahmed شرّح الموضوع بشكل رائع ومبسط، وجاب مثال واقعي:
 • أحيانًا الـ Feature بسيطة ما تحتاج طبقة Domain 
 • استخدام كل شيء بدون داعي = استهلاك موارد وعبور فاضي

لكن في Features أخرى، الطبقة نفسها تصبح ضرورية، ووقتها استخدامها منطقي تمامًا.

الدرس:

لا نسوي طبقات أو أدوات “عشان أظهر إني فاهم”
استخدمها دائمًا في محلها الصحيح حسب الحاجة.

text:
في مرة كنت فاكر إن ال mixin مجرد Syntax زيادة في Dart
لكن لما فهمته صح، غير طريقة تفكيري في تصميم الكود.

خليني أقولك الفرق اللي فعلاً بيفرق
في البرمجة عندنا نوعين من العلاقات:
● extends → "هو نوع من" (is-a)
● with → "عنده القدرة على" (can-do)

طيب يعني إيه mixin؟
mixin في Dart بيسمحلك تضيف سلوك (behavior) لكلاس
من غير ما تكسره في inheritance hierarchy معقدة.
بدل ما تعمل شجرة وراثة طويلة عشان تضيف Logging أو Validation أو Pagination
تقدر ببساطة تعمل:
mixin Logger { void log(String message) { print(message); } } class AuthService with Logger {} 
كده ضفت سلوك reusable
من غير ما تقول إن AuthService "نوع من" Logger.

ليه ده مهم فعلاً؟
لأن inheritance بيخلق coupling عالي جدا.
لكن mixin بيخليك تركب behaviors modular.
ده بيخلي الكود:
أوضح
أسهل في الاختبار
أقل تعقيد في الـ hierarchy
أقرب لمبدأ Composition over Inheritance

إمتى تستخدم mixin؟
• لما عندك cross-cutting behavior (Logging – Retry – Caching)
• لما مش محتاج constructor
• لما العلاقة "يقدر يعمل" مش "هو نوع من"
إمتى متستخدموش؟
• لو فيه state معقد
• لو فيه lifecycle خاص
• لو محتاج dependency injection مباشر
أكتر حاجة بحبها في mixin إنه بيخليك تفكر:
هل أنا بعمل وراثة فعلًا؟ ولا أنا بس محتاج أضيف قدرة للكلاس؟
السؤال ده لوحده بيمنع التصميم سيء من البداية.
اللي جاي من Kotlin هيلاحظ إن أقرب حاجة ليه
هي Interface فيه default implementation.
لكن في Dart الموضوع وأوضح شوية.
لو اشتغلت قبل كده على Flutter،
أكيد استخدمت mixin من غير ما تحس
زي logger وال safe call للAPIs

هل شايف إن mixin بيخليك تميل أكتر ل composition؟
ولا شايفه مجرد أداة تنظيم كود؟
