text:
Why I built a custom Dart CLI to handle B2B "Data Chaos." 🛠️💻

Most people see Dart as "the language for Flutter UI." I see it as the perfect engine for high-performance business automation.

Recently, I was faced with a massive B2B challenge: Mapping 50,000+ wholesale SKUs from a legacy SQL database into a clean, Firestore-ready format for a mobile app.

Instead of doing it manually or fighting with clunky middleware, I built a Custom CLI in Dart.

Why a CLI?

Speed: It processes complex logic trees faster than any GUI.

Automation: It handles repetitive bulk updates to "Tiered Pricing" with zero human error.

Precision: It validates "Net-30" credit data before it ever touches the production app.

I’m obsessed with this level of engineering. In the high-budget world of Complex Checkouts, you need more than just a pretty screen—you need a developer who can build the tooling to keep the data clean and the workflows synchronized.

I don't just build the "front door" of your app; I build the engine room that keeps your B2B operations running at scale.

– API architecture
– Performance under load
– Security best practices
– Long-term maintainability

Because in production, design impresses users. Architecture keeps them.

A beautiful app with a weak backend will always break under pressure.
But a well-architected system scales.

What do you think is more difficult — building the UI or designing the backend architecture?

text:
Why I Almost Always Expect a Gradle Error Before a Unity Build

Almost every time I hit Build in Unity, I expect a Gradle error.

But why?
Why does it happen in so many of my projects?

Unity + Gradle has 20–30 common errors that developers around the world run into.

But in my case, it’s usually the same 1 or 2 errors.
I’ve seen them so many times that I can often predict them before the build even starts — and prevent them.

Sometimes I don’t.
I let them happen.
Maybe as a reminder. Maybe out of habit.

But I know I can fix them.

So what’s really causing it?

In my situation, it comes down to something simple: network access.

When Unity builds for Android, it uses Gradle to resolve dependencies.
During the process, Gradle contacts repositories like Googles Maven repository (for example: dl.google.com) to download required Android libraries and plugins.

If the build machine cannot properly access those servers, dependency resolution fails.
And when dependency resolution fails → the build fails.

In regions where access to some Google servers is unstable or restricted, this becomes a recurring issue.
It’s not a Unity bug.
It’s not necessarily a Gradle bug.
It’s a connectivity problem.

Once I understood that, everything changed.

My usual fixes:

- Switching to a reliable DNS
- Using a stable VPN connection
- Making sure required dependencies are cached locally before critical builds

When you know the root cause, the “random” error stops being random.

Do you also have recurring Gradle errors that you can predict before they even happen?

text:
🚀 “Vibe coding” is 100% real… and honestly addictive.
With AI, you can go from random idea → beautiful working screen in under 5 minutes.
Widgets? Done.
API calls? Generated.
Navigation + state? Handled.
That part feels like pure magic. 🔥
But here’s the reality check:
Users don’t care how fast you built the first screen.
They care if your app still feels smooth, stable, and fast after 50+ screens, real user data, terrible internet, edge cases, and 6 months of new feature requests.
That’s exactly where Flutter separates the pros from the hobbyists.
One codebase.
iOS + Android + Web + Desktop.
Natively compiled performance.
The smoothest hot reload on the planet.
And a UI system that actually scales as your app grows.
The uncomfortable truth?
AI-generated code is fast… but often fragile.
Missing null checks, hallucinated packages, weird state bugs — I’ve debugged them all.
So my honest take after shipping real apps:
→ AI = rocket fuel for speed
→ Flutter = the parachute that actually gets you to landing safely
My daily workflow now:
Use AI to blast through boilerplate and exploration…
Then lean on real Flutter fundamentals to make it production-grade, maintainable, and bulletproof.
AI makes you faster.
Flutter makes you dangerously good.
That’s the stack I’m betting on in 2026.

text:
Most Flutter mobile apps start clean.
Then features grow. Deadlines shrink.
And suddenly… refactoring feels dangerous.
The problem isn’t Flutter.
It’s architecture.

in this series, I’ll explain the SOLID principles and how to apply them in real-world Flutter development to build scalable, testable, and maintainable apps.

I in SOLID — Interface Segregation Principle
(And why your repositories are probably too fat)

Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use.
In Flutter projects… this is violated all the time.
🚨 The “God Repository” Anti-Pattern
Let’s say you define a repository like this:

abstract class UserRepository {
 Future<User> getUser();
 Future<void> updateUser(User user);
 Future<void> deleteUser();
 Future<void> uploadAvatar(File file);
 Future<List<Order>> getOrders();
 Future<void> changePassword(String password);
}

Seems convenient.
But now imagine:
The Profile screen only needs getUser() and updateUser()
The Orders screen only needs getOrders()
The Security screen only needs changePassword()
Yet every feature depends on the entire interface.
That’s a violation of ISP.
🧨 The Real Problem
This leads to:
Bloated mocks in tests
Unused dependencies injected everywhere
Increased coupling between features

That’s fragile architecture.
✅ Applying Interface Segregation in Flutter
Instead of one large interface, split by responsibility.
1️⃣ Smaller, Focused Contracts

abstract class UserReader {
 Future<User> getUser();
}

abstract class UserUpdater {
 Future<void> updateUser(User user);
}

abstract class UserSecurity {
 Future<void> changePassword(String password);
}

abstract class UserOrders {
 Future<List<Order>> getOrders();
}

Now each feature depends only on what it needs.
2️⃣ Compose When Necessary
Your concrete implementation can still combine them:

class ApiUserRepository 
 implements UserReader, UserUpdater, UserSecurity, UserOrders {
 
 // Implement all methods here
}

But your UI layer only receives the slice it requires.
Example:

class ProfileController {
 final UserReader reader;
 final UserUpdater updater;

 ProfileController(this.reader, this.updater);
}

Now:
Profile logic doesn’t depend on orders
Orders logic doesn’t depend on password management
Changes stay isolated
That’s ISP.
ISP gives you:
✔ Smaller, more focused abstractions
 ✔ Cleaner dependency injection
 ✔ Easier mocking
 ✔ Reduced ripple effects
 ✔ Better modular architecture

ISP is not about creating many tiny interfaces blindly.
It’s about aligning abstractions with feature boundaries.
Ask yourself:
“Does this screen really need all of these methods?”
If not — your interface is too fat.
Slim interfaces create flexible systems.
Fat interfaces create hidden coupling.

text:

text:
🔧 Faced a Gradle issue after upgrading to Flutter 3.29.0? You're not alone! 🚨
After updating my app to Flutter 3.29.0, I hit a build error:
“Your app is using an unsupported Gradle project...”
 “You are applying Flutter’s Gradle plugin imperatively…” 🚫
"FAILURE: Build failed with an exception.
* Where:
Script 'C:\flutter\packages\flutter_tools\gradle\app_plugin_loader.gradle' line: 9
* What went wrong:
FAILURE: Build failed with an exception."
🛠️ What caused it?
 Flutter 3.29 requires a new Gradle setup. The older way of applying plugins using apply from: no longer works. ⚡
✅ How I fixed it:
Switched to the new plugin block in android/build.gradle:
plugins {
 id "dev.flutter.flutter-gradle-plugin" version "1.0.0"
}
Removed outdated buildscript {}, allProjects and apply from: lines. ✂️
"pluginManagement {
 repositories {
 gradlePluginPortal()
 google()
 mavenCentral()
 }
 plugins {
 id "dev.flutter.flutter-gradle-plugin" version "1.0.0"
 }
}" Add this at the top of settings.gradle file.
🧠 Lesson learned: Always check the Flutter upgrade guide when updating major versions. 📚

text:
One of the common problem that I face

*- Android Gradle: Error and Solution


While working on a Flutter project, I encountered an error related to Gradle when attempting to run the flutter build apk command. The error message was as follows:

FAILURE: Build failed with an exception. 
* What went wrong: 
Execution failed for task ':app:compileDebugJavaWithJavac'. 
> Could not find tools.jar. Please check that JAVA_HOME points to a valid JDK installation.

-Root Cause:

This issue occurred because the JAVA_HOME environment variable was incorrectly pointing to JRE instead of JDK.


✅ Steps to Resolve the Issue:

1. Ensure JDK is Installed:

Verified that the appropriate JDK version was installed. It can be downloaded from Oracle's website.



2. Set JAVA_HOME Environment Variable Correctly:
Edited the system environment settings (on Windows, for example):

Went to System Settings → Environment Variables.

Added the path to the JDK:


JAVA_HOME=C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-XX 
PATH=%JAVA_HOME%\bin


3. Verify the Configuration:
Used the following command in the terminal to confirm the setup:

echo %JAVA_HOME%


4. Restarted the Project Build:
After updating the settings, I ran the following commands:

flutter clean 
flutter pub get 
flutter build apk



🎯 Result:
The build process completed successfully without any errors.

💡 Tip:
Always ensure compatibility between your Flutter, Gradle, and JDK versions. Using supported versions saves a lot of time and trouble.

Have you encountered a similar issue? I’m sharing my solution and would love to hear about your experiences as well!


---

Let me know if you'd like further tweaks or additions!

text:
The Life of Flutter Developers 🙃

After 8 hours of non-stop coding, squashing bugs like a ninja, perfecting UI like a pixel artist, and integrating every API, Exception handling bla bla bla…..

I finally said:
“It’s done.”
Time to show the masterpiece to the manager.

So I confidently typed:

flutter build --release

And then…

Boom.
Red. Terminal. Vomit.

“Some plugin failed.”
“Obscure Gradle error.”
“minSdkVersion???”
“Try again with –stacktrace.”
😶‍🌫️😵‍💫😵‍💫😵‍💫😵‍💫😵‍💫😵‍💫😵‍💫😵‍💫

Me: “But it was working in debug…”
Terminal: “I don’t care.”

At that point, I questioned my career, my life choices, and why flutter clean feels like a magic ritual rather than a command, not every time😉

Moral of the story?
If your app works in debug, don’t celebrate yet.
Because flutter build --release is the real final boss.

Debug mode is the dream. Release mode is reality.

text:
🚧 Inheriting a Flutter Project Without a Keystore – What I Learned
Recently, I took over a Flutter project and ran into an unexpected issue when trying to build the APK for release. The debug build worked fine, but the release build failed.
After checking the logs, I discovered the issue: the build.gradle file was referencing a signing config tied to a missing keystore file. The previous developer didn’t hand over the keystore or credentials, making it impossible to generate a signed release APK.
What I Did:
Generated a new keystore file
Updated the build.gradle with the new signing details
Rebuilt the APK successfully
Key Lessons:
Always request the keystore file and credentials when inheriting a project
Don’t wait until release time to test your build process
Proper documentation and handover practices matter
It was a frustrating but eye-opening experience — a reminder that smooth dev handovers are just as critical as clean code.

text:
𝐓𝐰𝐨 𝐰𝐞𝐞𝐤𝐬. 𝐒𝐚𝐦𝐞 𝐞𝐫𝐫𝐨𝐫. 𝐍𝐨𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐞𝐝… 𝐮𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐥 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐝𝐢𝐝.


For almost 2 weeks, my Flutter build kept failing with a Gradle error:
"𝑪𝒐𝒖𝒍𝒅 𝒏𝒐𝒕 𝒓𝒆𝒂𝒅 𝒘𝒐𝒓𝒌𝒔𝒑𝒂𝒄𝒆 𝒎𝒆𝒕𝒂𝒅𝒂𝒕𝒂…
𝒄𝒉𝒆𝒄𝒌𝑫𝒆𝒃𝒖𝒈𝑨𝒂𝒓𝑴𝒆𝒕𝒂𝒅𝒂𝒕𝒂 𝒇𝒂𝒊𝒍𝒆𝒅"

𝗜 𝗱𝗶𝗱 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 “𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁”:
Deleted the .gradle folder (multiple times)
Ran flutter clean
Rebuilt again… and again… and again
Read countless threads and docs
Still failed.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗽𝗼𝗶𝗻𝘁?
I changed my location and switched to a different network.
Same code.
Same machine.
Fresh build.
It worked instantly.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲’𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗜 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗲𝗱
Sometimes, the problem isn’t your code.
Sometimes, it’s not even your setup.
Unstable or restricted networks can silently corrupt Gradle downloads, leaving you with misleading local build errors that look like configuration issues.
What looked like a “Flutter/Gradle problem” was actually an environment problem.

𝐃𝐨𝐧’𝐭 𝐚𝐬𝐬𝐮𝐦𝐞 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐦𝐞𝐚𝐧𝐬 𝐲𝐨𝐮’𝐫𝐞 𝐝𝐨𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐬𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐰𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐠

Debugging isn’t always about writing better code, sometimes it’s about questioning the environment you’re working in.
What’s the weirdest bug or issue you’ve faced that ended up having nothing to do with your code?
Let’s learn from each other.

text:
🚨 Flutter Devs — Here’s the One Build Error Nobody Talks About: 16KB Alignment

You build. You sign. You upload to Play Console.
 And suddenly…

“Your app’s native binaries must be 16KB aligned.”

No logs. No hints. No Stack Overflow answer that actually works.

That’s exactly what I ran into recently — and what led me to write this piece:
 👉 What Nobody Tells You About 16KB Alignment in Flutter (Until Your Build Breaks)

Here’s the surprising truth:
 It’s not your code. It’s your build toolchain.
If your Android Gradle Plugin is below 8.5, it can’t detect or fix unaligned native binaries inside Flutter plugins (like sqlite3 or drift).
 After upgrading Gradle + AGP + Kotlin, I finally saw what was going wrong — and why alignment checks silently failed before.
In this post, I break down:

🔍 How to locate the exact .so files causing 16KB misalignment
🧰 The right order to upgrade Gradle, AGP, and Kotlin
🧹 Why flutter clean isn’t enough without updated packaging tools
📊 Real output from grep and APK Analyzer with libsqlite3.so examples

This issue is affecting more Flutter projects than most realize — especially post-AGP 8.5 updates.

If your release builds keep failing Play Store uploads, this article might save you hours of blind debugging.

Read here → https://lnkd.in/dMZGHTSM

text:
Having opened over 100 tabs in Chrome, I decided to create a browser to solve this problem.

Jokes aside, I often have several browser windows open because I need to work with different profiles. Modern browsers offer a few features and plugins to address this, but as someone who enjoys experimenting with new things, I saw an opportunity. With the help of AI, I can build a fully functional prototype and learn about various technologies, including their development and maintenance costs.

I had some experience with iOS development, having built apps for the iPhone in my early career. However, I’d never created a desktop app. Two years ago, I might have opted for a web-based technology like Electron. However, the cost of learning new technology has significantly decreased, and I was genuinely curious to explore Flutter. Reviews suggest it can create cross-platform apps with near-native performance.

I spent a few evenings working on the browser, and I’m quite satisfied with the result. The macOS version is fast and responsive. Once I develop a Windows/Linux version, I’ll have a broader perspective, so we’ll consider Flutter as a potential stack in the future.

As for the browser, I’ve named it Moose Browser, a sidecar for you main browser. I’ve been using it for a few days now, making improvements daily. Many original UX decisions failed once I started using it.

Now, I have a great story to share with my development teams. Check the demo

text:
🚨 Major Google Play Update for Android & Flutter Devs 🚨

From Nov 1, 2025, Google Play will reject app updates that don’t support 16 KB memory pages.

Why this matters:
• Newer Android 15 devices use 16 KB pages (vs 4 KB earlier).
• Pure Dart Flutter apps → already compliant.
• Plugins with native .so libraries → need to be rebuilt.
• Skipping this can mean crashes, failed installs, or outright rejection on the Play Store.

Here’s the survival checklist 🛠️:
 1️⃣ Upgrade → AGP 8.5.1+, latest Gradle, NDK r28+
 2️⃣ Update → Flutter & plugins (many are already 16 KB–ready)
 3️⃣ Rebuild → flutter clean && flutter build appbundle
 4️⃣ Test → on Android 15 with adb shell getconf PAGE_SIZE

💡 Think of this as the next 64-bit migration. Smooth if you prepare early, messy if you ignore it.

👉 Are your plugins and toolchains ready yet?

text:
I got a notification from Google Play last night.
It was a member of our BlooDoChallenge community reporting a critical issue.

If you're a founder, you know the feeling ... you are failing the people that put the trust in you to deliver top notch experience. 
It wasn't the case this time. I failed them.

One feeling and a thought later, I figured, chatGPT would instantly know what to do.

So after reproducing the error, I opened the chat, pasted the error, and what I initially tought it would be a 30 minute task, turned into a 6 hour long debate, long running builds and no result.

I was running in circles around these suggestions:
❌Identified MissingPluginException → plugin not registered on the running Flutter engine
❌Verified MainActivity and MethodChannel setup were correct
❌Compare Debug vs Release manifests → launcher activity was correct
❌Remove custom Application + manually create FlutterEngine
❌Aligne Android Gradle Plugin versions (application + library)
❌Performe full clean builds to remove stale artifacts

When all hope was lost, I opened a new console and typed: claude

Pasted in the error and I waited ....
First run failed, but it was a completely different approach
Then, in the second run, VICTORY 🚀

💡 Claude analyzed the entire code base again, something I couldn't possibly do with chatGPT pasting in file by file, and the problem was resolved. The fix made total sense.

Total time with claude: 37 minutes ⏲️

If you are still second guessing these AI tools, guess again, but fast.

I've tried Grok, ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, Copilot, Gemini, Antigravity, Perplexity, Bolt.new, Firebase Studio, Lovable.dev, and one pattern arises every time:

👉You need to understand how the tool works and how to use it in order to achieve the results you want.

If you haven't started yet, now is the best time to do it! 🤖

text:
Flutter 3.32 – Release build failed to strip debug symbols from native libraries after upgrade when trying to build app bundle for Google Play Store

For me, the issue was resolved by installing the latest Android SDK Command-line Tools.

Here’s how I fixed it:

1. Open Android Studio.
2. Go to Settings > Languages & Frameworks > SDK Tools.
3. Check Android SDK Command-line Tools (latest).
4. Click Apply and wait for the download to complete.
5. Open a terminal and run:
flutter doctor --android-licenses
6. Accept all licenses.
7. Then run:
flutter clean
flutter pub get
flutter build appbundle

After these steps, the build worked successfully and the issue was resolved.

text:
Flutter 3.29 has just been released, and after I updated it, our app failed to build.

Flutter showed errors like “cannot find symbol” and failed to compile.
After spending an entire day investigating—clearing the cache, changing the Java version, clearing Gradle, and clearing the pub cache—I finally found the issue.

The API
import io.flutter.plugin.common.PluginRegistry.Registrar 
has been removed, but Flutter failed to mention in the breaking changes that V1 embedding would be removed.
https://lnkd.in/gA2FuPi8

As a result, many libraries that still rely on this import are now broken. We had to manually pull those dependencies, remove this import, and modify some code related to PluginRegistry.Registrar.

If you’ve encountered this issue, actively maintained libraries will likely push fixes soon. However, for abandoned libraries, this is a critical problem—especially for teams that need to upgrade to Flutter 3.29.0 quickly.

How to fix it? Read more details in my Medium. Since I have a hard time to include code snippet in here
https://lnkd.in/gn6ayG9R

In my opinion, it would be helpful if Flutter avoided removing breaking changes without clear communication, especially after the challenges in the previous release (PowerVR Renderer). Or perhaps they simply forgot to mention it since this API had been deprecated for a long time. Hopefully, this can be improved in future updates to make transitions smoother for developers

Follow more on Flutter Mekong—where we share real-world experiences so that no one has to repeat the same mistakes.
https://lnkd.in/gqjPD2Hk

embedding
لما ترفع key بالغلط وتدوّخ على حل 

كنت شغالة على مشروع Flutter، و فيه Map key اترفعت بالغلط على GitHub وقام بعتلي إيميل بيقول إن فيه key ظهر في الكوميت، ومعاه نوعه كمان بالتفصيل في الميل.

مسحت الـ key وبدأت أعمل filter للفايل علشان أشيله من الـ history كله.
في الأول استخدمت الطريقة القديمة، وطلعتلي مشكلة بسبب الـ \ لأنها أوامر Linux، فالحل كان إنّي أشيلهم.و ظبطت عادي
بس الاسهل الطريقة الجديدة دي 

git filter-repo --path app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml --invert-paths
وبعدها 
git push origin main --force
كل حاجة كانت ماشية تمام،
لحد ما جيت أشغل الكود وطلعلي ده:

Build failed due to use of deleted Android V1 embedding

فضلت أدور كتير على الحل،كل راي بتعديل
ولحد ما ChatGPT قالي “اعملي مشروع جديد”، كنت بقول أكيد ليها حل تاني 

المشكلة مش في الكود خالص،المشكلة إن الـ filter مسح ملفات أساسية من Android folder
اللي بتخص الـ Flutter embedding.وبكده المشروع بقى شايف v1 embedding اللي خلاص Flutter ما بيدعموش.وانا كل شويه اعدل
الحل كان أسهل مما تخيلت:
flutter create .

رجّع كل ملفات Android الناقصة، والمشروع اشتغل عادي جدًا 

وطبعًا أستخدم
.env أو secure storage أو Remote Config
وأضيفهم في .gitignore للkeys 
بس لاني كنت خلاص مش محتاجه الkeyونسيت امسحه قبل ما ارفع ف عمل المشكله

🚫 Your app works… until it doesn’t.
But real users don’t care about logs — they care when the UI disappears.

🧠 That’s where Error Boundaries & Fallback UI come in.

🔧 Use patterns like:
 • 𝙁𝙡𝙪𝙩𝙩𝙚𝙧𝙀𝙧𝙧𝙤𝙧.𝙤𝙣𝙀𝙧𝙧𝙤𝙧 = (𝙁𝙡𝙪𝙩𝙩𝙚𝙧𝙀𝙧𝙧𝙤𝙧𝘿𝙚𝙩𝙖𝙞𝙡𝙨 𝙙𝙚𝙩𝙖𝙞𝙡𝙨) {}

 • Show fallback UIs for null data, failed states, or empty lists
 • Use 𝘽𝙡𝙤𝙘𝘽𝙪𝙞𝙡𝙙𝙚𝙧’s 𝙗𝙪𝙞𝙡𝙙𝙒𝙝𝙚𝙣 to guard against invalid states

text:
🛑 The file that stopped my entire project.



The pubspec.lock / bonsoir version conflict.

If you are a Flutter developer, you know the pain of Version Solving Failed.
I got completely stuck integrating a casting feature. My terminal was screaming errors about "getters not defined" and version conflicts with a package called bonsoir. Nothing compiled.
The culprit? A conflict deep inside pubspec.lock.
I learned a valuable lesson: sometimes you have to burn it down to build it back up.
👉 I deleted pubspec.lock.
👉 I used dependency_overrides in pubspec.yaml to force the correct versions.
👉 flutter pub get saved the day.
We are back in business! 🚀

text:
Inventory Quotient finally compiled and built 🥲🎉

When I was done with the frontend of Inventory Quotient, I tried building it to preview how it would look in deployment. I kept running into build errors. It never built. And the error message wasn’t helpful at all.
This was the last part of the log:
```
* What went wrong:
Execution failed for task ':app:compileReleaseKotlin'.
> A failure occurred while executing org.jetbrains.kotlin.compilerRunner.GradleCompilerRunnerWithWorkers$GradleKotlinCompilerWorkAction
 > Compilation error. See log for more details
BUILD FAILED in 4m 2s

```
I couldn’t for the life of me figure it out. So of course, I copied and pasted it into ChatGPT.
Spoiler alert: the answers didn’t work 😂

It led me down such a deep hole. This took days. Even when I started working on other stuff, I would come back and try a “new fix.”

At some point, my only salvation was version control. I had to reset my codebase to the last recognizable state 🤦🏽‍♂️
So I put it away for a while and focused on my backend, but this issue was still sitting quietly at the back of my mind.

Then I was randomly scrolling (can’t even remember where), and I saw someone mention they were having issues building their React Native app and it turned out to be dependency mismatches.

I went back to ChatGPT and asked if that could be the issue.
Finally… good answers.
It suggested I run expo-doctor.
In hindsight, I should have done that from the beginning. But hey, hindsight is 20/20.

After running expo-doctor, the issues surfaced:
 – Dependency mismatches
 – Duplicate dependencies
 – App icon not being a square
 – Two legacy dependency warnings (which could be ignored)

Worked through them one after the other with ChatGPT, and voila…
The app compiled 🥳🥳🥳
What a feeling.

Sometimes the problem isn’t some deep mysterious bug. It’s just your dependencies fighting quietly behind the scenes.

Onwards 🚀

text:
A few days ago I built a project involving Kubernetes orchestration for Urumi.AI Gen AI hiring challenge.

The goal was to build a Store Provisioning Platform (dashboard: Store Weaver) that could run on local Kubernetes (Kind/k3d/Minikube) and be production-ready with the same Helm charts, provisioning isolated ecommerce stores on demand (WooCommerce or Medusa) with clean teardown, persistence, and scalability.

Features Implemented:

-> Namespace-per-store multi-tenant isolation — Deployments, StatefulSets, Services, PVCs, Ingress, Secrets, and init Jobs all in that namespace.
-> Kubernetes-native provisioning via Helm (values-local / values-prod), secrets at provision time, no secrets in repo.
-> Idempotent flows — slug + collision handling; on failure: Helm uninstall + namespace delete; retry only for failed stores; delete always cleans release, namespace, and DB.
-> Readiness and failure handling — monitor service with 5-min timeout and optional HTTP health checks; cleanup on failure.
-> Resource guardrails — MAX_STORES, per-user limit (X-User-Id), MAX_CONCURRENT_PROVISIONS; optional ResourceQuota in prod
Store Weaver — React + Vite + shadcn, Node.js backend, PostgreSQL, and live store detail from the Kubernetes API.

I also got to discuss this project end-to-end and my previous experience with Naman Malhotra and Vedanshu Jain.

text:
Day 11 of 28.....

I just spent 4 hours building a notification popup.

Not the backend.
Not the push service.
Just the little "Success!" toast that slides in from the corner.

You know why?

Because npm install react-toastify is a trap.

They don't tell you about:
→ Memory leaks when components unmount mid-timeout
→ Screen readers that miss them entirely (no ARIA live regions)
→ The z-index wars when your toast hides behind a modal
→ Stacking 3 notifications that overlap like a car crash

So I architected it from scratch.

React Context for global state (no prop drilling).
React Portal to document.body (escapes all parents).
Proper cleanup with useEffect return functions.
CSS custom properties for the stacking offset.

The result? 
Notifications that actually notify.
Toasts that don't burn your app.

text:
🚀 The ScientistsHub Android app is currently in Play Store testing.
Not public yet.

But production lessons already hit.

What we built:
A content-heavy science platform in React Native:
• Category-based browsing (Space & Astronomy, Discoveries, Health & Biology, Technology, etc.)
• Full article detail with HTML rendering
• Author profiles
• Bookmarks + offline reading
• Search
• In-app browser
• Custom bottom navigation

All in TypeScript.
Feature-based architecture.
Server state handled with TanStack Query.

Now the real lessons 👇

🔴 Android package rename chaos

I changed:
• applicationId in build.gradle
• Kotlin package folder structure
• AndroidManifest.xml
• MainActivity.kt
• MainApplication.kt

Everything looked correct.

Build still failed.

Why?
Even after updating all source files, the generated files inside android/build and android/app/build were still referencing the old package name.

And gradlew clean didn’t remove everything.

Fix:
Manually delete android/build and android/app/build.
Reset Metro cache.
Full rebuild.

Small change. Non-obvious failure.

🔴 Infinite scroll is easy… until it’s not

useInfiniteQuery looks clean — until you manage:
• Cursor-based pagination
• Pull-to-refresh that resets only page 1
• FlatList footer loading state
• Avoiding page 2 firing before page 1 renders

State sync gets tricky fast.

🔴 Dark mode at scale

15+ screens. Shared components.
One missed backgroundColor = random white flash in dark mode.

Rule: No hardcoded hex values.
Every color flows through useTheme().

🔴 HTML rendering is not plug-and-play

Scientific articles = nested lists, references, inline links, blockquotes.
react-native-render-html works — but tuning tagStyles, handling link presses, and preventing layout overflow took serious iteration.

Stack:
React Native 0.83 · TypeScript · React Navigation · TanStack Query · Reanimated · Gesture Handler · FastImage

If you’re building a content-heavy mobile app:

Your week 1 architecture decisions will either save you…
Or haunt you by week 6.

Happy to share the feature-based folder structure that kept this project sane.

text:
Managing dependency conflicts in legacy React Native projects

We’ve all experienced it: you’re working on an older project, you try to install a new library, and the entire build fails.

Between Gradle errors and CocoaPods conflicts, what should be a simple task can quickly turn into hours of troubleshooting. In the past, my process was very manual:

1️⃣ Searching NPM for the most stable version.
2️⃣ Installing it and try for a successful build.
If it failed, I would spend time trial-and-erroring different versions until the conflicts were resolved.

Another way to solve this problem by Microsoft’s align-deps: Using @rnx-kit/align-deps takes the guesswork out of the equation. You specify your React Native version, and it automatically aligns your dependencies to versions that are guaranteed to work together.

3️⃣ Lately, I’ve been using AI as a 'version consultant.' 
I ask it to find the exact version that fits my current setup. If a conflict still pops up, I just feed the error back to the AI to find the next stable alternative. It’s a huge time-saver.

We’ve all been there, sometimes the conflict is so deep in the Android or iOS native code that nothing seems to work. You end up uninstalling the package entirely and trying a different one. And honestly? There are those days where you just end up writing the logic yourself from scratch just to get the project moving. 

How do you handle dependency management in your projects? Are you using any specific tools to prevent version conflicts?

text:
Just shipped BrewTrack to production - A React Native app that lets coffee enthusiasts track their brews, beans, and grinders with Firebase backend. 250K tokens and countless iterations later, here are my 3 technical lessons from going dev → production:

1. Firebase + Expo SDK 54 integration is deceptively complex: Google Sign-In, Firestore user-scoped collections (brews, beans, grinders), and Storage for coffee photos all need coordinated auth flows. Writing Firestore security rules that properly isolate user data while allowing efficient queries meant understanding both Firebase's rule language AND query constraints. Getting google-services.json, expo-auth-session, and Firebase Web SDK to work together took multiple dependency version iterations.

2. Annoying bugs usually have simple solutions: Firestore silently rejects writes with undefined values - discovered this when optional fields broke prod writes (don't come after me, this is my first time building a full-stack app from scratch). Solution: implemented a custom "omitUndefined()" filter with feature flags for instant rollback. EAS Build failed three times because "metro.config.js" works locally but breaks in cloud builds. Added "react-native-worklets" as a peer dependency and configured bundle settings specifically for production.

3. Advanced UX needs architectural planning upfront: Dual-swipe navigation (swipe between tabs, swipe between log steps) required "react-native-gesture-handler" + "react-native-reanimated" coordination. Built feature flags (ENABLE_DUAL_SWIPE, ENABLE_STEP_SWIPE) and snapshot-based rollback scripts before implementing, not after breaking things.

The result: production APK with Google auth, secure multi-collection Firestore, image uploads, CSV,JSON export, and gesture-based navigation—all deployed via EAS Build.

What unexpected edge case broke your last (AI) production deploy?

text:
From "Ticket Resolved" to "Error Prevented": Building Resilient React Apps

In my 6+ years as an Application Support Professional, I’ve handled countless tickets where a user says, "The page is blank." Often, the culprit is a failed API call that the frontend didn't know how to handle.

As I expand my skills into React Native and web development, my goal is to build interfaces that don't just look good, but stay standing when things go wrong. 

Use "Error Boundaries" for a Better User Experience
In React, a JavaScript error in part of the UI shouldn't crash the whole app.

By default, if a component throws an error during rendering, React will unmount the entire component tree—leaving the user with a blank white screen.

Implement Error Boundaries. These are special components that catch JavaScript errors anywhere in their child component tree and display a fallback UI (like "Something went wrong, please try again") instead of crashing.

In the SaaS and banking platforms I’ve supported, visibility is everything. A good Error Boundary doesn't just show a message; it logs the error to a service like Sentry or LogRocket, so developers can fix it before the next user sees it.

My experience at Ayur Sattva Technovation and RED-CELL POWER taught me that a proactive developer is a support engineer's best friend. We build for the "worst-case scenario" to ensure a "best-case" user experience.

How do you handle graceful failures in your apps? Do you use global Error Boundaries or component-specific ones? Let’s discuss!

text:
The "React vs. React Native" Trap 🪤


One of the most expensive assumptions a leadership team can make is thinking a great React (web) developer can automatically build a production-ready React Native (mobile) app.
On the surface, it makes sense: both use JavaScript, and both share the same design philosophy. If your app is incredibly simple, a web developer can actually get pretty far. But when building a scalable, production-grade mobile product, assuming web and mobile are interchangeable is a trap that routinely costs startups months of runway.

The Core Difference: The "Bridge" When you build a website, React speaks directly to the browser. In mobile, React Native has to use a "Bridge" to translate your web-style code into native iOS or Android commands.
This bridge is exactly where the "web equivalence" breaks down.
Here is where the traditional web mindset stalls mobile development:

📱 The Hardware Wall: Web devs are used to screens and clicks. Mobile requires deep integration with native hardware (camera, GPS, background tasks) requiring specific iOS/Android knowledge.

📶 The "Always Online" Myth: Web apps assume a steady connection. Mobile apps live on subways and spotty cellular networks, demanding a deeply different "offline-first" architecture.

⚙️ The Custom Code Trap: React Native has great pre-built components, but the second you need a highly custom feature, your team has to write deeply technical Native Code (Swift or Kotlin).

🚀 The App Store Reality: Updating a website takes seconds. Deploying a mobile app requires complex build pipelines, rigid App Store approvals, and completely different debugging tools.

The Business Impact When leadership tries to stretch web developers into native mobile roles to save money, the opposite usually happens.
Instead of moving faster, teams routinely lose 8 to 12 weeks forcing web code into a mobile environment—battling sluggish performance and failed App Store submissions—only to realize they have to hire a specialized mobile developer to fix the foundation anyway.

The Bottom Line: React Native is a powerful bridge, but true native mobile expertise is the difference between a successful launch and a buggy, delayed release. If mobile is a core part of your roadmap, allocate the right native expertise from day one. Protect your momentum.

text:
Spent today debugging a classic cross-architecture trap while integrating Godot + Expo React Native. (Probably would never have closed it without Codex 5.3
So I was trying to embed a Godot Game application within react native app, which was possible since last year after the react-native-godot lib was out(built on libGodot). It had many constraints such as Godot native artifacts being only able to support ISA ARM64 devices, while I was developing on an x86_64 Windows machine.
That mismatch showed up in multiple ways:
ARM64 emulator image would not boot on this host (arm64 not supported by QEMU2 on x86_64 host)
x86/x86_64 emulator builds failed because required Godot static libs for that ABI were missing
Build errors referenced missing libgodot-cpp.a for x86_64
Hence I moved the run target from emulator to a physical ARM64 Android phone and adjusted the workflow:
Verified device architecture + ADB connectivity
Built/installed directly to phone
Ran as Expo dev client (not Expo Go)
Fixed bundler conflicts by pinning Metro and using --no-bundler
Why this mattered
This wasn’t a “random Gradle issue.” It was an ISA/ABI alignment problem:
Host CPU architecture
Emulator system image architecture
Native dependency prebuilt architecture
Target device architecture
Once all four were aligned around ARM64 (with the phone as target), the app finally appeared on my android phone
Big reminder: in native-mobile work, architecture compatibility is often the first thing to validate...

text:
My LangGraph agent collected 21 fields of booking data. React silently threw all of it away.

The chat agent talks to customers, figures out what they need, and when they're ready to book it packages everything into a "Start Booking" button. Service type, addresses, dates, preferences. 21 fields.

Customer clicks the button. Booking wizard opens. Every field empty.

Here's what happened:

The booking wizard resets its state every time it loads. Clean slate. That's by design — you don't want leftover data from a previous session polluting a new booking.

The chat agent writes handoff data to that same state. Then the user navigates to the wizard. The wizard loads, resets everything, and the handoff data is gone.

First fix: store the data in localStorage instead. The wizard can't touch that. Then use a useEffect to watch for the wizard to finish resetting, read from localStorage, and put the data back.

Didn't work.

The wizard set the step counter to 0 then immediately to 1. React batched both updates into one render. It saw the value go from 1 to 1. No change detected. The useEffect never fired. The data sat in localStorage unread.

React saw the beginning and the end. It missed the middle.

Second fix: stop fighting the reset. Put the localStorage read inside the reset function itself. The function that wipes the state also checks for handoff data and merges it in during the wipe. One operation. No watcher. No race condition.

When two parts of your app fight over the same state, don't referee from the outside. Put the logic where the conflict lives.

What's the most frustrating state management bug you've dealt with?

text:
Most Next.js apps have a "hidden" UX bug: The 200ms white flash. 🔦❌

Fixing a "Hydration Mismatch" is easy. Fixing it without ruining the User Experience is where the real engineering happens.

The common approach is to move localStorage logic into a useEffect. While this silences the warning, it introduces a Flash of Unstyled Content (FOUC) that ruins the user experience.

High-performance apps don't rely on client-side patches. They move the Source of Truth to the Server.

By using Cookies in the Root Layout:
✅ Zero Flickering: HTML arrives correctly styled from the first byte.
✅ Better Core Web Vitals: No layout shifts or late paints.
✅ Predictable UI: No more suppressHydrationWarning hacks.

Check the evolution in the image below.

text:
🧠💻 DEV IQ CHALLENGE: Can You Guess the Bug?

A client reports:

“The app works perfectly on desktop… but crashes instantly on mobile after login.”

Here’s what we know:
✔ No recent backend changes
✔ No server errors
✔ Works perfectly on Wi-Fi
❌ Fails on cellular

So what’s the most likely culprit?

A) Memory leak
B) API timeout
C) CORS issue
D) Environment-specific configuration bug

At Finally Free Productions, this is the kind of real-world debugging puzzle we love solving. The smallest environment difference can expose the biggest blind spots in your application architecture.

Is it network-related? Configuration-based? Something lurking in mobile request handling?

👇 Drop your answer in the comments!

We’ll reveal the correct answer tomorrow — and explain exactly how we’d diagnose and fix it.

text:
A while back, I shared how we slashed export times in Recso by dynamically calculating CRF and using intermediate JPEG compositions to speed up Remotion. But recently, a new, much smaller bottleneck started bothering me.
You know that agonizing pause between clicking the "Export" button and the progress bar actually moving? Yeah, that. It was only a few seconds, but when you're testing constantly, every second feels like a thorn.
I decided to profile exactly what was happening before the first frame even rendered. It turned out to be a classic case of sequential execution and unnecessary overhead.
Here is how I cleaned up the compile step and shaved off the wait time:
- Skipping selectComposition() Previously, the app launched a full Puppeteer browser, loaded the bundle, and evaluated React just to read composition metadata (like width and height) that we are now calculating using native services and some maths. 
The Fix: Bypassed it entirely. We now build the composition object directly from our pre-computed values. Savings: ~2-5s on every single export.

- Parallel Execution Chrome resolution and video probing were running sequentially (await chrome, then await probe). 
The Fix: Wrapped them in a Promise.all() so they run concurrently. Savings: ~200-800ms.

- Smarter Cross-Drive Linking (linkVideoToBundle) If a hard link failed across different drives, the fallback was a brutally slow full file copy of a multi-GB video. 
The Fix: I added an intermediate fallback, if the hard link fails, try a symlink (which is instant across drives) before finally resorting to a full copy. 
Savings: ~1-10s when the source video is on a different drive.

- Pre-warming Chrome The first export always had to resolve and download Chrome on demand, causing a noticeable cold-start delay. The Fix: Called preWarm() at app startup so the Chrome path is cached long before the user ever hits the export button. Savings: ~50-200ms in production (and minutes on the first dev run).

The Result: Eliminating ~3 to 15 seconds of pure "dead time" before the actual export even starts. It’s a relatively small optimization compared to changing video encoders, but the app feels infinitely snappier and more responsive now.

Sometimes the biggest UX upgrades are just about getting out of the user's way faster. 

What is the most satisfying "small" performance bottleneck you’ve squashed recently?

text:
Today’s focus: polish, reliability, and fewer papercuts 🦝✨

Spent the day working on the WRDLNKDN web stack and honestly this is the kind of work I love. Not flashy, but it makes everything feel better to use and easier to maintain.

UI and theming wins

Finished migrating the signup flow off scattered CSS and into MUI sx and theme so styling lives in one place and stays consistent.
Swapped FontAwesome for simple-icons with MUI SvgIcon so brand icons are consistent and we dropped a dependency.
Fixed top nav links that were not navigating by adding a theme level LinkBehavior for React Router and switching nav items to proper MUI Links with href.
Tweaked the Feed Post and Link buttons so they actually focus the composer and have clearer, calmer hover states.

Local Supabase sanity restored

- Tracked down a recurring Supabase reset issue where things ended in a 502 after “Restarting containers”.
- Migrations and seeds were fine, the failure was the CLI post restart health check.
- Switched the default reset to DB only with SUPABASE_DB_ONLY=true to avoid container restarts and the 502.
- Documented the behavior and workaround so future devs do not have to rediscover it the hard way.
- Bonus small win: fixed a missing export in the signup theme that was breaking the Vite build.

You can check out the live progress here and see the chaos slowly turning into something useful:

👉 https://lnkd.in/ehi2RxSi

Huge shoutout to James Hood 🛡️ 🧬 🤖 🐈‍⬛ and 👑 April Drake, MBA. Absolute joy to work with, thoughtful, collaborative, and always finding sharp edges before users do. If you are a developer who likes building things that actually help people and enjoys working with good humans, you should come check this project out 🦝👏

If you are wiring MUI with React Router or wrestling Supabase CLI reset issues, happy to compare notes.

text:
What Building Small React Projects Is Teaching Me
I used to spend hours watching tutorials. It felt productive. Notes taken. Concepts understood. But nothing really stuck until I started building small React projects on my own.
There’s a big difference between following along and figuring things out yourself.
When you build:
 • You face real bugs
 • You make structural decisions
 • You understand state flow deeply
 • You connect concepts instead of memorizing them
Tutorials introduce ideas. Projects force understanding.
Small projects have been powerful because they remove pressure. I’m not trying to build the next big app. I’m just solving one small problem at a time. And that consistency compounds fast.
Learning by doing is slower at first. But it builds confidence that watching videos never can.

text:
The repo is live. You can run it right now. 

We've talked about what we're building. Now here's something you can actually try today. Nangila-SPICE: an open-build transient circuit simulator. This is a public snapshot, a correctness-first baseline, not the finished product.

This isn't a demo. It's a real, reproducible correctness baseline you can run against ngspice on your own machine. 

Four commands: 
pip install . 
brew install ngspice 
cargo build -p nangila-node --bin nangila-node 
nangila correctness-report 

That last command runs 10 mandatory benchmark circuits, from a simple RC filter all the way up to auto-synthesized ISCAS logic, and validates Nangila's waveforms against ngspice. All 10/10 pass. Want the full suite? Add --include-extended and all 13/13 pass too.

What "open build" means:
We ship snapshots as we build. This one captures where we are right now: the Rust solver works, the correctness harness is real, and the GPU and partitioned paths are honestly marked as in-progress in the README.

Every claim we've made is reproducible on your machine.

If you're a chip designer, EDA researcher, or just curious what a Rust-based SPICE solver looks like, star the repo, run the benchmarks, and tell us what breaks.

Github → https://lnkd.in/eFmTi4kU

text:
🐳 I thought Docker was simple… until my React hot reload stopped working.
My app was running inside Docker.
But every time I changed the code:
❌ No “Compiling…”
❌ No browser refresh
❌ And worst of all — react-scripts: not found
That’s when I stopped guessing and started debugging properly.
Here’s what I learned:
🔹 How Docker Compose actually works (services, build, image, ports, volumes)
🔹 Why bind mounts override image layers
🔹 The real difference between build-time and run-time
🔹 Why dev and production Docker setups must be different
🔹 How file watching works in Linux containers
🔹 Why polling fixes hot reload issues
The biggest realization?
Docker wasn’t broken.
My mental model was.
Documented the full debugging journey on Hashnode 👇
link - https://lnkd.in/egXC7EB2

text:
"It's not you, it's me 💔" — 𝐂𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐝𝐞, right before it quiet-quit my codebase.

I tried Claude on Figma's Make and it surprised me with the jaw-dropping UI! In a few hours, I had an entire beautifully clean React-based flow. Seriously next-level.

But that's exactly where the magic stops.
Once I started requesting changes for a consistent design language, the magic turned into dark magic. It was literally doing the opposite: forking every single view, admitting its fault, and repeating the cycle! After 60 iterations that went absolutely nowhere, I decided to stop trying to tweak another markdown file or argue with the model.

So, time for something more technical:

1. Created a Vite plugin that instantly breaks the build/hot-reload if it generates anything that doesn't adhere to the design semantics, throwing a clear error saying why it failed and what needs to be done to bypass it. It is no longer an intent or a question; it is a must, or otherwise, it will be handing back a broken project.

2. In a totally different context but similar timing, I took the exact same approach on a native Swift project with a Swift plugin.

Logically, if it breaks the policy, it breaks the project. Simple, right?

It kept going for 25 minutes and I saw no failures. Yaay! After another 30 minutes or so, I saw the celebration for the things it delivered.

Except... it didn't deliver anything.

It basically stayed in a random loop of reading, thinking, and maybe changing a color value only to revert it back. Almost every round after, in every "thinking" block, I saw it exploring ways to get around the failure instead of actually just following the instructions.

One thing was clear: The tighter I made the rules, the less it actually touched the code. (It did excel at one thing though: flawlessly respecting my // DO NOT TOUCH THIS FILE comment, and I got it to document its behavior).

On Xcode, two rounds went bust with Claude in a very similar fashion. I switched to Codex, and BOOM! It worked! I got it running for an hour or so, and it produced working, testable code with the least amount of boilerplate, making it much easier for me to improve further.

This example just highlights the importance of having much more control over the inference loop. Otherwise, your best shot is just a guess.

The hard learnings:

 - Written instructions are easy to treat as suggestions or even dismiss entirely. Model bias can actively go against your instructions if you don't have tools to steer it. 

- There are other scenarios where off-the-shelf LLMs excel, but don't waste your time expecting them to act like disciplined systems engineers.

- Setting your CI as the first defense gate for AI-generated code is going to be as important as having the code in the first place.

Has anyone else tried to back an AI into a corner just to watch it stop working—or maybe actually excel?

text:
𝐖𝐡𝐲 𝐒𝐜𝐚𝐥𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐌𝐚𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐅𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐝 𝐃𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐥𝐨𝐩𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭? 🏗️
Creating a UI that "looks good" is only half the battle. The real challenge and where I find the most excitement is building a frontend that is maintainable, scalable, and performance-driven.
Reflecting on my journey from 𝐂𝐨𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠𝐬 𝐅𝐢𝐫𝐬𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐃𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐥𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐇𝐮𝐛 𝐂𝐨𝐫𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧, I’ve focused on three pillars to ensure high-quality web applications:
1️⃣ 𝐂𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐧𝐭-𝐃𝐫𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐧 𝐀𝐫𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞: Using React to build reusable, modular UI components that save development time and ensure design consistency. 
2️⃣ 𝐒𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐞 𝐌𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭: Leveraging Redux for a predictable data flow, especially when dealing with complex user interactions and large datasets. 3️⃣ 𝐂𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐧 𝐈𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧: Implementing robust REST API connections with a focus on handling "Loading," "Error," and "Empty" states to provide a seamless user experience.
Whether it's styling with 𝐓𝐚𝐢𝐥𝐰𝐢𝐧𝐝 𝐂𝐒𝐒 for a mobile-first approach or ensuring type safety with 𝐓𝐲𝐩𝐞𝐒𝐜𝐫𝐢𝐩𝐭, my goal is always the same: Write code that is as clean as the UI it powers.
I'm curious what is your "must-have" library in your React stack right now? Let's talk tech in the comments! 👇

text:
I tried to build a simple landing page....
It turned into a space simulation.

Introducing ZENTR
A motion first digital design experiment that slightly got out of hand.

Live site: https://lnkd.in/eBKHP_uw
GitHub: https://lnkd.in/eEwx45zq

What started as
“Let me just test a layout idea”
Ended as:
• A fully animated starfield background
• Shooting stars flying across the screen
• Glassmorphism UI panels floating like premium SaaS pricing pages
• 5 routed pages, all lazy loaded
• A custom scroll reveal hook
• Performance tweaks that made my laptop question my intentions

Stack used:
• React 19
• Vite 7
• Tailwind CSS 4
• React Router 7
• Canvas 2D
• CSS animations
• IntersectionObserver

Things I obsessed over more than I should have:
• Replacing WebGL with Canvas 2D to drop GPU usage from “NASA simulator” to “calm productivity”
• Using React.memo so components do not re-render like they are excited puppies
• will-change hints so animations behave like professionals
• display swap so fonts do not ghost users

There are:
• 6 South African themed concept projects
• 4 expandable case studies with animated stat counters
• A journal layout
• A connect page with Johannesburg and Cape Town offices
• Enough motion to make you slightly uncomfortable in a good way

Every component is modular.
Every section has intention.
Every animation has a reason.
Well… almost every animation.

ZENTR is not a client project.
It is a playground for structure, rhythm, and digital systems.

Sometimes you build to get paid.
Sometimes you build to get sharper.
This one was the second.

Check NexlinksolutionsZA out at https://lnkd.in/dsPSqmfx or alternatively contact us at nexlinksolutionsza@gmail.com

Feedback is welcome.
Roasts are also welcome.
Mercy is optional.

text:
Over the past few days, I’ve been building a mini learning platform from scratch using React + Tailwind.
What started as a simple UI project has now evolved into a structured, scalable system.
Here’s what I’ve implemented so far:
🔐 Authentication system with protected routes
 📚 Course management with save/remove functionality
 📊 Progress tracking per course
 👤 Editable user profile with avatar upload
 💾 Persistent data using localStorage
 🧠 Safe state architecture (no duplicate routes, no render crashes)
 🛡️ Route protection using custom ProtectedRoute logic
I debugged real-world issues like:
 – Duplicate route conflicts
 – Context wrapping errors
 – Undefined component crashes
 – State synchronization across pages
This project helped me move from just “building components” to thinking in terms of:
• System architecture
 • State management
 • Data persistence
 • User flow design
 • Scalable routing structure
And this is just version 1.
Next steps:
 – Connect to Firebase backend

text:
🔥 Boost React Native Performance - (Part 1)

At IFS Guide, our React Native app had 50+ screens with many Native Modules/Packages.
Builds on Android and iOS often took minutes and sometimes failed at the end, forcing us to rebuild.

After optimizing, we cut build times by 60–70%. That’s why I believe improving the build phase is one of the most important things React Native developers should care about.


👉 Build only one ABI
If you’re targeting just one CPU architecture, limit your build to that ABI. It saves time and reduces unnecessary complexity.

👉 Enable Configuration Caching
Gradle can reuse previous results instead of re-running everything from scratch. This cuts build times significantly.

👉 Use a Maven Mirror
Point dependencies to a local or closer mirror. Faster downloads = faster builds.

👉 Compiler Cache (Android)
Enable caching for Android compilers so unchanged code doesn’t get recompiled each time.

👉 Compiler Cache (iOS)
Same applies for iOS, cache your compiler outputs and enjoy shorter iteration cycles.

🤝 Got a React Native project? Let’s talk! DM or email me anytime.

♻️ Repost to help others learn.

text:
My last 2 days with React Native 😵‍💫

Sprint started Monday.

Task was literally just change colors for 2 apps.
Sounds like a joke, right? I finished that part in a few hours.

Then came:
“Can you also update splash screen, app icon, adaptive icon?”

Cool.
I became a Figma designer for ~3 hours (I am not one).

Waited half a day because staging env wasn’t ready. Finally got it.
Configured multi env. Tried to build.
Neither Gradle nor Xcode wanted peace.

Next day, somehow managed to build 4 apps (2 apps × iOS & Android).
Sent for QA.
QA failed 🙂
Fixed bugs. Went home thinking tomorrow is just distribution.

Saturday: builds started failing again.
RN version was ancient (0.72.x). Every build broke a different package.
 
Tried upgrading RN (bad idea, this close to submission).
Upgraded RN, React, migrated Java → Kotlin, Obj-C/C++ → Swift.
Felt smart for 10 minutes.
Reality: 80+ packages needed updates.
 
Rolled back. Patched random stuff. Downgraded SDKs. Fixed packages that don’t even exist on npm anymore.
Builds finally worked. Slept.

Sunday morning: App Store said no.
Reason: Hermes generates bitcode. Apple doesn’t want bitcode anymore.
I didn’t even know what bitcode was before today.
Failed upload 17 times.
Burned all my AI tokens.
Finally figured out how to strip bitcode from Hermes and uploaded.
🎉

Immediately after upload:
staging API URL changed.

So yeah… do it all again.

Moral of the story:
1) “Small task” doesn’t mean small effort
2) Technical debt always shows up at the worst time
3) Apple doesn’t care about your sprint
4) Upgrade your framework before it’s too late

Anyway, adding “survived React Native release week” to my skills.
Good night 😴

text:
Do I even know React Native?

This past year I’ve been working with Swift, but there came a need to update a RN app from 0.73.2 to 0.76.9. It didn’t seem like a big deal, not even a huge gap. But in that time, it feels like the framework’s developers COMPLETELY rewrote everything 😂

I spent a whole day just trying to get the build to run somehow, but it just wouldn’t come to life. After many failed attempts, I accepted that my code just wasn’t compatible with the new architecture.

But I didn’t give up and manually, file by file, moved everything to a new project with the necessary changes.

I actually enjoyed it — I realized how many solutions I had implemented that now just work out of the box. And as a bonus — my computer doesn’t take off like a rocket when I work on the project, and I was honestly surprised by the build speed.

That doesn’t mean I didn’t get mad at the React Native devs — like, could they really not do it properly from the start? But then I thought — who am I — and just expressed my sincere gratitude, because the new approach is so much better!

P.S. The next project I’ll be writing — with Swift.

text:
Debug Build Worked, Release APK Failed – Issue Finally Resolved.
For the past 5 days, I was stuck on a confusing issue in my React Native expo Android app.
The API was working perfectly in the development (debug) build, but it kept failing in the release APK. After a lot of debugging, I finally found the root cause:
One API was using HTTPS, while the other was using HTTP.
Android release builds block HTTP (cleartext) requests by default, even though they work in debug mode. Once I switched all APIs to HTTPS, the issue was resolved immediately.
Lesson learned:
 If something works in debug but fails in release, always check HTTP vs HTTPS first.
Special thanks to everyone on LinkedIn and Stack Overflow who took the time to reply to my post and share their insights. Your help and guidance truly made a difference.
Hope this helps someone else avoid the same frustration!

text:
Hello!
Recently, I ran into an error while building a newly initialized React Native project on Windows. The only feedback I got was a message saying that cmake.exe failed to run, along with a stack trace from native code.
Curious about what was going wrong, I decided to try something simple: I find the cmake.exe file and double-clicked it to see what would happen. As soon as I did, an error message showed up, so it was clear that something was wrong with the installed CMake.
That gave me an idea: what if I installed CMake manually and linked it to my React Native project?
So, I downloaded and installed CMake separately from the official site, then configured my environment to use this version instead of the one installed with the Android SDK. This fixed the build error!
To help others avoid the same issue, I wrote a quick article about it on Medium.

Today, while creating a React Native app using React Native CLI, I encountered an issue during the 𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐝𝐥𝐞 𝐛𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐝 process. I got the dreaded "𝘤𝘰𝘶𝘭𝘥 𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘮𝘰𝘷𝘦 𝘵𝘰 𝘵𝘦𝘮𝘱𝘰𝘳𝘢𝘳𝘺 𝘸𝘰𝘳𝘬𝘴𝘱𝘢𝘤𝘦" error, resulting in a failed build. After some digging, I discovered the culprit: the Gradle distribution bundled with the latest version of React Native CLI (v8.6).

𝐇𝐞𝐫𝐞'𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐚𝐫𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐝: If you're facing similar issues, it's likely because the Gradle distribution (v8.6) doesn't play nice with certain configurations at this moment. To resolve this, you need to downgrade your Gradle version to either 8.3 or 8.4.

This little hiccup reminds us of the importance of staying vigilant with our development environments. If you're encountering issues like this, it's often worth investigating the versions of the tools you're using.

Happy coding, and may your builds be swift and error-free! 🚀

text:
The weirdest React Native bug I’ve ever fixed 🐛🔍

Last week, I faced a bug that made me question everything I knew about React Native.

✅ The problem: The app crashed only on certain Android devices when a specific screen opened. No errors in the console, nothing in Crashlytics.

🛠 My approach:
- Logged EVERYTHING (yes, even variables I didn’t think mattered).
- Tried reproducing on multiple emulators & real devices.
- Narrowed it down to a 3rd party library call that failed silently.

💡 The fix: Updating the library fixed the crash — but only after clearing the build cache (cd android && ./gradlew clean). Without that, the old buggy code kept creeping back in.

Lesson learned:
Sometimes, the issue isn’t your code — it’s the environment. Clearing caches and rebuilding from scratch can save you hours of frustration.

Have you faced a bug so weird you thought your laptop was haunted? 👻

text:
🚀 The 16KB Page Size Upgrade: My React Native Journey to v0.82.1

I recently undertook a significant upgrade for a legacy React Native app (from v0.74.1 to v0.82.1) to introduce 16KB memory page size support. This is crucial for modern Android compatibility and memory optimization.

My journey hit two major roadblocks that taught me vital lessons in RN native development:

🧱 The First Block: Native Build Chaos
My initial attempt involved upgrading to RN 0.79 and trying to modify the existing code in the android folder. This resulted in endless build failures and crashes, even with the help of AI tools.

💡 Lesson 1: Replace, Don't Modify. The breakthrough came when I replaced the entire android folder with a clean copy from a fresh RN 0.79 project. This instantly resolved all configuration conflicts and legacy build issues.

🔍 The Second Block: The Hidden Dependency
After successfully compiling, my 16KB compatibility test still failed. My project was updated, but the support was missing.

💡 Lesson 2: Native Libraries are the Key. The problem wasn't React Native itself, but third-party dependencies. They had only introduced 16KB support in their latest versions. My RN 0.79-compatible versions were too old. The solution was to jump directly to the latest stable React Native (v0.82.1) and upgrade every single dependency to its absolute latest version to ensure all their underlying .so native files were correctly compiled.

✅ The Final Takeaway
I learned that upgrading the main framework is only the first step. For features relying on low-level native support like 16KB page size, you must:

Directly replace the android folder on major version jumps.

Focus on the latest version of all native dependencies—that's where the required .so file support lives.

Go to the latest RN version that supports the feature to minimize dependency conflicts.

This demanding project gave me an invaluable, granular understanding of my app's native codebase and Importance of Unnoticed part of the android folder files and why they are very important and not that important. 

Victory achieved! 🎉

text:
Your React Native Build Just Failed. Here's Why (And It Has Nothing To Do With Your Code)

You wrote perfect code. 
Dependencies are updated. 
Tests pass. 

Then Windows hits you with:
"ninja: error: Filename longer than 260 characters"

Welcome to the most frustrating bug that isn't actually a bug.


𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺:

Windows has a 260-character path limit from 1983. 

Your React Native project structure? Easily 300+ characters deep in node_modules

Your Mac teammates have zero issues.

CI/CD is green. 

But your local Windows build? Dead.


𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗞𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘀 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗶𝘁𝘆:

→ Looks like a dependency issue (it's not)
→ Clearing cache doesn't help
→ Reinstalling node_modules wastes 30 minutes
→ Google gives you 47 different "solutions" that don't work
→ You're blocked for hours on what should be a 5-minute fix


𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗖𝘂𝗹𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘀:

Deep node_modules nesting 

+

Auto-generated CMake paths 

+ 

Packages with long names (react-native-keyboard-controller) 

+ 

Your project path (D:\SuperAwesomeApps\NotVeryAwesomeApps\pretty-awesome-app...)
= Path length explosion


𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗶𝘅 (𝟱 𝗠𝗶𝗻𝘂𝘁𝗲𝘀):

Enable Windows long path support:

→ Registry: Set LongPathsEnabled to 1
→ Restart your machine
→ Clean build and rebuild

Alternative if you can't modify registry:

➡️ Move project to C:\dev\ instead of C:\Users\YourName\Documents...


𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀:

Every Windows developer hits this eventually. 

It costs teams hours of debugging time because the error message is cryptic and the solution isn't obvious.

One registry change. 

Five minutes. Problem solved forever.


Have you hit this error? Drop your horror story below.

text:
CI/CD for React Native: From 6 Hours to 30 Minutes

Ever spent an entire day wrestling with React Native builds? You’re not alone.
Cross-platform development = double the complexity. iOS works fine, Android crashes. Manual builds and versioning nightmares eat up precious dev time.

𝟲 𝗚𝗮𝗺𝗲-𝗖𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗣𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗲𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗦𝗮𝘃𝗲𝗱 𝗢𝘂𝗿 𝗦𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆:

𝟭. 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗩𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Stop the version mismatch chaos. One script updates package.json, Android Gradle, and iOS Info.plist simultaneously.

𝟮. 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝗦𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗲

 • Tests first, builds second (no failed builds wasting runner time)
 • Platform-specific runners: Ubuntu for Android, macOS for iOS
 • Parallel processing = faster feedback

𝟯. 𝗘𝗻𝘃𝗶𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗗𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗥𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁
.env files for dev/staging/prod with corresponding build scripts. No more “it works on my machine” moments.

𝟰. 𝗣𝗿𝗲-𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗶𝘁 𝗚𝘂𝗮𝗿𝗱𝘀
Husky + lint-staged catch issues before they hit the pipeline. Your CI will thank you.

𝟱. 𝗙𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗲 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
Automated TestFlight and Play Store uploads. Deploy while you sleep.
In one of my earlier posts, I talked about using Fastlane to automate deployments in React Native projects. 

𝟲. 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺 𝗡𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀
Slack alerts keep everyone in the loop instantly.

Results:
 6 hours → 30 minutes build time ⏰
 Fewer bugs reaching testers
 Smoother onboarding & faster releases

Start Small:
Version automation → CI testing → Environment setup → Full pipeline
Good CI/CD gives your team confidence to ship faster and sleep better.

What is your biggest React Native deployment challenge..? Lets discuss it in the comments..!
I’ll share code solutions for any pain points you mention, plus detailed examples for these 6 key deployment areas if you are interested or need clarification.

text:
Tackling a complex technical challenge in mobile app development today while working on a voice feature for my app.
The goal was to enable users to record and upload audio entries to Firebase Storage. We integrated expo-av for recording and implemented the upload logic.
Initial hurdle: A persistent FirebaseError: storage/unknown when trying to upload the audio Blob, despite verifying configurations and rules, simplifying uploads, and testing various troubleshooting steps.
Suspecting environment issues (potentially Expo Go limitations), we shifted focus to building a custom Development Build using EAS CLI to test in a more controlled native environment on an Android emulator.
New, unexpected obstacle: The successfully installed Development Build consistently failed to connect to the Metro dev server and download the code bundle, throwing a java.io.IOException: Failed to download remote update. We spent considerable time troubleshooting this loading error – rebuilding, clearing caches, checking configurations – but it remained unresolved by the end of the session.
It was a tough debugging session, highlighting the complexities of mobile development environments and cloud storage integration. Taking a break now, but determined to crack these issues.
Has anyone else encountered these specific storage/unknown errors with Firebase Storage uploads from Expo/React Native, or the Failed to download remote update error with EAS Development Builds on Android emulators? Would appreciate any insights!

text:
“Sometimes I open old code I wrote… and wonder who let me near a keyboard.”
But that’s the best part.
Because now when I look back,
 I don’t see just bugs and bad logic —
 I see growth.
Every awkward component, every failed API call, every broken screen…
 was a step toward the developer I’m becoming today.
React Native didn’t just teach me how to build apps.
 It taught me how to keep showing up, even when the docs didn’t make sense and nothing was working.
If your code looks perfect today, you’re not pushing hard enough.
 If it makes you cringe after 6 months — that’s progress.
💬 Who else has looked at their old code and thought: “Yeah… never again.”

text:
We finally started working on the actual app for Campus Eats!

This is day 16 of our 30 days product building challenge

I’ve never built a native app before, so this is all very new to me — and honestly, a bit scary. I'm using React Native with Expo, and things got tricky fast, especially since we’re using it inside a monorepo. That alone made setup a lot more complicated than I expected.

I spent the first 5 hours just trying to get the project to run. Nothing worked at first. I had to dig through docs, read articles, and try a bunch of things before finally getting it running in development. That felt like a small victory.
But it didn’t stop there.

I needed to create a development build to test on my phone. Each build took 15–20 minutes… and kept failing. 😩

Tried again. Failed. Fixed something. Failed again.

After 7 or 8 tries and 3 hours, it finally worked. That moment felt sooo good. 😮‍💨

Once that was sorted, I focused on setting up the project structure, installing libraries, and configuring the tools.
Then finally, I got to the fun part — building screens:
- Login screen (took around 2 hours)
- OTP verification
- Onboarding
- Bottom tab navigation

It feels amazing to see the first screens come to life.

It was definitely one of those tough but rewarding days. I struggled a lot, but I also learned a lot — stuff that will definitely help as I go forward.

Gaurav raj

text:
Why You Might Want to Pause Upgrading to React Native 0.82
After working with React Native 0.82 on task given to me by 
hashtag
#hng, I ran into multiple issues , particularly with native modules like react‑native‑nitro‑modules and react‑native‑nitro‑sqlite.

Example: React native failed to assembleRelease with the error code
 (Execution failed for task ':react-native-nitro-modules:configureCMakeRelWithDebInfo[arm64-v8a]'.
> A build operation failed.)

 Example2 : The nitro-sqlite module failed at build-time with “Project with path ‘:react-native-nitro-modules’ could not be found” when running react-native run-android.
3 I also faced C++/NDK-toolchain errors when building release variants because native modules weren’t aligned with certain NDK versions.

While RN 0.82 brings exciting features, the ecosystem of native addons still needs time to catch up. If you depend heavily on those native modules, you may hit roadblocks.

My advice: Unless you have capacity to troubleshoot and patch native module integrations (or wait for updates), consider sticking with the previous stable version (e.g., RN 0.81 or whatever your safe version is).

If you must upgrade:
Audit all native modules you use for compatibility with RN 0.82
Test both Debug and Release builds thoroughly (including NDK/C++ compilation)

Be prepared for manual fixes in android/settings.gradle, build.gradle, and NDK version mismatches.

RN 0.82 is promising—but not yet turnkey if you rely on native-heavy libraries. Plan accordingly, test thoroughly, and avoid surprises in production.

Facing similar problem or contribution ?? please share your thoughts on the comment section.

text:
From Expo to React Native CLI: Lessons from the Transition + Solving Gradle Errors
Migrating Expo projects to a React Native CLI setup. While Expo is amazing for rapid development, sometimes you need that deeper native control (especially for custom native modules or SDK integrations). And that's where the real learning began!
Here’s a quick breakdown of what I tackled:
Why I moved to CLI:
Needed access to native code & Gradle build configs
Wanted to integrate native modules not supported by Expo Go
Needed to generate a custom release APK manually
What went wrong:
Endless Gradle build errors like checkReleaseAarMetadata, mismatched SDK versions, missing keystore, and more.
Errors like Execution failed for task ':app:checkReleaseAarMetadata' were new territory.
Solved by syncing dependencies, updating minSdkVersion, and cleaning the build cache.
What helped:
Manually configuring android/build.gradle and android/app/build.gradle
Double-checking react-native.config.js and native modules
Using ./gradlew clean and re-running builds
Migrating from Expo to CLI isn't just about changing commands. It’s about understanding how the native layers work under the hood. It’s painful at first, but SO rewarding once the build finally succeeds and you hold that .apk file in our hands.

text:
🚀 Fixing Gradle Version Mismatch & Autolinking Issues in React Native (v0.74.5) 🚀

Hey Devs! 🌟

I recently encountered an issue while working on a React Native project where I ran into a Gradle version mismatch and an autolinking error. It was a bit of a journey, but I wanted to share the steps I took to resolve it, hoping it might help others facing the same problem.

The Issue:
I got the following error:

"Failed to apply plugin 'com.android.internal.version-check'.
Minimum supported Gradle version is 8.7. Current version is 8.6.
Along with some autolinking issues related to the React Native version I was using."

🛠️ Solution: Step-by-Step Fix
1-Update Gradle Distribution:
 -Edited the gradle-wrapper.properties file to update the distributionUrl: properties
distributionUrl=https://lnkd.in/dAaUkReQ

2-Modify "app/build.gradle":
 -Added autolinkLibrariesWithApp() within the react block.
 -Removed old autolinking-related lines:
apply from: file("../../node_modules/@react-native-community/cli-platform-android/native_modules.gradle")
applyNativeModulesAppBuildGradle(project)

3-Update "settings.gradle":
 -Removed the outdated autolinking line.
 -Added new configuration above rootProject.name:
pluginManagement { includeBuild("../node_modules/@react-native/gradle-plugin") }
plugins { id("com.facebook.react.settings") }
extensions.configure(com.facebook.react.ReactSettingsExtension) { ex -> ex.autolinkLibrariesFromCommand() }

4-Clean and Rebuild:
 -Deleted .build, .gradle folders from android.
 -Removed node_modules and yarn.lock.
 -Rebuilt the project using:
 yarn android

After following these steps, my project built successfully, and the issues were resolved! 🙌

Tech Stack:
 -React Native Version: 0.74.5
 -Gradle Version: 8.6

Issue URL: https://lnkd.in/dVpSBcqw

If you're struggling with similar issues, I hope these steps can guide you through the fix. Feel free to connect with me if you have any questions or need further assistance!

text:
I recently decided to dive into Android development using React Native, and like most first steps, it was a mix of excitement and confusion. No guidance, no mentorship — just me trying to figure things out one step at a time.

The first hurdle was setting up the development environment. I followed the official React Native documentation and started by configuring environment variables, installing Node.js, and making sure everything was ready to run React Native commands.

Then I used Expo to initialize the project, since it's what the docs recommend for a smoother setup. It really helped simplify some of the early complexities, especially when just starting out.

Next came installing Android Studio. This part took a bit of patience. I had to make sure the Java Development Kit (JDK) and Android SDK were properly installed, and that I selected the correct options in the Android Studio setup. Once that was done, I set up an Android Virtual Device (AVD) — basically, an emulator — so I could test the app right from my PC. I could’ve used USB debugging with my physical phone too, but using the emulator felt more comfortable and consistent for now.

Even with all the setup complete, there were smaller issues along the way — path errors, emulator problems, and a few failed builds. But the official documentation really helped, and slowly I started to understand how things fit together.

React Native uses TypeScript by default with Expo, so I had to learn the basics of .tsx files and how type annotations work. Wasn’t too hard, just new. After a few tweaks and a lot of retries, I finally saw the words "Hello World" show up on the screen — and it felt surprisingly rewarding.

This was just a simple app, but getting everything up and running on my own taught me more than any tutorial could. I’m planning to build more projects in React Native and continue learning as I go.

If you’re thinking of starting with mobile development, it’s definitely doable — just take it one step at a time, and don’t skip the docs they help a lot.

text:
My company wanted me to go native on a React Native app. I’ve written C++ and Kotlin before, but layering C++ ➝ Kotlin ➝ React Native? That was uncharted territory.

I did my research, wrote what I could, then ran the build. And guess what? It failed.
All because of Gradle.

I had to work directly inside the android/ folder—no JavaScript in sight. Installed NDK side-by-side version 26 just to run gradlew locally. The worst part? It takes 51 minutes to complete, only to throw an error at the end. That’s 51 minutes per trial run.

Things came to a complete stop when I ran into JSI (JavaScript Interface) and Hermes issues. That meant it was time to do the actual bridging. After endless back-and-forth and countless attempts with ChatGPT 😂, I finally paused and decided to deep dive on my own.

Read everything I could find, connected the dots—and it hit me:
My problem was that I hadn’t properly synced with the C++ code I was building. That was the blocker all along.

Moral of the story:
You might ask, “Why go native?”
Because development isn’t just about writing code—it’s about scalability. And for the long-term vision I had, native was the best path.

I don’t usually post about bugs, but this one was worth sharing.

Like, connect, and share.
See you next time!
