text:
📘 React Native
Q: Explain the React Native lifecycle.

The React Native component lifecycle consists of several phases: Mounting, Updating, Unmounting, and Error Handling.

✨ Mounting: The component is created and added to the DOM - perfect for initializing data or making API calls.

✨ Updating: Props or state changes? The component updates seamlessly to reflect those changes.

✨ Unmounting: When it's time to remove the component from the DOM, cleanup tasks ensure smooth performance.

✨ Error Handling: Safeguard your app by catching errors, preventing crashes, and delivering a stable user experience.

text:
How I use Claude (Part 1.)

A while ago I was put in charge of a React Native project. The application was old and every attempt to build it failed. Xcode could not be outsmarted by moving and replacing libraries on the file system. Since this mobile application is one of the critical pieces of the suite this company runs its business on, something had to be done.

The only option forward was to save the business logic, salvage as much as possible from the front end, and put the whole application on a whole new stack. A new project with React Native and Expo was generated and the porting began.

In the initial stages, Grok and Claude on the web was used. Code was pasted into the browser where it was analyzed and output generated. This was quite productive. Later, when Claude Code was licensed, our productivity reached the next level. Every software shop is different, however, let me share some highlights of how Claude was harnessed for our purposes.

Changed APIs

When a library does not work with the current platform, such as React 19, the code must be updated to conform to the new API, or if the library does not support the new platform at all, it needs to be replaced. Claude performed the task well, whether it was just a simple update to the calls or it was a rewrite. Examples of the latter is GraphQL client or Google Maps API, both of which have undergone so many changes that they simply did not work any more.

Replacement libraries

For the libraries that are no longer maintained, Claude recommended replacements. The recommendations proved adequate in most situations. Claude even detected components already in the codebase, but not used in the older regions of the code!

Finding the code

Because of naming conventions, it is not always easy to find the code that generates a particular area of the UI. Pasting in a screenshot of the UI and letting Claude find the source saves time. Claude matches the patterns of the UI snippet and outputs the file name and line number that built it. In rare cases, it guesses and provides multiple locations.

text:
My First Project : AnimeTrackerMobile
Thrilled to share the completion of my cross-platform mobile application, built to simplify anime tracking and discovery. This project was a powerful reminder that sometimes the biggest coding challenges are infrastructural, not just algorithmic!

What I Built : 
A fully functional, cross-platform Anime Tracker Mobile Application. The app allows users to browse, search, and manage watch lists across structured navigation (Home, My List, Stats). The goal was a clean, fast, and scalable mobile-first user interface.
Tools & Technologies Used : 
 Primary Framework: React Native (Cross-platform mobile development)
 Development Platform: Expo (Fast development, debugging, and build process)
 Navigation: Expo Router (File-system based routing and tab navigation)
 Environment: Visual Studio Code, Node.js, and PowerShell.
Key Outcomes & Learnings:
 Cross-Platform Efficiency: Successfully implemented a single codebase running seamlessly on both iOS and Android.
 Robust Navigation: Mastered Expo Router to create an intuitive and structured Bottom Tab Navigation, ensuring the app is scalable.
 Debugging Beyond Code (The HEVC Lesson): The single biggest challenge was diagnosing why my video assets failed to process in the development server. The issue wasn't the .mp4 extension, but the underlying HEVC (H.265) video codec.
 Solution: Identified the root cause and successfully converted the file to the web-standard H.264 using VLC Media Player. This experience taught me the critical importance of understanding media architecture and codecs in mobile development.
My Project Journey : 
The development of the core features was efficient, but the debugging phase was intensive, requiring me to troubleshoot environment issues (like clearing the Expo cache and resolving network blocks). Highly recommend the React Native + Expo stack for rapid development. Lot of worries arised . But I learned many new things about creating video and apps (Even though still there are flaws) . Thanks to Launch Ed for giving a good opportunity ☺️

text:
📱 React Native: The Real Cross-Platform Advantage (Beyond the Hype)
Everyone talks about “write once, run anywhere” — but React Native’s real value goes much deeper 👇
🔹 One Codebase, Two Platforms
 Build for iOS and Android using a shared codebase, while still accessing native APIs when needed. Faster delivery, less duplication.
🔹 Near-Native Performance (When Done Right)
 With the new architecture (JSI, Fabric, TurboModules), React Native bridges the performance gap — but only if engineers understand rendering, memory, and threading.
🔹 Faster Time-to-Market
 Hot reload, reusable components, and a rich ecosystem help teams ship features weeks faster compared to separate native teams.
🔹 Web Skills → Mobile Impact
 Strong React.js developers can transition smoothly into mobile development, reducing hiring friction and ramp-up time.
🔹 Not a Silver Bullet
 Complex animations, heavy device integrations, or poor architectural choices can hurt performance.
 👉 Cross-platform success depends on engineering discipline, not just the framework.
💡 Leadership Insight:
 React Native works best when teams treat it as mobile engineering, not “web in a wrapper”. Invest in performance profiling, native understanding, and clean architecture.
💬 Are you using React Native in production? What trade-offs worked (or failed) for you?

text:
It looks like another reason to exist for react native just ended.

Last month I was dealing with a React Native app, we all know it's many promises. 

Cross-platform, easy language, write once, run everywhere, blah blah blah...

The reality is completely different. 

Ultra long build time, bloated packages, node_modules folder is big as a black hole (just like in the meme) and: So many compilation errors. 

Good luck creating even a hello world app, you need to install thousand of npm packages. 

If you try to add some native bindings like a decent camera support for each platform, good luck not going crazy.

And I decided to give it a go to native development after 9 years.

Of course I took help from AI, yes, I have 15 years of experience and AI failed many times. 

But that experience allowed me to review the code and push the AI agent to the right direction.

First I started with Android part. Then replicating the same views and functionality on iOS was easy.

Even the code syntax feels like a breeze. 

No more endless xml file hell on android, no more trying to connect weird boxes in Xcode.

Jetpack Compose and SwiftUI is fun to write. Easy to read and understand.

Result?

A super fast two apps, nice native API, lighting loading times. 

Also building time is 10x times faster. 

No more waiting for 45000 items to compile on Xcode for 10 minutes to build a hello world.

So, it looks like, native is going to be the new king.

text:
Push Notifications in React Native — what nobody warns you about

The first time I implemented push notifications in a React Native app, I thought:
“It’s just FCM + APNs. How hard can it be?”
Turns out… very hard, once the app goes to production.
Some real issues we faced 👇

1️⃣ FCM vs APNs confusion
Android works.
iOS doesn’t.
Same payload, same logic — totally different behavior.
APNs is strict.
One small mistake and notifications just disappear… silently.

2️⃣ Certificates, keys & environments
 • Development vs Production tokens
 • Sandbox vs Live APNs
 • Expired certificates
 • Wrong Firebase configuration
Everything works locally…
 …and breaks after release.

3️⃣ iOS permissions are unforgiving
 • Permission denied once = no prompt again
 • Background modes not enabled = no delivery
 • App killed = no data handling
And iOS tells you nothing about why it failed.

4️⃣ Payload issues
 • Notification vs data messages
 • Foreground vs background behavior
 • iOS needs exact payload structure
 • Android is more forgiving (sometimes too forgiving)
One extra field can break iOS delivery completely.

5️⃣ SHA keys & Firebase setup
Wrong SHA-1 / SHA-256 →
• Notifications not received
 • Login breaks
 • Release behaves differently than debug
This one alone can waste hours.

6️⃣ Testing is misleading
Works on emulator ❌
Works on debug build ❌
Fails on TestFlight / Play Store ❌

Push notifications show their real behavior only after store builds.

What I learned
Push notifications are not a “feature”.
They’re an infrastructure problem.
You need:
 • Platform understanding (iOS & Android)
 • Correct Firebase + APNs setup
 • Clean payload design
 • Real device + release testing
Once it works — it’s stable.
Getting there is where most teams struggle.

I’ll be sharing more real-world React Native learnings like this — things you only learn after shipping and fixing production apps.
If you’re working on mobile apps (or hiring in this space), happy to connect.

text:
Your React Native Build Just Failed. Here's Why (And It Has Nothing To Do With Your Code)

You wrote perfect code. 
Dependencies are updated. 
Tests pass. 

Then Windows hits you with:
"ninja: error: Filename longer than 260 characters"

Welcome to the most frustrating bug that isn't actually a bug.


𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺:

Windows has a 260-character path limit from 1983. 

Your React Native project structure? Easily 300+ characters deep in node_modules

Your Mac teammates have zero issues.

CI/CD is green. 

But your local Windows build? Dead.


𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗞𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘀 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗶𝘁𝘆:

→ Looks like a dependency issue (it's not)
→ Clearing cache doesn't help
→ Reinstalling node_modules wastes 30 minutes
→ Google gives you 47 different "solutions" that don't work
→ You're blocked for hours on what should be a 5-minute fix


𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗖𝘂𝗹𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘀:

Deep node_modules nesting 

+

Auto-generated CMake paths 

+ 

Packages with long names (react-native-keyboard-controller) 

+ 

Your project path (D:\SuperAwesomeApps\NotVeryAwesomeApps\pretty-awesome-app...)
= Path length explosion


𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗶𝘅 (𝟱 𝗠𝗶𝗻𝘂𝘁𝗲𝘀):

Enable Windows long path support:

→ Registry: Set LongPathsEnabled to 1
→ Restart your machine
→ Clean build and rebuild

Alternative if you can't modify registry:

➡️ Move project to C:\dev\ instead of C:\Users\YourName\Documents...


𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀:

Every Windows developer hits this eventually. 

It costs teams hours of debugging time because the error message is cryptic and the solution isn't obvious.

One registry change. 
Five minutes. Problem solved forever.

I wrote a complete breakdown of this issue, why it happens, and three different solutions based on your constraints.

Link in comments. 👇


Have you hit this error? Drop your horror story below.

🚀 Azure DevOps: More Than Just CI/CD

Many teams think Azure DevOps is only about pipelines.

In reality, it’s a complete DevOps ecosystem that connects:

✅ Boards – Agile planning & sprint tracking
✅ Repos – Secure Git-based version control
✅ Pipelines – Automated CI/CD workflows
✅ Test Plans – Integrated quality management
✅ Artifacts – Package & dependency management

When implemented strategically, Azure DevOps helps organizations:
🔹 Reduce deployment failures
🔹 Improve release velocity
🔹 Increase Dev & Ops collaboration
🔹 Ensure end-to-end traceability

DevOps isn’t just automation.
It’s culture + governance + continuous improvement.
If deployments still rely on manual approvals and last-minute fixes…
It’s time to rethink your DevOps maturity.

text:
In this article, Google details “Coruna”: a modular iPhone exploit kit chaining 23 iOS bugs—silent install just by visiting a booby-trapped site. No clicks, no prompts, just gone. 
The wild part is the supply chain: espionage tooling showing up in Ukrainian site analytics scripts… then later bolted onto Chinese crypto/gambling sites to drain wallets (iVerify estimates ~42k devices hit in that for-profit wave alone). 
Also: it checks for Lockdown Mode and walks away. That’s a rare security feature with a measurable “nope” effect. 

Is this our mobile “EternalBlue moment”—state-grade capability leaking into the resale market? 
What’s your baseline: iOS 26 upgrade pressure, Lockdown Mode for high-risk users, or assume browser = blast radius?

text:
If your iOS CI build takes 30 minutes, stop calling it CI.




Everyone 𝘬𝘯𝘰𝘸𝘴 CI/CD.
But very few have it working like a real system.
Most pipelines? Slow. Fragile. Frustrating.

The real bottlenecks nobody talks about:

1. 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗦𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 – 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗶𝗴𝗴𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗸
Not broken pods. Not dependencies.
It’s signing failures that waste days.
Fix it with Fastlane Match. Centralize certs and profiles. Kill the “works on my machine” excuse.

2. 𝗦𝗹𝗼𝘄 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝘀 – 𝗙𝗲𝗲𝗱𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗿
If your pipeline takes 30 minutes, that’s not CI.
Cache dependencies.
Break apps into Swift Packages or frameworks. Rebuild only what changed.

3. 𝗙𝗹𝗮𝗸𝘆 𝗧𝗲𝘀𝘁𝘀 – 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗶𝗹𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗿𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗿
No one respects a pipeline they don’t trust.
Quarantine flaky tests.
Parallelize test runs. Save hours instead of minutes.

Final step: Track it like a product.
• Average build time trend
• Failures by stage

That’s how you find the real leaks.

What’s the most unconventional hack your team has used to speed up iOS CI builds?

text:
I’m happy to share this PurposeSync project I worked on. Check it out here: https://lnkd.in/g9qNPf-4

For the Builders & Recruiters:

Building PurposeSync wasn't just about calling an API; it was a simulation of a modern Senior Engineer's workflow. Here is the architecture that powers the platform:

1. 🧠 AI Engineering & LLM Orchestration
Model: Integrated Google Gemini via a custom Node.js controller.
Prompt Engineering: Designed a "Persona-Locked" system using strict System Instructions to force specific output formats (JSON) and reading levels (Grade-8 English) for accessibility.
State Management: Implemented a "Sliding Window" context strategy to maintain conversational memory without blowing up token costs.

2. 📱 Mobile-First PWA Architecture
Native Feel: Leveraged Service Workers and Web Manifests to make the web app installable on iOS/Android home screens.
Push System: Built a custom VAPID-key encrypted notification system (bypassing expensive 3rd party tools) to re-engage users even when the app is closed.

3. 🏗️ Robust Full-Stack Foundation
Frontend: React.js + Vite for high-performance rendering.
Backend: Node.js + Express REST API with secure environment variable management.
Database: PostgreSQL relational schema modeling complex user-chart-feedback relationships.
Deployment: CI/CD pipelines Render (Frontend & Backend).

4. 🚀 Product Engineering
Feedback Loops: Built integrated feedback components to capture qualitative user data and drive feature iteration.
User Retention: Engineered "Daily Pulse" automated cron jobs to drive daily active usage (DAU).

text:
🚀 Excited to share my latest project on Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipeline using Jenkins, Docker, and GitHub! 🎉

Project Overview: This project outlines the process of CI/CD pipeline using Jenkins, Docker, and GitHub. I installed Jenkins on a Windows system with WSL Ubuntu, configured a Jenkins pipeline to pull a Jenkinsfile from GitHub, and automated stages for cloning the repository, building a Docker image, and deploying the container. DockerHub was used to pull and customize an Nginx web server image, hosting a website at http://localhost:8086. Git integration in Jenkins automated pipeline triggers, and Ngrok handled external access issues by providing a public URL for webhooks. This setup ensures seamless automation from code changes to deployment.

📋 Workflow:
Install Jenkins 🖥️
Set up Jenkins ⚙️
Create a Jenkins Pipeline 🛠️
Pull Nginx Web Server from DockerHub 🐳
Host a Simple Website 🌐
Set up Git integration for Pipeline Automation 🔄

GitHub 🔗
https://lnkd.in/d_4Xuatc

text:
🚀 Why CI/CD is a game-changer in Flutter development?

In Flutter projects, managing frequent builds, testing, and deployment manually can be time-consuming. That’s where CI/CD pipelines come in:

🔹 Continuous Integration (CI):
Every code commit is automatically tested & integrated → fewer bugs, better collaboration.

🔹 Continuous Deployment/Delivery (CD):
Automates release to stores or testing platforms like Firebase App Distribution, making updates faster & reliable.

For Flutter, CI/CD ensures:
✅ Faster builds & testing across Android & iOS
✅ Consistent releases without manual errors
✅ Smooth collaboration in cross-platform teams

In short: CI/CD = Speed + Quality + Automation for Flutter apps.

In this week’s issue of Securing the Backbone, let's break down why critical infrastructure orgs need to start treating their CI/CD systems like the high-value targets they are.

Attackers don’t need to breach your network…

text:
Just shipped 𝐔𝐧𝐦𝐚𝐬𝐤 𝐋𝐚𝐛 to the App Store - an iOS app that extracts skin, hair, teeth, and glasses from a photo using on-device semantic segmentation (no cloud, no uploads). 

Website: https://lnkd.in/g2qgCJT7 

What this app is useful for:
• Quickly split a face photo into separate feature masks (skin/hair/teeth/glasses) for research workflows, dataset creation, visual experiments, and content pipelines. 

CI/CD highlights:
• Built a GitHub 𝐀𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬 CI/CD pipeline to continuously build and sign the app on macOS runners, run CocoaPods, archive, export IPA, and automate release steps. 
• Implemented Slack build notifications (start/status) for faster feedback loops and team visibility. 
• Automated iOS code signing by installing certificates + provisioning profiles from encrypted GitHub Secrets (base64), using a temporary keychain for secure CI signing. 
• Integrated App Store Connect API key handling in CI for upload automation (removes “manual Xcode Accounts” dependency). 
• The pipeline is actively used (35+ workflow runs) with typical runtimes in the ~2–6 minute range per run. 

Reliability / observability:
• Added Sentry for crash analytics and stability monitoring (crash reporting, issue triage, release health), so I can catch regressions quickly and keep the App Store build stable.


Feel free to reach out with questions. 
If you’re a potential user (research/creator), try the App Store build from the site and share feedback.

text:
Setting up CI/CD using Xcode Cloud is surprisingly smoother and more seamless compared to many third-party CI/CD platforms — especially for iOS projects.
If you're building apps within the Apple ecosystem, the native integration makes the entire pipeline feel effortless.
Here’s a quick step-by-step overview to get started:

1- You must have paid Apple developer account.
2- You need to create a workflow.
3- Select the Product for which you want to write this Workflow (In case your project supports multiple versions, like dev, prod etc.)
4- You need to define Start Condition - It can be on code push in branch, it could be on applying tag with specific format, it could be on pull request etc.
5- You need to define the environment - Like on which Xcode version Xcode cloud should upload your app.
6- Then you need to define Actions - It could be Archive, Test etc.
7- And lastly you can define Post actions - You can connect with slack to get notifications.
8- Most importantly you need to install Xcode cloud on Github repo, for this you must have owner role. If you are using Bitbucket then Admin role is enough.
9- For third party dependencies like Pods you can write Script for that follow this link https://lnkd.in/dc7tei3K

graph TD

E --> F(EKS Cluster)

10. Key Takeaways
1. Treat Git as the single source of truth; let controllers (Argo CD) converge state.
2. Combine IRSA + fine-grained RBAC for pod and human identity.
3. Use Karpenter and diversified Spot capacity to minimise cost while maintaining SLA.
4. Embed security (image scanning, policy enforcement) and quality gates early in the pipeline.
5. Build a managed observability backbone to detect drift, right-size resources and enforce SLOs.

I've been talking about a common "language" for reporting and observing CI/CD pipelines for years, and finally, we see the first "words" of this language entering the "dictionary" of observability - the OpenTelemetry open specification.

With the recent release of 
hashtag
#OpenTelemetry's Semantic Conventions, v1.27.0, you can find designated attributes for reporting CI/CD pipelines, under the new CICD namespace. This includes attributes for the pipeline name and run ID, the individual tasks and their respective name, type and run ID, and the deployment environment.

We only founded the 
hashtag
#CICD 
hashtag
#Observability Working Group under the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) less than half a year ago (see here: https://lnkd.in/dAmGNpHR), 
it's great to see things moving forward. Congrats to all the contributors in the working group and the broader Semantic Conventions Special Interest Group, and a special thanks to Adriel Perkins who spearheaded it! 👏 

Check out the OTel docs for more on the new attributes registry:
https://lnkd.in/d6CBFvRx

This is just the first step. If you're interested to learn more about the CI/CD observability standardization efforts, check out my blog:
https://lnkd.in/dABTjckd

And chime in on the working group discussions on slack, calls and github (see details at the end of the blog).

text:
I thought I was just fixing tickets…turns out I was learning DevOps!

Working in Tech Support for Azure DevOps at Microsoft has been like getting a backstage pass to how modern software is actually built with all the bugs, builds, and "why is this pipeline failing again?" moments included.

Azure Boards - Where tasks, bugs, and ideas live. Basically, the team's brain organized into columns like “To Do,” “Doing,” and “Still Doing Because It’s Complicated.”

Azure Pipelines - The magical scripts that build and deploy apps. YAML looked like alphabet soup at first, but now it kinda makes sense!

CI/CD - Fancy acronym for making sure your code gets from laptop to production without manual drama.

Tech Support Life - Solving everything from “Why won’t my pipeline trigger?” to “Why does it trigger too many times?”

Every support ticket has been a learning opportunity. And honestly, there’s no better way to understand DevOps than by helping others debug it.
To anyone starting out: you don’t need to be an expert to jump in. Just be curious, break things (safely), and ask questions.

text:
Stop talking about AI and start building with it on Android 🚀

I’ve just open-sourced my latest project: Play with AI, a playground for integrating LLMs into a native Android environment, along with some playful use scenarios on a map.

As you may have seen from my latest posts, I believe that our role is evolving. Long gone are the days where we were 'just' Android developers, juggling the quirks of Samsung devices. Nowadays we have to be Product Engineers with focus on specific frameworks (Android in my case) that are eager to get their hands 'dirty' with AI on any possible level. It is exciting as much as it is challenging, but it is still fun!

🛠️ The Tech Stack:
- UI: 100% Jetpack Compose (Declarative & state-driven).
- Architecture: Clean Architecture + MVVM for separation of concerns
- Modular structure: Each module does what it should and nothing more
- Concurrency: Kotlin Coroutines & Flow for reactive data streams
- DI: Hilt for scalability and testability
- Design-system ready: Comes packed with its own little design language called 'SoFa' (from Sotiris Falieris. Yeah, I know, cringy)
- A11y, dark mode, localization: All the bells and whistles of a modern app
- CI/CD pipeline: Automate these last steps after the git push

Check out the code here: https://lnkd.in/dE8_Ppjj

I’m currently looking for my next challenge in Android development. If your team is looking for someone who loves clean code and isn't afraid of the bleeding edge, let’s chat!

text:
ليه الـ Monorepo هو المستقبل لمشاريع Flutter الكبيرة؟ وتجربتي مع Melos 
لما مشروع الـ Flutter بيكبر ويبدأ يتقسم لـ Packages كتير (Data, Domain, UI, Core)، بتبدأ تظهر تحديات تخلي التطوير أبطأ وممل، زي إنك تضطر تدخل جوه كل Package وتعمل pub get أو تشغل الـ build_runner يدوياً.
في شغلي الأخير ، كان الحل هو استخدام Melos كـ Monorepo management tool. دي أكتر مميزات تقنية فرقت معايا جداً:

1️⃣ الـ Scripts السحرية (Melos Scripts):
بدل ما أكتب أوامر طويلة، بقيت أعرف Scripts في ملف الـ melos.yaml تشغل الـ build_runner أو الـ tests لكل الـ packages "في وقت واحد" وبالترتيب الصح للـ dependencies.

2️⃣ إدارة الـ Versions والـ Changelog:
بضغطة واحدة melos version بتقدر تحدث إصدارات كل الـ packages اللي حصل فيها تغيير بس، وبيربطهم ببعض أوتوماتيكياً، وده بيمنع أي Conflict بين الـ versions.

3️⃣ الـ IDE Support (Melos Bootstrap):
عملية الـ Bootstrapping بتخلي الـ VS Code أو Android Studio يشوف الـ Packages كأنهم جزء واحد، وده بيسهل جداً الـ Navigation والـ Refactoring بين الأكواد المختلفة.

4️⃣ تكامل الـ CI/CD:
سهل عليا جداً إني أعمل Pipeline واحدة على GitHub Actions تتأكد إن كل الـ Packages مفيهاش مشاكل (Lints & Tests) قبل ما يتعمل لها Merge.
النتيجة؟
الوقت اللي كان بيضيع في إدارة المشروع قل بنسبة كبيرة، والتركيز بقى أكتر على بناء الـ Business Logic وجودة الكود باستخدام Clean Architecture.
حابب أسمع منكم، إيه أكتر أداة بتساعدكم في تنظيم الـ Large-scale projects؟

text:
Continuous integration logs are noisy, verbose, and expensive to analyze at scale. As more teams experiment with LLM-assisted debugging, the cost and latency of processing large build logs becomes a practical bottleneck.

In our recent paper, LogSieve, we introduce a task-aware log reduction approach designed specifically for CI diagnostics. Rather than compressing logs generically, LogSieve filters low-information lines while preserving context needed for build failure analysis.

Across 20 open-source Android projects, the approach reduced log volume by roughly 40 percent with minimal impact on diagnostic utility. This has implications for cost, latency, and energy efficiency when logs are analyzed by LLM-based tooling or automated pipelines.

text:
The Intern Who “Broke” the App

An intern in our team pushed a commit that crashed the app for Million + users.

QA didn’t catch it — because the crash happened only on devices running Android 8 and below.

No one in the test matrix had that OS anymore.
CI pipeline was green.
Code review was rushed — Friday evening merge.

And production? Gone.
Within an hour, we found the culprit —
a missing null check on a legacy API call.
One line.

Everyone blamed the intern.
🔹But he was the only one who stayed late, rolled back, fixed the issue, and wrote automated tests to prevent it next time.
Two weeks later, that same intern didn’t get converted.

Feedback:
 🔹“Weak in data structures.”

QA didn’t have the device.
 🔹Review didn’t have the time.
 🔹The intern had the courage.
 🔹Still — the system failed him.

Building a community for real Android engineers. Let’s connect Imthiyas Alam — this deserves to reach every Android dev out there.

text:
Most mobile teams don’t struggle with building apps.
 They struggle with releasing them safely.
So I recently built a small project to practice mobile CI/CD done the right way.
It’s a complete Android delivery pipeline using:
 GitHub Actions, Gradle, Fastlane, Firebase Distribution, and SonarCloud.
One design choice I focused on was separating CI and CD with manual gates:
CI → validate & analyze
 CD → release only when intentionally triggered
This avoids accidental deployments, improves governance, and fits how mobile releases work in real enterprise environments.
Simple idea. Big impact.
Always interesting how small pipeline decisions affect reliability and confidence in production.

text:
Stop saying you need a $3,000 laptop to build software.


See those red Xs in the screenshot? 👇
Those aren't failures. That is me rewriting package.json files, debugging Java 17 dependencies, and fixing Capacitor config errors—live.

And I did it all from my smartphone.


There is a massive misconception that you need a high-end PC, Android Studio, and three monitors to build a native mobile app.
I am building Visual Guardian, a safety tech startup. I don't have a dev team or a fancy rig. I have my phone and a mission.
Today, I set up a full CI/CD pipeline on GitHub Actions.
I edited the YAML workflows in a mobile browser


I forced npm to align dependency versions.
I debugged the Gradle build process.
The result? The green checkmark at the top. A fully compiled, signed Android APK, built entirely in the cloud, orchestrated from a device that fits in my pocket.

Resourcefulness is a better skill than buying expensive hardware.


Question for my network: What is the craziest workaround you’ve ever used to get a project across the finish line when you didn't have the "right" tools?


P.S. If I can build the backend, frontend, and release pipeline for a safety app on a phone, you can definitely launch that idea you've been sitting on. No excuses.

Alhamdulillah! After a few hours of learning on KodeKloud, I've managed to set up my own little Jenkins architecture using Python scripts that I've created and uploaded to my GitHub. For more context, this basic project contains the following:

• A Jenkins controller with parallel execution enabled, installed and running on a Red Hat node
• Two Jenkins agents (One on an Ubuntu node and the other on a Red Hat node, both configured with a single executor)
• Python scripts to partially streamline the installation and configuration of both the Jenkins controller and agents
• A Jenkins job that automatically deploys the latest version of my SymmEncrypt web application from GitHub, which you can find here [1]
• Intentional configuration errors introduced to simulate both successful and failed builds for troubleshooting practice
• A single Google Cloud project serving as a test environment and hosting the Jenkins infrastructure for this project

Although this project may not be very advanced, it helped me deepen my understanding of CI/CD pipelines and Jenkins. Feel free to leave any constructive feedback in the comments, and try out the scripts I’ve shared on my GitHub [2]. You’re also welcome to check out my

text:
Why Every DevOps Engineer Needs a Developer Mindset 🚀

Just wrapped up setting up my first Android CI/CD pipeline on Bitbucket Pipelines after working with GitHub Actions. As a Java developer turned DevOps engineer, this reminded me why a developer background is a superpower in DevOps.
The Challenge 🔧
What started as a “simple” Android pipeline turned into a debugging marathon:
🔴 Flutter Plugin Issues
 CI kept pulling old versions despite flutter pub upgrade.
 💡 Developer mindset: I knew pubspec.lock was locking versions (just like Maven’s pom.lock).
🔴 Signing Key Mismatch
 Google Play rejected builds due to wrong SHA1.
 💡 Developer mindset: My Java/Gradle experience helped me check build.gradle.kts and fix signing configs.
🔴 Bitbucket vs GitHub Actions
 Different syntax, caching & env handling.
 💡 Developer mindset: Instead of copy-pasting configs, I adapted my knowledge of build lifecycles.
Why Developer Experience Matters 💡
🔍 Deep Troubleshooting – Parse compiler errors, understand build tools, isolate issues systematically.
 🏗️ Cross-Platform Knowledge – Concepts like dependency management, build → test → deploy are universal.
 🔧 Confidence to Dive Deep – Edited build.gradle.kts myself, managed keystores, reasoned about failures.
 🎯 Better Collaboration – Ask the right questions, bridge dev & infra teams, propose informed fixes.

Key Takeaway:
DevOps isn’t just automation—it’s about understanding how code builds, deploys & fails. My time as a Java developer taught me more about debugging pipelines than years of ops alone.
💬 To aspiring DevOps engineers: Learn development fundamentals.
 💬 To developers moving into DevOps: Your coding experience is your secret weapon.
Have you had your developer background save the day in DevOps? Drop your stories below! 👇

text:
🚀 End-to-End CI/CD Pipeline with Jenkins, SonarQube, Nexus, and Tomcat! 

I recently built a **Jenkins pipeline** that streamlines the entire **code, build, test, and deploy** process by integrating: 

🔹 SonarQube – Ensuring code quality and security with static analysis 
🔹 Nexus – Managing artifacts efficiently for a structured deployment 
🔹 Tomcat – Automating application deployment for faster releases 


 How does it work?
1️⃣ Code is committed → Jenkins triggers the pipeline 
2️⃣ SonarQube scans the code for vulnerabilities and best practices 
3️⃣ **Build & artifact creation** using Maven/Gradle 
4️⃣ Artifacts pushed to Nexus for version control 
5️⃣ Automated deployment to Tomcat for seamless delivery 

Benefits:
✅ Improved **code quality & security** 
✅ Centralized **artifact storage & versioning** 
✅ **Automated deployments** for efficiency 
✅ Faster, reliable, and **repeatable CI/CD workflow** 

This setup ensures a **robust and scalable DevOps pipeline**, making software delivery **faster, safer, and more efficient**! 🔥 

What’s your approach to integrating these tools? Let’s discuss best practices! 👇

text:
Last week I built four native apps to wrap Voqara's timer.
In single day.

Windows. Mac. iOS. Android. Plus CI pipelines for each that spit out installable builds.

About 8 hours total.

Then I spent nearly the same amount of time improving Voqara's onboarding flow. Existing code, just working out some quirks.

Same AI tooling, completely different pace.

The native apps required relatively minimal input to get to the finish line. A few changes to knock out bugs along the way and we were done.

The onboarding flow required a short leash, lots of back and forth, and at times dropping into the code myself to prevent AI from chasing its tail.

Here's the uncomfortable takeaway:

AI makes building apps easy.
It does not make building good products easy.

AI is phenomenal at 0 -> 1.
Give it a blank slate and it can move almost autonomously.

But once context matters - business rules, edge cases, messy workflows, tricky UX - progress slows down fast.

That's the part people underestimate.

The hard part of software was never typing code.
It's building a product that solves the *right* problems - and making hard things feel simple for users.

If AI can clone your product overnight, your moat was implementation effort, not the product.

And implementation effort is rapidly becoming a commodity.
…more

Today Sauce Labs announced the general availability of our Real Device Access API. I couldn’t be more proud of what our team has built. 

Is your mobile testing cloud ready for the AI era? AI is now embedded at the OS and device layer. Mobile applications increasingly rely on hardware acceleration and long-lived interactions.Traditional UI automation frameworks weren’t designed or are ready for this new generation of AI-native workflows.

With the Real Device Access API, we are exposing the device itself as programmable infrastructure, giving developers direct, device-level control through simple HTTP requests. No framework required. This is a fundamental step forward for the future of mobile testing. No other mobile testing cloud offers this level of device-level control.

Huge thank you Product, Engineering, and GTM teams who made this possible Ömer Büyükoglu Marek Krysiuk Andrzej Figas Ashwini Sathe Larry Goldman!!! Proud to be building the next generation of mobile infrastructure at Sauce Labs. 🚀

text:
I can’t believe I’m finally doing this, hitting “post” on LinkedIn!

For the longest time, I kept postponing it, even after several nudges from my facilitator Oluyomi O. , about showing up and sharing my journey.

I joined the Women Techsters Fellowship with a “tabula rasa” mindset, coming from a completely different space and intentionally pivoting into tech. I knew I had a lot to learn, and I was ready for it.

Months later, I can confidently say this experience has stretched me in the best ways possible.

Along the way, I have:
 • Unlearnt limiting beliefs
 • Learnt new technical and product skills
 • Relearnt how to approach problems with critical thinking
 • Grown in boldness and confidence

One of the most defining parts of this journey was our Capstone Project, where I had the opportunity to serve as both Product Manager and Team Lead for my group.

Truthfully, I initially wanted to run away from the leadership role. Imposter syndrome knocked hard. But somehow, growth demanded that I step up and I’m grateful I did.

Working alongside such a brilliant and committed team made the experience even more rewarding: My supportive co -product Manager Ogechi Okonkwo 
 
 • Tenacious Product Designers Adeoye Jolaade Racheal Edesiri Ogumor 
 • Diligent Backend Developer Elizabeth Abay 
 • Enthusiastic Mobile Developer Halima Abdulkadir 
 • Hardworking Data Scientists Christiana Favour Mbah Nsikak Okechukwu Endurance Iloba 
 • Zealous Cybersecurity Ladies Thelma Nwoke Omowumi Ogunleye 
 • Ardent DevOps Engineer Tupochele Chatuwa 

Introducing ResQme!

Our capstone project, ResQme, is an emergency response mobile application designed to reduce delays in attending to road accident victims.

The goal is simple but powerful: leverage technology to help ensure quicker responses during emergencies because when accidents happen, every second counts.

Completing this project has been both challenging and fulfilling, and it reinforces my commitment to building meaningful solutions through technology.

This journey has reminded me that growth often begins at the edge of discomfort. Saying “yes” to opportunities that scare you might just be the breakthrough you need!

I am grateful to Tech4Dev for the opportunity to be part of this life-changing journey through the Women Techsters Fellowship. 

Here’s to continuous learning, courageous leadership, and building solutions that truly matter.

Over the past month, I’ve been diving beyond application development into real-world backend infrastructure and AI system deployment.

Here’s what I explored and implemented:

✅ Configured Nginx Reverse Proxy for routing and service exposure
✅ Deployed backend services on a DigitalOcean VPS
✅ Hosted LLM models locally using Ollama
✅ Integrated AI backend services with Express.js
✅ Explored agent-based systems using OpenClaw
✅ Worked with Linux server management, networking, and production-style configurations
✅ Continued parallel development in Android (Jetpack Compose + MVVM)

Instead of only building features, this phase was focused on understanding:

🔹 How real servers handle traffic
🔹 Reverse proxy & request forwarding
🔹 Self-hosted AI infrastructure
🔹 Backend + AI service orchestration
🔹 Production deployment mindset

From mobile apps → scalable backend → self-hosted AI systems — this journey is helping me understand how modern full-stack platforms actually run in production.

Still learning. Still building. 🚀

Design, develop, and maintain Android mobile test automation using tools such as LambdaTest, Appium, BitRise, and other modern frameworks.

text:
This is the Periodic table of DevOps Tools. 

Just like the chemical version, it organizes over 400 industry-leading tools into functional "families" across the entire software delivery lifecycle, including:
- AIOps & Analytics (e.g., Datadog, Splunk)
- Security & DevSecOps (e.g., Snyk, Checkmarx)
- Continuous Integration & Deployment (e.g., Jenkins, Harness)
- Cloud & Infrastructure as Code (e.g., AWS, Terraform)
- Collaboration & Enterprise Agile Planning (e.g., Slack, Jira)

Whether you’re building a pipeline from scratch or looking to optimize an existing one, this framework helps teams identify the right "elements" to drive faster, more secure, and more reliable releases.

Key Takeaway: DevOps isn't just about the tools, it's about the culture, but having the right map for your toolchain makes the journey a lot smoother.

Why teams often choose Amazon RDS over running PostgreSQL on EC2 (managed backups, patching, maintenance)

🚀 What is Software Engineering in 2026? 💻🔥

Software Engineering in 2026 is NOT just about writing code. It’s about building intelligent, scalable, secure digital ecosystems that power the modern world. 🌍⚡

Today’s software engineer is a strategist 🧠 + problem solver 🛠️ + AI collaborator 🤖 + cloud architect ☁️ — all in one.

In 2026, software engineering means:

✅ Building AI-powered applications
✅ Designing cloud-native systems
✅ Working with microservices & APIs
✅ Automating everything with DevOps 🔄
✅ Securing data in a cyber-threat world 🔐
✅ Creating seamless mobile + web experiences 📱💻

It’s no longer “just development.”
It’s digital transformation engineering. 💡

From fintech platforms 💳 to healthcare systems 🏥, from e-commerce giants 🛒 to education apps 🎓 — software engineers are shaping how the world works.

And here’s the reality 👇
In 2026, engineers who survive and thrive are those who:

🔥 Continuously learn
🔥 Understand business impact
🔥 Think scalable
🔥 Embrace AI instead of fearing it
🔥 Focus on performance + security

The future doesn’t belong to coders.
It belongs to solution builders. 🏗️

If you're entering tech, don’t just learn syntax.
Learn systems. Learn architecture. Learn impact. 🎯

Software Engineering in 2026 = Innovation + Intelligence + Influence. 🚀

🚀 Ищем Project Manager в IT‑проект: создаём крутые мобильные приложения!
Мы разрабатываем собственный IT‑продукт — мобильные и десктоп‑приложения, веб‑сервис и Telegram‑бота. И нам нужен Project Manager с опытом в разработке мобильных приложений — тот, кто возьмёт на себя управление проектом и приведёт команду к успеху!

💻 Что предстоит делать:
Планировать проект на всех этапах: формировать roadmap и спринты, декомпозировать задачи и определять приоритеты, следить за сроками и загрузкой команды.
-Управлять требованиями: собирать и структурировать их, формировать и поддерживать ТЗ, уточнять бизнес‑логику, контролировать изменения и синхронизировать ожидания бизнеса и разработки.
-Координировать разнопрофильную команду (backend, mobile, web, DevOps, QA): расставлять приоритеты, устранять блокировщики, контролировать выполнение задач и поддерживать прозрачные процессы.
🔍 Мы ждём от вас:
-Опыт работы Project Manager от 2 лет — обязательно с опытом в разработке мобильных приложений.
-Опыт в IT‑проектах.
-Умение работать с требованиями и документацией.
-Системное мышление, навыки приоритизации и принятия решений.
-Способность держать проект под контролем в условиях высокой динамики.
-Понимание релизных процессов мобильных приложений.
🎇 Что мы предлагаем:
-Полностью удалённую работу — работайте из любой точки мира.
-Гибкий график: начало дня можно сдвигать — мы ценим результат, а не количество отработанных часов.
-Технически сложный и интересный продукт — будет над чем поработать и чему научиться.
-Минимум бюрократии: вы сможете напрямую влиять на процессы и итоговый результат.
-Возможность работать с профессиональной командой и расти вместе с проектом.

text:
Mac vs Windows

What is better for work?

Most developers choose their laptop based on эмоtions, not tasks

Over the past few years I’ve worked on both ThinkPad and MacBook

I expected the difference to be in the hardware

Turns out — the difference is in the philosophy of work

MacBook
open it → you work
Unix out of the box
battery lasts forever
minimal setup

ThinkPad
more flexibility
more control
easier to upgrade
familiar Windows environment

And here’s what’s interesting

70% of the debates in the comments are not about performance
They’re about identity

“A real developer should…”
“Serious engineering is only on…”
“macOS is for hipsters…”
“Windows is pain and suffering…”

But in reality, it all comes down to your stack and tasks

Frontend
Backend
Mobile
DevOps

The answers will differ

Right now I use a Mac

But I still consider ThinkPad the benchmark of a true “work machine”

And I’m genuinely curious

What are you working on and why?

text:
Hi Connections!

Deloitte Consulting Southeast Asia is calling experienced professionals based in Indonesia and Malaysia to join our Innovation Cloud Development Center (ICDC) team as a DevOps Engineer.

🔍 What will you do as a DevOps Engineer?
As a DevOps Engineer, you will build and automate infrastructure, create OpenShift clusters, and develop Ansible playbooks to support hybrid cloud and banking platforms. You will play a key role in designing resilient systems, implementing best practices, and ensuring our platforms are secure, observable, and scalable.

🧩 The ideal DevOps Engineer profile:
- Bachelor’s degree in computer science, engineering, or an equivalent field
- A detail‑oriented, highly technical professional with 2+ years of hands‑on cloud infrastructure experience
- Experience working in enterprise environments; exposure to AWS is an advantage
- Kubernetes & OpenShift: Prior experience with Kubernetes in an enterprise setup, preferably Red Hat OpenShift, ideally within a banking environment
- Hybrid Cloud Architecture: Hands‑on experience designing solutions across hybrid environments, with exposure to Azure and/or AWS, preferably in regulated industries
- Observability & Metrics: Experience implementing observability for mobile and core banking platforms, with an understanding of business‑critical metrics, not just technical ones
- DevOps & CI/CD: Proven experience designing and architecting CI/CD pipelines for regulated applications, adhering to stringent security and compliance requirements

📩 How to apply
If this opportunity aligns with your background, please submit your latest resume:

MY‑based candidates
- Email: vhakim@deloitte.com
- Subject: ICDC – MY – DevOps Engineer – Your Name

ID‑based candidates
- Apply via this link: https://lnkd.in/gMFZrGEk

*Please note that only shortlisted candidates will be contacted

It’s a high-pressure moment — but your approach makes all the difference.

✅ 𝑹𝒐𝒍𝒍 𝒃𝒂𝒄𝒌 or switch to 𝒔𝒕𝒂𝒏𝒅𝒃𝒚
✅ Use 𝒎𝒐𝒏𝒊𝒕𝒐𝒓𝒊𝒏𝒈 𝒕𝒐𝒐𝒍𝒔 like Grafana, Prometheus, ELK
✅ 𝑪𝒐𝒎𝒎𝒖𝒏𝒊𝒄𝒂𝒕𝒆 with your team — clearly and fast
✅ Trace the issue in your 𝑪𝑰/𝑪𝑫 𝒑𝒊𝒑𝒆𝒍𝒊𝒏𝒆
✅ Do a 𝑹𝒐𝒐𝒕 𝑪𝒂𝒖𝒔𝒆 𝑨𝒏𝒂𝒍𝒚𝒔𝒊𝒔 after stabilizing things

💡 Interviewers look for your thought process — not just your 𝒕𝒐𝒐𝒍 𝒌𝒏𝒐𝒘𝒍𝒆𝒅𝒈𝒆.

This is how real DevOps engineers think.

🔗 Watch the video & share your thoughts in the comments 👇
Let’s learn, grow, and build together! 💪

🔔 Follow Geetha S for more practical DevOps & Cloud interview content.
🔁 Repost if this scenario made you pause and think — “I’ve seen this in real interviews!” 😅

text:
🚀 What is NOT DevOps? And What is DevOps? 🤔
Many people think DevOps is just about using tools or hiring a "DevOps Engineer." But DevOps is more than that—it’s a mindset, a culture, and a way of working that bridges the gap between development and operations.
💡 DevOps is NOT:
❌ Just automation or tools.
❌ A fixed set of rules for every company.
❌ A replacement for Agile—it's a complementary approach!
✅ DevOps IS:
🔹 Collaboration & teamwork
🔹 Automation for efficiency
🔹 Continuous improvement & faster delivery
In simple words: Anything that helps you deploy your product better, cheaper, faster, and more securely is DevOps!
📖 Want to dive deeper? Read my full blog here: [ https://lnkd.in/ePskSQ7Q ]

Let’s discuss! What’s your take on DevOps? 💬👇

text:
I just solved a problem that costs people ₹1+ Crore in lost wealth.

Last month, my friend Rahul asked: "I earn ₹80K/month but still feel broke. Where's my money going?"

I showed him Rich Dad Poor Dad's Cashflow Quadrant:
- E (Employee) - Trading time for money
- S (Self-Employed) - You ARE the business
- B (Business) - Systems work for you
- I (Investor) - Money works for you

His problem? 100% of income from E quadrant. Zero passive income.

So I built WealthWise - a finance tracker that implements principles from 5 bestselling books:

📚 Books Implemented:
- Rich Dad Poor Dad → Cashflow Quadrant tracking
- The Psychology of Money → "Enough" number calculator
- The Millionaire Fastlane → Fastlane Score (0-100)
- I Will Teach You to Be Rich → 6 Money Buckets system
- Die with Zero → Experience value calculator

🎯 Key Features:

**Fastlane Score:**
Measures how "Fastlane" your income is
- 0-25: Slowlane (job dependent)
- 25-50: Transitioning
- 50-75: Fastlane
- 75-100: Financial Freedom

**"Enough" Number:**
Calculates when you can retire
Formula: Annual Expenses × 30 years
When passive income ≥ expenses, you're done!

**Lifestyle Inflation Detector:**
Alerts when spending growth > income growth
(This caught Rahul spending 28% more despite only 12% income increase)

📊 Results After 3 Months:

Rahul's transformation:
- Fastlane Score: 0 → 35
- Started freelancing (S quadrant): +₹12K/month
- Began dividend investing (I quadrant): +₹2K/month
- Identified ₹15K in unnecessary expenses
- Time to "Enough": Reduced from 25 years → 15 years

💻 Tech Stack:
Java, SQLite, JDBC, MVC Architecture

The app calculates:
✅ Your position in the Cashflow Quadrant
✅ Time remaining to financial independence
✅ Cost of delaying investments (compound interest)
✅ Asset vs Liability classification
✅ Automated spending alerts

Most interesting learning:
The compound interest visualizer shows that delaying investments by 5 years costs ₹1+ Crore in lost wealth. That alone convinced 8 friends to start investing immediately.

🔗 Open source on GitHub: https://lnkd.in/gPqrHGQd

What's YOUR Fastlane Score? 
Are you in E/S quadrants or building B/I income? 💭

text:
🔥🚀 The Fastlane Mindset: 
Escape the 9-to-5 Trap Before It’s Too Late ⚡

What if the real reason you’re stuck isn’t lack of money or time… but the wrong map?

You’ve been told to save, invest, and wait 40 years for freedom. MJ DeMarco calls it the “Slowlane.” It’s safe. Predictable. And completely broken for entrepreneurs who want impact now.

💡 DeMarco was a broke limo driver watching other people ride in the back seats of his cars. Same city. Same hours. Same world — but a different lane.

One night, he realized something: those people didn’t get rich by working harder. They built systems that worked harder for them. That moment became his switch — from trading time for money to owning a process that multiplied value even when he slept.

It wasn’t instant. He failed. He rebuilt. But he stopped chasing shortcuts and started designing a business that solved real problems — scalable, automated, customer-focused. That’s how “The Millionaire Fastlane” was born.

You’ve been in that same traffic jam — doing “everything right,” yet moving inches while others speed ahead. But maybe you’re not lazy. Maybe you’re just driving the wrong vehicle.


🌟 Lessons You Can Act On:
1️⃣ Stop worshiping “more effort.” The game changes when you design leverage, not labor.
2️⃣ Build a system that earns while you sleep — not a job that collapses if you rest.
3️⃣ Speed doesn’t mean recklessness. It means focused velocity on scalable value.
4️⃣ Solve painful problems, not trendy ones. Customers pay for relief, not novelty.
5️⃣ Treat time as your most expensive currency — spend it on creation, not maintenance.
6️⃣ Don’t wait for permission. The Fastlane starts the moment you decide you’re done crawling.


🌟 30-Day Power Moves:
💡 Audit your daily work. Eliminate one task that doesn’t scale.
🔥 Launch a micro-product or service that solves a real pain, not a “cool idea.”
🚀 Create one system (automation, delegation, process) that runs without you.
💡 Track time like profit — every hour should earn or build future leverage.

🚩 Biggest Traps:
Building for validation, not customers.
Confusing busy with progress.
Mistaking control for freedom.

Consequences:
Ignore this and years will vanish in polite frustration — the kind where you smile at results you don’t believe in.

✍️ Final Thought:
Freedom isn’t earned by waiting. It’s built — one bold, leveraged decision at a time.

👉 Question:
Are you driving your dream — or just fueling someone else’s?

text:
Do You Have a “
hashtag
#Shadow ERP” or a real Decision-Making fastlane?
Every company has an ERP.
And almost every company has a second one — unofficial, improvised, invisible.
You see it in Excel spreadsheets tracking late orders. In inboxes full of escalations. In weekly meetings where departments reconnect what the system failed to handle. 

This is the shadow ERP. It runs the exceptions.
The official ERP manages perfectly what goes right. Orders flow. Deliveries ship. Payments clear. It is built for stability.
But business and organisational stress lives in what goes wrong.

When a delivery is late or an invoice is blocked, the system does not decide. People do! Customer service calls planning. Planning checks with procurement. Managers escalate. Ownership blurs. 
The problem moves sideways instead of upward toward a clear decision.
That manual coordination consumes more leadership time than the transaction itself.

A real decision-making fastlane changes that. 
Exceptions are detected, assigned, and automatically escalated if they stall. Root cause is made explicit. Corrective action is tracked. Collaboration happens inside the issue, not across scattered emails.
ERP protects the happy flow.

A decision fastlane protects performance when the flow breaks.
The real question is simple:
Are you managing bottlenecks through a shadow ERP — or through a system that forces decisions forward?
Do you think AI can ever help you if you keep this offline shadow ERP? really?

text:
A ghost in Google Play store rejected our latest production release

Last week, we hit a wall. One of our Android releases, running through GitHub Actions and Fastlane, was rejected by Google Play Store.

The reason?
A conflict between our Play Store declaration (no ads) versus the presence of an ad-related permission in the app. A transitive dependency from Firebase. Simple enough, or so I thought.

I tried two textbook fixes:
• Force-remove the permission from the merged manifest.
• Explicitly exclude the problematic dependency in Gradle.

Re-ran the pipeline. Same error. This went on for another hour.

At that point, I made a call: prioritize getting the build to business users over fighting the machine. I built the bundle manually and uploaded it directly via the Play Console.

It passed instantly!! 🤔

To add to the mystery, all subsequent automated uploads via Fastlane have worked fine since.

So, what was the real issue?
It wasn't the code. It was a state discrepancy - a ghost in the machine. The evidence points to a temporary caching or state problem between the Play Store API and our automation tool. The manual upload likely cleared that faulty state.

This is a critical lesson for platform teams. Our sophisticated CI/CD pipelines are powerful, trusty even, but they are not infallible. They introduce layers of abstraction that can obscure the root cause of a problem.

My two key takeaways:
• Trust the system, just not blindly: When automation fails cryptically, a manual run is a good diagnostic tool. It instantly isolates the problem to the automation layer.
• Audit the entire chain: It's not enough to audit code. Comprehend the behavior of your entire deployment chain - from IDE to build server to automation to the final platform API.

Automation is about reliability, not just speed. When it falters, the most valuable tool is a pragmatic engineer who knows when to step outside the system to fix it.

When was the last time a 'ghost in the machine' blocked your release? Share your war story!

text:
Most people think wealth is about luck, age, or endless savings.

But MJ Demarco flips the script.

He says there are three lanes in life:

☛ The Sidewalk: where people live for today, spend recklessly, and end up broke.

☛ The Slowlane: where people save every penny, invest cautiously, and hope to retire rich at 65.

☛ The Fastlane: where people create value, build scalable systems, and buy freedom now, not decades later.

The sidewalk and slowlane are traps.

They give the illusion of security but keep you stuck in mediocrity.

The Fastlane is different.

It’s not about cutting lattes or waiting on pensions.

It’s about control.

↳ Control of your time.
↳ Control of your income.
↳ Control of your freedom.

DeMarco shows that real wealth comes when you stop trading hours for money and start building assets that:

⇛Scale without your constant presence.
⇛Solve problems for a lot of people.
⇛Grow faster than your spending habits.

That’s the Millionaire Fastlane.

Not waiting till old age.

Not hoping the stock market behaves.

But leveraging entrepreneurship, systems, and value creation to live rich while you’re still young.

And here’s the wild-card entry—the Fastlane is open to anyone bold enough to leave the sidewalk.

You just need to choose.

🚶Sidewalk.
🚃Slowlane.
🛫Or Fastlane.

Your future depends on it.

text:
The 5 Commandments of Building a Fastlane Business

If you’re serious about creating a business that grows rapidly and stands the test of time, these principles are non-negotiable:

1️⃣ Need:
Solve a real problem or fulfill a genuine need. Businesses that succeed do so because they serve.

2️⃣ Entry: 
Choose a field or market that’s tough to enter. Higher barriers to entry mean fewer competitors.

3️⃣ Control: 
Own your destiny. Retain control over your business to ensure long-term sustainability and success.

4️⃣ Scale: 
Think big. Opt for a business model that lets you impact many people, not just a few.

5️⃣ Time: 
Build systems that work without you. True freedom comes when your business doesn’t demand your constant presence.

Focus on these commandments, and you’ll build something that not only scales but also lasts.

Which commandment resonates with you the most?

text:
Schools & colleges teach us: Study ➜ Job ➜ Save 10% ➜ Retire at 65.
That’s the Slowlane: safe… and painfully slow.

MJ DeMarco’s The Fastlane says: build assets that scale—businesses, products, or digital systems—so money isn’t tied to your hours.

➜ 
hashtag
#Slowlane: Trade decades for “someday”. Earn ₹60k/month, save 10% → 40 years to ₹3 Cr.
➜ 
hashtag
#Fastlane: Build a system that pays you even when you’re not working. Any product that nets ₹1L/month in 2–5 years.

➜ Why people miss it:
We ask, “How do I get rich?” Instead ask, “What am I doing that keeps me poor?” 

➜ What actually works:
Solve a real problem (NECST: Need, Entry, Control, Scale, Time). Own the system (product > pay cheque).

Gut-check: Are your dreams alive—or on life support? If “someday” keeps moving, change lanes.

💡 Great businesses don’t start with ideas; they start with problems worth solving.

---------

P.S. The Fastlane is a way to build wealth quickly by creating scalable systems or businesses that make money even when you’re not working.

text:
If you think getting rich takes 40 years… You’re already playing the wrong game.
(Insights from The Millionaire Fastlane)

I know this post is going to ruffle a lot of feathers. 
But here’s the truth: this book is a complete mindset shift.

It challenges everything we’ve been taught about wealth. It makes you uncomfortable—but for all the right reasons.

Most people spend their lives in the Slowlane:

❌ Work hard, save, and hope to retire rich
❌ Trade time for money, waiting decades for "wealth"
❌ Follow the same broken formula that keeps them stuck

🚀 𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘔𝘪𝘭𝘭𝘪𝘰𝘯𝘢𝘪𝘳𝘦 𝘍𝘢𝘴𝘵𝘭𝘢𝘯𝘦 reveals why slow wealth is a myth—and how to break free. Here’s the 4-part mindset shift that separates the wealthy from everyone else.

𝗟𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝟭: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗪𝗲𝗮𝗹𝘁𝗵 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗺𝘂𝗹𝗮 𝗶𝘀 𝗡𝗢𝗧 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸

Most people follow the Slowlane:

↳ Work for decades
↳ Save & invest small amounts
↳ Retire in old age

The Fastlane?

↳ Control your income (business, not a salary)
↳ Use leverage (scale + automation)
↳ Move fast—don't wait 40 years

🚀 Key takeaway: If you trade time for money, you’ll never be truly wealthy.

𝗟𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝟮: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟱 𝗙𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀

If your business or income source follows these 5 rules, you’re on the Fastlane:

1. Control – You own the system (not an employer)
2. Entry – High barriers = less competition
3. Need – Solve a problem, don’t chase money
4. Time – It scales beyond your hours
5. Scale – You can serve millions, not just one

🚀 Key takeaway: If you can’t scale or automate it, it’s not Fastlane wealth.

𝗟𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝟯: 𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗝𝗼𝗯 𝗪𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗡𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗠𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗪𝗲𝗮𝗹𝘁𝗵𝘆

Even a high salary is a slow trap.

❌ You trade time for money
❌ You can be fired at any time
❌ Raises don’t match inflation

🚀 Key takeaway: The Fastlane mindset is about building assets that generate wealth without trading time for money.

𝗟𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝟰: 𝗠𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘆 𝗜𝘀 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗚𝗼𝗮𝗹—𝗧𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗜𝘀

↳ If you make $1M but work 80-hour weeks, are you rich?
↳ True wealth = money + freedom

The goal?

📌 Build an income system that works without you
📌 Get time freedom while scaling your wealth

🚀 Key takeaway: Control your time, or you’ll never control your life.

𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗴𝗲𝘁 𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝘀𝗹𝗼𝘄… 𝗼𝗿 𝗴𝗲𝘁 𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁.

Which mindset are you following—Slowlane or Fastlane?  👇

♻️ Repost to share these insights.
➕ Follow Shivani Virdi for more.

text:
📚 Post 32 – The Millionaire Fastlane (Part 2) – MJ DeMarco
We previously explored the 3 financial paths:
❌ Sidewalk → no plan, only spending
⏳ Slowlane → save & wait until 60+
🚀 Fastlane → leverage time for money; build scalable systems

Many people have business ideas but fail because they ignore the CENTs Framework, DeMarco’s 5 Commandments of Wealth Creation:

🔑 The 5 CENTs Commandments

1️⃣ C – Control
Relying fully on others is risky (one supplier, employer, or platform).
Focus on multiple income sources and control.
Example: a job alone = vulnerability.

2️⃣ E – Entry
If anyone can copy your idea, it won’t last.
Build barriers: skills, brand, tech, or capital.

3️⃣ N – Need
People buy solutions, not “cool ideas.”
Always ask: “What problem am I solving?”

4️⃣ T – Time
If money = hours worked, it’s a job, not a business.
Fastlane wealth comes from systems that earn while you sleep 🌙.

5️⃣ S – Scale
Can you serve 100, 1,000, 1,000,000?
Local bakery = limited; franchise = scalable.
Big impact → big income.

✅ Conclusion
Wealth = Need × Scale × Control
Combine with patience & vision → Fastlane success.
Tip: Test any business idea against CENTs. Fail 1–2 commandments? Rethink it.

text:
Barbados is once again debating traffic congestion. The real question is not flyovers versus buses. It is this: What kind of mobility system are we building for the next generation?

Professor Winston Moore of The University of the West Indies, Cave Hill Campus reminds us that congestion is multi-layered. It is shaped by commuting patterns, school locations, work schedules, junction design and how well our systems use real-time data.

Solutions do not have to be singular: adaptive traffic signals, dedicated school buses, transit hubs, staggered work hours, smarter data use. These are combinable tools within a broader smart city approach.

At the same time, voices such as Dr. Danielle Evanson, a sustainable mobility advocate, caution that expanding roads can sometimes increase traffic over time. With high vehicle ownership and growing car imports, Barbados must confront a structural reality: are we designing around cars, or around people?

Mobility decisions intersect with fuel costs, land use planning, air quality, climate resilience and national development. In a small island context, every infrastructure choice has long-term consequences.

So perhaps the real debate is not about adding lanes. It is about adding vision.

Are we trying to move more cars, or move more people, more efficiently and sustainably? Read full article below.

text:
1.The Fastlane Mindset
 - Wealth is built by creating value and leveraging systems, not by slow savings or traditional retirement plans (the "Slowlane"). 
 - Focus on scalable, controllable businesses that generate passive income. 

2. Break Free from the "Slowlane" 
 - The traditional path (job, save, retire at 65) is flawed—it’s slow, risky (dependent on markets/employers), and limits freedom. 
 - Fastlane entrepreneurs prioritize rapid wealth creation through entrepreneurship. 

 3. The 5 Fastlane Commandments 
 - Need: Solve a pressing need for a large market. 
 - Entry: Avoid saturated, low-barrier industries (e.g., dropshipping, MLMs). 
 - Control:Own and control your business (don’t rely on others). 
 - Scale: Build systems that scale (e.g., automation, delegation). 
 - Time: Separate time from income (earn passively, not hourly). 

4. Wealth = Net Worth, Not Income 
 - True wealth is measured by assets that grow without your time (e.g., businesses, royalties, investments). 
 - Avoid "fake riches" (luxury purchases that drain wealth). 

5. Take Action & Persist 
 - Execution beats ideas—start now, fail fast, and iterate. 
 - Success requires persistence, learning from failures, and staying committed to the Fastlane principles. 

In short: Build a scalable business that solves real problems, focus on systems over linear income, and escape the Slowlane’s limitations.

text:
What happens when you bring together multiple experts across disciplines? You can help local businesses find success and solutions to problems. A perfect example of this occurred when our counterparts at FastLane MEP pulled in Matthew Davies and Geoffrey Frank in the Structures Group at UDRI to help out Captor Corporation, located in Tipp City, Ohio. 
 
Captor Corporation is a manufacturer of high-quality electromagnetic interference (EMI) filters and components. These EMI filters are primarily used in defense and aerospace to filter out interference and protect sensitive systems in military applications. Without an EMI filter, communications could be compromised or have poor quality. In addition, sensitive equipment could be susceptible to an outside threat and cause a malfunction. Captor’s longtime supplier of analytical services was no longer a viable option, and they needed help quickly. 
 
The structures Group at UDRI supported this work by performing finite element analyses to verify that one of Captor’s EMI filters would perform as designed. Thermal analyses verified that the operating temperature would not exceed acceptable ranges of the electrical components utilized in the filter. Structural analyses verified that the filter would survive the acceleration loads and vibration, shock, and acoustic loading that might be experienced during operation. 
 
Scott Timms, the president of Captor Corporation, stated that “FastLane offers small manufacturers like us, access to a wide range of resources that we would normally not have access to. Using FastLane and UDRI has made our life much easier. You have added tremendous value to our products and saved us millions of dollars in software and expertise and the payback is just huge. It’s hard to put a number on it.”

text:
A successful fastlane business is built on five key commandments:

1.The Commandment of Need: Your business must address a genuine need or solve a significant problem for people. Avoid entering saturated markets, such as opening a restaurant where there are already hundreds of similar options.

2.The Commandment of Entry: There should be high barriers to entry for your business. This reduces competition and increases the value of your enterprise. 

3.The Commandment of Control: Maintain control over your business. Avoid scenarios where other entities have significant control over your operations, similar to how Amazon controls its sellers rather than the other way around.

4.The Commandment of Scale: Your business must have the potential for scalability. For example, apps and software can scale rapidly, unlike a traditional ice cream shop.

5.The Commandment of Time: Ensure your business is not tied to your personal time. It should be able to generate revenue even when you are not actively working, allowing you the freedom to take time off while still earning. 

** Takeaways from The Millionaire Fastlane**

text:
I grew up hearing the same advice many of us did:
 Work hard. Be patient. Save little by little. One day, it will all pay off.
It sounded wise. Responsible. Safe.

Then I picked up M.J. DeMarco’s The Millionaire Fastlane  and it basically said,
“That plan might keep you busy… but it won’t necessarily make you wealthy.”

What a pumpkin! 
And that’s where the discomfort begins.

The book questions something many of us are deeply attached to:
 Is working hard for decades really the smartest path… or just the most familiar one?

It questions the traditional “slow lane” approach:
 Work for decades.
 Save a little.
 Retire late.
 Hope it’s enough.
Instead, it introduces a different mindset. One that prioritizes leverage, value creation, and ownership.

A key takeaway that stood out to me:
 Wealth is not about time spent. It’s about value created.
The Fastlane isn’t about shortcuts or overnight success.
 It’s about Process; building systems, solving real problems, and creating outcomes that are not directly tied to hours worked.

It asks hard but necessary questions:
 – Are you building something you own, or are you trading time endlessly?
 – Are your efforts scalable, or capped by your availability?
 – Are you intentional about where your energy goes?

For me, it’s a reminder to think beyond effort and focus more on impact, structure, and long-term leverage.

Have you read The Millionaire Fastlane?
What idea from it challenged your thinking the most?
Is trading time for money really the smartest path to wealth?

My entrepreneurial journey really began after reading 'The Millionaire Fastlane' and internalizing its advice: stop looking for ideas, start listening for complaints.

I realized the problem wasn't a lack of materials; it was a total breakdown of organization. We're all trying to learn in chaos.

That's the day the idea for 'StuEcos' (the name for now) was born. Not from a vision, but from a universal, everyday complaint.

I'm now all in on building the solution. (More on that soon).

It got me thinking... what other massive opportunities are we missing because they're disguised as everyday complaints?

text:
Most people are stuck in the "Slowlane" of wealth-building, believing that working 9-5, saving diligently, and retiring at 65 is the only path to financial security. But what if everything you've been told about wealth is wrong? 
 
MJ DeMarco's The Millionaire Fastlane flips conventional wisdom on its head. Instead of slow and steady, the book reveals a faster path to financial freedom: entrepreneurship, value creation, and building scalable systems. 
 
Here are five key takeaways for anyone serious about reclaiming their time and building lasting wealth: 
 
1- The Slowlane is a trap. 
 
Relying on a job and savings will not lead to the freedom you want. To escape, think like an entrepreneur, not an employee. 
 
2 - Time is more valuable than money – 
 
You can always make more money, but time is finite. Build systems that don’t require you to trade hours for dollars. 
 
3 - Leverage is key 
 
Use technology, people, and systems to scale your efforts. The more you can multiply your impact, the faster you grow. 
 
4 - Control beats luck 
 
Real wealth isn’t built on the stock market or luck. Own systems and assets where you can directly influence the outcome. 
 
5 - Value creation drives wealth 
 
Solve problems for others at scale, and wealth follows. Focus on creating value, not chasing money. 
 
The Millionaire Fastlane shows us that the real road to wealth is about creating value and building systems that scale. 
 
Have you read it? I love to hear your thoughts on it? 
 
Thanks for reading! 
 
www.building-out.com

text:
If you think getting rich takes 40 years… You’re already playing the wrong game.
(Insights from The Millionaire Fastlane)

I know this post is going to ruffle a lot of feathers. 
But here’s the truth: this book is a complete mindset shift.

It challenges everything we’ve been taught about wealth. It makes you uncomfortable—but for all the right reasons.

Most people spend their lives in the Slowlane:

❌ Work hard, save, and hope to retire rich
❌ Trade time for money, waiting decades for "wealth"
❌ Follow the same broken formula that keeps them stuck

🚀 𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘔𝘪𝘭𝘭𝘪𝘰𝘯𝘢𝘪𝘳𝘦 𝘍𝘢𝘴𝘵𝘭𝘢𝘯𝘦 reveals why slow wealth is a myth—and how to break free. Here’s the 4-part mindset shift that separates the wealthy from everyone else.

𝗟𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝟭: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗪𝗲𝗮𝗹𝘁𝗵 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗺𝘂𝗹𝗮 𝗶𝘀 𝗡𝗢𝗧 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸

Most people follow the Slowlane:

↳ Work for decades
↳ Save & invest small amounts
↳ Retire in old age

The Fastlane?

↳ Control your income (business, not a salary)
↳ Use leverage (scale + automation)
↳ Move fast—don't wait 40 years

🚀 Key takeaway: If you trade time for money, you’ll never be truly wealthy.

𝗟𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝟮: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟱 𝗙𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀

If your business or income source follows these 5 rules, you’re on the Fastlane:

1. Control – You own the system (not an employer)
2. Entry – High barriers = less competition
3. Need – Solve a problem, don’t chase money
4. Time – It scales beyond your hours
5. Scale – You can serve millions, not just one

🚀 Key takeaway: If you can’t scale or automate it, it’s not Fastlane wealth.

𝗟𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝟯: 𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗝𝗼𝗯 𝗪𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗡𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗠𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗪𝗲𝗮𝗹𝘁𝗵𝘆

Even a high salary is a slow trap.

❌ You trade time for money
❌ You can be fired at any time
❌ Raises don’t match inflation

🚀 Key takeaway: The Fastlane mindset is about building assets that generate wealth without trading time for money.

𝗟𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝟰: 𝗠𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘆 𝗜𝘀 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗚𝗼𝗮𝗹—𝗧𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗜𝘀

↳ If you make $1M but work 80-hour weeks, are you rich?
↳ True wealth = money + freedom

The goal?

📌 Build an income system that works without you
📌 Get time freedom while scaling your wealth

text:
Summary of "The Millionaire Fastlane" by MJ DeMarco

MJ DeMarco’s The Millionaire Fastlane challenges the conventional wisdom of slow and steady wealth-building through a traditional career path. DeMarco divides wealth-building into three "lanes": the Sidewalk, the Slowlane, and the Fastlane, each representing a different approach to life and financial freedom.

👉Key Concepts:
1.   The Sidewalk
The Sidewalk represents financial irresponsibility and living paycheck to paycheck, often characterized by impulsive spending, lack of savings, and dependency on external factors for security. Sidewalkers prioritize instant gratification, leaving them financially vulnerable.
2.   The Slowlane
The Slowlane is the path most people are taught to follow: go to school, get a stable job, save diligently, invest in the stock market, and retire in old age. While safer than the Sidewalk, the Slowlane is slow and uncertain, as it depends on factors like market performance, inflation, and health during retirement years.
3.   The Fastlane
The Fastlane is DeMarco's recommended approach to wealth. It involves leveraging entrepreneurship and scalable systems to achieve financial freedom quickly. This path requires effort, creativity, and risk-taking but offers the potential for significant rewards in a shorter timeframe.

👉 Wealth Equation
DeMarco emphasizes that wealth is created by leveraging control, scalability, and time. He critiques traditional financial advice for its dependency on uncontrollable external factors and stresses the importance of creating value and solving problems for a large audience.

👉Commandments of the Fastlane
To succeed in the Fastlane, DeMarco outlines five commandments:
•   Need: Solve a real problem or fulfill a genuine need.
•   Entry: Choose ventures with barriers to entry to reduce competition.
•   Control: Maintain control over your business and revenue streams.
•   Scale: Focus on scalable models to grow income exponentially.
•   Time: Build systems that detach your income from your time.
Mindset Shifts
DeMarco stresses adopting a producer mindset instead of a consumer mindset. Producers create value and solve problems, while consumers merely spend money. He also encourages readers to think in terms of "wealth units" (time and freedom) rather than just money.

The Millionaire Fastlane inspires readers to rethink societal norms about wealth, work, and retirement. By rejecting the Slowlane and embracing the Fastlane, individuals can achieve financial freedom and live life on their terms, often far earlier than traditional methods allow.

This book is both a motivational guide and a practical roadmap for those willing to think differently and work strategically for financial independence.

text:
The Mumbai Dabbawala and the Millionaire Fastlane: A Lesson in Wealth Creation

💡 What does a book on wealth creation have in common with Mumbai’s iconic dabbawalas? A lot more than you think.

Reading The Millionaire Fastlane by MJ DeMarco, I stumbled upon a powerful realization: true wealth lies in creating scalable solutions that serve real-world problems. The book divides life into three financial lanes:
1️⃣ The Sidewalk: Living paycheck to paycheck, dependent on circumstances.
2️⃣ The Slowlane: Playing it safe, relying on traditional paths like saving for decades.
3️⃣ The Fastlane: Building scalable systems that generate wealth independent of your direct time or effort.
Now, let’s bring this idea closer to home with an Indian twist: the Mumbai Dabbawala System.

🌟 Over 5,000 dabbawalas deliver over 200,000 tiffins daily across Mumbai with clockwork precision. They aren’t just individuals—they are part of a self-sustaining, scalable system. While their income may not place them in the millionaire bracket, the underlying principles align with the Fastlane mentality:
System Thinking: They built a process where each person's role contributes to a larger, efficient system.
Value Delivery: They solve a pressing problem—ensuring fresh, home-cooked meals reach office-goers on time.
Scalability: The system is capable of serving hundreds of thousands without breaking down.

📖 MJ DeMarco emphasizes that wealth isn’t about chasing money; it’s about creating impact. The dabbawalas, though often overlooked, have achieved an unparalleled level of operational excellence. Imagine applying similar principles—solving pressing problems, building systems, and scaling them—to our entrepreneurial dreams!

💬 Takeaway: The Fastlane mindset isn’t just for Silicon Valley startups. It’s rooted in identifying opportunities, creating value, and scaling effectively. As Indians, we are surrounded by inspiration like the dabbawalas—reminders that the Fastlane can be found in the most unassuming places.
What problem are you solving today that has the potential to scale? Let’s brainstorm—your Fastlane might just be around the corner. 🚀
