Juggling work, personal, and OSS repos means constantly managing Git identities. One wrong config and you're committing under the wrong email — silently, every time.
GitPilot fixes that. 🚀
→ Auto-applies the right Git identity per repo using path-matching rules 
→ Scans your filesystem and groups repos by identity — flags mismatches instantly 
→ Generates commit messages with on-device AI. No cloud. No API keys. No Ollama.

The AI runs entirely on your machine via a bundled llama-server sidecar. Your diffs never leave your device.

Built with: Tauri 2 · Rust · libgit2 · llama.cpp · React · TypeScript · SQLite

text:
Still copy-pasting Git commands from Stack Overflow? Let’s change that. 🚀

Whether you're a developer, team lead, or tech founder, mastering Git and GitHub isn’t optional anymore — it’s foundational.

Over the years, I’ve realized that strong Git habits don’t just prevent chaos… they accelerate collaboration.

Here are a few Git commands every professional should truly understand (not just memorize):

🔹 git clone – Start strong. Clone repositories the right way and set up clean working environments.
 🔹 git branch – Work smarter. Feature branches keep development isolated and organized.
 🔹 git checkout / git switch – Move efficiently between branches.
 🔹 git add & git commit – Commit with intention. Clear, meaningful commit messages save hours later.
 🔹 git pull – Stay updated without breaking your local workflow.
 🔹 git push – Share progress and collaborate effectively.
 🔹 git merge & git rebase – Understand the difference. One preserves history; the other keeps it clean.

But knowing commands is only half the story.

Managing GitHub projects effectively is where real impact happens.

Here’s what elevates teams:

✅ Structured repositories with clear README documentation
 ✅ Well-defined branching strategies (e.g., feature, develop, main)
 ✅ Meaningful pull requests with proper descriptions
 ✅ Code reviews that focus on quality, not ego
 ✅ GitHub Issues & Project Boards to track progress transparently
 ✅ Automated workflows with GitHub Actions for CI/CD

When Git is used strategically, it becomes more than version control — it becomes a collaboration engine.

If you’re mentoring junior developers or leading a team, invest time in teaching Git fundamentals properly. It pays dividends in productivity and code quality.

💬 What’s one Git lesson you learned the hard way?

text:
Hey everyone! I'm looking for my next adventure in test automation.
After an incredible but way too short run at SELF ID, building mobile test frameworks for our React Native app, I'm ready to bring my expertise to a new team that values quality, automation, and collaboration.

What I bring to the table:
With a decade of experience as an automation developer, I don't just write tests. Embedded with a development team, I learn about the wants and needs of the stakeholders - the developers, the designers, the business analysts, the business itself - and construct a test automation framework, two week sprint by two week sprint, that truly fits their needs.

The quicker the automation framework is stood up, the quicker I can get to the truly fun stuff: Making sure the brand-new untested features fresh off the developer's local machine meets not just the spec, wireframes, requirements, and design, but also to make sure it matches those undocumented expectations that were discussed but may have not been carefully documented... something that AI will never be able to do.

My most recent project? DetoxDemo (https://lnkd.in/e_byEWxb) - a complete mobile automation framework showcasing:
* Mobile test automation with Detox + TypeScript for React Native iOS apps
* Page Object architecture that keeps tests clean and maintainable
* Allure Reports integration with visual test results published via CI/CD
* GitHub Actions workflows with configurable test execution options

On top of my extensive software testing experience, the last ten years has been focused on test automation. I've built automation frameworks from scratch at SELF Id and ThreatStack. I’ve created automated development courses for Test Automation University. I’ve written extensively about testing (check out my blog "Adventures in Automation"), and organized the Ministry of Testing - Boston meetup for years.

What I'm looking for:
Software Engineer in Test or SDET roles where I can design automation strategies, sharing my testing worldview with developers, and build frameworks that the entire team can use to check their work before merging into main. Remote work is preferred, but I am open to hybrid opportunities in the Boston area or Southeastern Massachusetts.

My toolbox includes: Detox, some Playwright, Selenium WebDriver, Ruby/Capybara, Java, TypeScript/JavaScript, React Native, CI/CD pipelines, and Allure Reports. But more importantly - I know how to research new tools, validate stakeholder needs, and implement solutions that fit your tech stack.
If you know of opportunities, I'd love to connect. Drop me a message or comment below. I'm always happy to chat about testing, automation, or that one flaky test that's driving you crazy.

I will be speaking at Joe Colantonio TestGuild in April 2026. See you then!

text:
GitHub Actions macOS-15 Breaking Changes

If your pipeline stopped working in the last three days and you still looking around, that is because GitHub Actions recently reduced iOS runtime support on macOS 15 runners to only the three most recent Xcode versions due to disk space constraints.

For example, the default Xcode 16.0 no longer includes iOS 18.0 SDK and that issue raised already as many engineers use the default Xcode version on macOS 15 and for so we have to simply enforce the pipeline to select one of the latest Xcode versions.

GitHub will make Xcode 16.4 the default on August 25, 2025, which should resolve this issue permanently since 16.4 includes full iOS runtime support.

This affects Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, and other CI services using GitHub's runner images. Links to issue discussion, GitHub's official policy changes, and technical details are in the comments below.

text:
The GitHub iOS team has optimized iOS app testing by reducing CI pipeline time through new runner features! they achieved faster test execution, better resource efficiency, and a more reliable workflow. 

This update shortens feedback loops, improving iOS developers' productivity and overall app quality. 

Apple silicon hosts allowed the team to scale their jobs out across many shorter, faster steps, with GitHub Actions abstracting over the complexity of distributing CI jobs.

Additionally, they divided each module’s CI into two separate steps, build and test, using xcodebuild’s build-without-testing and test-without-building. 

This helped the team identify long-running unit tests or highlight fast unit tests that finished in seconds, saving time by focusing only on building or testing when needed.

Check out GitHub’s blog to see how they implemented these advancements and the impact on iOS CI/CD workflows.

text:
I paused KMP because I didn’t have a Mac. CI just removed that blocker 👇

I migrated an Android codebase to Kotlin Multiplatform (KMP) and set up CI on macos-14 runners (GitHub Actions). The iOS side builds cleanly in CI (no local Mac). This is a first milestone, not “full iOS support.”

What’s shipping (Debug):
 • shared-debug-xcframework: universal bundle (sim + device) to Embed & Sign in Xcode.
 • shared-ios-sim-framework: simulator-only .framework for quick wiring.
 • Gradle steps compile device + simulator, run sim tests, and assemble the XCFramework.

Why this matters:
 • Validate iOS build health early and unblock shared-module work.
 • Keep velocity on Windows/Linux; defer hardware spend.
 • Produce reproducible artifacts.

Next step: add a minimal iOS container that calls the shared App() entry point to prove end-to-end delivery. Release builds + SPM binary target come after.

TL;DR: iOS compilation via CI on macOS runners; XCFramework + sim framework artifacts ready for Xcode.

Question: Any gotchas when wiring a Debug XCFramework into a thin Xcode shell, or tips for scaling Kotlin/Native caches on CI?

🚀 Automating Multi-Platform Builds with GitHub Actions 🌍📦
Imagine pushing your code once and having it seamlessly built for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android—without manual intervention! This is the power of CI/CD automation with GitHub Actions. 🛠️
This diagram showcases a GitHub-driven CI/CD pipeline that automates multi-platform builds. Here's how it works:
✅ A developer pushes code to GitHub
 ✅ GitHub Actions triggers workflows for different environments
 ✅ Builds are generated for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android
 ✅ The artifacts are packaged and prepared for deployment
 ✅ Mobile builds are directly pushed to the App Store and Google Play 📲
Why does this matter?
 💡 Efficiency – No more manual builds for each platform
 💡 Consistency – Ensures uniform deployments across all OS
 💡 Speed – Reduces release cycles from days to minutes
 💡 Scalability – Supports teams working on diverse platforms
This is the real power of DevOps automation in action! Have you implemented GitHub Actions for multi-platform builds yet? Share your experiences!

text:
“Just build a native app.”


That’s the advice I keep seeing from people who’ve never had to weigh up the cost of maintaining two separate codebases with a small team.

We’re turning our SaaS platform into an iOS app right now. And we’re using a cross-platform framework to do it.
Not because it’s trendy. Because it’s the right call for a small team that needs to ship fast and iterate without doubling the workload.

Native is great if you’ve got the team and the budget. Most companies don’t. And the gap between native and cross-platform in 2026 is nowhere near what the purists want you to believe.

The best technical decisions aren’t about what’s theoretically ideal. They’re about what actually gets into your users’ hands while the business can still afford to keep building.

Perfect is the enemy of shipped. Every time.

text:
Your iOS app doesn't fail because of bad Swift code. It fails because of bad backend integration.

Most founders assume the hard part is building the app. Then they hit the backend wall.

What actually happens:

The API documentation is incomplete or outdated.
Response times are slow. Error handling is inconsistent.
Authentication breaks in production but worked in testing.

Result: 3-6 weeks added to the timeline. Sometimes more.

Backend integration is the hidden cost that kills MVP momentum. Founders underestimate this complexity by 300-400%.

The "simple" API integration that was supposed to take 3 days? It takes 2 weeks. The timeline slips. The budget stretches. The launch date moves again.

Speed matters when backend integration is built into the process from day one. When the iOS developer has seen these problems before and knows how to architect around flaky APIs, slow endpoints, and poor documentation.

What backend integration challenge has added the most time to your project?

text:
🚀 Reducing App Launch Time in SwiftUI (iOS)

App launch time is your product’s first performance review. Users don’t measure milliseconds — they feel responsiveness. A slow first frame creates friction before your app even delivers value. 

Here’s how I approach launch optimization in SwiftUI apps:

🔎 Measure first
Use Xcode Instruments (App Launch + Time Profiler) and add os_signpost to track key milestones. Never optimize blindly.

⚡ Keep @main lightweight
Avoid heavy dependency injection, analytics setup, database migrations, or remote config calls during launch. Nothing non-essential should block the first frame.

🧱 Design a simple first view
Minimize deep hierarchies, expensive modifiers, and heavy .onAppear logic. Render fast — enhance after.

🧵 Respect the main thread
Move networking, JSON decoding, and disk I/O to background tasks using Swift Concurrency.

🕒 Defer strategically
Lazy load services and preload secondary features after the UI is visible.

📊 Monitor in production
Simulator results lie. Track real user launch metrics using analytics tools and continuously iterate.

A simple rule: meaningful UI in under 2 seconds. 

SwiftUI is declarative — but performance discipline is still architectural.

text:
How I built a iOS App Cloner that lets me install multiple instances of the same app on a device.

This will be split into 3 parts, 

On the first part we will discuss how we can simply clone a app, on the second part I will explain how to separate the sandboxes and secure storages iPhone offers and the third part I will explain spoofing the location or setting app level proxies. 

The problem:
iOS ties each app to a unique bundle ID = one instance. Want two? you need a second distinct identity to identify the app.

To do this it's very simple, iOS apps or IPA files are simply zip files. Renaming a .ipa to a .zip file allows you to view and extract the main binary along with its assets. 

Our main target here though would be the info.plist which will tell the iOS's IPA parser on how to parse the app. Inside the extracted app, you'll find the Info.plist — think of it as the app's ID card. It contains the CFBundleIdentifier (the bundle ID), the app's display name, and which binary to execute.

To clone the app, we just need to change that bundle ID to something unique. If the original is com.example.myapp, we mutate it into something like com.fxample.myapp — a deterministic change based on a seed number. Same seed, same clone. Every time.

We also append the seed to the display name so you can tell your clones apart on the home screen. Clone 1, Clone 2 — you get the idea.
But here's the catch — iOS apps are code signed. The moment you touch anything inside the bundle, the signature breaks and iOS refuses to install it.

So after modifying the plist, we re-sign the binary using ldid, an open-source tool that handles ad-hoc code signing. We also rewrite the app's entitlements — the permissions blob embedded in the binary — to match our new bundle ID.

Finally, we zip everything back up into a .ipa and it's ready to install.
That's it for Part 1. With just a plist edit, a re-sign, and a repackage, you now have two completely independent instances of the same app sitting on one device.

In Part 2, we'll go deeper — how do we make sure each clone gets its own keychain, its own app group containers, and its own sandbox? Because right now, they share the same identity under the hood, and that causes problems.

Stay tuned.

Checkout my GitHub for the scripts
https://lnkd.in/eCSp-sQR

text:
DevOps is not just about tools. It is about mindset also...




As part of my new client project, another key implementation is blue green deployment.

Instead of updating a live application directly, two identical environments are maintained. 

This allows automatic rolling updates reducing the risk of downtime.

🟦 Blue runs the current live version.

🟩 Green contains the new release.

Traffic is only switched to the new environment once it has been tested and confirmed to be healthy. 

If something goes wrong, traffic can quickly move back to the previous version.

This approach helps achieve

• Safer deployments
• Minimal downtime
• Faster rollback
• More reliable releases

For me, this is where DevOps goes beyond technical skills.

It is about designing systems with reliability in mind, planning for failure, and reducing risk before users are ever affected.

The goal is not just to deploy software, but to deploy it safely and consistently.

text:
Things I wish someone had told me before building a mobile app from an existing SaaS product:

→ Don’t try to port every feature. Field users need 20% of what your desktop product does. Find that 20% and make it bulletproof.

→ Offline matters more than you think. A field sales rep with no signal and a loading spinner is a field sales rep who stops using your app.

→ The App Store review process will test your patience. Build it into your timeline or it will blow your deadline.

→ Your backend isn’t ready for mobile. API calls that work fine on desktop with fast broadband feel painfully slow on 4G. Optimise before you launch, not after.

→ Cross-platform frameworks have trade-offs. Accept them early. Fighting the framework is slower than working with it.

We’re in the middle of this build right now. 

Some of these I knew going in. Some I learned the hard way this month.

What would you add to this list?

text:
For years, I thought I was doing DevOps right.

CI/CD pipelines ✅
Terraform automation ✅
Kubernetes deployments ✅

But something was missing.

I was deploying infrastructure — not building systems developers actually depended on.

So I built SWIPE: a Self-service Workload Infrastructure & Platform Engine on AWS EKS.

The goal? A developer should be able to deploy an application without touching infrastructure.

Here's what I learned building it:

→ Kubernetes problems are usually platform problems
→ Complexity is the enemy of good platforms
→ Make it work → Make it safe → Make it fast
→ The best DevOps work is invisible

The biggest mindset shift wasn't technical — it was this:

Stop asking "how do I deploy this?"
Start asking "how can developers deploy without needing me?"

That question changes everything.

📖 Full breakdown in my latest Medium article below.

text:
You built an iOS app with ChatGPT in 3 hours. It looks perfect in the simulator. Then you tried to submit it to the App Store.

AI tools like Bolt.new and Cursor can generate a working prototype faster than ever. The demo works. The UI looks clean. Your investors are impressed.

But here's what AI doesn't know:

Apple's 200+ review guidelines. Security best practices for user data. How to structure code someone can maintain next month. The difference between "works on my laptop" and "10,000 users trust it with their payment info."

The gap between prototype and production isn't about making it work. It's about making it work safely, pass App Store review, and survive your first feature update without collapsing.

AI is incredible for speed. Humans are still required for judgment.

SwiftUI development studios in 2026 use AI as an accelerant, not a replacement. The result: 7-14 day builds with production-grade code, not 3-hour prototypes that require a full rewrite.

What's the real cost of going fast without going right?

text:
𝐒𝐡𝐢𝐩𝐩𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚𝐧 𝐚𝐩𝐩 𝐨𝐧 𝐦𝐨𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐢𝐞𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐦𝐨𝐬𝐭 𝐩𝐞𝐨𝐩𝐥𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐤. 𝐌𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐭 𝐢𝐬𝐧’𝐭. 𝐓𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐢𝐬 𝐝𝐚𝐲 𝟑𝟒/𝟒𝟐 𝐨𝐟 𝐛𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚 𝐏𝐌 𝐭𝐨𝐨𝐥 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐬𝐭𝐮𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐩𝐮𝐛𝐥𝐢𝐜.

Today I thought about what it would actually take to publish this PM tool on iOS and Android.

Technically, the hardest part is already done. The backend exists, the APIs are there, and the logic lives outside the interface.

If I had to ship tomorrow, the steps would be simple.

𝐅𝐨𝐫 𝐢𝐎𝐒: Xcode and an Apple Developer account. Build the interface in Swift, connect it to Supabase and the existing APIs, then go through Apple’s review process.
𝐅𝐨𝐫 𝐀𝐧𝐝𝐫𝐨𝐢𝐝: Android Studio and a Google Play Developer account. Same idea, different ecosystem.

The real constraints are not technical. App store rules. Updates. Maintenance. And making sure AI features behave correctly on mobile networks.

Shipping is fast. Owning the app long term is the real commitment.

You can explore the web version here:
👉 https://www.my-pm-tool.com

text:
🚀 Building Reliable Software Delivery with CI/CD on AWS ☁️⚙️

Deploying an application is one thing — delivering it reliably and consistently is another.

To deepen my DevOps capabilities alongside my cloud engineering focus, I implemented an end-to-end CI/CD pipeline that automates the delivery of a containerized application from source code to Kubernetes on AWS.

This article explores how Jenkins, Docker, AWS ECR, and EKS work together to create a repeatable, production-ready delivery workflow.

Key highlights include:

⚙️ Automated versioning for traceable deployments

 🐳 Immutable container image tagging for consistency

 ☸️ Kubernetes deployment to AWS EKS

 🔐 Secure credential management and repeatable builds

 🔁 Git version alignment with running workloads

The result is a delivery pipeline that improves reliability, traceability, and deployment confidence.

text:
From Web App to App Stores the real grind behind launching a SaaS 🚀

Most people see the final product in the App Store.

They don’t see what happens before that.

Right now, I’m taking TransportYours™ from a web app to full mobile presence on Android and iOS.

Here’s what that actually looks like:

• Web app built and iterated fast
• Wrapped into mobile using Median
• Generating Android AAB builds
• Uploading to Google Play Console
• Handling store compliance & release notes
• Apple Store verification process
• Debugging ADB connection issues
• Fixing signing keys and build errors
• Testing on real devices

It’s not glamorous.

Sometimes it’s:
“Why is the emulator not detected?”
“Why is ADB not showing devices?”
“Why is this build rejected?”

But this is what building means.

No-code and AI help you move fast.

But app store deployment?
That still requires precision, patience, and understanding the system.

Going from:
💻 transportyours.app
to
📱 Available in Google Play & Apple App Store

is a different level of commitment.

Distribution matters.

Because in logistics, if drivers can’t access your app easily, you don’t exist.

Behind every “simple” startup launch,
there are hundreds of invisible technical decisions.

Still building.
Still shipping.

text:
𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆𝗲𝗱 𝗮 𝗖𝗮𝗻𝗱𝘆𝗖𝗿𝘂𝘀𝗵 𝗔𝗽𝗽 𝗼𝗻 𝗔𝗪𝗦 𝗘𝗞𝗦 𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮 𝗙𝘂𝗹𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗦𝗲𝗰𝗢𝗽𝘀 𝗣𝗶𝗽𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲. 𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗜 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗲𝗱

Most developers write code. The best developers ship it securely, automatically, and at scale. But here's the truth nobody tells you: 𝘄𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗶𝘀 𝗼𝗻𝗹𝘆 𝗵𝗮𝗹𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗹𝗲. 𝗚𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗯𝗹𝘆 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗴𝗮𝗽𝘀? 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗵𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘀.

What if every push to your repository could automatically build, test, scan for vulnerabilities, and deploy your application without you lifting a finger? That's exactly what I built.

My colleagues and I, Oranekwulu Mary-Ann, Ohia Uche recently completed a DevSecOps project deploying a CandyCrush application on AWS Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) using a fully automated GitHub Actions pipeline. This wasn't just about getting an app running in the cloud. It was about building a production-grade workflow that mirrors what top engineering teams use every day.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗶𝗽𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀:

 • 𝗖𝗜/𝗖𝗗 — Every code change automatically triggers a build, test, and deployment process with zero manual intervention.

 • 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗦𝗰𝗮𝗻𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 — Vulnerabilities are caught at the earliest stage, long before they reach production.

 • 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗤𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗖𝗵𝗲𝗰𝗸𝘀 — Keeping the codebase clean and consistent as the project scales.

 • 𝗔𝗪𝗦 𝗘𝗞𝗦 — A managed, scalable Kubernetes cluster handling all the infrastructure complexity.

 • 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 — The automation engine tying the entire pipeline together.

The core principle here is shifting left, embedding security and quality into the beginning of development, not the end. The result is faster releases, fewer incidents, and a team that ships with confidence.

If you're starting your DevOps journey, start building pipelines. The mindset shift alone is worth it.

text:
Overcoming Deployment Failures

Deployments often encounter failures due to unforeseen issues in production, this can result in downtime and erode user trust.

To combat this, adopt Continuous Deployment (CD) practices. These involve:

1. Automation: Implement automated tests to ensure code quality before deployment.

2. Regular Deployments: Schedule frequent deployments to make changes smaller and easier to manage.


3. Canary Releases: Roll out new features to a small set of users initially to monitor performance and catch issues early.


4. Blue-Green Deployments: Use alternating environments (blue and green) to minimize risks during deployment and facilitate quick rollbacks if necessary.


5. Monitoring and Rollback Strategies: Establish robust monitoring to detect issues rapidly and have strategies in place for quick recovery from failures.

What other methods have you used to resolve deployment failures

text:
I’ve been building and shipping web apps as side projects for a while. Lately, I’ve been using Cursor + Claude to move faster.
I’d never touched iOS or Swift.
Tried once a few months back couldn't get past the build errors and shelved it. Gave it another shot with Opus 4.6. Shipped a full native iOS app in 2 weekends.
It’s called Justo, a free tourist safety app starting with Rio de Janeiro as the first city, with more cities planned.
Tourists commonly deal with the same problems: safety issues, price gouging, and scams. I wanted to build something practical around that, and Rio was a great first place to start because there’s solid public data to build on and the app can stay free.
Most of the build was done with Claude.
It includes:
 • A safety/incident heat map using public data and community reports
 • Crowdsourced alerts for nearby incidents
 • Price-gouging reports to help tourists sanity-check pricing
 • Automatic face blur on photos (privacy-by-default)
 • A one-tap SOS flow with emergency numbers and key Portuguese phrases
What made the workflow smoother was writing things down. I kept a CLAUDE.md for project context, a MEMORY.md for recurring gotchas, and docs for auth setup and App Store submission.
A few iOS things that surprised me coming from web:
 • Supabase/Postgres timestamps can include fractional seconds, and Swift decoding will fail unless you handle it
 • Bundling config isn’t like web; files can get skipped unless you explicitly include them as build resources
 • TestFlight/App Store distribution requires the Apple Developer Program, plus profiles, certificates, and entitlements
 • Rebuilt the Explore tab halfway through and switched it to a venues-first approach with Google Places
It’s on the App Store now. If you’ve been sitting on a side project because you think you need to learn everything upfront, you can pick it up as you go.
What are you building?

App Store link in the first comment.

text:
🚨 Why is DevOps cloud security the cornerstone of modern IT success? 🚨

In today's fast-paced digital landscape, securing your 
hashtag
#DevOps pipelines in the 
hashtag
#Cloud isn't just an option—it's a necessity.

From the deep dive on devops.com, here’s why it matters:

🔍 Cloud environments introduce unique security challenges that traditional methods can miss.
🔍 Continuous integration and deployment amplify risks without proper safeguards.
🔍 Misconfigurations remain one of the top threats causing vulnerabilities.

Key insights:
• Security must be integrated early in the DevOps lifecycle to reduce risks.
• Automated security testing in CI/CD pipelines helps catch issues before deployment.
• Collaboration between DevOps and security teams is crucial for effective protection.

Actionable tips to fortify your DevOps cloud security:
• Implement automated security scans in your CI/CD workflows.
• Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools carefully and validate configurations.
• Prioritize education and training on cloud-specific security risks.
• Continuously monitor and audit your cloud environments.

Deploy to Live Codebases
Most developers practice in isolation.

They build projects.
They complete courses.
They simulate real work.

But simulation is not production.

Real growth begins when your code:
• Ships to live infrastructure
 • Gets reviewed under strict standards
 • Impacts real users
 • Can break real systems

That is the difference.
Inside our system, you don’t build demos.

You deploy features into an active SaaS environment.
Your pull requests are reviewed. Your changes are measured. Your impact is visible.

No sandbox. No fake deadlines. No simulated pressure.

Just structured execution inside a real product.
If you want to bridge the gap between learning and production-readiness, you must ship where it matters.

Live codebases change how you think.
And that changes everything.

✅ Configured environment variables to dynamically control the application UI

A major focus of this project was understanding secure configuration management in cloud environments.
Instead of hardcoding values inside the application:
• I used Azure App Service environment variables
• Configuration changes were applied without redeploying
• Sensitive settings were kept outside the source code
This approach demonstrates real-world cloud security best practices such as:
✔ Protecting secrets from exposure in repositories
✔ Supporting secure CI/CD workflows
✔ Enabling safe runtime configuration changes
✔ Following cloud-native security design principles

text:
Something I’ve been learning recently:

State management becomes your biggest problem before performance does.

In small apps, state feels easy.

In real-world iOS apps?
Multiple screens.
Async data.
Caching.
Background refresh.
Deep links.
Feature flags.

Suddenly, state is everywhere.

Most bugs aren’t UI bugs.
They’re inconsistent state bugs.

Wrong source of truth.
Outdated data.
Unexpected re-renders.
Race conditions.

The real learning for me:
Architecture isn’t about patterns.
It’s about controlling state complexity.

If state is predictable, your app feels stable.
If state is messy, nothing scales.

That shift changed how I design features now.

text:
Your backend goes down every time you deploy. Here's how I fixed that on AWS — Using CodeDeploy.

Imagine you are shopping on Amazon and suddenly the website goes down. Or you are mid-stream on Netflix, and it just stops. That is exactly what happens when a backend is deployed on a single server — every update means downtime, and downtime means lost users, lost trust, and lost revenue.
And what if the deployment itself goes wrong? Now you're stuck with a broken app and scrambling to fix it manually.
This is the Single Point of Failure problem — and it's more common than you think.

I have written a blog with great details(step by step) for implementation: https://lnkd.in/eSf_Fsrj

The CI/CD pipeline I'm sharing today solves both. By deploying across multiple EC2 instances one at a time, your app stays live throughout the entire update — users never feel a thing. And if something goes wrong mid-deployment, AWS CodeDeploy automatically rolls back to the last working version before any real damage is done.
No downtime. No panic. Just a smooth, safe release — every single time. 

Here is the Workflow:

Code push triggers GitHub Actions
 │
 ▼
Build Docker image
 │
 ▼
Push image to Docker Hub
 │
 ▼
Upload appspec.yml & scripts to S3
 │
 ▼
Notify AWS CodeDeploy
 │
 ▼
CodeDeploy pulls from S3
 │
 ▼
Rolling deployment to EC2
 │
 ▼
◆ Deploy to one EC2 instance
 │
 ▼
◆ Instance deployment success?
 ├── NO → Pull image, stop old, start new container ---→ retry
 └── YES → Move to next instance
 │
 ▼
 ◆ All instances updated?
 ├── NO → loops back
 └── YES → ✅ Deployment complete

You might reasonably ask: why do we need to upload files to S3? CodeDeploy could just pull from GitHub directly. The answer is reliability and atomicity.
S3 is designed for extremely high-durability storage (eleven nines of durability, 99.999999999%). By uploading a zip archive of your repository to S3 at this exact moment in time, you are creating an immutable deployment artifact — a precise snapshot of the code and scripts that correspond to this specific commit.

text:
When I decided to publish my first .NET MAUI app for iOS, I thought it would be as simple as clicking “Deploy” and celebrating. Spoiler: it wasn’t.

Between certificates, provisioning profiles, builds that only failed on Fridays, and Apple’s cryptic messages, I started writing everything down. Not as a super technical guide, but more like a survival diary.

Now I’ve decided to share it. Not because I’ve become some kind of guru (far from it), but because if this trauma compilation helps someone avoid wasting an entire weekend decoding Xcode errors, Visual Studio bugs, and Apple responses… it’s already worth it.

If it helps you, let me know. If you find something wrong, tell me – the goal is to improve and help more people.

If it doesn’t help… at least pretend it was cool (Just kidding… lol)

text:
📱 My first iOS app - how it actually gets from laptop to App Store

I had this idea to build an iOS app - not just as a side project to maybe make some money, but out of pure curiosity.
I've spent 9 years building for the web and deploying apps in all kinds of ways…
 …but I had no clue what it actually takes to get a mobile app from your laptop to the App Store.
Here’s what I’ve learned so far 👇

🚀 The journey
1️⃣ You make a local build when your app feels "ready to see the world."
2️⃣ You push it to App Store Connect.
3️⃣ You create a release there.

At this point you might think - "Cool, real users can download it now."
Nope 😅
Then comes the Apple Review.
After you create a release in App Store Connect, it's tested and reviewed by Apple.
Only if it passes all their checks, you're allowed to distribute it.
Your freshly baked app is finally live! 🎉

🔁 "Oh shit, here we go again..."
You spot a few tweaks you want to make.
You fix them, prepare a new build… and then?
Yep - another release. Another review. Another wait.

🕒 The real pace
To give you a sense of speed:
For another project I host on Railway (a SaaS built with Ruby on Rails), it takes me less than 3 minutes to deploy a new feature.
For the iOS app?
- First release: ~2 days for approval
- Next ones: ~24 hours
- Most recent: almost instant, just 5 hours! 🎉
Feels like progress - and a good reminder that not all "deploys" are created equal 😄

text:
If you think AI is going to destroy B2B apps, just wait until you try to: 

1. Deploy your app to a real cloud service like AWS. Good luck debugging for days. AWS is like mailing a package at the U.S. Postal Service -- the worst service for a low price. 

2. Try to package up your app as a MacOS application. Get ready for insane bureaucratic delays in your app "notarization" process - something Apple requires to let people download and use your app on MacOS. 

3. Try to share your mobile app with friends. Get ready for literally a manual human review of your iOS app before the Apple Store agrees to host it. And that's after dealing with a terrible UX at appstoreconnect.apple.com that feels like a time machine back to 2013. 

I'm excited about the AI-powered revolution in computing to come, as we're ready for a long-overdue overturning of the quasi-monopolistic tech giants that -- with few regular people noticing -- have turned tech development into a bureaucratic nightmare for developers. 

What's slowing down tech development today? It's not code. It's not ideas. It's the institutional barriers that have calcified slow development practices in recent years.

text:
🚀 How I Reduced Android App Startup Time by 30%! 🚀

As Android developers, app startup time is one of the most critical factors for user experience. A slow app can frustrate users and increase churn, while a fast app keeps them engaged from the first tap.

Here’s what I implemented to achieve ~30% faster startup:

✨ Key Optimizations:
 1. Lazy Initialization – Only load objects when needed instead of on app launch.
 2. Optimize Layouts – Flatten view hierarchies and use ConstraintLayout effectively.
 3. Avoid Heavy Work on Main Thread – Move database queries, network calls, and complex calculations to background threads.
 4. Use Splash Wisely – Keep splash screens minimal and avoid unnecessary operations.
 5. ProGuard / R8 – Enable code shrinking and obfuscation to reduce APK size and improve performance.

💡 Tip: Always profile your app with Android Studio Profiler or Startup Profiler to identify bottlenecks.

A fast app is a happy user! 🚀

text:
Emerging Android threat worth monitoring — PromptSpy isn't confirmed at-scale in the wild yet, but identified distribution infrastructure suggests active testing, and the AI-assisted persistence mechanism makes it unusually resilient once installed.
Key protections now:
Don't sideload APKs — both known distribution domains deliver outside Google Play. Enforce policy against installing apps from unknown sources, especially on corporate devices.
Restrict Accessibility permissions — this malware's entire capability depends on Accessibility Service access. Audit which apps hold it and treat unsolicited requests as a red flag.
Enable Google Play Protect — it now detects known PromptSpy variants and provides automatic blocking on devices with Play Services enabled.
Safe Mode is your remediation path — standard uninstall is blocked by invisible overlays. If infected, reboot into Safe Mode to remove the app through system settings.
Watch this one closely. AI-assisted malware that adapts to any device UI without hardcoded selectors is a meaningful evolution in mobile threat capability.

text:
Android is supposed to be THE open mobile platform. It’s literally called the Android Open Source Project (AOSP).

Now Google is proposing a policy (effective September 2026) that would require developers to:
- Register directly with Google 
- Pay a publishing fee
- Submit government identification

What does this mean? This means the death of alternative app stores and distribution channels like F-Droid, IzzyOnDroid, and even Github project APKs. More private information and credentials collected. Raises the barrier to entry for application development.

This is being justified as a security improvement, but malware already slips through app store like the Play Store and even Apple's app stores. Centralization doesn’t magically eliminate risk; it just consolidates control.

If this passes, the primary motivations choosing Android over iOS are eliminated: openness and user choice.

As developers, we should care about:
- Platform neutrality
- Distribution freedom
- The right for users to decide what runs on their devices

If this matters to you, take a look:
https://lnkd.in/e4f-Tt69

text:
Android Studio Panda 2 introduces a new way to build Android apps.

Google has shipped some genuinely exciting agentic features in this release, and I think they deserve more attention from the dev community.

Here's what's new:

✨ AI-Powered New Project Flow — describe your app in a prompt, optionally attach images for style inspiration, and the agent builds a working prototype for you. It writes the code, handles build errors automatically, and even deploys to the emulator to verify each screen looks right. From idea to running app.

🔁 Version Upgrade Assistant — updating dependencies used to mean hours of chasing cascading build errors. Now the agent handles it: it reads the errors, adjusts versions, rebuilds, and loops until everything compiles. You focus on features, not dependency conflicts.

🧠 Gemini 3.1 Pro — the latest model is now available in Android Studio via your API key, with improved reasoning for complex bugs, code completion, and UI logic.

The thing that excites me most? This isn't just autocomplete or a chatbot. It's an agent that can plan, build, test, and self-correct. That's a meaningful shift in what an IDE can do.

text:
Android's Shocking App Change 🚨 🤯

Google is implementing changes to its Android app distribution process, a shift occurring nearly twenty years after the launch of the operating system. Developers outside the Google Play Store will now be required to register with their real names and pay a fee. Failure to comply will block their apps from installation on virtually all Android devices. This move follows Google’s daily scanning of 350 billion apps, including those sideloaded. Concerns have been raised by organizations like TheGuardian Project, which supports developers facing legal restrictions. Verification aims to curb malware, but also potentially impacts legitimate applications, particularly those from developers in sanctioned countries. This represents a significant evolution in the platform’s security model, reflecting a heightened focus on control within the Android ecosystem.

📰 Full story+ 🎧 multilingual audio summaries: https://lnkd.in/ezYmkpfN

It looks like you've got a bit of "app bloat" going on—specifically with those three different QR scanners! Most modern Android phones actually have that feature built directly into the camera or the quick settings panel, so you probably don't need any of those extra apps taking up space.
​If you're looking to tidy up or find something specific, here is a quick breakdown of what’s on your screen:
​Redundant Apps
​QR Scanners: You have three separate apps for this (QR Scanner, QR Scanner, and QR Scanner & Barcode Scan...). You can likely uninstall all of them.
​Weather: You have both the default Weather app and WeatherBug.
​Entertainment & Media
​Video: Pluto TV, Tubi (great for free movies), and TikTok.
​Games: Solitaire, Tap Color Pro, Tile Explorer, and Vita Mahjong.
​Social/Comm: Telegram, Pinterest, and Pandora for music.
​Utility & Shopping
​Tools: Recorder, Settings, and Thermal Effect Camera Filters.
​Shopping: Temu and theSCOOP.
​A quick tip for decluttering:
If you want to remove those extra QR scanners, just long-press the app icon and select Uninstall. This will free up storage and make your app drawer much easier to navigate.
​Would you like me to help you find where the built-in QR scanner is on your phone so you can safely delete those extra apps?

text:
Take a look at your Firebase analytics today.

If you still see a significant chunk of your mobile users running Android 11, your app's security might be an illusion for them.

In the rush to grow active user bases, many mobile teams are terrified to drop support for older operating systems. 

Supporting an End-of-Life (EOL) operating system does not mean your app is user-friendly. It is reckless.

Android 11 stopped receiving official security patches two full years ago. When users stay on it, they aren't just missing out on new features; they are carrying a ticking time bomb in their pockets.

Here is what you are exposing your app (and your users) to by keeping Android 11 in your support matrix:

1️⃣ "Forever-Day" Exploits: When a new vulnerability is discovered in the Linux kernel today, modern OS versions get patched. Android 11 doesn't. That zero-day backdoor stays open permanently.

2️⃣ Overlay Attacks: Modern Android versions aggressively block fake UI overlays. Android 11 lacks these safeguards, making it incredibly easy for malware to draw a fake payment or login screen over your legitimate app.

3️⃣ Compromised Biometrics: Security standards rot. The biometric and Keystore protocols in Android 11 can no longer guarantee integrity against modern spoofing techniques.

You can build the most secure, heavily encrypted app in the world. But if it is running on a compromised operating system, your security is entirely bypassed at the hardware level. You are building a secure house on a rotting foundation.
It is time to have the hard conversation with Product and Leadership.

We need to stop enabling bad security hygiene. Force the upgrade, deprecate gracefully, and protect your users from themselves.

How frequently does your team review and enforce OS deprecation policies?

text:
It’s been nearly 20 years since 
hashtag
#Google revealed 
hashtag
#Android, which the company described as the first “truly open” mobile operating system, setting Google-powered phones apart from the iPhone’s aggressively managed experience. Over time, though, Android has become more aligned with Apple’s approach. Later this year, Google plans to seriously curtail that freedom in the name of security. Google will officially debut Android 
hashtag
#DeveloperVerification, which will require app makers outside the Play Store to register with their real names and pay a fee to Google. Failure to do so will block their apps from installation (sometimes called sideloading) on virtually all Android devices. This might make your phone a little 
hashtag
#safer, sure, but it won’t stop people from getting scammed. At the same time, it could rob the Android ecosystem of what made it ‘special’ in the first place. There were virtually no rules in the early days, allowing developers to publish apps that tinkered with undocumented system features, infringed on copyrights, and leveraged exploits to gain root access. Today, Google has numerous security layers that detect and remove malware, and that has undeniably made Android safer. Why is Google suddenly so interested in forcing these particular reforms on non-Google developers? Christoph Hebeisen, director of security intelligence research at Lookout, suggests there’s a bit of 
hashtag
#AppleEnvy happening. “I think Google probably looked at Apple and wondered ‘why has it worked for them?’” More from Ryan Whitwam: https://lnkd.in/et6XAuQA

text:
How Android Actually Runs Our Code

When I first started, I thought Android phones “understand” Kotlin or Java.
Turns out they don’t, at least, not the way we think. The CPU only understands machine code, simple ones and zeros.

Let’s imagine our source code as sentences written in Arabic:
- XML defines the UI, or Kotlin Compose defines what shows on screen
- Kotlin/Java handles the app logic
The Dude called CPU, doesn’t understand Arabic. It only understands a universal “sign language” (machine code: low level binary instructions,ones and zeros).
What happens next?

Compile to Bytecode: Kotlin is turned into Java bytecode
Like English, a universal recipe the machine can follow. Still not something the CPU can read.

Convert to DEX: Bytecode becomes DEX, Android’s optimized format
We can think of it as English translated into a shorthand that ART can process efficiently but still not enough for CPU

Package into APK:
Gradle bundles , .dex code ,Resources (images, strings), Manifest (app info)
Result → APK, the app we install

Install & Run:
ART (Android Runtime), like a sign language interpreter, translates DEX into native machine code. CPU executes it. User sees “Hello Android” on the screen.

Kotlin → Bytecode → DEX → ART → Machine Code → CPU → Screen

When it comes to Flutter, KMP (or others), the concept is the same: code becomes machine code. The details? That’s a story for another post.

🐞 If you spot any mistakes, happy to be corrected in the comments!

text:
Android users… we need to talk.

Not only are your texts green… apparently you’re a security liability now too. 😅

A newly reported Android malware strain is making the rounds, hiding in third-party app stores, preloaded apps, and sketchy downloads. Once installed, it can:

• Exfiltrate data
• Abuse permissions
• Maintain persistence
• Potentially open the door to additional payloads

So yes… your group chat may roast you for the green bubbles. But your SOC team? They’re roasting you for sideloading “FreeFlashlightProUltra.apk.”

📱 Reality check:
Mobile devices are now full-fledged endpoints. They hold MFA tokens, corporate email, Slack, customer data, and sometimes VPN access.
A compromised phone isn’t just a personal problem anymore, it’s an enterprise risk.

🔐 Quick reminders for orgs:
Enforce MDM/EDR on mobile
Block sideloading where possible
Monitor abnormal DNS & outbound traffic
Educate users (nicely… maybe 😄)

Android isn’t the villain. Poor hygiene is.

Stay patched. Stay skeptical. And maybe… embrace the blue bubble life.

text:
Deployment isn’t just releasing code it’s managing risk smartly.

Here’s a quick breakdown of popular deployment strategies every engineer should know:

 Blue/Green – Two identical environments. Switch traffic instantly for zero-downtime releases.

Canary – Roll out to a small % of users first. Monitor, then expand safely.

A/B Testing – Split traffic between versions to compare performance or user behaviour.

Feature Flags – Release code to production but enable features selectively. No redeploy needed.

Rolling Update – Gradually replace old instances with new ones. Smooth and resource-efficient.

Live A/B (Model/Service testing) – Run multiple versions in production to validate real-world impact.

The goal isn’t just faster releases it’s safer releases with real user confidence.

Which deployment strategy does your team rely on the most and why?

Comment below, or repost to share this with your DevOps/Engineering network.

Shruthi Chikkela

text:
🚨⚠️ DEADLY WARNING FOR ANDROID USERS ⚠️🚨
❌ STOP Installing Unknown APK Files ❌

📱 That “FREE MOD APK” you downloaded?
It could be monitoring you right now.

Cybercriminals don’t need physical access anymore.
They just need you to tap “Install.”


---

💀 What Malicious Apps Secretly Do:

🔴 Activate your camera & microphone silently
🔴 Read private chats and messages
🔴 Steal bank OTPs & saved passwords
🔴 Track your live location 24/7
🔴 Record your screen in the background
🔴 Access gallery, contacts & sensitive documents

Most high-risk apps don’t come from the official store.
They spread via Telegram groups, random download sites, cracked app forums, and “Premium Free” links.


---

⚠️ Reality Check

You install it.
You click “Allow All Permissions.”
You forget about it.

But the attacker doesn’t.

Your device becomes a remote-controlled endpoint.


---

🔥 Signs You May Already Be Compromised:

⚡ Sudden battery drain
⚡ Phone overheating without heavy usage
⚡ Unknown apps appearing
⚡ Unusual data usage spikes
⚡ Random pop-ups or background activity


---

🛡 PROTECT YOURSELF NOW:

✅ Never install unknown APK files
✅ Disable “Install from Unknown Sources”
✅ Review app permissions carefully
✅ Keep Android OS updated
✅ Enable and monitor Play Protect

Your phone is not just a device.
It’s your identity, finances, and digital footprint.

Don’t trade security for “free.”

text:
What I Learned Last Week That Every CTO and VP Eng Needs to Know.

Last week I went deep into mobile app security research. What I found should concern every engineering leader.
The uncomfortable truth: Your iOS and Android apps can be fully decompiled in hours. Not by nation-state hackers. By anyone with basic skills and AI tools.
Here's what could be extracted from production apps right now:
→ API keys and secrets
→ Authentication flows
→ Backend endpoints
→ Encryption keys
→ Business logic

The game has changed.
Tools like Frida, Ghidra, and latest AI coding assistants have democratized reverse engineering. What took specialists weeks now takes a motivated individual days.

I've put together a prioritized action plan:
🔴 IMMEDIATE (This Week)
□ Audit your mobile apps for hardcoded secrets
□ Inventory all API keys embedded in binaries
□ Check if credentials are in plaintext or weakly obfuscated
□ Review your SSL pinning implementation

🟡 SHORT-TERM (This Month)
□ Migrate secrets to runtime fetching (not embedded)
□ Implement Apple App Attest / Google Play Integrity
□ Add server-side validation for all sensitive operations
□ Deploy proper certificate pinning (server-controlled)
□ Implement jailbreak/root detection with server verification

🟢 LONG-TERM (6 Months)
□ Adopt a zero-trust mobile architecture
□ Implement device attestation for all API calls
□ Deploy behavioral analytics for anomaly detection
□ Consider commercial RASP solutions (Approov, Guardsquare, Promon)
□ Build server-side validation that assumes client compromise

The mindset shift:
Stop thinking "How do I protect my app?"
Start thinking "How do I build systems that remain secure when my app is fully compromised?"
Because it will be. It's just a matter of when.

text:
🚨 New Android Banking Trojan Alert – Massiv
Cybersecurity researchers at ThreatFabric have uncovered Massiv, a new Android trojan disguised as IPTV streaming apps.
This malware enables Device Takeover (DTO) attacks, allowing attackers to:
 • Remotely control infected devices
 • Intercept SMS & OTPs
 • Deploy fake banking overlays
 • Perform fraudulent transactions directly from the victim’s phone
Massiv abuses Android accessibility services and advanced UI-tree analysis to bypass screen protections making fraud appear legitimate to banks.
 📱 Targets: Mobile banking users
 🎯 Initial campaigns: Portugal & Greece
 ⚠️ Risk: Financial theft, account abuse, identity fraud
 The takeaway?
 Installing apps from unknown IPTV links can lead to full banking compromise.

text:
Proposed Architecture for Enterprise Application Development and Deployment

I’d like to share a clean and scalable architecture that I’ve been using in one of my enterprise apps built with Flutter and .NET.

Overview of the architecture:
Frontend (Flutter): Cross-platform development for iOS, Android, and Web using a single codebase.

Backend (.NET Core 9): RESTful APIs hosted on Azure App Services, connected to SQL Server and using Blob Storage for unstructured data.

Authentication & Security: JWT-based authentication with an API Gateway handling routing and validation.

DevOps & CI/CD: Using GitHub Actions with YAML-defined pipelines to deploy to DEV, QA, and PROD environments.

Observability: Full monitoring and diagnostics via Azure Application Insights.
This architecture supports scalability, clean separation of concerns, and rapid deployments with full visibility. It’s well-suited for applications requiring secure access, cloud-native features, and enterprise readiness.

If you're interested in feedback or implementation suggestions, happy to discuss.

text:
🚀 Introducing Flux Mobile CLI v0.2.0 – Deploy Flutter Apps to Production in ONE Command!

Tired of:
❌ Expensive CI/CD platforms ($$$)
❌ Slow cloud runners (queue times, cold starts)
❌ Complex YAML configs (debugging nightmares)
❌ Uploading your code to third-party servers?

Flux Mobile CLI is a FREE, open-source tool that runs entirely on YOUR machine.

✅ Build + Deploy in ONE unified command
✅ Interactive confirmation prompts (no accidental deploys!)
✅ Full iOS automation – even auto-submits to App Store review
✅ Full Android automation – deploy to any track
✅ Multi-locale release notes
✅ Flavor support (staging/production)
✅ 100% free – no subscriptions, no per-minute charges

Before:
$ fluxm build --release-type aab
$ fluxm deploy-android --track production
(Two steps, room for errors)

After:
$ fluxm release android --track production --notes "Bug fixes"
(One command. Done. ✨)

Perfect for:
🎯 Solo developers & small teams
🎯 Teams wanting full control over their deployments
🎯 Anyone tired of paying for slow CI/CD

👉 Get started: npm install -g flux-mobile-cli@beta
📖 Docs: https://lnkd.in/d2V9eHjw
